talk_title: High-plex 3D microscopy for spatial profiling of tumor microenvironments
tumor_types: melanoma; colorectal cancer; ovarian cancer; lung adenocarcinoma; tuberculosis
moa_summary: The presenter described cyclic immunofluorescence–based high‑plex 3D microscopy (up to 150 channels) applied to thick (35 µm) FFPE sections and light‑sheet fluorescence imaging, enabling whole‑cell morphology, subcellular organelle localization, and spatial context (e.g., cytotoxic synapse distance, tertiary lymphoid structure architecture) to be captured in three dimensions, which resolves misidentification and provides functional readouts that are impossible in standard 2D sections.
key_findings: Thick (35‑40 µm) FFPE sections retain ~70 % of cells intact versus <5 % in standard 5 µm sections, dramatically reducing lineage misidentification. | 3D imaging revealed that ~40 % of CD8+ T cells in tumors exhibit LAG‑3 staining only in deeper z‑planes, explaining false‑negative checkpoint marker detection in 2D slides. | Morphological analysis showed average T cells make 5–10 functional contacts with neighbors; synapse distances contract from ~1 µm to ~30 nm during cytolytic engagement. | Serial reconstruction of ~470 tertiary lymphoid structures in lung adenocarcinoma and tuberculosis demonstrated heterogeneous developmental stages within a single follicle, which 2D sections misclassify. | Light‑sheet 3D imaging of a 2 mm‑thick colon specimen captured ~30 million cells, allowing tracing of nerve‑immune interactions and vascular‑associated immune niches.

talk_title: AI approaches to quantitative analysis of 3D tissue imaging
moa_summary: The talk outlined how foundation models, segmentation networks, synthetic‑staining GANs and diffusion models, and optical‑flow based slice interpolation are applied to 2D and 3D histopathology data (H&E, cyclic IF, light‑sheet) to generate cell‑level segmentations, virtual multiplex stains, and fill missing z‑planes, thereby enabling quantitative morphometric and spatial analyses that were previously infeasible.
key_findings: Foundation models can embed H&E tiles into high‑dimensional vectors that predict patient outcomes and highlight prognostic regions such as venous invasion without additional training. | A GAN‑based pipeline converts quantitative phase images into realistic H&E, allowing existing H&E‑trained models to be applied to label‑free imaging. | Diffusion models successfully synthesize multiplex IHC (e.g., insulin/glucagon) from a single H&E slide, enabling retrospective multi‑omic analysis of archived specimens. | Optical‑flow algorithms reconstruct missing intermediate sections in CODA and Visium datasets, improving continuity for 3D reconstructions and spatial transcriptomics. | AI‑driven nucleus and tissue segmentation using models like Segment‑Anything achieve >90 % concordance with manual annotations across multiple organ systems.

talk_title: Integrating Digital Pathology, AI, and Molecular Diagnostics for Biomarker Development
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; colorectal cancer; soft tissue sarcoma; pan-cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; spatial_biology; biomarker_development; translational; platform_technology; basic_science; AI_ML
moa_summary: John Ifraty reviewed how AI image embeddings derived from H&E slides can capture morphologic correlates of actionable mutations (e.g., ALK fusions, MSI, EGFR) and discussed the need for clinical‑grade performance (high sensitivity, specificity, PPV). He highlighted that current AI models achieve AUROC ~0.9 for MSI but only ~0.6‑0.7 for most other mutations, emphasizing confounders, sample quality, and rare tumor entities as major barriers. He also described emerging multimodal and spatial platforms that combine H&E, multiplex immunofluorescence, and spatial transcriptomics to generate richer biomarkers, while noting throughput and reproducibility challenges.
key_findings: Clinical ALK FISH assay demonstrated 100% sensitivity, 99% specificity, and 100% PPV in validation against RNA‑Seq, setting a high bar for AI‑based predictors. | AI model trained on public colorectal cancer slides achieved AUROC ~0.9 for MSI prediction, the highest performance among mutation‑prediction tasks. | Predictive performance for EGFR, KRAS, PIK3CA, and other mutations ranged from AUROC 0.5‑0.7, indicating limited clinical utility at present. | Foundation models fine‑tuned on institution‑specific data (e.g., Campanella et al.) can raise EGFR prediction AUROC to ~0.9 and enable triage of lung cancer cases for rapid molecular testing. | ctDNA assay (Guardant360) identified ALK fusions in only ~50% of cases, illustrating that sub‑optimal sensitivity can still be useful for longitudinal monitoring. | Spatial multiplex IF and transcriptomics platforms (e.g., Singular Genomics 18‑plex IF + 500‑plex RNA) now achieve 10 samples per slide throughput, moving toward clinical feasibility.

talk_title: Integrating Epidemiology and Basic Science to Elucidate Prostate Cancer Inflammation, Telomere Biomarkers, Statin and Digoxin Repurposing
affiliation: Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Department of Epidemiology; Joint appointments in Oncology and Urology, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine
moa_summary: Platz integrates population‑based epidemiology with pathology and molecular assays to show that intraprostatic inflammation, driven in part by infections such as CMV, increases prostate cancer risk; telomere length variability in cancer cells combined with short stromal telomeres predicts lethal disease; and long‑term use of statins or digoxin is inversely associated with aggressive prostate cancer, suggesting drug‑repurposing opportunities.
key_findings: Intraprostatic inflammation measured in unbiased end‑of‑study biopsies is prospectively associated with increased prostate cancer risk (dose‑response with percent inflamed area). | CMV seropositivity is positively associated with greater intraprostatic inflammation in the PCPT cohort. | High variability of telomere length among cancer cells together with short stromal telomeres confers a 14‑fold higher risk of prostate cancer death, replicated at ~5‑fold in independent cohorts. | Long‑term statin use is linked to a ~50% reduction in metastatic prostate cancer and death, independent of cholesterol levels. | Long‑term digoxin use shows a similar inverse association with both organ‑confined and lethal prostate cancer. | Lower stromal telomere length correlates with reduced lamin B1 expression, supporting a senescent fibroblast hypothesis.
primary_n: n=~2,000 men in HPFS with tissue data
primary_population: men with high telomere variability in cancer cells and short stromal telomeres

talk_title: Highlights of Recent Clinical Trials in Precision Oncology and Emerging Therapies
affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Oncology
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; colorectal cancer; ovarian cancer; breast cancer; multiple myeloma; solid tumors
topic_categories: clinical_trial; targeted_therapy; ADC; CAR_T; immunotherapy; biomarker_development; ctDNA; combination_therapy; precision_therapy; translational
moa_summary: KRAS G12D covalent inhibitor zoldonrasib forms a tricomplex that blocks RAS‑RAF signaling, achieving high ORR and ctDNA clearance in NSCLC. Pan‑RAS PROTAC deraxone rasib degrades mutant and wild‑type RAS, synergizing with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in pancreatic cancer. VE‑1 and PKMYT inhibitors arrest G2/M by targeting CDK1, showing activity in FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer with cyclin E1 amplification. ADCs (T‑DXd, CLDN6‑ADC, ZW91) deliver topoisomerase or DNA‑damaging payloads, enhanced by sequential PARP inhibition. CAR‑T (cilta‑cel) provides durable MRD‑negative remissions in smoldering myeloma. Emerging targets (CCR8, mesothelin) showed limited efficacy, highlighting resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: Zoldonrasib achieved a 52% ORR and median PFS of 11 months in KRAS G12D mutant NSCLC (n=40) with only minimal grade 3 toxicities. | Deraxone rasib combined with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel yielded a 59% ORR and 90% disease control in first‑line metastatic KRAS‑mutant pancreatic cancer, albeit with 73% grade 3+ adverse events. | Elacestrant produced a 32% partial response rate in KRAS G12C NSCLC patients who had progressed on prior G12C inhibitors. | VE‑1 plus PKMYT inhibition resulted in a 60% ORR in FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer harboring cyclin E1 amplification, demonstrating target‑driven efficacy. | Sequential combination of trastuzumab deruxtecan with olaparib achieved a 54% overall response rate, supporting schedule‑optimized ADC‑PARP synergy. | Claudin‑6 and folate receptor ADCs each generated >50% response rates across multiple tumor types, even in low‑expressing cohorts. | Cilta‑cel induced 100% MRD negativity and objective responses in high‑risk smoldering myeloma with no grade 3+ CRS, indicating curative potential in pre‑malignancy.

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: In Vivo CAR T Cell Platforms and Paradigms
moa_summary: The speaker proposes shifting from ex vivo CAR-T manufacturing to in vivo generation using targeted viral-like particles (VLPs) or lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). By blinding the VSV glycoprotein and adding targeting ligands (e.g., anti-CD7 or anti-TCR), the system selectively transduces T cells in vivo, bypassing the need for lymphodepleting chemotherapy and complex GMP facilities.
key_findings: In vivo BCMA CAR-T generation via TCR-targeted VLPs led to stringent complete or very good partial responses in 4 out of 5 multiple myeloma patients. | In vivo BCMA CAR-T therapy showed a bimodal toxicity profile: an initial infusion reaction followed by traditional cytokine release syndrome (CRS). | mRNA-LNP delivery of anti-CD19 CARs achieved transient B-cell aplasia and reduction in SLEDAI scores in refractory SLE patients. | Preclinical NHP data showed persistent B-cell aplasia following CD7-targeted VLP delivery of CAR20, though anti-CAR antibodies developed in 7-14 days. | Calculations suggest that only 0.05% to 2% transduction efficiency of total body T cells is required to reach the therapeutic dose of ~200 million CAR-positive cells.
primary_value: stringent complete response or very good partial response

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: CAR T Cells for Pediatric Brain Tumors
moa_summary: The study evaluates GD2-targeted CAR T cells for H3-mutant diffuse midline gliomas. The research demonstrates that lymphodepletion is critical for CAR T persistence; without it, patients develop robust anti-CAR antibody and T-cell responses (including IgA in the CNS) that reject the therapy. Re-introducing lymphodepletion every four cycles (Arm C) restored durable responses.
key_findings: Intracerebroventricular (ICV) delivery is 10-fold more potent than IV delivery in preclinical models. | Arm A (IV + ICV + lymphodepletion) showed significant tumor shrinkage and improved OS, but high-grade CRS occurred after IV infusion. | Arm B (ICV only, no lymphodepletion) resulted in transient responses (<3 months) due to rapid immune rejection of CAR T cells. | Anti-CAR responses include CD4+ (IL-2, IFN-gamma) and CD8+ (degranulating, 4-1BB+) T cells, as well as IgA antibodies produced by B cells within the CNS. | Arm C (ICV + lymphodepletion every 4 cycles) resulted in durable responses with median OS not reached and a plateau of >50% survival.
primary_value: favorable median overall survival (compared to historical 9-11 months)

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: Precision Targeted Engineering of CAR T Cells
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; breast cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: The speaker describes a CRISPR HDR knock-in strategy to create 'armored' CAR T cells that secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-12, IL-2) under the control of endogenous tumor-specific promoters (NR4A2, RGS16). This approach ensures that potent cytokines are expressed only at the tumor site, reducing systemic toxicity compared to NFAT-inducible promoters while enhancing T cell polyfunctionality and host anti-tumor immunity.
key_findings: NR4A2 is highly tumor-restricted and safe for driving high-toxicity transgenes like IL-12, whereas RGS16 is more potent but less restricted. | IL-12 driven by NR4A2 showed potent anti-tumor effects without the systemic weight loss seen with RGS16 or PD-1 promoters. | IL-2 driven by the RGS16 promoter showed the strongest anti-tumor effect and was well-tolerated. | A 'next-generation' knock-in strategy using a P2A linker to preserve endogenous RGS16 expression significantly reduced peripheral GFP expression while maintaining high tumor expression. | The one-step CRISPR knock-in of both the armoring gene and the CAR into the TRAC locus increased CAR expression without reducing transgene output.

talk_title: Mechanistic insights into CTLA-4 blockade and novel immune structures
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, James P. Allison Institute
tumor_types: melanoma; colorectal cancer; bladder cancer; urothelial carcinoma
moa_summary: Blocking CTLA‑4 removes its high‑affinity competition with CD28 for B7 ligands, restoring costimulatory signaling during T‑cell priming. This generates a unique ICOS‑high CD4 effector subset that collaborates with ICOS‑ligand‑expressing dendritic cells to form spatial 'tetrads', which remodel the myeloid compartment and prime stem‑like TCF7+ CD8 T cells with high granzyme expression. The resulting effector‑only immune infiltrate underlies durable tumor regressions and distinguishes CTLA‑4 blockade from PD‑1 inhibition.
key_findings: Anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment expands ICOS‑positive CD4 T cells; absence of these cells predicts lack of response. | CTLA‑4 blockade induces novel spatial 'tetrad' structures (CD4 ICOS+, CD8, dendritic cell) that are enriched in responders and absent with PD‑1 blockade. | In CTLA‑4 knockout mice, CD4 cells co‑express multiple lineage transcription factors (BCL6, GATA3, RORγC), indicating deregulated differentiation. | Adding ICOS‑ligand to a tumor vaccine converts a non‑curative anti‑CTLA‑4 regimen into 100% tumor eradication in a resistant B16 model. | Combination of anti‑CTLA‑4 with anti‑PD‑1 shifts the exhausted CD8 population to a stem‑like TCF7+ phenotype and improves survival (>50% at 3 years in melanoma). | Long‑term follow‑up of ipilimumab trials shows ~20% of melanoma patients achieve 10‑year survival, whereas median pre‑trial survival was 6–7 months.

talk_title: Circular RNAs in cancer: functional landscape and therapeutic potential
affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Center, Department of Medical Biophysics, University of Toronto
tumor_types: prostate cancer; small cell lung cancer; neuroendocrine prostate cancer
moa_summary: Circular RMST (circRMST) is highly expressed in neuroendocrine prostate and small‑cell lung cancers, where it binds transcription factors NKX2.1 and SOX2, stabilizing NKX2.1 protein by blocking Hsp70‑mediated lysosomal degradation. Loss of circRMST reduces NKX2.1 half‑life, down‑regulates ASL1, and blocks lineage transdifferentiation and tumor growth in mouse models. The covalently closed structure also confers exceptional stability in plasma, enabling biomarker and vaccine applications.
key_findings: Identified ~80,000 circRNAs in 200 prostate cancers; average 7,000 per tumor; up to 400,000 circRNAs cataloged genome‑wide. | Functional screen of 10,000 circRNAs across seven cancer types revealed ~1,300 essential circRNAs, >90% previously uncharacterized and >95% act independently of linear host gene. | circRMST is >100‑fold enriched in neuroendocrine prostate cancer and small‑cell lung cancer versus adenocarcinoma, and is the dominant transcript from its locus. | CRISPR deletion of intronic MIR repeats reduces circRMST expression to ~30% and impairs tumor cell viability in vitro and xenograft growth in vivo. | circRMST knockdown shortens NKX2.1 protein half‑life from 17 h to ~5 h via loss of Hsp70‑binding site protection, leading to loss of ASL1 expression and blockade of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. | circRMST is detectable in patient plasma with a >1,000‑fold higher circular/linear ratio than in tumor tissue, suggesting a liquid‑biopsy biomarker. | Circular RNA translation can generate novel peptides; immunopeptidomics identified 80 circRNA‑derived peptides, including 12 from reverse‑strand translation, providing new vaccine antigens.

talk_title: Developing CAR T‑cell therapies for T‑cell malignancies
affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis, Director of the Section of Cellular Therapy and the Center for Gene and Cellular Immunotherapy
tumor_types: T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia; peripheral T-cell lymphoma; acute myeloid leukemia
moa_summary: The work introduces universal allogeneic CAR‑T platforms that multiplex‑edit donor T cells to delete CD7 (and TCRα) for T‑ALL and to delete CD2 (and TCRα) for T‑cell lymphomas, enabling potent antigen‑specific killing while avoiding fratricide. Epitope‑masking of CD2 or incorporation of the CD2 cytoplasmic tail restores costimulatory signaling lost after CD2 knockout. Long‑acting IL‑7 fusion (NTi‑7) administered early after infusion dramatically expands CAR‑T persistence and cures murine models. Small‑molecule PI3γδ and JAK inhibitors, or CD40L blockade, suppress IL‑6–driven CRS without impairing cytotoxicity.
key_findings: Multiplex CRISPR editing of donor T cells to delete CD7 and TCRα enables universal CAR‑T cells that achieved an 82% complete remission rate in the first‑in‑human UCART7 Phase I trial for relapsed T‑ALL. | Deletion of CD2 in CAR‑T cells caused severe functional exhaustion in vitro and loss of antitumor activity in vivo, which was rescued by epitope‑masking of CD2 or by inserting the CD2 cytoplasmic domain into the CAR construct. | Long‑acting IL‑7 (NTi‑7) given between days 1‑7 after CAR‑T infusion expands CAR‑T cells 10,000‑ to 100,000‑fold and cures murine models of T‑cell lymphoma, B‑cell lymphoma, and AML, whereas later dosing is ineffective. | A high‑throughput screen of 700 kinase inhibitors identified PI3γδ inhibitor (Devolasib) and JAK1/2 inhibitor (Ruxolitinib) as synergistic suppressors of IL‑6 production without impairing CAR‑T cytotoxicity; combined prophylaxis eliminated grade ≥2 CRS in a randomized patient cohort. | CD4⁺ CAR‑T cells are the primary source of IL‑6 via membrane‑bound CD40L, while CD8⁺ cells mediate killing; CD40L knockout or blockade markedly reduces CRS, highlighting a mechanistic link between CD40L signaling and cytokine release.
primary_population: relapsed/refractory T‑cell ALL, Phase I UCART7 cohort

talk_title: Open-access chemical probes and Target 2035 initiative for the druggable proteome
affiliation: Senior Scientist, Princess Margaret Cancer Centre; Professor, University of Toronto
tumor_types: acute myeloid leukemia; glioblastoma; clear cell renal carcinoma; multiple myeloma; acute lymphoblastic leukemia; breast cancer
topic_categories: chemical_biology; epigenetics; targeted_protein_degradation; platform_technology; AI_ML; basic_science
moa_summary: The SGC develops open-access chemical probes that selectively inhibit epigenetic regulators such as WDR5, PRMT5, PRMT1 and NSD2. Probe binding disrupts protein–protein interactions or catalytic activity, leading to transcriptional reprogramming, loss of oncogenic histone marks, and selective growth suppression in cancer models dependent on these epigenetic pathways. A PROTAC-derived NSD2 degrader recruits FBXO22, forming a covalent adduct that eliminates NSD2 protein and its H3K36me2 mark, restoring dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cells.
key_findings: Developed ~200 high‑quality chemical probes, including 50–70 epigenetic modulators, all freely available to the community. | Identified OICR-9429 as a potent, selective WDR5‑MLL interaction inhibitor that suppresses growth of MLL‑rearranged leukemia and p53‑gain‑of‑function breast cancer cells. | Discovered a PROTAC that recruits FBXO22 to degrade NSD2, eliminating H3K36me2 and selectively killing NSD2‑mutant ALL and multiple myeloma cells after 18–21 days. | NSD2 degradation restored dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cell lines, suggesting a combinatorial therapeutic strategy. | Machine‑learning‑guided DNA‑encoded library screens across 18 WDR proteins yielded low‑micromolar, ligand‑efficient hits for ~1/3 of the targets, expanding the druggable proteome.

talk_title: Epigenomic heterogeneity and chromatin variants in metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Medicine
moa_summary: Chromatin variant analysis using ATAC‑seq identified repetitive element‑driven accessible regions that recruit AR, FOXA1 and other transcription factors, forming de novo regulatory elements that amplify AR expression in LINE1+ metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer, leading to intrinsic resistance to AR‑targeted therapy.
key_findings: Unsupervised clustering of ATAC‑seq data from 71 mCRPC samples defined four subgroups based on repetitive element chromatin variants (LINE1+, SETL, SINE1+, constant). | LINE1+ subgroup showed intrinsic resistance to androgen‑receptor selective inhibitors, with markedly shorter survival. | AR locus amplification (up to >150 copies) and ecDNA positivity were enriched in LINE1+ tumors. | Repetitive element‑derived accessible sites in LINE1+ tumors physically interact with the AR promoter (Hi‑C), recruiting AR, FOXA1, and OCTB13. | Chromatin variant classification did not align with gene‑expression‑based subtypes, highlighting orthogonal heterogeneity.
primary_population: LINE1+ mCRPC patients treated with AR selective inhibitors

talk_title: SMARCA4 loss drives cell‑state disorder and dedifferentiated endometrial cancer
affiliation: University of Alberta, Department of Oncology
moa_summary: Loss of SMARCA4 disrupts the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, leading to global DNA hypomethylation and stochastic chromatin accessibility changes that prevent cells from stabilizing in a differentiated state, resulting in histologic dedifferentiation and therapy resistance in endometrial cancer.
key_findings: 80% of dedifferentiated endometrial cancer (DDEC) cases show loss of SMARCA4 and/or ARID1B, components of the SWI/SNF complex. | CRISPR‑mediated SMARCA4 knockout in endometrial cancer cells reduces in‑vitro tumorigenicity but, after serial in‑vivo passaging, restores growth and yields mixed well‑ and poorly‑differentiated histology. | SMARCA4‑deficient tumors display global DNA hypomethylation and distinct chromatin accessibility patterns compared with wild‑type. | Single‑cell multi‑omics reveal that SMARCA4 loss increases cell‑state plasticity, generating an uncommitted transcriptional state without a stem‑cell or embryonic signature. | Patient DDEC samples retain heterogeneous E‑cadherin expression, supporting a model of cell‑state disorder rather than true dedifferentiation.

talk_title: LKB1 loss‑driven adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) and therapeutic vulnerabilities in KRAS‑mutant lung cancer
affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern, Department of Oncology
moa_summary: LKB1 loss elevates ROS, transiently activating NF‑κB (RELA) which sustains adenocarcinoma survival; prolonged stress leads to a double‑positive p63⁺/RELA⁺ state that secretes IL‑1α, reprograms CAFs to a leukocyte‑like phenotype, induces IL‑1β‑driven neutrophil NET formation, and drives squamous transdifferentiation (AST) that confers resistance to KRAS‑G12C inhibition; combined NF‑κB inhibition, ROS modulation, and IL‑1 blockade synergize with KRAS‑G12C inhibitor to prevent or eradicate squamous lesions.
key_findings: LKB1 loss in KRAS‑mutant mouse models induces spontaneous adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) that is enriched after targeted therapy. | High squamous transcriptional signature predicts shorter treatment duration in LKB1‑mutant lung cancer patients. | KLK6A expression serves as a predictive biomarker for poor response to aggressive therapy in LKB1‑deficient tumors. | ROS accumulation activates NF‑κB; NF‑κB inhibition accelerates p63⁺ squamous differentiation, while ROS scavenging (NAC) reduces RELA activation. | p63⁺ squamous cells secrete IL‑1α, reprogramming CAFs to IL‑1β‑producing leukocyte‑like CAFs that trigger neutrophil NET formation, promoting squamous tumor growth. | Combined KRAS‑G12C inhibitor (IMC‑6236) with IL‑1 receptor antagonist or NET inhibitor (GSK‑484) markedly reduces squamous tumor burden and improves overall tumor control.
primary_value: shorter in patients with high squamous signature

talk_title: Dendritic cell subsets, cross‑presentation mechanisms, and mRNA vaccine priming
affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis, Professor of Pathology and Immunology and Medicine
moa_summary: Murphy's work defines transcriptional enhancers that program dendritic cell subset differentiation (DC1s via IRF8 enhancer, DC2s via Notch2, Klf4, ZEB2) and identifies WDFY4 and Rab43 as DC1‑specific factors required for vesicular cross‑presentation. Using these mouse models, he shows that conventional DCs are essential for mRNA‑LNP vaccine‑induced CD8 T‑cell priming, but the response is independent of DC1‑specific cross‑presentation pathways and instead relies on cross‑dressing of peptide‑MHC complexes, a process requiring type I IFN and residual class I on DCs.
key_findings: Batf3‑/- mice lacking DC1s show tumor and viral responses as weak as Rag‑deficient mice, demonstrating DC1 necessity for classic cross‑presentation. | Targeted deletion of three IRF8 enhancers abolishes DC1 development, providing a refined genetic tool superior to Batf3 knockout. | WDFY4 is uniquely expressed in DC1s; Wdfy4‑/- mice fail to cross‑present cell‑associated antigens and cannot reject fibrosarcoma, confirming its essential role in vesicular cross‑presentation. | Rab43 co‑localizes with WDFY4 in DC1s and traffics with phagocytosed Listeria, supporting a vesicular, non‑cytosolic cross‑presentation pathway. | mRNA‑LNP vaccination induces CD8 tetramer responses that persist after DC1 or WDFY4 loss, indicating DC1‑independent priming. | Complete depletion of conventional DCs (CD11c‑DTR) abolishes the mRNA‑vaccine response, proving DC dependence but not subset specificity. | Class I MHC loss on DCs (CD11c‑Cre β2M flox) eliminates tumor‑induced cross‑presentation but only partially reduces mRNA‑vaccine responses, implicating cross‑dressing as a major mechanism. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq of tetramer+ CD8 T cells shows that DC1 priming yields fewer but more proliferative effectors, whereas DC2 priming yields broader effector populations.

talk_title: SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex vulnerabilities and therapeutic targeting of DCAF5
affiliation: St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, Executive Vice President and Director of the Comprehensive Cancer Center
tumor_types: rhabdoid tumor; small cell carcinoma of the ovary; pediatric cancer
moa_summary: Roberts demonstrated that loss of the SWI/SNF subunit SMARCB1 destabilizes the complex, leading to degradation of residual subunits by the E3 ligase adaptor DCAF5. Genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of DCAF5 restores SWI/SNF complex abundance at enhancers, rescues transcription of developmental genes, and induces tumor regression in SMARCB1-deficient models without major toxicity, establishing DCAF5 as a synthetic‑lethal vulnerability.
key_findings: CRISPR screens of SMARCB1‑deficient rhabdoid tumor lines identified DCAF5 as a highly selective dependency (Q‑value ~10⁻²²). | Mass‑spec revealed that DCAF5 knockdown increases abundance of nine SWI/SNF subunits, confirming DCAF5‑mediated degradation. | DiGly tagging showed direct ubiquitination of multiple SWI/SNF subunits by DCAF5. | DCAF5 loss restores 30‑70% of wild‑type enhancer‑driven transcription in SMARCB1‑null cells, reactivating developmental gene programs. | In vivo degradation of DCAF5 eradicated established SMARCB1‑deficient tumors; no regrowth observed after 100 days. | Germline DCAF5 knockout mice are viable with only mild growth phenotype, indicating a therapeutic window. | Initial small‑molecule binders of DCAF5 have been identified in collaboration with Gray and Fisher labs, enabling a drug‑discovery program.

talk_title: Chromatin priming drives early onset of BRCA1‑heterozygous basal‑like breast cancer
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Cancer Biology, Basser Center for BRCA
moa_summary: Li's group generated a BRCA1‑heterozygous mouse model that reveals early chromatin accessibility changes in alveolar luminal progenitor cells, priming AP1‑dependent transcriptional programs and WNT10A activation. Partial inhibition of AP1 delays tumor onset, while WNT10A overexpression accelerates basal‑like tumor formation, establishing these pathways as early drivers of hereditary breast cancer.
key_findings: BRCA1‑heterozygous mice develop basal‑like mammary tumors ~20 weeks earlier than WT mice after Cre‑mediated BRCA1 loss. | ATAC‑seq of normal alveolar cells shows a distinct subcluster with intermediate chromatin openness that aligns with tumor profiles. | AP1 (c‑Jun, JunD, FOSL2) motifs are enriched in these open regions; c‑Jun protein is up‑regulated in early lesions. | Dominant‑negative Jun expression delays tumor formation only in heterozygous mice, indicating AP1 dependency. | WNT10A locus exhibits increased accessibility and expression; WNT10A knockdown impairs organoid growth and in‑vivo tumorigenicity. | WNT10A overexpression accelerates tumor onset and raises tumor grade in mouse cohorts. | RUNX/CBFB transcription factors are up‑regulated in pre‑malignant and tumor cells; CBFB and RUNX2 knockdown reduces proliferation of derived cancer cell lines.

talk_title: Epigenetic plasticity drives pancreatic cancer metastasis via KLF5 and chromatin modifiers
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Bloomberg Distinguished Professor of Epigenetics
moa_summary: Feinberg's work shows that epigenetic plasticity, measured as DNA methylation entropy, creates a transitional chromatin state in early pancreatic lesions and in metastatic clones. A CRISPR screen identified KLF5 as a key transcriptional modulator that drives metastasis by up‑regulating epigenetic modifiers (NCAPD2, MTHFD1) leading to loss of heterochromatin (H3K9me2/3) and increased euchromatin, which promotes EMT and tumor cell migration.
key_findings: PanIN lesions display DNA methylation patterns intermediate between acinar and ductal cells, indicating an epigenetically plastic transition zone. | UHRF1 knockdown abolishes soft‑agar growth of ALL cells, linking it to epigenetic entropy in leukemia. | CRISPR screen of metastatic PDAC PDX identified KLF5 as the top gene whose loss impairs metastatic growth but not primary tumor growth. | KLF5 knockdown restores heterochromatin (H3K9me2/3) in metastatic cells and reduces migration in Boyden assays. | KLF5 regulates NCAPD2 and MTHFD1; their knockdown also restores heterochromatin, placing them downstream of KLF5. | High KLF5 expression in human metastatic PDAC correlates with poor survival in TCGA data.

talk_title: From fetal DNA to cancer early detection: innovations in circulating nucleic acids
affiliation: Vice Chancellor and President, The Chinese University of Hong Kong; Li Ka Shing Professor of Medicine, Department of Chemical Pathology
topic_categories: liquid_biopsy; early_detection; biomarker_development; translational; platform_technology; fragmentomics; epigenetics
moa_summary: Lo demonstrated that cell‑free DNA released by tumors or fetuses can be quantitatively measured using digital PCR, next‑generation sequencing and novel fragmentomics signatures (size, end‑motif, methylation). By exploiting tumor‑specific nucleic acid markers such as EBV DNA in nasopharyngeal carcinoma or chromosome‑specific copy‑number changes in trisomy 21, the approach enables highly accurate non‑invasive prenatal testing and cancer early‑detection, with fragment length and end‑motif patterns providing additional tissue‑origin information.
key_findings: Discovery of cell‑free fetal DNA in maternal plasma enabled non‑invasive prenatal testing with >99% accuracy using digital PCR of chromosome‑specific targets. | A genome‑wide NIPT based on low‑coverage sequencing accurately detected trisomy 21 in 28 pregnancies (100% sensitivity and specificity). | Plasma EBV DNA serves as a highly specific biomarker for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, detecting 34 cancers in a 20,000‑person Hong Kong screen and shifting 70% of cases to stage I‑II with a 10‑fold mortality reduction (3% vs 40%). | Fragmentomics signatures (shorter fragment size, distinct end‑motif patterns) differentiate cancer‑derived cfDNA from normal cfDNA and improve detection AUC to 0.92 for liver cancer. | Integration of fragment size, end‑motif, and methylation data enables tissue‑of‑origin inference, forming the basis of multi‑cancer early detection platforms such as GRAIL’s Galleri test.

talk_title: Quantitative fluorescence protein assays and selective biomarkers for ADC choice in breast cancer
affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Department of Pathology and Medicine, Director of Yale Cancer Center Tissue Microarray Facility
tumor_types: breast cancer; melanoma; lung cancer; bladder cancer
moa_summary: Rim's laboratory developed the Troplex quantitative immunofluorescence platform that converts fluorescence intensity into absolute protein concentration (attomoles/mm²) for HER2, Trop‑2 and other membrane antigens. By standardizing staining with cell‑line calibrators, the assay achieves <10% CV and enables continuous measurement of target density, which correlates with ADC efficacy and informs a proposed selective biomarker framework for choosing between competing HER2‑ or Trop‑2‑directed ADCs.
key_findings: The Troplex assay quantifies HER2 and Trop‑2 in absolute units with inter‑ and intra‑assay CV <10% using cell‑line standards. | In a retrospective Yale cohort (n=316), quantitative HER2 levels distinguished patients likely to benefit from trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, with a hazard ratio of 0.70 per 50‑unit increase (p<0.05). | High Trop‑2 expression was associated with significantly shorter overall survival on T‑DXd (median OS 6.2 mo vs 13.9 mo for low Trop‑2, p<0.01). | Combined HER2‑high/Trop‑2‑low tumors showed the best outcomes on HER2‑directed ADCs, suggesting a selective biomarker strategy for drug choice. | The assay received New York State Department of Health (NYSDOH) CLIA approval, enabling clinical deployment.

talk_title: Genomics and environmental risk factors in East Asian lung adenocarcinoma
affiliation: National Cancer Center, Japan; Vice President of Japanese Cancer Association; Director of JCGAT
topic_categories: genomics; cancer_vaccines; biomarker_development; epigenetics; environmental_exposure; other
moa_summary: Kono presented large-scale national genomic data showing distinct mutation patterns in never-smoker versus smoker lung adenocarcinoma, with EGFR mutations predominating in never smokers and KRAS in smokers, and highlighted environmental contributors such as passive smoking, air pollution, and germline polymorphisms influencing tumor development.
key_findings: Never smokers comprised ~30% of advanced lung cancer cases in the Japanese national database. | EGFR mutations are the most frequent driver in never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma, while KRAS dominates in smokers. | Passive smoking increases tumor mutational burden via APOBEC signatures but does not affect EGFR mutation frequency. | Air pollution (PM2.5) correlates with higher EGFR-positive lung cancer incidence without directly causing EGFR mutations. | Germline polymorphisms (e.g., TERT, HLA class II) confer greater risk in never smokers, especially for EGFR-positive disease.

talk_title: Germline susceptibility and genomic risk in never-smoker lung cancer
tumor_types: lung adenocarcinoma; small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: Lo Piccolo described large-scale germline sequencing efforts revealing that rare pathogenic variants in established cancer genes (TP53, SMAD6, IREB2) and polygenic risk scores contribute to lung cancer risk in never smokers, especially in young-onset and EGFR‑positive disease, and highlighted a case where BRCA2 loss of heterozygosity sensitized a patient to platinum and PARP inhibition.
key_findings: Young‑onset lung cancer (≤45 y) is enriched for targetable fusions (ALK, ROS1) and EGFR mutations. | Rare pathogenic germline variants in TP53, SMAD6, and IREB2 are significantly enriched in young‑onset or fusion‑driven tumors. | Polygenic risk scores derived from smoking‑associated GWAS still confer modest risk (OR ≈ 1.3 per SD) in never‑smoker cohorts. | Large (>1 Mb) rare deletions are over‑represented in young‑onset, fusion‑positive cases. | A BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant with tumor LOH predicted exceptional response to platinum chemotherapy followed by PARP inhibition.
primary_metric: ORR to platinum + PARP inhibitor in BRCA2 germline carrier
primary_value: durable response >4 y to platinum, 16 m on PARP inhibitor
primary_population: stage IV BRCA2 germline mutant lung cancer

talk_title: Differential treatment response by smoking status in lung cancer
affiliation: Samsung Medical Center, Republic of Korea
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; immunotherapy; clinical_trial; biomarker_development; resistance_mechanisms; translational
moa_summary: Lee demonstrated that never‑smoker lung cancer shows superior benefit from EGFR TKIs but diminished response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, correlating with lower TMB and PD‑L1; he identified biomarkers (PBAP loss, DDR1, transcriptional MMR‑like signature) that predict ICI sensitivity or hyperprogression, and highlighted that anti‑angiogenic addition improves TKI efficacy mainly in smokers.
key_findings: EGFR TKI hazard ratio 0.7 in never smokers versus smokers, confirming greater efficacy. | Addition of anti‑angiogenic agents to EGFR TKI improves outcomes more in smokers than never smokers. | Never smokers have significantly lower TMB and PD‑L1 scores, resulting in poor ICI monotherapy response (ORR ~10%). | PBAP loss‑of‑function mutations (~10% of never smokers) predict durable ICI responses (5‑year remission). | High DDR1 expression associates with hyperprogression on ICI in never smokers.
primary_metric: EGFR TKI hazard ratio (never smoker vs smoker)

talk_title: Molecular evolution and prognostic classification of never‑smoker lung adenocarcinoma (Sherlock study)
topic_categories: genomics; mutational_signatures; evolutionary_trajectory; prognostic_biomarkers; environmental_exposure; translational
moa_summary: Landi described comprehensive whole‑genome and transcriptomic profiling of ~1,200 lung adenocarcinomas, revealing that never‑smoker tumors follow distinct evolutionary paths: EGFR‑mutant, copy‑number‑rich, AT1‑cell‑origin cancers evolve slowly, whereas KRKR‑mutant, low‑CNV tumors arise rapidly; environmental exposures (PM2.5, indoor coal) imprint specific mutational signatures, and integrated methylation and expression signatures provide prognostic stratification.
key_findings: Three genomic subtypes (piano, mezzo‑forte, forte) correlate with copy‑number burden and driver mutations. | Endogenous signatures (SBS1, SBS5, APOBEC) dominate; exogenous SBS4 and Signature 22A (aristolochic acid) appear in specific regions. | High outdoor PM2.5 exposure associates with increased SBS5 and reduced telomere length. | KRAS‑mutant tumors have shorter latency (≈5 y) and earlier age at diagnosis compared with EGFR‑mutant tumors (≈6 y). | AT1‑origin methylation signature distinguishes low‑grade, indolent tumors and aligns with the steady transcriptomic group.

talk_title: Tumor-agnostic PD-1 blockade, ctDNA‑guided MRD and organ‑preserving immunotherapy
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Solid Tumor Oncology Division
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer; gastric cancer; cholangiocarcinoma; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; small intestine cancer; glioma; urothelial cancer; prostate cancer; rectal cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; clinical_trial; biomarker_development; ctDNA; tumor_agnostic; combination_therapy; prevention; translational
moa_summary: Pembrolizumab blocks the PD‑1 immune checkpoint, reactivating exhausted T cells against neoantigens generated by the high mutational burden of MMR‑deficient/MSI‑H tumors. In the adjuvant setting, ctDNA detection identifies residual disease; PD‑1 blockade eradicates this microscopic disease, converting ctDNA‑positive patients to long‑term disease‑free status. In the neoadjuvant rectal setting, sustained PD‑1 inhibition induces complete clinical responses, allowing omission of surgery.
key_findings: All 49 MMR‑deficient rectal cancer patients achieved a clinical complete response after 6 months of pembrolizumab, enabling non‑operative management (100% cCR). | Two‑year recurrence‑free survival was 96/49 (≈96%) in the rectal cohort and 85% in the 54‑patient non‑rectal cohort treated with pembrolizumab. | In a ctDNA‑guided MRD trial, 25 ctDNA‑positive patients who received pembrolizumab had recurrence‑free outcomes comparable to 125 ctDNA‑negative patients observed, whereas untreated ctDNA‑positive patients progressed rapidly. | Adverse events of any grade occurred in only 35% of non‑rectal patients, indicating a favorable safety profile for organ‑preserving immunotherapy. | Retrospective analysis of 837 Lynch syndrome carriers showed >80% reduction in secondary cancer incidence among those who had received PD‑1 blockade versus untreated controls.
primary_metric: clinical complete response (cCR) in rectal cohort

talk_title: Population-based cancer surveillance informs disparities and policy
affiliation: Senior Vice President of Surveillance, Prevention, and Health Services Research, American Cancer Society, Atlanta, GA
tumor_types: lung cancer; colorectal cancer; breast cancer; melanoma; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Dr. Jemal presented integrated analyses of national cancer registry and survey data revealing sex-specific lung cancer incidence reversals, early-onset colorectal cancer birth-cohort effects, and the impact of ACA Medicaid expansion on insurance coverage, stage at diagnosis, and five-year survival, linking surveillance metrics to policy outcomes.
key_findings: Lung cancer incidence in women under age 50 now exceeds that of men, a reversal not explained by smoking prevalence alone. | Birth-cohort analysis shows a fourfold increase in rectal cancer risk and twofold increase in colon cancer risk for cohorts born after 1950, prompting USPSTF to lower colorectal screening start age to 45. | Geographic clusters with 40% higher colorectal cancer mortality identified in the Lower Mississippi Corridor, leading to targeted screening initiatives and legislative action. | Medicaid expansion under the ACA reduced uninsured rates more than private-market subsidies and improved five-year overall survival in high-poverty rural areas. | Dependent coverage expansion increased early-stage diagnosis and fertility-sparing treatment rates for cervical cancer in women aged 21‑25.
primary_population: US cancer patients overall (2023 Cancer Stats article)

talk_title: Extracellular vesicles orchestrate pre‑metastatic niche and systemic disease
affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Department of Pediatrics and Pediatric Neuro‑Oncology
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; metabolism; thrombosis; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science; other
moa_summary: Lyden demonstrated that tumor‑derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) carry organ‑specific integrin zip‑codes that direct EV uptake to pre‑metastatic niches; β2‑integrin‑positive EVs trigger lung thrombosis, while exomeres rich in metabolic enzymes induce fatty‑liver inflammation via Kupffer‑cell TNF‑α activation. Blocking β2‑integrin or TNF‑α mitigates these systemic effects. Activated T‑cell EVs loaded with granzyme B enter tumor nuclei, up‑regulating antigen‑presentation genes and synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 to regress pancreatic tumors.
key_findings: Integrin αVβ5‑positive EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells home to the lung and establish pre‑metastatic niches, whereas αVβ3‑positive EVs target the brain. | β2‑integrin‑bearing EVs derived from melanoma or pancreatic cancer induce rapid lung clot formation in naive mice; anti‑β2‑integrin antibody reduces fibrin deposition and improves platelet counts. | Exomeres, the smallest EV fraction, preferentially accumulate in the liver, reprogram Kupffer cells to a TNF‑α‑high, fatty‑acid‑low state, and generate fatty‑liver disease in mice. | Pharmacologic blockade of β2‑integrin or TNF‑α prevents EV‑driven thrombosis and fatty‑liver pathology and modestly delays metastatic tumor growth. | Activated T‑cell EVs package granzyme B, enter tumor cell nuclei, up‑regulate antigen‑presentation pathways, and when combined with anti‑PD‑1 produce durable tumor regressions in pancreatic cancer mouse models (median survival extended 30–40 days). | Circulating β2‑integrin‑positive EV levels prospectively predict clot events in pancreatic cancer patients, outperforming standard coagulation tests.

talk_title: From fusion biology to functional genomics and therapeutic strategies in pediatric cancers
affiliation: Department of Pediatric Oncology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute
tumor_types: acute lymphoblastic leukemia; Ewing sarcoma; neuroblastoma; pediatric solid tumors; brain tumors
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; PROTAC_degrader; molecular_glue; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Stegmaier's work integrates large‑scale functional genomics (CRISPR and chemical screens) with fusion biology to identify pediatric cancer dependencies such as TRIM8, ETV6, and UGT3A2, revealing a Goldilocks dosage requirement for EWS‑FLI1. She leverages degrader technologies (PROTACs, molecular glues) and bifunctional molecules to selectively degrade or rewire the fusion protein, demonstrating that loss of TRIM8 elevates EWS‑FLI1 levels and that targeted degradation of the fusion can induce apoptosis in Ewing sarcoma models.
key_findings: EWS‑FLI1 dosage is a Goldilocks dependency: both over‑expression and complete repression are deleterious to Ewing sarcoma cells. | TRIM8 is a selective Ewing sarcoma dependency; its knockout increases EWS‑FLI1 levels and is lethal in vitro and in vivo. | ETV6 competes with EWS‑FLI1 on chromatin; CRISPR or dTAG‑mediated loss of ETV6 leads to excess EWS‑FLI1 activity and cell death. | STAG2 loss predicts metastatic disease and poor prognosis in Ewing sarcoma, validated in isogenic knockout models. | Chemical PRISM screening identified compound R112 as uniquely active in Ewing sarcoma; activity is rescued by UGT3A2 knockout, implicating UDP‑glucose metabolism as a novel vulnerability. | A genomics‑guided case of a pediatric B‑ALL patient with RCST1‑ABL2 fusion achieved durable complete remission when imatinib was added to standard therapy. | Development of high‑throughput GFP‑mCherry reporter assays enabled screening of >100,000 molecules, yielding several candidates that induce proteasome‑dependent degradation of the EWS‑FLI1 fusion.

talk_title: Building and Leveraging the Cancer Dependency Map for Target Discovery
moa_summary: The Cancer Dependency Map integrates large-scale CRISPR and shRNA genetic perturbation screens with multi-omics profiling and drug sensitivity data across >1,000 cancer cell lines, using the Chronos gene effect score to correct biases, thereby linking molecular alterations to gene dependencies and identifying synthetic lethal vulnerabilities such as PRMT5 in MTAP‑deleted cancers and WRN in MSI cancers.
key_findings: Identified PRMT5 as a synthetic lethal dependency in MTAP‑deleted cancers, validated by three concurrent manuscripts and ongoing clinical trials of PRMT5 inhibitors. | Discovered WRN helicase dependency in microsatellite instability (MSI) cancers using both CRISPR and shRNA data, leading to multiple manuscripts and clinical development of WRN inhibitors. | Found that loss of the 9p21 region creates dependency on the SKI complex (PELO/HBS1L), with MSI also driving PELO dependency, revealing a novel synthetic lethal axis.

talk_title: Advancing DepMap: New Models, Paralog Screens, and Future Directions
moa_summary: Expands the DepMap resource by integrating organoid and 3D spheroid models, performing large‑scale paralog pair CRISPR screens to uncover hidden dependencies (e.g., AKT1 vs AKT1/2), and developing high‑dimensional phenotypic assays and AI‑enabled portals to predict combination and resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: Generated a library of >27,000 paralog pairs and screened ~300 models, revealing context‑specific dependencies such as AKT1 alone versus AKT1+AKT2 co‑knockout. | Integrated 150 organoid and neurosphere models into DepMap, showing lineage‑specific dependencies (e.g., ITGAV vs ITGV1) that differ from 2D cell lines. | Developed the Prism bar‑coded pooled drug sensitivity assay covering 900 cell lines and 265 compounds, enabling rapid assessment of compound selectivity and synthetic lethal interactions.

talk_title: Computational Oncology: Molecular Logic of Cancer Development, Immunotherapy Response, and Resistance
affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Division of Population Sciences; Harvard Medical School
tumor_types: prostate cancer; kidney cancer; melanoma; bladder cancer; breast cancer; pediatric solid tumors
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; computational_biology; AI_ML; biomarker_development; translational; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Van Allen's work integrates germline, somatic, and immune genomic data using interpretable AI and computational pathology to define molecular logic that drives oncogenesis, immunotherapy response, and therapeutic resistance. By mapping regulatory non‑coding mutations, germline risk alleles, and chromatin remodeler alterations (e.g., PBRM1), his algorithms predict patient outcomes and uncover novel dependencies such as MDM4 in lethal prostate cancer.
key_findings: Developed a novel algorithm to detect recurrent non‑coding regulatory mutations, exemplified by functional validation of a breast cancer XPP1‑proximal region. | Demonstrated germline BRCA2 enrichment in advanced prostate cancer and extended germline risk analysis to CHEK2, DNA‑repair genes, and structural variants in pediatric solid tumors. | Identified loss‑of‑function mutations in PBRM1 as a predictive biomarker for response to nivolumab in renal cell carcinoma, with replication in the Checkpoint 025 trial (nivolumab arm only). | Created an interpretable deep‑learning pathology pipeline that quantifies tumor grade heterogeneity and immune cell features, improving response prediction over single‑gene biomarkers in the same renal cell carcinoma cohort. | Built the “Peanut” biologically‑engineered neural network to classify primary versus lethal prostate cancer, uncovering a novel association with MDM4 amplification that is being pursued for therapeutic targeting.

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Transitioning from Academia to Industry for Commercialization
moa_summary: The speaker developed an antibody against Substance P, a small neuropeptide, based on the discovery that it transactivates EGFR and HER2. The work focused on the transition from academic discovery and patenting to industrial drug development and commercialization strategies.
key_findings: Developed a patent for an antibody targeting Substance P to inhibit the transactivation of EGFR and HER2.

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Academic Entrepreneurship: Promise and Perils
moa_summary: The speaker identified an unknown protein (elongation factor two) that was highly active in glioblastoma compared to normal glia. He also developed a pharmacophore for P-glycoprotein (MDR1) which led to the development of VX-701.
key_findings: Identified elongation factor two as a differentially phosphorylated protein in glioblastoma compared to normal white matter. | Licensed a pharmacophore for P-glycoprotein to Vertex, resulting in the compound VX-701.

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Regulatory Pathways for First-in-Human Trials
moa_summary: The speaker discussed the regulatory requirements for Investigational New Drug (IND) applications, emphasizing the importance of pre-IND meetings to address safety, CMC (Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls), and non-clinical data to reduce the risk of clinical holds.
key_findings: Guinea pigs are better models for oxidative stress than rats because they do not produce ascorbate like humans.

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Funding and Scaling Academic Innovation
moa_summary: The speaker highlighted the role of non-traditional funding (e.g., Pelotonia) in bridging the gap between basic science and clinical application, specifically citing the early funding of ibrutinib in CLL mouse models which led to a multi-billion dollar drug.
key_findings: Pelotonia raised $350 million for cancer research with 100% of funds going to research. | Initial $100,000 funding for ibrutinib in a CLL mouse model contributed to the development of a multi-billion dollar drug that changed the standard of care for CLL.

talk_title: From Academic Genetics to Global Product Development: Lessons from Founding Foundation Medicine and Tango Therapeutics
affiliation: Chief Medical Officer, Global Product Development, Roche/Genentech
moa_summary: Garraway described how early cancer genetics research (e.g., MTAP deletion–PRMT5 dependency) was translated into two companies: Foundation Medicine (pan‑cancer sequencing diagnostics) and Tango Therapeutics (PRMT5 inhibitors), highlighting the importance of aligning scientific insight with market need and building networks to move from academia to pharma.
key_findings: Co‑founding Foundation Medicine leveraged massively parallel sequencing to create a whole‑panel diagnostic that became a Roche asset. | Discovery of MTAP deletion–PRMT5 synthetic lethality led to Tango Therapeutics, now developing PRMT5 inhibitors in the clinic. | Early career networking (e.g., with Eric Lander) was critical to secure venture capital and industry introductions. | Transitioning from academia to industry required understanding institutional motivations and building cross‑functional teams.

talk_title: Building a Lung‑Cancer Early Detection Diagnostic: From Molecular Pap Smear to Percepta
affiliation: Former Pulmonary & Critical Care Physician‑Scientist, Boston University Medical Center; Former R&D Lead, Johnson & Johnson
moa_summary: Spira detailed the development of a bronchial brush‑based transcriptomic assay (80‑gene panel) to detect early lung cancer, the challenges of VC pitching, the role of an institutional Ignition Fund, and the eventual acquisition by Veracyte, resulting in the commercial Percepta test.
key_findings: Initial pitch to VCs failed due to lack of clear funding ask; revised pitch led to first round of capital from two venture groups. | Two clinical validation trials published in NEJM 2015 demonstrated diagnostic performance, facilitating acquisition by Veracyte. | Institutional Ignition Fund ($50k) provided critical deck development support and mentorship. | Angel funding from high‑net‑worth individuals helped bridge the gap between seed and Series A.

talk_title: Academic Cell‑Therapy Development for HPV‑Associated Cancers: Infrastructure, Resilience, and FDA Navigation
affiliation: Professor of Medicine, Rutgers Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Henrichs described the creation of a GMP‑compliant cellular therapy targeting HPV E6/E7 antigens, the necessity of institutional GMP facilities, and the strategic use of pre‑IND versus direct IND filing to accelerate early‑phase trials.
key_findings: Rutgers Cancer Institute’s GMP facility enabled rapid IND filing for an HPV‑directed cell therapy. | Early clinical data showed 58% ORR and durable complete responses in HPV‑positive patients. | Pre‑IND meetings can de‑risk programs, but a well‑prepared IND can also prompt rapid FDA feedback. | Academic teams can leverage FDA staff with academic backgrounds for more collaborative interactions.

talk_title: Resilience and Funding Challenges in Building a Breast‑Cancer Therapeutic Company (TerraNovir)
moa_summary: Tsondal highlighted the difficulty of securing early capital for a metastasis‑focused breast cancer program, emphasizing the Catch‑2022 dilemma (need team to raise money, need money to build team) and the importance of resilience and patient advocacy in advancing the program.
key_findings: Incorporated TerraNovir in the US in 2025 to access American capital markets. | Identified a gap in understanding metastatic versus primary tumor biology as a strategic focus. | Described challenges when VCs assign junior analysts unfamiliar with the target, underscoring the need for clear scientific communication.

talk_title: Venture Studios and Alternative Paths to Company Formation in Oncology
moa_summary: Schwaebel described Alloy Therapeutics’ venture‑studio model that provides seed funding, CRO‑like services, and partnership pathways for academic innovators, illustrating how such structures can de‑risk early projects and accelerate translation.
key_findings: Alloy’s venture studio seeds projects with both capital and operational support (e.g., CRO services). | Studio model enables academic founders to focus on science while leveraging in‑house expertise for regulatory and business development. | Alternative structures such as Deerfield’s academic partnerships, CurioBio’s pre‑money evaluation, and roll‑up funds are expanding the ecosystem.

talk_title: Community-engaged research partnerships to increase cancer prevention reach
affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Wetter described a community-engaged partnership model that aligns research activities with the priorities of federally qualified health centers, leverages existing evidence‑based interventions, and uses digital health tools (population health management, text messaging, AI chatbots) to achieve population‑level reach for tobacco cessation and cancer screening interventions.
key_findings: First tobacco cessation trial aimed for 6,000 enrollees but enrolled 12,000 patients (100% reach of eligible tobacco users). | COVID rapid‑cycle messaging trial enrolled 107,000 of 160,000 eligible patients (67% reach) using 37 message workflows across 227 cohorts. | Second COVID at‑home test trial enrolled 33,000 participants after pandemic peak. | CDC‑funded colorectal cancer screening program increased screening rates from 36% to 43%, a relative 20% increase and accounted for ~50% of national CHC screening gains.

talk_title: Social capital interventions and digital health to improve cancer prevention in persistent poverty neighborhoods
affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, New York City
moa_summary: Tamimi presented two social‑capital–focused interventions: a school‑based cancer risk education curriculum (Cares For You) that engages middle‑school students as health ambassadors, and a health‑system intervention (Bridge to Quit) that boosts patient‑portal enrollment through navigator support and low‑literacy education, thereby creating a digital conduit for smoking‑cessation referrals.
key_findings: Cares For You was implemented in four middle schools, reaching 288 students; 95% reported learning new science content during STEM Day. | Bridge to Quit increased patient‑portal enrollment from a baseline of 34‑44% to 61% after 18 months, surpassing the 50% target. | CDC‑funded colorectal cancer screening program raised screening rates from 36% to 43%, representing an almost 20% relative increase. | High satisfaction with educational components: 90% found speakers interesting, 85% learned about new jobs.

talk_title: Upstream income interventions and their impact on cancer‑related health behaviors
moa_summary: Bondy described two upstream socioeconomic interventions—state‑funded guaranteed cash transfers and the Earned Income Tax Credit—evaluated through randomized and observational designs to assess their effects on stress, health behaviors (diet, exercise, sleep) and downstream colorectal cancer risk factors.
key_findings: The guaranteed income pilot randomized 3,000 participants (2,200 pregnant women, 800 foster youth) to differing cash amounts. | A 75% survey response rate was achieved across baseline, 3‑month, 9‑month, exit, and 6‑month post‑exit assessments. | Planned analyses will examine changes in diet, exercise, sleep quality, and stress using the Hope Index. | EITC focus groups are being used to explore how tax credits influence colorectal cancer screening behaviors.
primary_population: California residents eligible for state pilot

talk_title: Iterative engineering of GPCR-targeted synthetic TCR and STAR T cells in advanced solid tumors
moa_summary: STAR T cells fuse a CAR extracellular domain with TCR signaling machinery, enabling low-antigen, high-sensitivity activation; CXCR2 expression improves tumor trafficking, and membrane-bound IL-15 provides autocrine survival signals, together enhancing expansion, persistence, and antitumor activity in GPC3-positive solid tumors.
key_findings: Version 2 STAR T cells achieved ORR 33.3% and DCR 66.7% in three heavily pretreated AFP-positive gastric cancer patients. | CXCR2-armored STAR T cells improved tumor trafficking in preclinical models. | Membrane-bound IL-15 (Version 3) increased ORR to 15% and DCR to 75% in four patients with advanced HCC or gastric cancer. | Grade ≥3 CRS occurred in 42.9% of patients; no treatment‑related deaths were observed.

talk_title: Cellular architecture of infused TILs predicts efficacy of autologous TIL therapy in acral melanoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cellular_therapy; TIL; clinical_trial; biomarker_development; single_cell
moa_summary: Autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes expanded ex vivo retain functional subsets; infusion of products enriched for TFH and intermediate exhausted CD8 T cells seeds progenitor‑like clones that persist and differentiate in vivo, driving durable tumor regression in acral melanoma.
key_findings: Three of four acral melanoma patients achieved disease control (75% DCR). | Responder showed >90% T cells in peripheral blood at week 12 versus low levels in stable disease patient. | Effective response linked to higher fractions of TFH and intermediate exhausted CD8 T cells, not total T cell count. | Clonal analysis revealed expanded progenitor‑like TCR clones persisting in blood up to 12 weeks post‑infusion.

talk_title: First‑in‑class CAR dendritic cell platform driving broad and durable antitumor immunity in solid tumors
affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis
moa_summary: CAR‑engineered type‑1 dendritic cells (CAR‑DC1) bearing a B7‑H3 scFv and a TLR4 intracellular domain capture tumor antigens, enhance cross‑presentation, and prime robust tumor‑specific CD8 T cell responses, leading to complete tumor rejection and long‑term immune memory even against antigen‑negative variants.
key_findings: TLR4‑signaling CAR‑DC1s induced strong costimulatory molecule upregulation (CCR7, CD86, CD70). | In vitro, CAR‑DC1s cross‑presented OVA and endogenous tumor peptides at markedly higher abundance than control DCs. | In the heterogeneous sarcoma model, 30/30 mice achieved complete tumor rejection after CAR‑DC1 treatment. | Mice cured by CAR‑DC1s rejected rechallenge with both B7‑H3‑positive and B7‑H3‑negative tumors, indicating epitope spreading.

talk_title: Decoding the medulloblastoma serpinosome prioritizes the oncofetal antigen GPC2 for potent CAR T cell therapy
moa_summary: GPC2‑directed CAR T cells with CD28 costimulatory domain and c‑Jun overexpression achieve low‑antigen detection, target GPC2‑high tumor‑initiating cells, and overcome antigen‑loss escape, leading to durable tumor control in medulloblastoma models and early clinical safety.
key_findings: GPC2 identified as top oncofetal surface antigen in medulloblastoma via surfaceome analysis. | CAR T cells required CD28 transmembrane domain to detect ~5,000‑6,000 GPC2 molecules/cell. | c‑Jun overexpression reduced antigen‑low escape and enhanced cytokine production. | Phase 1 trial (NCT‑pending) enrolled 4 patients with no dose‑limiting toxicities and detectable CAR T cells in CSF.

talk_title: NK TCR cells, a next‑generation platform expanding NK cell therapeutic potential
moa_summary: Cord‑blood NK cells are engineered to express a full CD3 complex and a tumor‑specific TCR (e.g., PRAME or NY‑ESO‑1), coupling antigen‑specific TCR signaling with innate NK cytotoxic receptors and IL‑15 support, resulting in rapid, serial killing of target cells with minimal off‑target activity.
key_findings: TCR‑NK cells killed target tumor spheroids faster than conventional TCR‑T cells (≈2 min vs ≈6 min per target). | In vivo, NK TCR treatment cured 5/5 myeloma‑bearing mice with durable engraftment in blood, bone marrow, and spleen. | No off‑target toxicity observed in peptide‑pulsed LCL assays or in treated patients (no CRS, ICANS, or GvHD). | First patient with NY‑ESO‑1‑positive myeloma achieved complete remission lasting >2 years after a single 22 M dose.
primary_population: NY-ESO-1 positive multiple myeloma, heavily pretreated

talk_title: Universal T cell therapy screening library and preclinical models of CAR T cell therapy
moa_summary: CRISPR‑all enables multiplexed knock‑in of CAR constructs and gene perturbations into the TRAC locus of primary T cells; the CACTUS library screens combinatorial edits to identify genetic configurations that enhance CAR T cell proliferation and antitumor activity against lymphoma.
key_findings: Synthetic chimeras (e.g., FAS‑OX40) and stemness factors (TCF7L1) consistently enriched across multiple screens. | Lymphoma‑specific hits (ARID family genes) emerged only in Toledo co‑culture screens. | In vivo screen identified similar top hits, with ~71% concordance between in vitro, in vivo, and deep‑learning model predictions.

talk_title: Triple disruption of CCDC6, ZEB1B, and ASB2 promotes therapeutic efficacy of GPC3‑targeted CAR T cells
moa_summary: IL‑15‑armed GPC3 CAR T cells achieve high expansion; integration‑mediated disruption of CBLB, CCDC6, and ASB2 further enhances proliferation and antitumor activity, likely by relieving negative regulation of TCR signaling and promoting survival pathways.
key_findings: One patient showed dramatic tumor reduction in 16 days with peak CAR T expansion of 14.7 B cells. | Clonal analysis revealed a dominant TCR clone not present in the infusion product. | Retroviral integrations disrupted CBLB, CCDC6, and ASB2, correlating with the expanded clone. | Triple knockout of these genes in IL‑15 CAR T cells led to greater T cell expansion and complete tumor control in HCC xenograft mice.
primary_population: IL-15 armored GPC3 CAR T recipient

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: The SynthBody Platform: A Multifunctional, Multivalent, Multispecific ADC Platform
moa_summary: The SynthBody platform utilizes a multivalent, multispecific architecture (Y6, X8, X12 formats) to create combinatorial synthetic targets that mimic high-density antigen expression (like HER2). By employing multi-tiered logic gate control and optimized 3D geometry, the platform increases binding strength, internalization rates, and payload delivery compared to monospecific ADCs. This cooperative binding approach allows for higher potency and resistance to soluble antigen sinks.
key_findings: Y6 synBody targeting CD38, BCMA, and GPRC5D showed in vitro potency approximately 80-fold stronger than BCMA-targeting belantamab in a high-bar myeloma resistance model. | SynBody demonstrated log-order increases in binding strength, internalization, trafficking to lysosomes, and payload delivery compared to monospecific controls. | SynBody is resistant to the presence of soluble targets, whereas belantamab is highly affected. | In vivo dose-ranging experiments showed 100% tumor growth inhibition (TGI) and 2 complete responses (CRs) at the highest dose tested with no body weight loss. | SynBody achieved equivalent efficacy to belantamab MMAE with 11X lower exposure. | A synBody targeting CD19, CD22, and CD79B showed log-order increases in killing potency compared to polatuzumab in both Ramos and HT NHL models.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: M7437: A Novel Anti-LY6E ADC with a Top1 Inhibitor Payload
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; head and neck cancer; pancreatic cancer; NSCLC; TNBC; gastric cancer
moa_summary: M7437 is an anti-LY6E ADC utilizing a transglutaminase-conjugated exatecan payload (DAR 4) and an LALA mutation to reduce Fc gamma receptor binding. It targets LY6E-expressing cancer cells, internalizes to the lysosome to release the Top1 inhibitor, and leverages a potent bystander effect to kill neighboring cells regardless of target expression. Preclinical data shows superior activity over MMAE-based benchmarks and other competitors like DATO-DXd in several PDX models.
key_findings: M7437 showed superior anti-tumor activity compared to 9B12 MMAE in head and neck and ovarian PDX models. | In a panel of PDX models, 17 reached the cutoff for preclinical regression and 8 showed complete regression across TNBC, ovarian, head and neck, NSCLC, pancreatic, and gastric cancer. | M7437 demonstrated superior activity compared to DATO-DXd in squamous and SCLC PDX models expressing both LY6E and Trop-2. | M7437 showed promising efficacy in a gastric cancer PDX model that was primary resistant to Enhertu (T-DXd). | GLP toxicity study in cynomolgus monkeys showed the drug was well tolerated, with primary toxicities being hematological (lymphocytes, neutrophils, reticulocytes). | In vitro PBMC assays showed proliferating NK cells were sensitive, while resting NK, B, and T cells were unaffected.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: Preclinical Evaluation of Target-Mediated Clearance and Alternative Payloads in Cadherin 17-Directed ADCs
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; stomach cancer
moa_summary: The study investigated the impact of target-mediated clearance (TMC) and P-gp efflux on Cadherin 17-directed ADCs. Results showed that normal expression of Cadherin 17 in the gut causes rapid clearance of the ADC (4-fold higher in humanized mice). Additionally, P-gp expression in colorectal cancer cells confers resistance to MMAE payloads, which can be overcome by using a Top1 inhibitor payload (exatecan) or P-gp inhibitors.
key_findings: Humanized mice expressing human Cadherin 17 showed rapid clearance of the ADC, with levels falling below detection within 48 hours, indicating a significant tissue sink in the gut. | In P-gp positive colorectal cancer models, exatecan-bearing ADCs maintained activity while MMAE-bearing ADCs lost activity. | Pharmacological inhibition of P-gp with ensequiror restored the response of MMAE-bearing ADCs in resistant LS513 cells. | Genetic knockout of P-gp in LS513 cells restored sensitivity to MMAE-bearing ADCs in vitro and in vivo. | Switching to an exatecan payload does not mitigate the target-mediated clearance liability caused by normal tissue expression. | TMC is driven by the antibody's binding to normal tissue and is independent of the ADC construct (free antibody showed same clearance rate).

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: Nuzepha: A Bicycle Drug Conjugate Targeting EphA2 in Solid Tumors
tumor_types: urothelial carcinoma; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC); lung cancer; ovarian cancer
moa_summary: Nuzepha is a bicycle drug conjugate (BDC) consisting of a small bicyclic peptide (4.5 kDa) targeting EphA2 linked to an MMAE payload. Due to its small size, it exhibits rapid renal clearance (half-life < 1 hour) and superior tumor penetration compared to antibodies. This pharmacology creates a 'drug-free interval' between weekly doses, which widens the therapeutic window and avoids the severe coagulopathy and hematological toxicities seen with EphA2-targeting antibody-based conjugates.
key_findings: Nuzepha demonstrated significant efficacy in HNSCC xenografts (Fadu, SCC9, Cal27), with doses of 3 and 5 mg/kg producing near complete regressions in aggressive lines. | The BDC format avoids the bleeding toxicity (coagulopathy) associated with antibody-based EphA2 conjugates. | The small size of the molecule allows for rapid delivery and penetration, making episodic dosing sufficient for efficacy. | The binding affinity for EphA2 is 2 nM. | An MMAF version of the construct was found to be decidedly less potent than the MMAE version.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: Targeting the Non-Shed Domain of MUC16 with a Proprietary Topo I ADC Platform
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; endometrial cancer; pancreatic cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: The speaker presents HOX16, an ADC targeting the non-shed domain of MUC16 to avoid target-mediated drug disposition caused by shed CA125. The platform utilizes a proprietary Topo I inhibitor payload with a DAR-6 configuration, PEG masking for hydrophobicity, and a 'carbon bridge cysteine repairing' technology to replace interchain disulfides for enhanced stability.
key_findings: HOX16 antibody binds the non-shed portion of MUC16 with sub-nanomolar affinity and is minimally impacted by exogenous CA125, unlike the DMAC antibody which showed a 22-fold IC50 shift. | HOX16 ADC demonstrated sub-nanomolar internalization efficiency. | Bystander effect was demonstrated by the ability to eliminate 100% of cells in a 3:1 mixture where 33% were antigen-negative (Raji-Luc). | In ovarian CDX models, HOX16 caused tumor regressions at 1 mg/kg, whereas the original DAR-4 MMIE construct (DMAC) was incapable of causing regressions at the same dose. | In vitro plasma stability showed <0.4% payload release over 21 days, which is >10-fold higher stability compared to the DRUG-DXd platform (which showed >4% release). | NHP tox studies showed the ADC was well-tolerated up to 60 mg/kg with no mortalities.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: The Prism AIR Assay for Characterizing Antibody Internalization and ADC Sensitivity
affiliation: Prisma Lab at the Broad Institute at MIT
moa_summary: The speaker describes the Prism AIR (Antibody Internalization Reporter) assay, which uses a universal secondary Fab-MMAF conjugate to mimic ADC function. This allows for the functional validation of any humanized IgG antibody's internalization and killing capacity without needing to synthesize a custom ADC for every target.
key_findings: Screening of 50 ADCs across 25 targets showed that sensitivity strongly correlates with target antigen expression (gene expression and copy number amplification). | Prism AIR showed strong concordance with actual ADC data for the target F3 (tozotamab), validating the AIR format as a proxy for ADC activity. | Differential analysis between datopotamab deruxtecan and datopotamab AIR (MMAF) identified SLC46A3 as a driver of sensitivity for non-cleavable linkers. | Ceticizumab showed poor correlation with its target antigen in the AIR format, which the speaker attributes to the instability of its specific linker chemistry releasing free payload.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: Preclinical and Clinical Evaluation of STRO-004, a Tissue Factor-Targeted ADC
tumor_types: head and neck cancer; NSCLC; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer; cervical cancer
moa_summary: STRO-004 is a site-specific DAR8 ADC targeting Tissue Factor (F3) with an exatecan (Topo I inhibitor) payload and a beta-glucuronidase cleavable linker. It is designed to reduce bleeding risk and ILD (via lack of FC gamma function) while increasing payload delivery and bystander killing compared to first-generation TF-MMAE ADCs.
key_findings: STRO-004 achieved an overall ORR of 64% and DCR of 90% across 70 PDX models in a mouse clinical trial. | In head and neck models, STRO-004 showed superior ORR (57% vs 38%) and median PFS (45 vs 27 days) compared to the TF-MMAE benchmark. | In pancreatic models, STRO-004 showed an ORR of 70% compared to 50% for the TF-MMAE benchmark. | STRO-004 was well-tolerated in NHPs up to 50 mg/kg with no significant heme toxicities or bleeding/eye toxicity. | Response is multifactorial; combining Tissue Factor and Schlafen 11 expression levels better captures response heterogeneity than either alone.

talk_title: Phase I intraperitoneal mesothelin-targeted CAR T therapy in esophageal and gastric cancer with peritoneal carcinomatosis
moa_summary: An autologous mesothelin-targeted CAR T (M28 Zeta-1XXPD1-DNR) with CD28 costimulation and a PD-1 dominant negative receptor is delivered intraperitoneally, providing regional tumor exposure, intrinsic checkpoint blockade, and prolonged peripheral persistence beyond day 100, but antitumor activity is limited by heterogeneous mesothelin expression.
key_findings: Screened 54 patients, 19 (35%) were mesothelin-positive and eligible. | Seven patients received intraperitoneal CAR T; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed. | CAR T cells persisted in peripheral blood beyond 100 days. | Best radiographic response was stable disease; no objective responses observed. | Low‑grade CRS was common and manageable; one patient experienced grade 3 IE‑CHS resolved with steroids.

talk_title: Phase I trial of UCMYM802 (Lego‑STIM armored mesothelin CAR T) in advanced solid tumors
tumor_types: peritoneal mesothelioma; pancreatic cancer; cholangiocarcinoma; ovarian cancer; lung adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: UCMYM802 is an mRNA‑CAR T product encoding an anti‑mesothelin scFv with 4‑1BB costimulation and a membrane‑bound CD14‑CD28 Lego‑STIM construct that enhances innate and adaptive immunity, yielding rapid but transient CAR expression and dose‑dependent T‑cell activation.
key_findings: Nine patients received at least one infusion; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed. | ORR 28.6% and DCR 57.1% across evaluable patients, with two partial responses. | Transient CAR expression peaks within 1 hour post‑infusion and declines after 8 hours. | Higher dose levels correlated with increased CAR and Lego expression and with central memory CD8 T‑cell expansion. | Low incidence of high‑grade CRS; cytokine release was modest compared with protein CAR T.

talk_title: First‑in‑human study of KSQ001EX CRISPR‑edited SOX1‑inactivated TIL therapy
moa_summary: Autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes are edited with CRISPR‑Cas9 to delete SOX1, a negative regulator of cytokine signaling, resulting in enhanced expansion, persistence, and a CD8‑centric cytotoxic phenotype after infusion.
