key_findings: Twelve patients received KSQ001EX; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed. | ORR 33% with one patient achieving a durable partial response >12 months. | Higher cell doses (>5.25 B) correlated with tumor reduction in 100% of patients. | Engineered TILs expanded and persisted in peripheral blood for up to 500 days. | Tumor biopsies showed increased CD8 cytotoxic T cells and remodeling of the microenvironment.

talk_title: Five‑year overall survival of tebentafusp in previously untreated metastatic uveal melanoma (Phase III)
moa_summary: Tebentafusp is an affinity‑enhanced soluble T‑cell receptor fused to anti‑CD3 that redirects polyclonal T cells to GP100‑presenting uveal melanoma cells, leading to tumor cell killing and durable immune activation independent of radiographic response.
key_findings: Five‑year OS hazard ratio 0.67 with survival doubling from 8% to 16% at 5 years. | Disease control rate improved to 46% versus 27% with investigator's choice. | Baseline ctDNA undetectable patients had markedly better OS (HR 0.5). | ctDNA clearance at week 9 occurred in 37% of patients and predicted superior survival (HR 0.32). | Treatment beyond radiographic progression yielded additional tumor regressions and prolonged post‑progression survival.

talk_title: Phase 1b/2a study of RZ001 adenoviral HTERT‑targeting ribozyme with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab in HCC
moa_summary: RZ001 is an adenoviral vector delivering a trans‑splicing ribozyme that replaces hTERT mRNA with a suicide HSV‑TK gene; in the presence of ganciclovir, tumor cells undergo DNA replication arrest and apoptosis while also inducing CD4/CD8 infiltration and VEGF down‑regulation, synergizing with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab.
key_findings: Intratumoral RZ001 combined with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab yielded ORR 40% (RECIST) and 61.5% (mRECIST). | No dose‑limiting toxicities; grade 3/4 treatment‑related AEs occurred in 53% but were manageable. | Rapid onset of responses with durable disease control observed at data cutoff. | All patients expressed hTERT; viral delivery was well tolerated without systemic replication.

talk_title: Phase 1/2 trial of LOAD‑703 adenoviral CD40L/4‑1BBL vector with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel ± atezolizumab in advanced pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: LOAD‑703 is an oncolytic adenovirus serotype 5/35 encoding membrane‑bound CD40L and 4‑1BBL, delivered intratumorally to activate dendritic cells, convert M2 to M1 macrophages, and provide co‑stimulatory signals to T cells, thereby turning a cold pancreatic tumor microenvironment into a Th1‑type inflamed state that synergizes with chemotherapy and PD‑L1 blockade.
key_findings: In arm 2 (LOAD‑703 + chemo + atezolizumab), ORR was 38% with three complete responses. | First‑line patients (n=13) achieved median OS 14.7 months and 1‑year survival 62%. | Peripheral CD8 effector memory T cells increased while Tregs decreased after treatment. | Tumor biopsies showed upregulation of Th1 cytokines and down‑regulation of fibroblast/TGF‑β signatures.

talk_title: Risk stratification and emerging intravesical/systemic therapies for non‑muscle invasive bladder cancer
affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Surgeon Scientist
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; ADC; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Abbash highlighted that BCG‑refractory non‑muscle invasive bladder cancer can be treated with checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab), intravesical adenoviral vectors (Adstiladrin), IL‑15 agonists (Anctiva) and FGFR3‑targeted agents, each achieving high complete response rates, and emphasized the need for molecular risk stratification to match patients to these modalities.
key_findings: Pembrolizumab achieved a 41% complete response rate in BCG‑unresponsive NMIBC. | Adstiladrin (intravesical adenoviral) yielded a 53% complete response rate for CIS at 3 months. | Anctiva (IL‑15 agonist) demonstrated a 71% complete response rate in BCG‑unresponsive disease. | Enfortumab vedotin combined with pembrolizumab (EV‑pembro) doubled median overall survival to 31.5 months in metastatic bladder cancer.

talk_title: Adjuvant ctDNA‑guided immunotherapy and peri‑operative enfortumab‑vedotin + pembrolizumab in muscle‑invasive bladder cancer
affiliation: Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, Lillian and Howard Stratton Professor of Medicine
topic_categories: immunotherapy; ADC; clinical_trial; biomarker_development; combination_therapy; translational
moa_summary: Galsky presented that adjuvant checkpoint blockade improves DFS, especially when patients are selected by ctDNA positivity; ctDNA‑guided atezolizumab (IMvigor11) showed benefit, and the MODERN trial tests escalation with LAG‑3 inhibition versus surveillance. In the peri‑operative setting, enfortumab‑vedotin + pembrolizumab yields a 57% pathologic CR, suggesting a bladder‑sparing strategy when combined with systemic therapy.
key_findings: Adjuvant nivolumab improves disease‑free survival after cystectomy (CheckMate 274). | In IMvigor11, ctDNA‑positive patients derived a DFS benefit from atezolizumab versus placebo. | MODERN trial uses ctDNA to randomize patients to nivolumab ± LAG‑3 (escalation) or surveillance (de‑escalation). | Peri‑operative EV‑pembro achieved a 57.1% pathologic complete response, markedly higher than cisplatin‑based neoadjuvant therapy. | Clinical complete response after neoadjuvant chemo‑PD‑1 therapy predicts excellent metastasis‑free survival, and undetectable ctDNA/utDNA correlates with true remission.
primary_value: significant improvement vs placebo (exact HR not disclosed)

talk_title: Modeling APOBEC‑driven resistance and rational sequential combinations in bladder cancer
affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Medical Oncologist & Physician Scientist
moa_summary: Faltas demonstrated that transient APOBEC3A expression creates double‑strand DNA breaks, induces late‑S‑phase stasis, opens chromatin at the SOX2 locus, and via FOXM1 binding establishes a durable SOX2‑high state (DASP) that confers multi‑drug resistance, enhanced migration, and EMT‑like plasticity; degradation of SOX2 with a PROTAC restores drug sensitivity.
key_findings: APOBEC3A exposure increased neratinib IC50 4.8‑fold and conferred cross‑resistance to HER2‑ADC (T‑DXd), FGFR3 inhibitors, and enfortumab‑vedotin. | A3A‑exposed cells showed persistent late‑S‑phase stasis, γH2AX and p‑RPA32 elevation, and sustained chromatin accessibility at the SOX2 promoter and distal enhancer. | FOXM1 binds the SOX2 promoter 11‑fold more in A3A‑exposed cells, driving SOX2 transcription. | SOX2‑PROTAC sensitized A3A‑driven resistant cells to trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, confirming SOX2 as a therapeutic vulnerability. | DASP signature (FOXM1, SOX2, S‑phase, EMT genes) predicts poorer progression‑free and overall survival across TCGA pan‑cancer cohorts.

talk_title: CHD7 drives lineage plasticity and replication competence in neuroendocrine prostate cancer
affiliation: University of British Columbia, Dr. Weizhu Wan's lab
moa_summary: CHD7 is progressively up‑regulated during androgen deprivation‑induced NEPC development, where it maintains open chromatin at lineage and replication stress genes, promotes NE transcription factor expression, and sustains ATR checkpoint activity, thereby driving lineage plasticity and cisplatin resistance.
key_findings: CHD7 expression is minimal in pre‑castration prostate tissue, induced early post‑castration, and sustained in terminal NEPC lesions. | CHD7 knockdown reduces NE marker expression, cell growth, DNA synthesis, and increases DNA damage and apoptosis. | CHD7 directly binds and maintains accessibility at ~1,805 loci, regulating 563 transcriptional targets involved in neural fate and DNA replication. | Loss of CHD7 impairs ATR checkpoint phosphorylation and sensitizes NEPC cells to cisplatin (IC50 markedly reduced). | Combining cisplatin with ATR checkpoint inhibition further decreases viability compared with either agent alone.

talk_title: KDM5B‑SOX9 signaling drives therapy‑induced neuroendocrine prostate cancer
moa_summary: KDM5B up‑regulation in therapy‑induced NEPC activates SOX9 transcription, which drives neuroendocrine marker expression; loss of KDM5B diminishes SOX9 and NE markers, while overexpression rescues them, establishing a KDM5B‑SOX9 axis essential for NE differentiation.
key_findings: KDM5B and SOX9 are co‑overexpressed in ~46% of NEPC cases and correlate with NSE levels. | KDM5B knockout reduces SOX9, NSE, and SYN expression in NEPC cell lines. | Restoring KDM5B rescues SOX9 and NE markers in a dose‑dependent manner. | Castrated PTEN‑p53 mice develop NE recurrence with high KDM5B, SOX9, NSE, and SYN. | Triple knockout of PTEN‑p53‑KDM5B prevents tumor recurrence and NE marker expression.

talk_title: Promoter methylation gains in aging and colon cancer are independent of replication
moa_summary: Long‑term organoid culture and BRAF V600E induction reveal that CpG‑island promoter hypermethylation accumulates with age independent of cell division, whereas non‑CGI hypomethylation tracks with replication, indicating distinct mechanisms for methylation gains versus losses during tumorigenesis.
key_findings: Global CpG methylation does not change acutely after BRAF V600E induction. | CGI promoters gain methylation over months in both control and BRAF lineages, independent of replication. | Non‑CGI sites lose methylation in a replication‑dependent manner, accelerated in BRAF organoids. | Transcription factor enrichment differs: control lineage shows stress/viral TFs; BRAF lineage enriches cancer‑related TFs. | External datasets (replication inhibition, mitomycin C) confirm that promoter hypermethylation is replication‑independent while loss is replication‑linked.

talk_title: Cooperativity between DNA methylation and EZH2 drives neuroendocrine prostate cancer
moa_summary: Loss of EZH2 in NEPC remodels the DNA methylome, increasing promoter hypermethylation and altering neuroendocrine gene expression; conversely, DNMT1 deletion redistributes H3K27me3, revealing a bidirectional crosstalk where each epigenetic silencer compensates for the other in a disease‑state‑specific manner.
key_findings: EZH2 deletion in NEPC mice leads to global DNA methylation changes at PRC2 target DMRs. | EZH2‑KO NEPC organoids show increased NE signature scores despite loss of H3K27me3. | DNMT1 deletion in NEPC models causes increased H3K27me3 at neuron‑related promoters, suppressing their expression. | Pharmacologic EZH2 inhibition mirrors genetic EZH2 loss; DNMT inhibition (decitabine) mirrors DNMT1 deletion, both reshaping H3K27me3 landscapes. | In adenocarcinoma context, DNMT1 loss upregulates neuroendocrine genes, highlighting cell‑state‑specific effects.

talk_title: Targeting the ubiquitin‑reading function of DNMT1 as a next‑generation epigenetic therapy
moa_summary: DNMT1 requires its ubiquitin‑interacting motifs (UIMs) to read UHRF1‑mediated H3 ubiquitination; mutating UIMs abolishes DNA methylation maintenance, induces viral‑mimicry gene expression, and reduces cancer cell viability, providing a druggable interface distinct from catalytic inhibition.
key_findings: UIM‑mutant DNMT1 fails to bind ubiquitinated nucleosomes despite retained catalytic activity. | Loss of UIM function leads to global hypomethylation comparable to DNMT1 knockdown. | UIM loss upregulates viral‑mimicry pathways and downregulates oncogenic signaling, mirroring decitabine effects. | Cell viability and colony formation are severely impaired in UIM mutants, similar to catalytic dead DNMT1. | Screening identified a natural product (afetinin) and de‑novo designed protein binders that inhibit DNMT1 UIMs at low‑nanomolar affinity.

talk_title: KDM2A‑driven epigenetic reprogramming promotes Barrett’s esophagus to adenocarcinoma progression
moa_summary: KDM2A amplification reduces H3K36me2 and elevates H3K27ac, reprogramming chromatin to open enhancer landscapes that drive two distinct epithelial programs—secretory (SPINK1/PSCA) and proliferative (MYC, HER2)—facilitating Barrett’s metaplasia progression to heterogeneous esophageal adenocarcinoma.
key_findings: KDM2A is the most amplified histone demethylase in EAC TCGA cohort. | KDM2A expression shows a trend of increase from Barrett through dysplasia to EAC. | High KDM2A correlates with reduced H3K36me2 and increased H3K27ac at oncogenic loci. | KDM2A overexpression in HET‑1A opens chromatin at MYC and other oncogenes, inducing secretory and proliferative gene programs. | Patient‑derived organoids with high KDM2A display enhanced enhancer activity and poorer prognosis.

talk_title: KMT2D is a master epigenetic regulator of lineage plasticity in lung squamous cell carcinoma
tumor_types: lung squamous cell carcinoma; lung adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Loss of KMT2D in KRAS/TP53‑driven lung adenocarcinoma reduces H3K4me1 at FOXA1/FOXA2‑bound enhancers, shifts cellular states toward a basal‑like, integrin α6β4 stem‑like cluster, and promotes a trajectory through GI‑like intermediates to squamous transcriptional programs (TRP63, SOX2), driven by redistribution of MEN1‑associated H3K4me3 and cooperation with FOXA1/FOXA2.
key_findings: KMT2D deletion shortens survival in KP mice initiated from both club and AT2 cells. | Adenosquamous lesions appear exclusively in KMT2D‑null tumors. | Single‑nucleus multi‑omics reveal enrichment of a basal‑like cluster and an integrin α6β4 stem‑like intermediate in KMT2D‑KO tumors. | Chromatin regions losing H3K4me1 in KMT2D‑KO are enriched for FOXA1/FOXA2 motifs and co‑factor binding (SMARCC1, EP300). | FOXA1 physically interacts with KMT2D; MEN1 inhibition (MI‑503) shows increased sensitivity in KMT2D‑KO cells.

talk_title: Asymmetric Emergence of Cardiovascular Disease in Hematologic Malignancy Survivors
affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis
moa_summary: Analyzed bi-directional risk between hematologic malignancy survivors and cardiovascular disease using All of Us cohorts, identifying high early CVD incidence especially in MDS and multiple myeloma, and implicating proteasome inhibitor treatment as a potential driver.
key_findings: CVD incidence sharply increases within first 2 years after hematologic malignancy diagnosis, median time 2.4 years. | MDS and multiple myeloma patients have the highest CVD risk in the first five years. | Arrhythmias are the most common CVD subtype post‑diagnosis. | Proteasome inhibitor exposure yields the highest composite cardiotoxicity score among treatments. | In vitro Cell Village assay shows proteasome inhibitor induces inflammatory/dysfunctional cardiac cell states and cell death.

talk_title: Decoding Melanoma Immunogenicity: A Comprehensive Proteogenomic, Immunopeptidomic, and Metabolomic Atlas
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; proteomics; genomics; metabolomics; spatial_biology; biomarker_development; basic_science
moa_summary: Integrated 13‑layer multi‑omics atlas of melanoma revealed that high HLA‑I peptide presentation and tumor antigens from MAGEA genes drive CD8 T‑cell infiltration and improved ICI survival, while distinct ubiquitination and glycosylation patterns define prognostic subgroups.
key_findings: Identified 1,138 unique tumor antigen candidates, >50% absent from IEDB. | Tumor antigen count positively correlates with CD8 T‑cell infiltration and ICI‑treated survival. | MAGEA3/4/12 antigen events strongly associate with increased CD8 T‑cell fraction. | A ubiquitin‑high interferon subtype shows worse survival; RNF4‑DHX9 and BIRC7‑AKT1 ubiquitin pairs are prognostic. | A lysosome‑high glycoproteomic subtype displays sex‑specific survival: poor in males, neutral/beneficial in females, linked to GPUMB fucosylation.

talk_title: Endocrine Therapy Adherence and 10‑Year Risk of Cardiometabolic Outcomes Among Women with Hormone Receptor‑Positive Breast Cancer
moa_summary: Using a prospective Kaiser cohort, proportion of days covered measured endocrine therapy adherence and sequentially doubly robust estimation linked longer AI use to higher 10‑year hypertension, dyslipidemia, and diabetes risk, while both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced mortality.
key_findings: Five‑year endocrine therapy adherence increased 10‑yr hypertension risk by 9 percentage points. | AI use, but not tamoxifen, was associated with higher dyslipidemia (33% vs 26%) and diabetes (13% vs 9.5%) risks. | Both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced all‑cause mortality (18% vs 28%) and breast cancer‑specific mortality (6‑8% vs 13%). | Adherent patients were younger, higher socioeconomic status, and had fewer baseline comorbidities.

talk_title: Comprehensive Phenotypic Mapping of ALT Regulation Expands the Landscape of Targetable Suppressors
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Department of Radiation Oncology
tumor_types: glioma; pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; soft tissue sarcoma; osteosarcoma; neuroblastoma
moa_summary: Developed Alt‑Flow‑FISH to quantify ALT activity, performed genome‑wide CRISPR screens identifying proteasome components and akirin‑2 as ALT suppressors; proteasome loss or inhibition accumulates PML, drives hyper‑ALT and cell death, suggesting proteasome inhibition as a therapeutic strategy for ALT cancers.
key_findings: Alt‑Flow‑FISH enables high‑throughput single‑cell ALT activity measurement. | Genome‑wide CRISPR screen uncovered numerous ALT suppressors, notably proteasome subunits (PSMB1, PSMB5, PSMB6) and akirin‑2. | Proteasome depletion leads to PML protein accumulation and hyper‑ALT phenotype with increased c‑circles. | Overexpression of PML alone induces hyper‑ALT and cell death; bortezomib recapitulates this effect. | Pathway analysis highlighted DNA repair, replication, proteasome, and Fanconi anemia pathways as ALT regulators.

talk_title: Protein Complex Remodeling in Metastatic Breast Cancer Cells
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Troy Atticher Lab
moa_summary: Integrated confocal imaging, IP‑MS, SEC‑MS, and single‑cell proteomics with self‑supervised deep learning to map dynamic protein complex reorganization in TNBC cells under chemotherapy, revealing treatment‑specific assembly/disassembly of complexes such as stress response, transcriptional activation, and SWI/SNF loss.
key_findings: HDAC8 relocalizes from cytosol/nucleus to nucleus after paclitaxel. | HDAC1 interaction partners shift with treatment, indicating dynamic complex remodeling. | Identified >100 protein complexes; ~50% are novel assemblies. | Stress response complex emerges uniquely in vernostat‑treated cells. | Transcriptional activation complex CBP‑300 appears only after paclitaxel. | SWI/SNF complex is lost under both chemotherapies.

talk_title: ASP2998 – TROP2‑Targeted Immunomodulatory ADC with Dual Payloads Demonstrates Potent Efficacy and Favorable Safety
affiliation: Astellas Pharma Global Development, Seattle, WA (collaboration with Sutro)
tumor_types: urothelial carcinoma; non‑small cell lung cancer; gastric cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: ASP2998 is a humanized IgG1 TROP2‑directed ADC delivering a topoisomerase‑I inhibitor and a STING agonist; tumor cell killing releases the STING agonist, activating innate immunity and T‑cell responses, resulting in tumor regression and durable immune memory.
key_findings: Dual‑payload ADC shows enhanced potency in PBMC co‑culture versus cytotoxic‑only ADCs. | In vivo MC38‑hTROP2 model: ASP2998 induces tumor regression and complete protection upon rechallenge. | PK data reveal >99.9% of drug remains intact; free payloads are ~1,000‑fold lower. | GLP repeat‑dose study shows manageable safety profile; target organs at high dose include cornea, kidney, urethra, trachea. | Low IL‑6 induction suggests limited cytokine release syndrome risk.
primary_metric: Tumor regression in large MC38‑hTROP2 model
primary_value: Complete regression at high dose, tumor stasis at lower dose

talk_title: Targeting EYA4 Disrupts Genome Stability in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer
affiliation: University of Texas San Antonio, Greehey Children’s Cancer Center
moa_summary: EYA4 binds ssDNA via an N‑terminal basic/AT‑hook region; DNA binding enhances its tyrosine phosphatase activity toward γH2AX and RAD51, promoting homologous recombination. Inhibition or depletion of EYA4 induces HR deficiency, γH2AX accumulation, and tumor growth suppression.
key_findings: EYA4 is hyper‑methylated yet overexpressed in TNBC; highest in MDA‑MB‑231. | EYA4 depletion reduces primary tumor growth and lung metastasis in vivo. | EYA4 binds ssDNA with nanomolar affinity; phosphomimetic mutation reduces binding. | DNA binding markedly stimulates EYA4 tyrosine phosphatase activity. | Small‑molecule inhibitor CID‑XXXX89 reduces HR efficiency (dose‑dependent GFP loss) and cell viability.

talk_title: Neural integration and synaptic networks drive glioblastoma progression
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; tumor_microenvironment; basic_science; translational
moa_summary: Perampanel, a non‑competitive AMPA‑receptor antagonist, blocks excitatory synaptic inputs from neurons to glioblastoma cells, reducing calcium‑mediated proliferation and invasion; additional neuronal‑derived signals (acetylcholine) also promote tumor growth, and GPM6A‑driven tumor microtubules facilitate invasion.
key_findings: Synaptic contacts from neurons to glioblastoma cells were visualized by EM in patient samples and xenografts. | Neuronal AMPA‑receptor activity drives calcium influx, proliferation, and invasion of glioblastoma cells. | Perampanel is being tested in the phase I/II PERSECH trial using RNA‑Seq connectivity scores and AI‑based MRI growth dynamics as primary endpoints. | Rabies‑based retrograde tracing reveals widespread, dynamic neuron‑to‑tumor connectivity across the whole brain. | Cholinergic signaling and GPM6A‑mediated tumor microtubules represent additional mechanisms of invasion.

talk_title: Screening platform to discover tumor‑microtubule disconnecting agents
moa_summary: PKC activation by TPPB reduces expression of the tumor‑microtubule marker TTYH2, leading to morphological disconnection of glioblastoma tumor microtubules and silencing of calcium‑mediated pacemaker activity, thereby sensitizing tumors to radiotherapy.
key_findings: A high‑content 2D tumor‑microtubule assay enabled quantitative segmentation of microtubule number, length, and cell viability. | Screen of 71 compounds identified PKC activators (PMA, TPPB) that disconnect microtubules without killing cells. | TPPB treatment reduced TTYH2 expression, decreased microtubule length, and silenced periodic calcium activity in three glioblastoma cell lines. | In vivo, TPPB combined with radiotherapy eradicated tumor cells in the invasion zone and extended mouse survival. | Spatial COMET analysis showed TTYH2 down‑regulation in the invasion zone and increased apoptosis (caspase‑3) after TPPB treatment.

talk_title: Monocyte and neutrophil dynamics shape glioblastoma myeloid microenvironment
moa_summary: In hypoxic necrotic GBM cores, neutrophils undergo MPO‑dependent NETosis; macrophages phagocytose NET debris, upregulating immunosuppressive markers (ARG1, PD‑L1) and suppressing T‑cell activity, thereby promoting tumor progression. MPO deficiency blocks NETosis, increases neutrophil numbers, reduces immunosuppression, and extends survival.
key_findings: Neutrophil infiltration occurs late in GBM progression and is enriched in necrotic, hypoxic cores. | Neutrophils undergo MPO‑dependent NETosis; macrophages engulf NETs and become immunosuppressive (ARG1, PD‑L1 upregulation). | MPO‑deficient tumors show increased neutrophil numbers, reduced immunosuppressive phenotype, and improved mouse survival. | Human GBM samples display macrophage engulfment of MPO, especially in mesenchymal subtype. | Neutrophil‑derived factors suppress T‑cell proliferation in vitro, an effect reversed in MPO‑knockout conditions.

talk_title: Single‑cell and spatial genomics map glioma heterogeneity and evolution
moa_summary: High‑resolution single‑cell and spatial transcriptomics reveal four developmental‑like glioma states (OPC‑like, NPC‑like, astrocytic‑like, mesenchymal‑like) whose relative abundance is shaped by genetics, microenvironmental cues (hypoxia, vasculature), and therapy; spatial mapping links necrotic cores to mesenchymal/inflammatory niches and peripheral zones to neurodevelopmental states, informing region‑specific therapeutic strategies.
key_findings: Large cohort (n>100) confirms four dominant glioblastoma cell states; mesenchymal compartment further subdivides into hypoxia‑ and stress‑associated programs. | Visium HD spatial analysis shows layered organization: necrotic core → mesenchymal/inflammatory → angiogenic hub → neurodevelopmental (OPC/NPC) zones. | Subclonal copy‑number events are spatially confined, with distinct clones populating leptomeningeal versus parenchymal regions. | Phylogenetic trees based on SNVs reveal that advanced clones are enriched for OPC/NPC‑like states, suggesting state‑specific evolutionary pressure. | IDH‑mutant gliomas display hypomethylation‑driven loss of differentiation; IDH inhibitors promote cancer‑cell differentiation, correlating with clinical response.

talk_title: Definitive non-operative management with tremelimumab plus durvalumab in resectable MSI‑high gastric/GEJ cancer (INFINITY cohort 2)
affiliation: Fondazione Istituto Nazionale dei Tumori di Milano, Milan, Italy
moa_summary: Dual checkpoint blockade with a single dose of anti‑CTLA‑4 (tremelimumab) and three cycles of anti‑PD‑L1 (durvalumab) induces deep tumor immune activation in MSI‑high gastric/GEJ cancer, enabling a composite clinical complete response that permits omission of surgery in the majority of patients.
key_findings: 71% (13/17) of patients achieved a composite clinical complete response after 3 months of tremelimumab + durvalumab and avoided surgery. | 24‑month PFS was 94.1% and OS was 100% with a median follow‑up of 27.1 months. | Gastrectomy‑free survival at 2 years was 70.6%, demonstrating feasibility of a non‑operative approach. | Grade 3 treatment‑related adverse events occurred in 17% of patients; no grade 4/5 events were observed. | Baseline ctDNA was detectable in 13/17 patients; ctDNA clearance correlated with clinical complete response (specificity 100%). | RNA‑seq revealed immune‑permissive signatures in responders versus stromal/EMT signatures in non‑responders.

talk_title: Phase 2 MATIS trial of CD39 inhibitor IPH5201 combined with durvalumab and platinum‑based chemotherapy in resectable NSCLC
affiliation: Gustave Roussy, University of Paris, France
moa_summary: Inhibition of CD39 with IPH5201 blocks extracellular ATP conversion to immunosuppressive adenosine, enhancing dendritic cell activation and T‑cell priming; combined with PD‑L1 blockade (durvalumab) and platinum‑based chemotherapy, this strategy increases pathological complete responses in resectable NSCLC.
key_findings: pCR was 27.5% in the intent‑to‑treat cohort (n=40), exceeding historical 25% benchmarks for chemo‑immunotherapy alone. | Patients with PD‑L1 >1% achieved a pCR of 35.7%; those with PD‑L1 >50% reached 50% pCR. | Higher baseline CD39 stromal density correlated with higher pCR/MPR rates. | 87.5% of patients proceeded to surgery; peri‑operative safety was comparable to standard chemo‑immunotherapy. | Grade 3 AEs occurred in 55% of patients, with 32% deemed drug‑related; one postoperative death due to pneumonia was reported.

talk_title: Phase 1 first‑in‑human study of anti‑CCR8 antibody denicitug in advanced solid tumors
affiliation: Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center & Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute, Boston, USA
tumor_types: non‑small cell lung cancer; gastric/gastroesophageal cancer; gynecologic malignancies; breast cancer; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: Denicitug is an afucosylated anti‑CCR8 monoclonal antibody that depletes intratumoral CCR8‑positive regulatory T cells via enhanced ADCC, leading to reduced immunosuppression and a concurrent increase in CD8‑effector T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: Denicitug monotherapy achieved an ORR of 8% and a DCR of 46% in a heavily pre‑treated cohort (median 3 prior lines). | Partial responses were observed at the lowest biologically active dose (10 mg) and were durable up to 14.6 months in one NSCLC patient. | Pharmacodynamic analyses showed near‑complete depletion of CCR8+ intratumoral Tregs and a concurrent expansion of CD8+ effector T cells across all dose levels. | Safety profile was manageable; most common treatment‑related AEs were rash, pruritus, diarrhea, and fatigue, with no grade 4/5 events. | No clear relationship between baseline CCR8 expression and clinical response was identified in this heterogeneous population.

talk_title: Multi‑omic dissection of primary versus acquired immunotherapy resistance in NSCLC (HUDSON trial cohort)
affiliation: Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center at Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, USA
topic_categories: biomarker_development; genomics; transcriptomics; single_cell; resistance_mechanisms; translational; other
moa_summary: Comprehensive multi‑omic profiling of IO‑resistant NSCLC reveals that acquired resistance is driven by genomic instability, EMT, lineage plasticity, and metabolic reprogramming, whereas primary resistance is associated with canonical driver alterations (STK11, KEAP1, KRAS) and immune‑exclusion signatures.
key_findings: Acquired resistance tumors displayed higher tumor mutation burden and greater subclonal diversity than primary resistance tumors. | Genomic scarring (large‑scale transitions, telomeric allele imbalance, HRD) was enriched in post‑IO samples of both resistance groups. | Mutations in FGF, mTOR, NRAS, RBM10, DNMT3A, and DNA‑damage‑repair genes were significantly enriched in the acquired resistance cohort. | Bulk RNA‑seq showed up‑regulation of interferon‑γ, inflammatory, and EMT pathways in acquired resistance tumors. | Single‑cell analysis identified expanded pluripotent stem‑like epithelial cells and tissue‑resident memory CD8 T cells in acquired resistance, alongside reduced antigen presentation.

talk_title: Mechanisms of early dissemination and phenotypic plasticity in melanoma metastasis
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; basic_science; metastasis; spatial_biology; epigenetics; mechanobiology
moa_summary: Hernando demonstrated that mechanical compression and narrow-pore migration induce loss of heterochromatin (H3K9me3) and nuclear de‑density, converting differentiated melanocytic cells into a mesenchymal‑like state with increased deformability and metastatic potential, mediated in part by RUNX1‑driven gene networks and metabolic adaptations such as FATP1/2‑mediated lipid uptake and CNDP1‑driven mitochondrial protection in brain metastases.
key_findings: Constriction through 3 µm pores converts melanocytic melanoma cells into a mesenchymal‑like phenotype with reduced H3K9me3 heterochromatin and increased nuclear diffusion. | Constricted cells exhibit significantly higher liver metastasis after intracardiac injection compared with unconstricted controls. | RUNX1 is required for emergence of intra‑tumor heterogeneity; RUNX1‑deficient mice retain a melanocytic tumor phenotype. | CNDP1 is up‑regulated in brain metastases, and its silencing reduces brain colonisation, mitochondrial function, and oxygen consumption, while overexpression enhances brain metastasis. | Mechanical stress induces chromatin hub re‑wiring via CTCF relocation, promoting expression of mesenchymal genes.

talk_title: Accelerating drug development in melanoma: neoadjuvant platforms and the third‑space hypothesis
topic_categories: immunotherapy; clinical_trial; combination_therapy; biomarker_development; early_detection; translational
moa_summary: Long outlined a biomarker‑driven neoadjuvant framework that uses early pathological response to stratify patients into responders (first/second space) and non‑responders (third space), enabling rapid go/no‑go decisions for novel agents; she introduced NeoPredict and NeoIreni trials that integrate IFN‑γ, PD‑L1, and TMB to predict resistance and guide combination immunotherapy or targeted approaches.
key_findings: 10‑year melanoma‑specific survival now exceeds 50% in advanced disease. | Neoadjuvant checkpoint blockade yields ~60% major pathological response, translating to near‑100% overall survival in responders. | Biomarker triad (high IFN‑γ, PD‑L1, TMB) predicts 100% 3‑year event‑free survival. | Multiple phase‑3 adjuvant trials (CheckMate 915, RELATIMAB, vibostalimab) failed to improve outcomes, highlighting the need for better patient selection. | NeoPredict test can identify third‑space patients before therapy, allowing early enrollment in experimental neoadjuvant trials.

talk_title: IL‑13Rα2 CAR‑T therapy in melanoma and neuroendocrine cancers
moa_summary: The speaker reported a phase‑I study of an IL‑13Rα2‑directed CAR‑T cell therapy, showing rapid in‑vivo expansion after lymphodepletion and occasional tumor regressions in IL13RA2‑expressing melanoma lesions, while highlighting heterogeneous responses due to variable antigen expression and tumor heterogeneity.
key_findings: IL13RA2 is expressed homogeneously in 10‑15% of melanomas and highly in neuroendocrine cancers. | CAR‑T cells achieve brisk peripheral expansion after low‑dose lymphodepletion. | A lung metastasis showed radiographic response, whereas a concurrent liver lesion progressed, underscoring intrapatient heterogeneity. | The trial has enrolled 19 patients with ongoing safety and efficacy assessment.
primary_value: robust expansion within first days post‑infusion

talk_title: Ultrasensitive ctDNA MRD detection, lymphatic fluid sampling, and liquid spatial ecotypes for immunotherapy response prediction
affiliation: Mayo Clinic, Department of Radiation Oncology
tumor_types: head and neck cancer; melanoma; non-small cell lung cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; liquid_biopsy; spatial_biology; biomarker_development; translational
moa_summary: Multi-mutation tracking and tumor-informed sequencing enable detection of ctDNA at parts-per-million levels; sampling lymphatic fluid from surgical drains enriches ctDNA, improving MRD detection and prognostication; cfDNA methylation patterns inferred from spatial transcriptomics define liquid spatial ecotypes that predict immunotherapy response or resistance.
key_findings: Multi-mutation tracking lowers MRD detection limit to ~1 part per million | Lymphatic fluid ctDNA detection at 24h post-surgery predicts worse PFS and outperforms pathology features | Liquid spatial ecotype 7 inferred from cfDNA methylation predicts immunotherapy response and improved survival across melanoma and NSCLC | Spatial ecotype 4 predicts resistance and worse survival, outperforming PD-L1 and TMB

talk_title: Ultra-sensitive tumor-informed whole genome ctDNA MRD assays and adaptive clinical trial designs
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Oncology
tumor_types: gastroesophageal cancer; mesothelioma; non-small cell lung cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Tumor-informed whole-genome sequencing tracks thousands of SNVs to achieve ultra-sensitive MRD detection; integrating machine learning reduces error rates, enabling ctDNA monitoring to guide adaptive trial designs and therapeutic escalation in early-stage cancers.
key_findings: Whole-genome tumor-informed ctDNA assay detects MRD at parts-per-million sensitivity across multiple cancer types | ctDNA persistence pre-surgery predicts inferior outcomes in low-TMB mesothelioma | Adaptive trial designs (e.g., TICTACTOE) will assess ctDNA MRD concordance with recurrence-free survival in early-stage NSCLC | ctDNA molecular response at 6-9 weeks predicts long-term outcomes in metastatic NSCLC treated with pembrolizumab

talk_title: Improving ctDNA assay sensitivity via increased cfDNA sampling and diagnostic priming agents
affiliation: Broad Institute, Gerstner Center for Cancer Diagnostics
moa_summary: MAESTRO enrichment uses allele-specific oligos to boost low-frequency mutations, enabling detection below 1 ppm; diagnostic priming agents (nanoparticles or Fc-silenced antibodies) transiently inhibit cfDNA clearance, increasing ctDNA yield for downstream assays.
key_findings: MAESTRO converts low VAF mutations to high VAF, achieving detection down to 0.25 ppm with linearity

talk_title: Co-evolution of tumor and stromal microenvironment in bone metastasis
tumor_types: breast cancer; prostate cancer; melanoma; thyroid cancer
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; metastasis; bone_metastasis; immunotherapy; preclinical; translational
moa_summary: Tumor cells exploit pre-existing bone niches via CXCL12/CXCR4 chemotaxis and E-selectin adhesion, inducing a non-canonical MET and Wnt activation for early seeding; later, tumor‑derived TGF‑β and JAG1 create a feedback loop with osteoclasts and osteoblasts, driving osteolysis and chemotherapy resistance, while DACH1‑mediated condensates suppress Wnt to switch signaling states.
key_findings: E-selectin engagement induces non‑canonical MET and Wnt signaling, promoting early bone seeding. | Tumor‑derived JAG1 activates osteoclast maturation and osteoblast cytokine production, establishing a TGF‑β/JAG1 positive feedback loop. | Neutralizing antibody 15d11 targeting JAG1 combined with chemotherapy reduces bone metastasis development by ~100% in mouse models. | DACH1 forms biomolecular condensates that suppress Wnt signaling, mediating the transition from early seeding to osteolytic growth. | IMAC (VK1⁺/CD163⁺/CCR3⁺) macrophages are relocalized to bone metastatic niches, supporting tumor growth and causing anemia by disrupting erythropoiesis islands.

talk_title: Targeting and detecting the pre‑metastatic niche in brain metastasis
topic_categories: preclinical; pre_metastatic_niche; brain_metastasis; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Tumor‑derived extracellular vesicles bearing CEACAM1/MIP direct cancer cells to the brain, where they disrupt the BBB via miR‑181c or VEGF‑A, induce astrocyte STAT3‑driven CDK5 upregulation (MLIN1 mediated) to suppress MHC‑I, and create a metabolic niche rich in GABA and glutamate; targeting VCAM‑1 with radiolabeled antibodies enables immunoPET detection and alpha‑particle therapy, while REG inhibition remodels the immune microenvironment, reducing M2 macrophages and MDSCs and enhancing CD8 T‑cell activity.
key_findings: CEACAM1/MIP on tumor EVs serves as a zip code directing metastasis to the brain. | miR‑181c and VEGF‑A from tumor EVs disrupt BBB tight junctions, facilitating tumor cell entry. | Astrocyte‑derived MLIN1 upregulates CDK5, leading to MHC‑I downregulation and immune evasion; rosuvastatin restores antigen presentation. | Neuronal GABA and glutamate fuel brain metastatic tumor cells, promoting proliferation. | Radiolabeled anti‑VCAM‑1 immunoPET detects pre‑metastatic niches before MRI-visible lesions. | REG inhibitor combined with radiation shows 70% partial and 24% complete responses in a Phase 1/2 brain metastasis trial.
primary_population: brain metastasis patients in ADORATION trial

talk_title: Telatrexib in TKI‑naive ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC: updated TRUST‑1 and TRUST‑2 data
affiliation: UC San Diego Moores Cancer Center, San Diego
moa_summary: Telatrexib is a CNS‑penetrant, selective ROS1 tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor that achieves high ORR and durable responses in ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC by potently inhibiting ROS1 while sparing TRKB, resulting in low neurologic toxicity.
key_findings: Pooled ORR in TKI‑naive ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC was 89.8% (n=157). | Intracranial ORR reached 76.5% in patients with brain metastases. | Median progression‑free survival was 46.1 months with 48.9% progression‑free at 4 years. | Median duration of response was 49.7 months; 53.4% of responders remained on therapy at 4 years. | Grade ≥3 AST/ALT elevations were low (8.3% and 11% respectively) and neurologic adverse events were rare.

talk_title: SHR‑A‑1811 ± atezolizumab as first‑line therapy for HER2‑mutant NSCLC: randomized phase II cohort
affiliation: Shanghai Lung Cancer Center, Shanghai Chest Hospital, Shanghai
moa_summary: SHR‑A‑1811 is a HER2‑directed ADC delivering a topoisomerase I payload; when combined with atezolizumab, it may enhance immunogenic cell death and improve durability of disease control in HER2‑mutant NSCLC.
key_findings: SHR‑A‑1811 monotherapy achieved ORR 81.3% (n=32) in HER2‑mutant NSCLC. | Adding atezolizumab increased 12‑month PFS to 79.2% versus 62.3% with monotherapy. | Grade ≥3 treatment‑related AEs were higher in the combination (77.8%) than monotherapy (40.6%). | Responses were consistent across subgroups (age, sex, brain mets, PD‑L1 status). | Dose of 4.8 mg/kg was selected as RP2D based on phase 1 dose‑exploration.

talk_title: HS10504, a fourth‑generation EGFR TKI targeting C797S, phase I first‑in‑human results
affiliation: First Affiliated Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University
moa_summary: HS10504 is a fourth‑generation EGFR inhibitor designed to bind both the sensitizing EGFR mutations and the C797S resistance mutation, retaining activity irrespective of T790M status, leading to rapid ctDNA clearance and tumor shrinkage.
key_findings: ORR at 400 mg was 52.9% versus 31.8% at 300 mg. | Median progression‑free survival reached 9.6 months in the 400 mg cohort. | Rapid ctDNA allele‑frequency reductions observed after one treatment cycle. | Grade ≥3 AEs occurred in 65.7% of 400 mg patients, most commonly lymphopenia and anemia. | One discontinuation due to decreased appetite; no treatment‑related deaths.

talk_title: Elirasib (D3S001), a next‑generation GDP‑bound KRASG12C inhibitor alone or with cetuximab in refractory CRC and PDAC: phase 1/2 results
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston
moa_summary: Elirasib binds the GDP‑bound KRASG12C pocket with high affinity, outcompeting nucleotide exchange and depleting active KRAS, while cetuximab blocks EGFR‑mediated feedback, together producing higher response rates in KRASG12C colorectal cancer.
key_findings: Monotherapy ORR in KRASG12C CRC was 40.5%; combination with cetuximab increased ORR to 62.1%. | In KRASG12C PDAC, monotherapy ORR reached 69.6% with median PFS 13.5 months. | Grade ≥3 TRAEs were low in monotherapy (15% CRC, 8.7% PDAC) but higher in combo (48.4%). | Rapid and deep ctDNA KRASG12C allele‑frequency reductions observed across cohorts. | Acquired resistance in combo arm frequently involved MET amplification, BRAF V600E, and MAP2K1 mutations.

talk_title: Namifotenib + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in first‑line metastatic pancreatic cancer: ACCENT trial
moa_summary: Namifotenib selectively inhibits focal adhesion kinase, reducing tumor‑cell survival signals and stromal fibrosis, thereby enhancing the efficacy of gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in metastatic pancreatic cancer.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 36% (vs 23% historical) with namifotenib + gem‑nab. | Five complete responses observed (8%). | Median PFS improved to 7.7 months compared with 5.5 months for gem‑nab alone. | Median OS reached 11.1 months, comparable to FOLFIRINOX historical data. | Safety profile comparable to standard gem‑nab; grade ≥3 neutropenia 39%.

talk_title: Fractionated dose‑dense actinium‑225 J591 anti‑PSMA therapy in metastatic prostate cancer: phase 1 study
moa_summary: Actinium‑225 labeled anti‑PSMA antibody J591 delivers high‑LET alpha particles directly to PSMA‑expressing prostate cancer cells, causing potent DNA damage while sparing surrounding tissue due to the short range of alpha emissions.
key_findings: Recommended phase 2 dose established at 60 kBq/kg for fractionated regimen. | ≈70% of patients achieved ≥50% PSA decline; 20% had partial radiographic responses. | Median overall survival was ~12 months in a heavily pre‑treated cohort. | Dose‑dense fractionated schedule showed acceptable hematologic toxicity (mainly thrombocytopenia). | Multiple‑cycle regimen was not recommended due to higher DLT rate.

talk_title: A Primer for Geospatial Science Analytics in Cancer Research and Translation
affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, University of Washington
moa_summary: Geospatial science links residential locations to environmental exposure datasets (e.g., air pollution, green space, socioeconomic indices) enabling epidemiologic analyses of cancer incidence, mortality, and disparities; translational tools such as the GeoExMap web app and EHR-linked outreach convert these insights into community‑focused interventions.
key_findings: Nearly 3,000 cancer‑related geospatial publications were indexed in 2025, demonstrating rapid growth of the field. | Geospatial analyses identified cancer mortality hotspots linked to lower education and mammography screening rates. | Neighborhood deprivation index was positively associated with breast cancer‑specific mortality among non‑Hispanic White women but not Black women. | Higher county‑level trauma measures correlated with increased all‑cancer mortality and lower cancer‑prevention behaviors. | Neighborhood socioeconomic status explained most variation in lung cancer mortality, whereas genetics dominated breast and prostate cancer mortality. | County‑level wildfire‑related PM2.5 exposure was associated with ~490 additional cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase. | The GeoExMap open‑source web app provides interactive mapping of >175 health and environmental variables with community‑tailored mitigation tips.
primary_population: Washington state patients at high risk for air pollution-related health effects

talk_title: Environmental Exposures Impact on Cancer Risk and Survival
affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Brigham and Women's Hospital
tumor_types: lung cancer; breast cancer; colorectal cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) and traffic‑related nitrogen dioxide (NO2) generate oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, DNA adducts and epigenetic alterations that increase cancer initiation and progression; wildfire‑derived PM2.5 appears more toxic, amplifying mortality risk in exposed cancer patients.
key_findings: A 10 µg/m³ increase in PM2.5 is associated with a 13% higher lung cancer incidence across global cohorts. | Wildfire‑specific PM2.5 exposure adds ~490 cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase, indicating higher toxicity than background PM2.5. | NO2 exposure shows a suggestive elevated risk for premenopausal breast cancer, especially among Black and non‑Hispanic White women. | Recent studies are shifting from total PM2.5 to source‑specific components (e.g., metal content, wildfire smoke) to pinpoint carcinogenic drivers. | Survival analyses using SEER‑Medicare data reveal that patients in high‑wildfire PM2.5 regions experience worse cancer‑specific mortality.

talk_title: Impact of Structural and Social Drivers of Health on Cancer Risk and Survival
tumor_types: prostate cancer; breast cancer; lung cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Structural racism manifested as redlining and multi‑domain segregation creates concentrated socioeconomic deprivation, leading to higher exposure to adverse environmental factors and limited healthcare access, which together increase tumor aggressiveness and mortality in affected populations.
key_findings: Black men with prostate cancer residing in the highest redlining neighborhoods have nearly double the hazard of prostate cancer mortality compared with White men in non‑redlined areas. | Across multiple states (e.g., California, Georgia, Louisiana, Detroit, New Jersey, Texas, Florida), a dose‑response relationship between redlining intensity and prostate cancer mortality was observed. | A composite county‑level structural racism index (housing, education, employment, income, health) was associated with higher odds of treatment delay among Black breast cancer patients, but not among White patients. | Multi‑domain structural racism measures reveal heterogeneous impacts across cancer types, underscoring the need for race‑specific composite indices. | Community advisory boards guided the selection of geospatial variables, ensuring relevance to lived experiences and facilitating translation of findings.
primary_value: up to ~2.0 in highest redlining quintile
primary_n: n=82,000 Black men; n=314,000 White men
primary_population: African American and non‑Hispanic White men with prostate cancer

talk_title: BIGHAT-299 first-in-class CCAM6-directed TCE for solid malignancies
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; lung adenocarcinoma; pancreatic cancer; gastric cancer
moa_summary: BIGHAT-299 is a conditionally active bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a low‑affinity VHH to CCAM6 on tumor cells and a pH‑conditional CD3 arm, achieving selective cytotoxicity against high‑density CCAM6 tumor cells while sparing neutrophils, leading to potent tumor regression in preclinical models.
key_findings: BIGHAT-299 exhibits a 1,000‑fold IC50 differential between CCAM6‑high tumor cells and neutrophils (20‑100 nM vs 1‑10 pM). | In CCAM6‑humanized mice, weekly dosing up to 2 mg/kg showed no neutrophil depletion or anemia, unlike high‑affinity controls. | Complete tumor regressions were observed in multiple CCAM6‑positive PDX models at 50 µg/kg weekly. | Pharmacokinetic studies in cynomolgus monkeys demonstrated multi‑day half‑life and sub‑Q bioavailability >120 mg/mL.

talk_title: ENA-101 first-in-class bispecific T‑cell engager targeting DART‑fox peptide‑HLA A3
tumor_types: lung squamous; triple‑negative breast cancer; head and neck; pan‑cancer (solid)
moa_summary: ENA‑101 is a bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a high‑affinity antibody specific for the DART‑fox A3 peptide presented by HLA‑A3 and an intermediate‑affinity anti‑CD3 scFv, delivering potent, peptide‑specific T‑cell activation and tumor cell killing while sparing normal HLA‑A3 positive tissues.
key_findings: ENA‑101 binds DART‑fox A3/HLA‑A3 with double‑digit picomolar affinity and shows no off‑target binding to HLA‑A3 alone. | In vitro cytotoxicity reaches sub‑10 pM EC50 against DART‑fox positive lines, with no activity against knockout controls. | No cytokine release syndrome signals observed in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations. | In NSG xenograft models, weekly dosing (50 µg/kg‑1 mg/kg) achieved complete tumor regressions without detectable toxicity.

talk_title: NRP1‑targeted peptide‑linked antibodies redirect viral memory T cells
tumor_types: triple‑negative breast cancer; non‑small cell lung carcinoma; renal cell carcinoma
moa_summary: A NRP1‑binding antibody (5D6) chemically or recombinantly linked to viral peptides (e.g., CMV NLV) is internalized, processed, and cross‑presented on HLA‑A2, enabling circulating virus‑specific T cells to recognize and kill NRP1‑positive tumor cells while sparing normal NRP1‑expressing tissues.
key_findings: 5D6‑NLV delivers >200 copies of CMV peptide per tumor cell, sufficient for TCR‑mediated killing. | In vitro, peptide‑linked 5D6 induces dose‑dependent IFN‑γ and TNF‑α release from CMV‑specific T cells. | In NSG xenograft models, a single dose of peptide‑linked 5D6 plus 5 × 10⁶ TCR‑T cells eradicated tumors without off‑target toxicity. | No cytokine release or cytotoxicity observed against NRP1‑positive endothelial or hematopoietic cells.

talk_title: PrimeBody platform enables tumor‑activated CD47 targeting with superior therapeutic index
tumor_types: gastric cancer; metastatic colorectal cancer; bladder cancer; breast cancer; non‑small cell lung cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: VORA101 is a high‑affinity SIRPα‑based CD47 blocker fused to an enhanced Fc and masked with a protease‑cleavable linker; tumor‑specific proteases remove the mask, restoring CD47 blockade and Fc‑mediated ADCP/ADCC while sparing normal tissues, thereby eliminating anemia and improving exposure.
key_findings: Masked VORA101 shows >500‑fold tumor‑selective activation in ex vivo human tumor tissue versus serum. | In CD47‑humanized mice, VORA101 prevented anemia at doses where unmasked migrolimab caused severe anemia. | VORA101 achieved 2‑fold higher serum concentrations than migrolimab due to reduced tissue sink. | In Raji xenografts, masked VORA101 induced complete tumor regressions, outperforming migrolimab.

talk_title: Integrated CD2 costimulation in trispecific T‑cell engagers improves T‑cell fitness
moa_summary: Evolve‑104 is a trispecific engager that combines anti‑CD3, tumor antigen binding, and a CD2 agonist (via CD58 engagement), delivering signal‑1 and signal‑2 simultaneously to T cells, resulting in enhanced proliferation, reduced exhaustion, and superior antitumor activity.
key_findings: CD2‑integrated Evolve‑104 induced 2‑fold higher CD8 proliferation and Ki67 positivity at day 14 versus conventional bispecifics. | Affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104 achieved ~60% tumor cell killing at 1:10 E:T ratio, outperforming bispecifics. | In NCI‑H292 xenografts, Evolve‑104 produced ~60% tumor growth inhibition, whereas bispecifics achieved ~40%. | Ex vivo TIL assays showed the greatest up‑regulation of CD25 and CD137 with affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104.

talk_title: Preclinical evaluation and safety of CVX250, a bispecific T‑cell engager targeting Cathepsin G peptide‑HLA in AML
affiliation: Crossbow Therapeutics / MD Anderson Cancer Center
moa_summary: CVX250 is a TCR‑mimetic bispecific that binds the CG1 peptide presented by HLA‑A2 on AML cells and CD3 on T cells, mediating picomolar‑potent cytotoxicity and bystander killing of CG1‑negative AML cells via cytokine‑mediated up‑regulation of death receptors.
key_findings: CVX250 kills AML cell lines with EC50 values in the single‑digit picomolar range, even at 33 CG1 copies per cell. | Bystander killing observed when target‑positive cells were mixed with target‑negative cells, indicating cytokine‑mediated effects. | No significant cytokine release or platelet activation detected in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations. | PK in cynomolgus monkeys showed IgG‑like half‑life (7‑14 days) with minimal immunogenicity.

talk_title: First‑in‑class monoclonal antibody targeting TM4SF5 for gastrointestinal cancers
tumor_types: hepatocellular carcinoma; colorectal cancer; esophageal cancer
moa_summary: RCT1213 binds a conformational epitope spanning both extracellular loops of TM4SF5, disrupting its interaction with IL‑6R/STAT3 and SLAMF7, thereby reducing immunosuppressive cytokine signaling, restoring NK‑cell recognition, and reshaping the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation.
key_findings: RCT1213 binds EC1/EC2 loops with sub‑nanomolar affinity and blocks TM4SF5‑IL‑6R interaction, reducing STAT3 phosphorylation. | In HCC mouse models, RCT1213 achieved ~80% tumor growth inhibition, comparable to atezolizumab + bevacizumab. | Combination of RCT1213 with atezolizumab and bevacizumab yielded complete responses in all tested mice. | In CRC PDX, dose‑dependent tumor suppression observed; responders showed increased CD8⁺ T cells and decreased Tregs.

talk_title: Targeting Dormant Residual Tumor Cells with Autophagy and mTOR Inhibition (CLEVER Trial)
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Abramson Cancer Center
moa_summary: In mouse models, transient inhibition of autophagy (chloroquine) or mTOR signaling (everolimus) selectively depletes dormant residual tumor cells, leading to dose‑dependent improvements in recurrence‑free survival; the parallel phase II CLEVER trial demonstrated feasibility, safety, and significant DTC burden reduction with similar agents, suggesting that targeting dormancy‑specific dependencies can prevent breast cancer recurrence.
key_findings: Gamma‑secretase inhibition of Notch during dormancy reduced recurrence in 50% of mice. | Genetic or pharmacologic autophagy inhibition (ATG5/7 knockdown or chloroquine) decreased residual tumor cell numbers and improved recurrence‑free survival in mice. | mTOR inhibition with everolimus, even transiently (3‑9 weeks), markedly prolonged recurrence‑free survival in mice. | In the CLEVER phase II trial (n=51), hydroxychloroquine, everolimus, or their combination reduced bone‑marrow DTC burden by ~80% versus observation. | Five‑year recurrence‑free survival across all CLEVER arms was ~96%, exceeding expectations for this high‑risk cohort.
primary_population: high-risk breast cancer patients with DTCs

talk_title: Residual Breast Cancer Cells Co‑opt SOX5‑Driven Endochondral Ossification to Maintain Dormancy
moa_summary: CRISPR‑Cas9 screening of dormant residual tumor cells identified SOX5 as a suppressor of proliferation; loss of SOX5 accelerates recurrence by increasing proliferative outgrowth of dormant cells and impairs a dormancy‑associated endochondral ossification program marked by proteoglycan accumulation and 18F‑NaF PET/CT signal, linking developmental ossification pathways to tumor cell quiescence.
key_findings: SOX5 loss (via two independent guide RNAs) accelerated tumor recurrence in orthotopic and experimental lung metastasis models (hazard ratio ~2.5‑3). | SOX5 knockout increased Ki‑67 positivity specifically in late residual lesions, indicating loss of dormancy‑enforced quiescence. | Dormant residual lesions displayed endochondral ossification signatures (proteoglycan and calcification staining) and progressive 18F‑NaF PET/CT uptake. | SOX5 knockdown markedly reduced calcification and PET tracer uptake in residual lesions, linking SOX5 to the ossification program. | Enrichment of chondrogenic/osteogenic gene signatures in post‑neoadjuvant patient samples correlated with delayed time to recurrence.

talk_title: Molecular Characterization of Human Disseminated Tumor Cells Reveals Embryonic‑Like Stemness and Early Dissemination
tumor_types: breast cancer; prostate cancer; lung cancer; esophageal cancer
moa_summary: Single‑cell genomic and transcriptomic analyses of bone‑marrow disseminated tumor cells (DTCs) from early‑stage (M0) and metastatic (M1) patients reveal that M0 DTCs lack late‑stage driver mutations, exhibit high stemness and embryonic‑like gene expression (similar to zygote/early‑embryo signatures), and their stemness scores strongly predict poor survival, indicating that early dissemination involves a primitive, pluripotent‑like state distinct from overt metastases.
key_findings: Dissemination of tumor cells occurs early, often before a detectable primary tumor, in both mouse models and patients. | M0 DTCs display embryonic stem‑cell–like transcriptional programs and highest stemness scores, exceeding those of adult erythroid progenitors. | High stemness scores in EpCAM+ DTCs correlate with markedly reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients. | M0 DTCs cluster transcriptionally with human embryonic stem cells, whereas M1 DTCs align with differentiated epithelial/mesenchymal signatures. | Phospho‑protein profiling of DTCs from a HER2‑positive patient after neoadjuvant therapy showed persistent activation of HER2, AKT, ERK, and STAT3 pathways, indicating therapy‑resistant signaling.

talk_title: Pan-cancer analysis of rare RAS alleles using AACR Project Genie
moa_summary: Analysis of >270k tumors from Project Genie revealed that non‑G12C KRAS alleles dominate (≈60% of KRAS mutations) and display lineage‑specific distribution, while HRAS and NRAS show distinct tissue enrichment and co‑mutation architectures, indicating isoform‑specific biological contexts that can inform lineage‑tailored RAS‑targeted strategies.
key_findings: Non‑G12C KRAS alleles comprise ~60% of KRAS mutations across 42,000 KRAS‑altered tumors, outnumbering G12C (≈16%). | KRAS G12D/V are broadly distributed in pancreatic, colorectal, and lung adenocarcinomas; G12C/A enriched in lung adenocarcinoma; G12R restricted to pancreas; Q61 spread across multiple lineages. | KRAS co‑mutations show tissue‑specific patterns (e.g., SMAD4, APC in colon) and mutual exclusivity with NF1, PTEN, RB1, EGFR, MET, BRAF. | HRAS mutations (<1% of tumors) are enriched in bladder, urothelial, lung, melanoma, thyroid, colorectal, and head‑and‑neck cancers, with codon 61 predominance and multi‑pathway co‑activations (PI3K, RTK, NOTCH1, CDK4/6). | NRAS mutations are enriched in melanoma, myeloid, colorectal, lung, and thyroid cancers; Q61 accounts for ~56% of NRAS mutations and display both pathway redundancy and lineage‑specific co‑mutations.

talk_title: Neomorphic p53 alleles drive organ‑specific metastatic programs
affiliation: Columbia University Medical Center, Irving Scholar
moa_summary: Mutant TP53 R172H occupies novel genomic loci in metastatic lesions, directly up‑regulating CSF1 via promoter and enhancer binding, which promotes macrophage‑mediated metastasis; organ‑specific mutant TP53 programs (e.g., EMT/glycolysis in lung, Hedgehog/STAT3 in liver) drive distinct metastatic phenotypes and correlate with survival differences across mutation classes.
key_findings: Mutant TP53 R172H binds novel promoter/enhancer regions at the CSF1 locus in metastatic tumors, increasing H3K27ac without changing TP53 protein levels. | CRISPR‑mediated CSF1 deletion in TP53‑mutant cells markedly reduces metastatic burden, an effect not seen in TP53‑null cells. | Organ‑specific metastatic signatures: lung metastases enriched for EMT and glycolysis; liver metastases for Hedgehog and IL6/STAT3 pathways. | In AACR GENIE SCC cohort, missense TP53 mutations associate with lung metastasis, while splice/nonsense variants show different patterns; TP53‑mutant patients have worse OS overall, but lung‑metastatic TP53‑mutants have better prognosis than liver‑metastatic ones. | Hotspot TP53 variants display distinct survival outcomes (e.g., R175 better OS than R282).
primary_value: HR 0.68 for missense vs. conformational TP53 mutations

talk_title: KMT2C mutation predicts platinum sensitivity across solid tumors
affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer; head and neck cancer; bladder cancer; cervical cancer; melanoma; gastric cancer; lung cancer; breast cancer; GU cancers
moa_summary: Loss‑of‑function KMT2C impairs homologous recombination repair, creating a functional HRD phenotype that sensitizes tumors to DNA cross‑linking platinum agents, as demonstrated by superior OS and PFS in KMT2C‑mutant patients, especially in colorectal cancer.
key_findings: KMT2C loss‑of‑function occurs in ~5‑6% of solid tumors and >10% in bladder, cervical, melanoma, endometrial cancers. | Across 19,000 platinum‑treated patients, KMT2C mutants showed a 2.9‑month OS advantage (19.6 mo vs 16.7 mo). | In colorectal cancer, KMT2C mutation doubled median OS (51 mo vs 25.3 mo) and improved PFS. | Benefit was specific to platinum agents; no OS advantage observed with irinotecan‑based therapy. | CRISPR KMT2C knockout increased oxaliplatin sensitivity in colorectal, head‑and‑neck, and endometrial cell lines; xenograft models confirmed enhanced tumor regression.
primary_population: all solid tumors, KMT2C mutant vs WT

talk_title: Rare class‑II BRAF alterations and fusions in pancreatic cancer
affiliation: Institution not specified (presenter of BRAF study)
moa_summary: Pancreatic cancers harbor a distinctive spectrum of class‑II BRAF alterations (predominantly N486K/P190 and fusions) that signal as dimers and are resistant to monomeric BRAF inhibitors, suggesting a need for dimer‑targeted or MEK‑based therapeutic strategies.
key_findings: Class‑II BRAF alterations constitute the majority of BRAF events in pancreatic cancer, unlike other tumors where class‑I V600E dominates. | N486K/P190 alterations account for 82% of class‑II events in pancreatic cancer (GECAN) and 36% in GENIE. | Class‑II BRAF alterations trend toward poorer overall survival compared with class‑I, though not statistically significant. | Class‑II alterations are rare in other solid tumors, highlighting a pancreas‑specific genomic pattern.
primary_value: trend toward worse OS for class‑II vs class‑I (not statistically significant)
primary_population: pancreatic cancer patients with BRAF alterations

talk_title: Ultima Genomics duplex whole‑genome cfDNA for clonal evolution tracking
moa_summary: High‑depth duplex whole‑genome sequencing (Ultima PPMseq) of cfDNA provides orders‑of‑magnitude more mutations than targeted panels, enabling precise reconstruction of clonal phylogenies, detection of low‑frequency driver events (e.g., TP53), and assignment of mutational processes, thereby improving longitudinal monitoring of tumor evolution.
key_findings: PPMseq cfDNA generated high‑confidence phylogenetic trees matching tissue‑derived trees, with greater clone resolution than whole‑exome cfDNA. | Whole‑genome cfDNA identified low‑frequency TP53 mutations and assigned them to specific subclones, which were missed by exome sequencing. | In a breast cancer patient on PI3K and CDK4/6 inhibitors, distinct subclones harboring different driver mutations were tracked over time, revealing dynamic clonal shifts. | Mutational signature analysis of cfDNA trees uncovered dominant APOBEC activity in specific branches. | Comparative analysis showed higher concordance between cfDNA whole‑genome trees and combined tissue+cfDNA exome trees than cfDNA exome alone.

talk_title: AI‑integrated gut microbiome analysis in early vs. late onset colorectal cancer
affiliation: City of Hope, Velasquez‑Villareal Lab, PhD student
moa_summary: Integrating AI agents with multi‑omic (WES, RNA‑seq) and social determinants data reveals that early‑onset colorectal cancer in Hispanic‑Latino patients exhibits reduced gut microbial diversity and specific enrichment of Bacteroides and Veillonella, linking microbiome shifts to clinical and socioeconomic factors such as BMI.
key_findings: Early‑onset CRC patients show significantly lower gut microbial alpha diversity compared with late‑onset patients. | Bacteroides and Veillonella are enriched in early‑onset cases; Segotella is enriched in late‑onset cases. | Veillonella abundance correlates with lower BMI, while Faecalibacterium is higher in obese individuals. | AI agents can query integrated datasets to rapidly assess relationships (e.g., Fusobacterium vs. onset) and generate hypothesis‑driven analyses.

session_title: Advancing Early Phase Pediatric Clinical Trials to Turn Promising Agents into Actionable Evidence
talk_title: Menin inhibitor revumenib development and pediatric integration
affiliation: Syndax Pharmaceuticals, Vice President of the Menin Program
tumor_types: acute lymphoblastic leukemia; acute myeloid leukemia
moa_summary: Revumenib is a high‑affinity protein‑protein interaction inhibitor that binds the scaffold protein menin, displacing the KMT2A‑menin transcription complex. This collapses the HOX/MEIS leukemic transcription program, leading to differentiation or apoptosis of KMT2A‑rearranged or NPM1‑mutant leukemia cells.
key_findings: Revumenib binds menin with high affinity and blocks KMT2A‑menin interaction, collapsing the HOX/MEIS transcription program in preclinical models. | In eight pediatric B‑ALL PDX models, seven showed significant survival benefit (2–12 months extension, some >12 months). | A single‑patient protocol enabled the first pediatric dose (1.5‑year‑old) in October 2019, before formal pediatric enrollment, providing early PK, safety, and efficacy data. | Exposure across the full pediatric weight range (5 kg–75 kg) was proportional to mg/kg dosing, supporting a unified dosing strategy. | Over 130 single‑patient protocols (25 infants, 78 children, 29 adolescents) were collected, demonstrating feasibility of rapid pediatric data acquisition. | Regulatory engagement (pre‑IND meeting, real‑time oncology review) allowed IND filing in mid‑2019, first adult dosing November 2019, and FDA approval by end‑2024 for KMT2A‑rearranged AML and 2025 for NPM1‑mutant AML.
primary_metric: patients enrolled in pediatric single‑patient protocols

talk_title: Pragmatic Lung S2302 Phase 3 Registrational Intent Trial
affiliation: Dartmouth Cancer Center, Interim Cancer Center Director and Associate Director for Clinical Research
moa_summary: A pragmatic, registrational Phase 3 trial compared ramucirumab plus pembrolizumab versus standard chemotherapy in previously treated NSCLC, using simplified eligibility and minimal data collection to assess overall survival and safety in routine practice.
key_findings: Pragmatic Lung enrolled 800 participants across >600 sites, exceeding accrual targets (40-60 patients/month). | Overall survival was not superior for ramucirumab/pembrolizumab versus standard chemotherapy. | Serious adverse events leading to discontinuation were lower in the chemotherapy-free arm. | Protocol development and activation completed in 200 days, half the typical timeline.

talk_title: Hybrid Decentralized Model for Early‑Phase Trial Recruitment
affiliation: Yale Cancer Center, Amy and Joseph Perella Professor of Medicine
moa_summary: Describes a hybrid decentralized approach that brings early‑phase trial procedures to community sites, using multilingual educational tools and patient navigators to increase enrollment of underrepresented populations.
key_findings: Underrepresented patient enrollment in early‑phase trials increased from 2% to ~22% after implementing hybrid decentralization. | Multilingual "Golden Book" myth‑busting tool was created in English and Spanish to improve trial literacy. | Community health workers and navigators facilitated trial participation despite transportation and language barriers.

talk_title: WIN Program: Training Diverse Clinical Trialists
affiliation: Virginia Commonwealth University Cancer Center (soon Fox Chase Cancer Center)
moa_summary: The WIN program provides a two‑year curriculum, protected time, and mentorship to underrepresented investigators, resulting in increased trial enrollment from minority and rural communities.
key_findings: Over 321 scholars trained; 98% remain engaged with community partners years after completion. | Scholars report >60% increase in understanding of trial design and community outreach. | Participating sites show higher minority accrual rates compared with national averages.

talk_title: Health Equity Leadership in Biotech: Embedding Diversity in Drug Development
moa_summary: Describes industry strategies to build affective and cognitive trust through repeated community presence, multilingual education, and partnership with patient advocates to improve trial diversity.
key_findings: Implementing community‑based education and screening programs increased awareness of clinical trials in target populations. | Balancing affective trust (relationship building) with cognitive trust (data sharing) was identified as critical for trial recruitment.

talk_title: Community Outreach and Rural Trial Accrual in Appalachia
affiliation: Ohio State University, Professor and Deputy Director for Population Sciences and Community Outreach
moa_summary: Highlights successful community‑based interventions, including mobile screening units and multilingual outreach, that achieved >20% minority accrual in Alliance cancer control and treatment trials across Appalachian regions.
key_findings: Consistently >20% racial/ethnic minority accrual to Alliance trials over three years in Appalachian sites. | Mobile mammography and lung‑van programs detected early‑stage cancers in remote communities lacking hospitals. | Using non‑clinical language (e.g., "lung screening" instead of "cancer") improved community receptivity.

talk_title: WIN Scholar Experience: Community‑Engaged Cervical Cancer Trial Design
affiliation: University of California San Diego, Assistant Professor and Radiation Oncologist
moa_summary: Describes how WIN training enabled design of a patient‑centered phase I cervical cancer trial with simplified logistics, multilingual materials, and community health worker support to improve enrollment of underserved patients.
key_findings: Trial design incorporated patient‑friendly consent and reduced visit burden. | Community health workers facilitated enrollment of patients from low‑income neighborhoods. | Scholars reported increased confidence in integrating community input into protocol development.

talk_title: Community‑Based Engagement for GU Cancer Patients in Rural California
affiliation: University of California San Diego, Genitourinary Medical Oncologist
tumor_types: prostate cancer; kidney cancer; bladder cancer
moa_summary: Outlines use of patient summits, multilingual outreach, and patient‑led storytelling to build trust and increase trial participation among rural GU cancer patients traveling long distances for care.
key_findings: Patient summits featuring trial participants increased willingness to enroll among rural GU cancer patients. | Travel distances up to 2.5 hours were mitigated through coordinated transportation and community partner support.

talk_title: Designing a Pragmatic Registry Trial for Metastatic Breast Cancer Across NY Registries
affiliation: Roswell Park Comprehensive Cancer Center, Breast Cancer Epidemiology
moa_summary: Proposes a pragmatic trial leveraging state cancer registries and patient advisory boards to recruit metastatic breast cancer patients, using remote consent, digital surveys, and community‑driven materials to ensure rural representation.
key_findings: Community advisory boards co‑designed patient‑facing materials and surveys. | Remote recruitment via registry data and REDCap enabled enrollment of patients from rural counties.

talk_title: Precision Approaches to HPV-Independent Head and Neck Cancer
affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Medical Oncology
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: Bertness highlighted that HPV‑negative HNSCC harbors EGFR overexpression and frequent loss of tumor suppressors (TP53, CDKN2A) leading to high TMB. She demonstrated that EGFR‑targeted therapies are limited by downstream resistance (PIK3CA, RAS, PTEN loss) and nuclear EGFR. Bispecific agents (EGFR/TGF‑β, EGFR/LGR5, EGFR/MET) improve response when combined with pembrolizumab. She also presented a synthetic‑lethal strategy where p53 loss creates dependence on Aurora A; combined Aurora A and WEE1 inhibition induces replication stress, mitotic catastrophe, and tumor regression in p53‑mutant models, including EGFR‑TKI‑resistant cells.
key_findings: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy improves HR to 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich HPV‑negative HNSCC (KEYNOTE‑048). | Ficaserafusp + pembrolizumab yields 54% response and median OS >20 months in HPV‑negative PD‑L1+ disease, now in phase III FORTIFY‑HN01. | Pitaseptumab + pembrolizumab achieves 66% ORR in HPV‑negative HNSCC, supporting two ongoing phase III trials. | Amivantamab monotherapy shows 45% response in refractory HPV‑negative HNSCC; combination with pembrolizumab reaches 56% response in first line. | Aurora A inhibition (VIC‑1911) blocks replication fork progression; combined with WEE1 inhibitor (davisertib) induces mitotic catastrophe and tumor regression in p53‑mutant HNSCC xenografts and PDXs. | Synergy of Aurora A + WEE1 inhibition is lost in CDC25‑mutant SCC61, indicating genotype‑specific resistance.

talk_title: HPV‑Associated Head and Neck Cancer Subtypes and Radiation Sensitivity
affiliation: University of North Carolina, Chair of Otolaryngology
topic_categories: immunotherapy; radiopharmaceuticals; combination_therapy; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; basic_science
moa_summary: Yarbrough defined two HPV‑positive HNSCC subtypes based on NF‑kappaB activation driven by TRAF3 or CYLD loss; low‑risk tumors exhibit high NF‑kappaB activity and intrinsic radiation sensitivity, while high‑risk tumors are radio‑resistant. Activation of NF‑kappaB via TLR5 agonist entolimod sensitizes high‑risk tumors to radiation, offering a therapeutic avenue to improve the therapeutic ratio.
key_findings: HPV+ HNSCC segregates into low‑risk (high NF‑kappaB, TRAF3/CYLD loss) and high‑risk subtypes with hazard ratios >3.5 for overall survival. | Low‑risk subtype shows markedly increased radiation sensitivity in vitro and in vivo. | NF‑kappaB activation via TLR5 agonist entolimod synergizes with radiation to cure high‑risk mouse tumors while protecting normal tissue. | Birinapant (SMAC mimetic) plus chemoradiation worsened survival, leading to abandonment of that strategy.
primary_metric: Hazard Ratio for survival (high vs low risk)
primary_population: HPV+ HNSCC patients across multiple cohorts

talk_title: Minimal Residual Disease and Treatment Optimization in Head and Neck Cancer
affiliation: University of Chicago, Associate Professor of Medical Oncology
tumor_types: HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma; HPV-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
topic_categories: liquid_biopsy; ctDNA; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; combination_therapy; immunotherapy; translational
moa_summary: Rosenberg presented ctDNA as a minimally invasive biomarker for MRD across HPV‑positive and HPV‑negative HNSCC. Early clearance of HPV ctDNA during neoadjuvant therapy predicts favorable PFS, while post‑treatment ctDNA positivity forecasts recurrence. Adaptive trial designs using ctDNA dynamics to de‑escalate radiation or chemotherapy, or to intensify immunotherapy, demonstrate improved response rates and survival, illustrating how real‑time molecular monitoring can guide treatment optimization.
key_findings: Baseline HPV ctDNA detected in 86% of patients; mid‑treatment clearance (>95%) identifies a cohort with superior PFS. | In the OPTIMA 2 neoadjuvant trial, ctDNA clearance after neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy correlated with improved PFS. | SYNERGY phase II trial using ctDNA‑guided adaptive chemo‑immunotherapy achieved 63% ORR, median OS 30.9 months, and allowed de‑escalation of chemotherapy in ~75% of patients. | Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts recurrence with 100% specificity in early studies, supporting its use for risk‑adapted radiation de‑escalation (DART trial). | Methylation‑based ctDNA assays provide MRD detection in HPV‑negative HNSCC, especially when serial sampling is employed.

talk_title: Agentic AI for Clinical Development in Oncology
affiliation: Brigham and Women's Hospital / Dana-Farber Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Developed an agentic AI system that ingests unstructured clinical notes, extracts immunotherapy-related adverse events, grades severity, attributes causality, and provides evidence-backed JSON output, achieving macro F1 0.93 and reducing curation time by 40% while maintaining accuracy and inter-annotator agreement.
key_findings: The agentic AI pipeline achieved a macro F1 of 0.93 for immunotherapy adverse event extraction from clinical notes. | Automated curation reduced manual review time from 10 minutes per note to approximately 0.2 minutes (two cents per note), a 40% time reduction. | Accuracy of adverse event determination was comparable to expert oncologists, with significantly improved inter-annotator agreement.

talk_title: Agentic AI in Clinical Tissue Medicine and Pathology
affiliation: University Medical Center Mainz, Institute of Pathology
moa_summary: Designed a modular agentic AI workflow for pathology that sequentially uses a foundation model to generate microscopic descriptions of whole-slide images, an LLM with retrieval-augmented generation to propose diagnoses and recommend immunohistochemical tests, and downstream agents to interpret IHC results and produce structured reports, all with on-premise deployment and reasoning traceability.
