key_findings: A prototype system successfully described a lung squamous cell carcinoma slide, identified p40 positivity and TTF-1 negativity, and generated a final diagnostic report. | The workflow operates on open-source models with low computational requirements and can run on-premise, preserving data privacy. | Incorporating reasoning traces provides auditability for clinical quality management.

session_title: Agentic AI as the Oncologist: Clinical Decision Support and Human AI Collaboration
talk_title: Agentic AI for Radiation Oncology: Imaging, Planning, and Operational Optimization
affiliation: Siemens Healthineers, Senior Vice President and Chief Expert for Healthcare AI
moa_summary: AI-driven imaging reconstruction (DeepResolve) accelerates MRI acquisition by 73% and has processed >44 M scans; AI auto‑contouring covers >200 organs at risk; end‑to‑end AI radiation‑therapy planning generates deliverable plans in under a second; an operational‑twin AI schedules patients, machines, and staff in real time to reduce treatment delays.
key_findings: DeepResolve AI reconstruction makes MRI scans up to 73% faster, enabling >44 M AI‑reconstructed scans in 2.5 years. | Regulatory‑approved auto‑contouring system identifies >200 organs at risk and clinical target volumes across disease sites. | AI‑driven radiation‑therapy planning produces a full treatment plan in <1 second on standard hardware for head‑and‑neck cases. | Operational‑twin AI scheduling integrates patient demand, machine capacity, and clinical priority to generate optimized appointment recommendations in one click.

session_title: Agentic AI as the Oncologist: Clinical Decision Support and Human AI Collaboration
talk_title: 4D Oncology Platform: Real‑Time ADC Evaluation and Agentic AI Treatment Selection
affiliation: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Founding Director, Center of Molecular Artificial Intelligence
tumor_types: gastric cancer; breast cancer; HER2‑positive cancer
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; ADC; targeted_therapy; biomarker_development; AI_ML; spatial_biology; platform_technology
moa_summary: Agentic AI integrates pathology, NCCN/ESMO guidelines, and real‑time organoid drug testing to prioritize FDA‑approved ADCs and targeted agents; live imaging of drug internalization, lysosomal cleavage, and payload release reveals functional resistance (e.g., lack of lysosome for Trop2‑ADC) and guides selection of HER2‑ADC or FGFR2‑targeted therapy within 30 days.
key_findings: Deep‑learning AI suggested FGFR2 IHC staining in addition to standard HER2, leading to identification of an off‑label FGFR2‑targetable lesion. | Organoid assay showed Trop2‑ADC failed to kill patient tumor due to absent lysosomal activity, despite target expression. | HER2‑ADC demonstrated robust tumor cell killing in organoid within 24 h, confirming efficacy before patient treatment. | The platform generated a ranked list of 56 FDA‑approved ADCs and predicted penetration, toxicity, and resistance mechanisms for each within 30 days. | Agentic AI integrated guideline‑based recommendations with experimental data to produce a treatment plan that was reviewed by the tumor board.

talk_title: Clonal hematopoiesis, TET2 deficiency and IL-1β inhibition in lung cancer
affiliation: Emory University Winship Cancer Institute, Department of Hematology
moa_summary: TET2-deficient clonal hematopoiesis skews the immune microenvironment toward immunosuppression, increasing myeloid and exhausted T cell populations, which promotes lung tumor development; IL-1β inhibition with canakinumab reduces cancer incidence in CH-negative mice but not in TET2-mutant contexts, highlighting a gene‑environment interaction.
key_findings: TET2-deficient hematopoiesis accelerates lung tumor growth in aged mice but not in young mice. | Aged TET2‑mutant mice show increased tumor‑infiltrating CD45+ cells, reduced NK cells, and exhausted PD‑1+ CD8 T cells. | Human NSCLC patients with TET2‑mutated CH display similar immune remodeling (increased monocytes, reduced NK cells). | In the REAL‑NADIM neoadjuvant trial, CH+ patients had significantly longer overall survival after chemoimmunotherapy despite similar pathologic response and PFS. | CH clones expand over disease course, especially in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
primary_value: significantly prolonged in CH+ patients after neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy

talk_title: Tumor‑infiltrating clonal hematopoiesis (TICH) in the TRACERx NSCLC cohort
affiliation: University of Cambridge, Department of Oncology
moa_summary: Clonal hematopoiesis clones, especially those with DNMT3A or TET2 mutations, can infiltrate NSCLC tumors (TICH), where they expand preferentially and remodel the immune microenvironment toward an inflammatory, interferon‑rich state, leading to poorer recurrence‑free survival, particularly in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
key_findings: 33% of TRACERx NSCLC patients harbor CH mutations; 43% of those infiltrate tumors (TICH). | TICH patients have significantly reduced disease‑free survival compared with CH‑negative patients. | CH clones expand over time in patients who do not receive adjuvant therapy. | TET2 and DNMT3A mutant clones achieve larger relative tumor‑infiltrating clone sizes than other CH mutations. | Single‑cell analyses reveal TET2‑mutant cells enriched in myeloid lineages and NK cells, while TICH tumors show increased γδ T cells, cycling T cells, and interferon response genes.

talk_title: TFF2 deficiency amplifies IL‑1β inflammation and drives aging‑associated gastric tumor progression
moa_summary: Aging reduces gastric TFF2‑expressing epithelial cells, leading to expansion of PMN‑MDSCs that secrete IL‑1β, which activates IL‑1R1‑positive CAFs and creates an immunosuppressive, tumor‑promoting niche; systemic administration of a stabilized TFF2‑MSA fusion reverses MDSC expansion, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and suppresses tumor growth in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mouse stomachs show a marked loss of TFF2⁺ epithelial cells. | Aging drives systemic inflammaging with increased PMN‑MDSCs, bone‑marrow myeloid bias, and elevated IL‑6/TNF‑α. | TFF2‑deficient mice recapitulate the aged immune phenotype (MDSC expansion, CD8⁺ T cell reduction). | TFF2‑MSA treatment reduces MDSC accumulation, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and normalizes bone‑marrow myelopoiesis. | In both ACKP orthotopic and MNU‑induced gastric cancer models, aged mice develop larger tumors and shorter survival, which are mitigated by TFF2‑MSA pretreatment.

talk_title: Aging‑mediated reactivation of metastatic melanoma via γδ T‑cell dysfunction
affiliation: University of California, Postdoctoral Fellow in Mitchison Lab
moa_summary: Aging creates a liver microenvironment that reduces functional γδ T cells, increases POSN1 expression in melanoma cells, and expands PMN‑MDSCs, collectively driving immunosuppressive γδ T‑cell phenotypes (PD‑1⁺, Vγ1⁺) and promoting metastatic outgrowth; depletion of γδ T cells further exacerbates metastasis, confirming their protective role in young hosts.
key_findings: Middle‑aged mice develop significantly more liver metastases than young or geriatric mice. | γδ T‑cell numbers drop specifically in middle‑aged livers, correlating with metastasis burden. | Depleting γδ T cells reduces primary tumor growth but increases liver metastasis, indicating site‑specific roles. | POSN1 overexpression in melanoma cells enhances both lung and liver metastasis and expands PMN‑MDSCs while decreasing γδ T‑cell infiltration. | Aged livers show increased arginase‑1 expression and MDSC accumulation, suggesting metabolic suppression of γδ T cells.

talk_title: Age‑stratified therapeutic strategies targeting chemo‑resistant subclones in triple‑negative breast cancer
affiliation: University of XYZ, Department of Oncology
moa_summary: A DNA‑barcode tracking system identifies a minor subpopulation (barcode 25) that survives paclitaxel, drives PD‑L1 up‑regulation, interferon‑γ signaling, EMT, and immunosuppression; pan‑HDAC inhibition selectively targets this clone, restoring chemosensitivity and enhancing anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy, especially in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mice respond less to paclitaxel and gain no additional benefit from anti‑PD‑L1 combination. | Only one barcode (25) expands after paclitaxel, conferring resistance in vitro and in vivo. | Barcode 25 tumors exhibit higher PD‑L1, CXCL10, vimentin, and EBI3 expression. | Pan‑HDAC inhibitor panobinostat is the top hit in a drug screen against barcode 25 cells. | Combining panobinostat with anti‑PD‑L1 markedly improves survival in aged mice, and triple therapy (paclitaxel + panobinostat + anti‑PD‑L1) achieves near‑complete control in young mice.

talk_title: Age‑dependent neuroimmune changes in glioblastoma reveal senescent microglia as a therapeutic target
moa_summary: Aging increases senescent microglia in the peritumoral brain, creating a pro‑inflammatory niche that limits the efficacy of radiation plus immunotherapy; senolytic treatment (dasatinib + quercetin) selectively clears these senescent microglia, enhancing survival in aged GBM models when combined with standard radiotherapy and immunotherapy.
key_findings: Patient glioblastoma cores show no age‑related transcriptional differences, but peritumoral tissue in older patients is enriched for senescent microglia. | Aged mice exhibit higher β‑galactosidase+ microglia in extracranial brain, not within the tumor core. | Senolytic TQ reduces senescent microglia in aged brains and improves survival when combined with radiation + anti‑PD‑1, but not in young mice. | In aged humanized GBM PDX mice, adding TQ to standard radiation + temozolomide further extends survival for a subset of animals.

talk_title: Multimodal and Agentic AI in Biomarker Discovery
affiliation: University of Dresden and University of Heidelberg, Germany
moa_summary: Kata described the evolution from classical machine learning to deep learning and foundation models for cancer biomarker development, defining three classes of AI‑based biomarkers (quantification, indirect inference, and outcome prediction) and introduced AI agents that can autonomously integrate multimodal clinical data to generate diagnostic and therapeutic recommendations.
key_findings: Class A AI biomarkers replicate human scoring (e.g., PD‑L1 IHC) with higher reproducibility. | Class B AI biomarkers infer genetic alterations (e.g., MSI, EGFR mutations) from pathology images for pre‑screening. | Class C AI biomarkers predict treatment outcomes directly from multimodal data, enabling new clinical decision tools.

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Stage Programs at Recursion
affiliation: Recursion Pharmaceuticals, VP of Translational Strategy
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; colorectal cancer; familial adenomatous polyposis
moa_summary: Recursion leveraged AI‑driven phenomaps to identify RBM39 as a functional analog of CDK12, enabling rapid degrader development (REC‑1245) that exploits splicing defects in MSI‑high, genomically unstable tumors; AI‑guided design produced a CDK7 inhibitor (REC‑617) optimized for ovarian cancer vulnerability; and AI‑derived repurposing identified a MEK1/2 inhibitor (REC‑4881) that reduces polyp burden in APC‑loss FAP patients.
key_findings: RBM39 degradation by REC‑1245 showed dose‑dependent activity in MSI‑high ovarian models and correlated with genomic instability across multiple preclinical models. | REC‑1245 entered the DAHLIA Phase 1/2 trial targeting MSI‑high and other high‑instability tumor types, with dose escalation ongoing. | REC‑617 demonstrated in vitro ovarian cell line growth inhibition and in vivo tumor regressions; causal modeling of >10,000 Tempus records linked high CDK7 expression to poor survival, guiding ovarian cancer selection. | A partial response was observed in an ovarian cancer patient treated with REC‑617 in the ELUCIDATE trial. | REC‑4881 achieved a 75% response rate and 43% median polyp burden reduction after 12 weeks in FAP patients, with 82% maintaining response (53% reduction) after a drug‑free interval.
primary_population: FAP patients treated 12 weeks with REC‑4881

talk_title: AI Transformation of Pharmaceutical R&D at AstraZeneca
affiliation: AstraZeneca, Chief AI and Science Innovation
moa_summary: Reis‑Filho outlined AstraZeneca's strategy to fine‑tune large multimodal foundation models with proprietary oncology data, enabling AI‑driven patient selection (e.g., Trop2 NMR) and accelerating ADC development, while highlighting the shift from handcrafted annotation to foundation‑model‑based quantification that reduced annotation effort from 40,000 to <5 hours.
key_findings: A quantitative continuous scoring system can measure subcellular Trop2 expression and derive a normalized membrane ratio that serves as a surrogate for ADC internalization. | Development of the Trop2 ADC biomarker required ~40,000 pathologist annotation hours for one indication; foundation models can reduce this to <5 hours or eliminate it. | AstraZeneca is building multimodal foundation models through partnerships (Tempus, Pathos, Model AI) to improve patient selection across diverse therapeutic classes.

talk_title: CODA: 3D Mapping of Tumors at Single‑Cell Resolution Across Organs and Species
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; ovarian cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: CODA reconstructs large tissue volumes (multi‑centimeter cubes) at sub‑micron resolution by iteratively registering serial H&E sections, segmenting tissue components with a CNN, and deconvolving cellular morphology, enabling 3D mapping of individual cells, vessels, nerves, and precancerous lesions across organs and species.
key_findings: Reconstructed a 3‑centimeter‑cube of human pancreas at sub‑micron resolution, positioning ~1 billion cells with >97% segmentation accuracy. | Identified 100–500 microscopic precursor lesions per healthy pancreas sample; disease‑adjacent pancreas contained up to 1 000 lesions. | Laser‑capture sequencing of individual lesions revealed distinct point‑mutation profiles, demonstrating multifocality in precancerous lesions. | Mapped entire fallopian tubes from seven healthy women and found STIC‑like lesions in all, challenging the prevailing belief that such lesions are rare in normal tissue. | Demonstrated that blood vessels and nerves follow a scale‑free fractal geometry across species (human, mouse, rhesus macaque) and developmental stages. | Developed generative AI tools (InterpolAI) to interpolate missing histology sections, achieving Pearson correlation >0.99 with ground‑truth images.

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Genomics Workflows for Precision Oncology
topic_categories: AI_ML; clinical_trial; translational; targeted_therapy; biomarker_development; platform_technology
moa_summary: AI methods are integrated into the MSK‑IMPACT clinical genomics pipeline to improve variant detection (deep learning callers, error‑modeling), functional interpretation (deep neural networks, AlphaMisSense, SpliceAI), and clinical decision support (LLMs with retrieval‑augmented generation, AI‑driven trial matching), thereby increasing sensitivity, reducing turnaround time, and expanding actionable insights across cancer types.
key_findings: Sequenced >100,000 patients using the MSK‑IMPACT 500‑gene tumor‑normal panel, generating a dataset that enables AI‑driven analyses. | Deep learning variant callers (e.g., DeepVariant) achieve higher sensitivity in difficult genomic regions compared with traditional callers. | AI‑based MSI detection (myMSI) reduced indeterminate cases relative to MSI‑Sensor, improving diagnostic confidence. | AlphaMisSense and SpliceAI improve pathogenicity prediction for missense and splice variants by incorporating protein structure and splicing models. | A neural network trained on 40,000 MSK‑IMPACT profiles predicts cancer type with ~80% accuracy overall and >90% for high‑confidence predictions. | LLM‑augmented tumor board agents (GPT‑4‑based) doubled decision‑support accuracy compared with GPT‑4 alone in a prospective evaluation.
primary_population: all solid tumors and hematologic malignancies

talk_title: AI-assisted mammography screening improves hard patient outcomes – evidence from randomized trials
moa_summary: Deep learning models applied to digital mammograms generate cancer detection scores that are integrated into radiology workflow, leading to higher detection of malignancies and improved downstream patient outcomes compared with standard radiologist-only interpretation.
key_findings: European randomized controlled trials of AI-assisted mammography screening demonstrated a statistically significant increase in cancer detection rates versus standard screening. | AI integration did not increase false-positive recall rates, maintaining comparable specificity to radiologist-only reads. | The trials showed downstream improvement in patient outcomes, including earlier-stage diagnosis and reduced mortality.

talk_title: Agentic AI for accelerating biomedical discovery
moa_summary: Bi Omni is an open‑source AI agent that integrates a curated environment of >100 biomedical tools, databases, and wet‑lab robots, enabling it to plan, execute, and iteratively refine experimental protocols, data analyses, and hypothesis generation, thereby automating and accelerating the scientific discovery loop.
key_findings: Bi Omni performed a full plasmid cloning design in ~10 minutes, producing a protocol that was experimentally validated as correct. | The agent completed a 30‑human multimodal data analysis (458 Excel files) in ~35 minutes, a task estimated to take weeks by a post‑doc. | In internal benchmarks across variant prioritization, GWAS gene discovery, perturbation screen design, scRNA‑seq analysis, drug repurposing, microbiome taxa analysis, and rare‑disease diagnosis, Bi Omni achieved accuracy comparable to or exceeding specialist tools despite no task‑specific engineering. | Over a three‑month public beta, >10,000 users executed ~70,000 automated research tasks, demonstrating broad utility across academia, pharma, and clinical settings.

talk_title: Foundation and virtual cell models for single‑cell biology
moa_summary: XCell extends the first‑generation scGBT foundation model by training a diffusion‑based language model on massive, diverse perturbation scRNA‑seq datasets (Pisces) to learn causal gene‑expression responses, while BioReason Pro aligns DNA‑ or protein‑sequence foundation models with large‑language‑model reasoning to predict pathway or GO‑term functions, using reinforcement learning and multimodal tokenization.
key_findings: Training on 26 M perturbed cells across 16 biological contexts enabled XCell to recover known protein‑protein interaction networks and improve causal inference over descriptive models. | Scaling XCell from 50 M to 5 B parameters yielded a monotonic decrease in training loss and increase in test‑set accuracy, establishing a clear scaling law for virtual‑cell models. | BioReason Pro achieved >80 % of predictions judged superior to human expert annotations on 200 novel proteins, outperforming baseline methods across GO‑term categories. | Both XCell and BioReason Pro were released open‑source; within two weeks, tens of thousands of users accessed the models, indicating strong community uptake.

talk_title: Real‑time AI augmentation for sepsis and palliative‑care in the clinic
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University; Founder & CEO, Bayesian Health
moa_summary: A real‑time multimodal transformer model ingests continuous EMR streams (labs, vitals, notes, meds) and outputs patient‑specific risk scores; integrated as an EHR‑embedded modular agent, it surfaces transparent alerts, auto‑generates order‑sets and documentation, and feeds back clinician responses for continuous monitoring and drift detection.
key_findings: AI‑augmented sepsis detection achieved 80%‑90% sensitivity while reducing false‑positive alerts by ~10‑fold compared with legacy rule‑based systems. | Median time‑to‑treatment was advanced by 3.5 hours, leading to a statistically significant reduction in in‑hospital mortality (p<0.01). | Clinician adoption of AI alerts reached 80%‑90% across four health systems, demonstrating high workflow integration. | In oncology‑focused palliative‑care deployment, early alerts increased appropriate consults and cut 30‑day readmissions by ~15%, also lowering total cost of care. | A governance layer continuously monitors model drift, enabling rapid root‑cause analysis and model re‑training without interrupting care.

talk_title: Foundation models for pathology and multimodal patient representation
affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: Self‑supervised foundation models (e.g., Titan) learn dense embeddings of whole‑slide pathology images; multimodal contrastive training (Threads) aligns these embeddings with molecular assays, while a large‑scale multimodal patient encoder integrates structured and unstructured clinical data into a single dynamic vector for downstream prediction of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment response.
key_findings: Titan achieved state‑of‑the‑art performance on slide‑level classification tasks while using a single vector per slide, outperforming prior region‑based models. | Contrastive training with IHC and transcriptomic data (Threads) improved few‑shot classification accuracy by >20 % on external cancer cohorts. | The 7.2 M‑patient multimodal encoder clustered known disease ontologies without supervision, demonstrating emergent medical knowledge. | Similarity search using the patient embeddings retrieved clinically relevant matches for rare‑cancer cases and potential trial candidates.

talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant trastuzumab tucatinib plus chemotherapy in HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, GI Oncology
moa_summary: Dual HER2 blockade with trastuzumab (antibody) and tucatinib (small‑molecule kinase inhibitor) inhibits HER2‑driven signaling in HER2‑amplified KRAS‑wild‑type rectal tumors, leading to high response rates and a 50% clinical complete response, enabling organ preservation in a subset of patients.
key_findings: HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer patients achieved a 78% ORR during a 6‑week HER2‑only lead‑in. | 50% of patients attained a clinical complete response after combined HER2 blockade and chemotherapy, allowing non‑operative management. | HER2 IHC 3+ staining predicted clinical complete response, whereas lower IHC scores correlated with residual disease. | ctDNA dynamics tracked response and early recurrence; persistent ctDNA after neoadjuvant therapy identified patients at high risk of relapse. | No grade ≥3 toxicities were observed, confirming safety of the HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant regimen.
primary_metric: overall response rate (ORR) after HER2‑only lead‑in

talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant zenodatumab in early‑stage HER2‑positive breast cancer (NEO3NHER)
moa_summary: Zenodatumab is a biparatopic HER2 antibody that simultaneously engages the trastuzumab and pertuzumab epitopes, enhancing HER2 clustering, blocking downstream signaling, and mediating ADCC, resulting in a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer without chemotherapy.
key_findings: Zenodatumab monotherapy achieved a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer. | An additional 20% of patients achieved RCB‑1, indicating near‑complete responses. | All pCRs occurred in HER2 IHC 3+ tumors; HER2 IHC 2+ patients did not achieve pCR. | No grade ≥3 treatment‑related adverse events were observed, confirming a favorable safety profile. | Higher HER2 RNA expression and PAM50 HER2‑enriched subtype correlated with response, though not statistically significant.

talk_title: Phase III perioperative atezolizumab plus chemotherapy in resectable stage II/III NSCLC (interim EFS and MRD analysis)
moa_summary: Atezolizumab blocks PD‑L1, restoring anti‑tumor T‑cell activity when combined with standard chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting, leading to improved EFS, higher MPR/pCR rates, and ctDNA clearance that predicts favorable outcomes.
key_findings: Atezolizumab plus chemotherapy improved 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo (HR 0.45). | MPR increased to 53.8% and pCR to 31.1% in the atezolizumab arm, far exceeding placebo. | Pre‑surgery ctDNA clearance occurred in 75% of atezolizumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates. | Grade ≥3 immune‑related adverse events were low (4.4%) and manageable. | ctDNA clearance was prognostic for EFS regardless of treatment arm.

talk_title: Post‑neoadjuvant ctDNA predicts non‑pCR and distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer (NSABP B‑59/GEPRDUOS substudy)
moa_summary: Tumor‑informed ctDNA sequencing detects residual molecular disease after neoadjuvant therapy; persistent ctDNA strongly predicts lack of pathological complete response and a >10‑fold increased risk of distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer.
key_findings: Only 9% of patients remained ctDNA‑positive after neoadjuvant therapy. | ctDNA‑negative patients had an 85% pCR rate versus 2% in ctDNA‑positive patients. | Persistent ctDNA conferred a hazard ratio of 10.2 for distant recurrence (p=0.0001). | ctDNA clearance showed 97.9% specificity for predicting non‑pCR.

talk_title: Perioperative nivolumab: EFS and ctDNA dynamics in resectable NSCLC (CheckMate 770 exploratory analysis)
moa_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1, reactivating anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; combined with chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting it improves event‑free survival, especially in patients achieving ctDNA clearance and in tumors harboring KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations.
key_findings: Perioperative nivolumab improved EFS (HR 0.45) and increased 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo. | ctDNA clearance before surgery occurred in 75% of nivolumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates. | Nivolumab benefit persisted across genomic subgroups; patients with KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations derived greater relative benefit versus placebo. | Machine‑learning analysis identified ctDNA clearance, non‑N2 nodal status, PCR, squamous histology, and nivolumab treatment as top predictors of prolonged EFS.

talk_title: Feasibility and efficacy of perioperative nivolumab ± relatlimab in potentially resectable HCC
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University / Ohio State University
moa_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1 while relatlimab blocks LAG‑3, together releasing T‑cell inhibition in the liver microenvironment; this dual checkpoint inhibition yields modest pathologic responses and favorable safety in resectable HCC.
key_findings: Both arms met feasibility criteria with no surgery‑delaying toxicities. | Pathologic or major response rates were 20% with nivolumab alone and 26.7% with the combination. | One‑year DFS was 73.3% (nivolumab) versus 100% (combo); two‑year OS favored the combination (84.8% vs 68.5%). | Grade 3+ immune‑related adverse events occurred in 46.7% (nivolumab) and 73.3% (combo), consistent with known safety profiles. | Decreasing AFP during neoadjuvant therapy correlated with absence of recurrence.

talk_title: Single-cell genome sequencing reveals low-frequency copy number alterations in normal breast epithelium
moa_summary: Using shallow single-cell genome sequencing, the study identified rare aneuploid cells in normal breast epithelium, predominantly luminal, with recurrent 1q gains and 10q/16q losses that mirror early breast cancer copy-number patterns, suggesting these events arise early and may represent founder lesions.
key_findings: Detected <1% of normal breast epithelial cells harboring copy-number gains (1q) or losses (10q, 16q) across BRCA1/2 carriers and wild‑type donors. | Aneuploid events were lineage‑restricted, occurring mainly in luminal cells and rarely in basal myoepithelium. | BRCA1/2 heterozygous samples showed higher frequency of high‑ploidy cells compared with wild‑type. | Spatial analysis of 537 reduction mammoplasties (3 million cells) revealed age‑correlated decline in cellularity and increased inflammatory macrophages and adipogenic fibroblast signatures.

talk_title: Single-cell DNA and ATAC profiling of normal breast epithelium reveals lineage‑specific aneuploidy and its correlation with age
moa_summary: High‑throughput single‑cell DNA and ATAC profiling of normal breast tissue identified that ~3‑4% of epithelial cells are aneuploid, with age‑dependent expansion of clones bearing 1q gain, 10q loss, and 16q loss, predominantly in luminal lineages, and that these events spatially cluster in ducts/TDLUs without histologic abnormality.
key_findings: Across 49 donors (≈83 k cells) ~3.9% of epithelial cells were aneuploid; 1q gain, 10q loss, and 16q loss were the most frequent events. | Aneuploid frequency positively correlated with donor age; expanded clones (vs sporadic) showed the strongest age association. | Aneuploid cells were enriched in luminal hormone‑responsive and luminal secretory lineages; basal cells harbored few events, mainly X‑chromosome loss. | Spatial transcriptomics mapped 1q‑gain/10q‑loss clones to ductal/TDLU regions that appeared histologically normal. | ATAC data linked 1q gain to increased accessibility at MDM4 and NF2; 10q loss associated with PTEN and CDH1 regulatory changes.

talk_title: Aneuploidy patterns as predictors of immune evasion and immunotherapy response across cancers
moa_summary: Retrospective analyses across TCGA, CPTAC, and real‑world cohorts identified chromosome‑9p loss and chromosome‑1q gain as recurrent aneuploidies that correlate with reduced cytotoxic T‑cell signatures and innate immune cell abundance, respectively, and predict poorer outcomes to checkpoint blockade in multiple tumor types; functional CRISPR‑engineered 9p13 deletions confer resistance to antigen‑specific T‑cell killing in vitro.
key_findings: 9p loss is strongly associated with an immune‑cold microenvironment and inferior survival on ICB in HPV‑negative head‑and‑neck cancer. | 1q gain correlates with reduced macrophage infiltration and predicts lack of response to ICB in MSI‑high endometrial cancer and melanoma, independent of TMB and PD‑L1. | Pan‑cancer regression controlling for overall aneuploidy identified 9p loss and 1q gain as top loci linked to immune evasion. | Isogenic deletion of a 15 Mb 9p13 region in oral cancer cells confers resistance to CAR‑T cell killing, implicating antigen‑processing genes in the region. | Mouse PDAC mini‑TCGA recapitulates human findings: 9p loss ↔ lower CD8⁺ T‑cells; 1q gain ↔ lower macrophage abundance.
primary_population: HPV‑negative head‑and‑neck cancer patients on ICB

talk_title: Regulatory perspective on rare cancer drug development
affiliation: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Oncology Center of Excellence
moa_summary: The speaker outlined FDA regulatory incentives, evidentiary standards, and flexible trial design options to accelerate development of therapies for rare cancers, emphasizing that substantial evidence is required regardless of prevalence and that randomized designs are preferred when feasible.

talk_title: Barriers to clinical development of therapeutics for chronic myelomonocytic leukemia
moa_summary: The presenter highlighted the heterogeneous mutational landscape of CMML, identified convergent GM‑CSF signaling as a therapeutic vulnerability, and described early proof‑of‑concept single‑arm trials targeting JAK2 (ruxolitinib) and GM‑CSF (lenzilumab) that suggest disease‑modifying potential across genetic subsets.
key_findings: CMML incidence is comparable to other myeloproliferative neoplasms that have approved therapies, supporting a viable economic case for drug development. | Single‑arm studies of ruxolitinib (JAK2 inhibitor) and lenzilumab (anti‑GM‑CSF) demonstrated proof‑of‑concept activity in CMML despite small patient numbers. | Phase III randomized trials in Europe (84 patients per arm) completed accrual in 18 months, showing that adequately powered studies are feasible for rare hematologic malignancies.

talk_title: Development of the first FDA‑approved treatment for KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Gynecologic Medical Oncology
moa_summary: Avutametinib, a first‑in‑class RAF/MEK clamp, inhibits MAPK signaling downstream of KRAS, while defactinib blocks focal adhesion kinase; together they produce synergistic tumor regression in KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, achieving >40% response rates and durable disease control.
key_findings: Combination avutametinib + defactinib yielded an overall response rate of 31% in recurrent low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, exceeding the 16‑26% rates seen with prior single‑agent MEK inhibitors. | KRAS‑mutant patients experienced a higher ORR of 44% and a median progression‑free survival of 22 months, establishing KRAS mutation as a predictive biomarker. | The phase 2 RAMP 201 study demonstrated durable responses with a median duration of response >31 months, supporting accelerated approval. | Breakthrough therapy and orphan drug designations facilitated rapid FDA review, leading to accelerated approval within six months of NDA submission. | A seamless phase 2/3 design allowed rapid accrual (>100 patients) and immediate transition to the confirmatory RAMP 301 randomized trial.

talk_title: Revolutionizing Phase I Trials Using the BOIN Framework and Backfill Implementation
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Biostatistics
moa_summary: Murray presented a Bayesian adaptive dose-finding framework (BOIN) with backfilling to optimize dose escalation, safety, and efficacy decisions in early-phase oncology trials, incorporating multidimensional toxicity scores, patient-reported outcomes, and flexible cohort sizes.
key_findings: Introduced a multidimensional toxicity scoring system ranging 0-1 to quantify cumulative adverse events across cycles. | Demonstrated that incorporating patient-reported CTCAE data alongside clinician-assessed AEs can inform dose recommendation. | Described the BOIN decision boundaries (lambda_e, lambda_d) for escalation/de-escalation and highlighted flexibility in cohort size. | Presented a backfill BOIN design (BFBOIN/BEBOIN) to allow safety‑cleared dose levels to be populated with additional patients, improving dose recommendation robustness.

talk_title: Bayesian Optimal Phase 2 Design (BOP2) for Simultaneous Toxicity and Efficacy Monitoring
moa_summary: The speaker introduced the Bayesian Optimal Phase 2 (BOP2) design, a model‑assisted approach that uses posterior probabilities to make real‑time stopping decisions for toxicity and futility in single‑arm and randomized trials, with flexible interim monitoring schedules.
key_findings: BOP2 provides decision tables pre‑tabulated for clinicians, eliminating on‑the‑fly calculations during the trial. | Demonstrated information‑dependent cutoffs that balance early stopping for unsafe or ineffective treatments while preserving power for promising agents. | Showed extensions to handle multiple efficacy endpoints, differing monitoring times for toxicity and efficacy, and incorporation of historical control data. | Implemented via the trialdesign.org web tool, generating protocol templates and operating characteristic simulations acceptable to regulatory reviewers.

talk_title: 27-hydroxycholesterol links hypercholesterolemia to estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer and metastasis
moa_summary: 27-hydroxycholesterol, a cholesterol metabolite, acts as a partial estrogen receptor agonist promoting ER+ breast tumor growth and, through selection pressure, generates 27HC‑resistant subclones that acquire high lipid uptake, ferroptosis resistance via GPX4 upregulation, and enhanced metastatic potential.
key_findings: 27HC binds ER as a partial agonist at physiologic concentrations and drives ER+ tumor growth in mice. | Genetic knockout of CYP27A1 reduces tumor growth and delays latency in MMTV-PyMT mice. | 27HC‑resistant subclones exhibit dramatically increased tumorigenicity and metastasis across breast cancer and melanoma models. | Resistant cells display non‑selective lipid uptake and become highly resistant to GPX4 inhibition, linking ferroptosis resistance to metastasis. | GPX4 knockout abolishes the metastatic advantage of 27HC‑selected cells, confirming GPX4 dependence.

talk_title: Epidemiology and shared genetics of atherosclerosis and cancer
moa_summary: Large‑scale claim analyses reveal that atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, but not non‑atherosclerotic forms, is associated with increased incidence of multiple cancers, suggesting shared pathogenic mechanisms beyond traditional risk factors.
key_findings: Non‑atherosclerotic cardiovascular conditions (valve disease, arrhythmia) do not increase cancer risk. | Atherosclerotic diseases (CAD, PAD, aortic aneurysm) show stepwise increased cancer incidence across multiple malignancies. | Meta‑analysis of Life’s Essential Eight risk factor control predicts up to 50% reduction in future cancer risk.

talk_title: Cancer‑driven atherosclerosis via TNF‑LRG1 axis and therapeutic targeting
moa_summary: Tumor‑derived TNF induces endothelial LRG1 expression, promoting intraplaque neovascularization and hemorrhage, thereby destabilizing atherosclerotic lesions; TNF blockade or LRG1 inhibition reduces plaque angiogenesis and size in mouse models.
key_findings: Mice bearing colon tumors develop plaques with abundant fragile neovessels and intraplaque hemorrhage. | RNA‑seq identifies LRG1 as the sole consistently up‑regulated angiogenic factor in tumor‑bearing mice. | Tumor‑derived TNF drives LRG1 expression in vascular tissue. | Etanercept treatment abolishes tumor‑induced plaque neovascularization and reduces plaque size. | shRNA knockdown of LRG1 recapitulates the protective effect of TNF blockade.

talk_title: Ischemic cardiovascular events reprogram bone marrow to promote tumor growth
moa_summary: Myocardial infarction induces long‑lasting epigenetic reprogramming of bone‑marrow myeloid progenitors, expanding Ly6C^high monocytic MDSCs that suppress T‑cell activation and increase regulatory T cells in tumors, thereby accelerating tumor growth and metastasis.
key_findings: Breast cancer survivors who experience a cardiovascular event have a 60% higher risk of recurrence and cancer‑specific death. | MI in mice doubles orthotopic breast tumor volume and increases lung metastases in melanoma models. | Tumors after MI show increased Ly6C^high monocytic MDSCs and FoxP3+ Tregs with reduced CD8+ T‑cell activation. | Depletion of Ly6C^high monocytes (Ccr2‑DTR) abolishes the MI‑induced tumor growth advantage. | Bone‑marrow from MI mice transferred to naïve recipients recapitulates accelerated tumor growth, indicating durable bone‑marrow imprinting. | ATAC‑seq reveals altered chromatin accessibility in myeloid progenitors, especially NLRP3‑positive MDPs, with reduced IRF8 motifs.
primary_population: breast cancer survivors with post‑diagnosis CV event

talk_title: Reverse cardio‑oncology: cardiovascular events accelerate cancer recurrence via immune reprogramming
moa_summary: Cancer elevates systemic TNF, which induces LRG1 expression in vascular tissue, promoting intraplaque neovascularization and hemorrhage, thereby linking tumor presence to plaque vulnerability.
key_findings: Cancer patients have higher circulating TNF levels (UK Biobank). | LRG1 is upregulated in atherosclerotic plaques from cancer patients. | Machine‑learning identifies prior cancer as a top predictor of intraplaque hemorrhage.

talk_title: Micronuclei-mediated horizontal DNA transfer and ecDNA formation
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; renal cell carcinoma; non-malignant
moa_summary: Induced missegregation of a chromosome into micronuclei triggers chromothripsis, generating ecDNA fragments that can be transferred to neighboring cells via tunneling nanotubes; transferred DNA can integrate into recipient genomes, become transcriptionally active, and confer new phenotypes.
key_findings: Targeted Y‑chromosome missegregation in DLD1 cells produces ~900 kb ecDNA fragments that amplify a neomycin resistance marker. | Micronuclei formation leads to ~30% of cells containing micronuclei, of which ~4% show mismatched fluorescent labeling, indicating intercellular DNA transfer. | DNA transfer occurs via tunneling nanotubes; transfer events are observed in ~1–4% of micronuclei across multiple cell types. | Co‑culture experiments demonstrate that female recipient cells acquire neomycin resistance after receiving Y‑chromosome fragments from male donor cells. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq confirms transcription of transferred neomycin resistance gene and adjacent Y‑linked genes in recipient cells, confirming functional integration. | DNA transfer is enhanced by mitotic errors and DNA damage, but is independent of genomic instability alone.

talk_title: Retention elements enable ecDNA propagation and therapeutic targeting
moa_summary: Retention elements on ecDNA bind mitotic chromosomes via mitotic bookmarking sites, enabling high‑efficiency hitchhiking; pharmacologic disruption of these elements impairs ecDNA tethering, induces micronucleation, and causes tumor regression in ecDNA‑driven xenografts.
key_findings: Thousands of retention element sequences were identified genome‑wide using Retain‑seq, many of which overlap mitotic bookmark promoters. | A single retention element increases ecDNA retention by ~50%; multiple elements raise retention >95% in live‑cell imaging. | Mathematical modeling predicts >95% retention efficacy is required for long‑term ecDNA propagation, matching experimental data. | In neuroblastoma patients, presence of ecDNA within micronuclei correlates with improved overall survival compared to patients without such micronuclei. | An Econic Biosciences inhibitor that disrupts ecDNA‑chromosome tethering causes dramatic tumor regressions in ecDNA‑positive PDX models but not in HSR‑positive controls.

talk_title: Homologous recombination deficiency as a genomic risk factor linked to radon exposure and health disparities in African American lung cancer
moa_summary: The work identifies a higher prevalence of homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) signatures and ancestry-enriched germline pathogenic variants in DNA repair genes among African American NSCLC patients, linking these genomic susceptibilities to radon and air pollution exposures that cause double-strand DNA breaks.
key_findings: African American NSCLC patients show a higher frequency of HRD mutational signatures compared to European Americans, especially in squamous cell carcinoma. | Pan‑cancer analysis revealed African ancestry associated with higher somatic HRD frequencies in 13 of 23 cancer types. | Identified 37 ancestry‑enriched germline pathogenic variants across 27 HR pathway genes, predominantly frameshift alterations. | Enrichment analysis showed HR and non‑homologous end joining pathways were significantly associated with lung cancer risk in African ancestry groups. | Radon exposure contributes to 3‑4% of lung cancer cases; high radon levels (≥4 pCi/L) equate to ~½ pack‑day smoking exposure.

talk_title: Environmental risk factors and exposomics in prostate cancer among men of African ancestry
affiliation: Boston University School of Public Health, Department of Environmental Health
moa_summary: The consortium integrates whole‑genome sequencing, epigenomic profiling, and untargeted exposomics to characterize how environmental chemicals (e.g., metals, DDT/DDE, PFAS) and endocrine‑disrupting exposures interact with genetic susceptibility to drive higher tumor mutational burden and aggressive prostate cancer phenotypes in African ancestry populations.
key_findings: Men of African ancestry exhibit markedly higher tumor mutational burden than European ancestry controls (123 SA, 7 Brazil, 53 Australia). | 63 epigenetic‑related driver mutations were specific to African ancestry, with only one European‑specific driver. | Prostate tumors from African ancestry individuals show widespread hypermethylation compared with European ancestry tumors. | Geographic variation in metal exposure (Al, Co, Cr, Fe, Mn) was observed, with higher levels in rural recruitment sites. | Antimony exposure correlated with higher ISUP grade in Chicago participants, while copper and zinc showed protective associations. | Mixture analysis identified manganese as a strong contributor to cancer risk models and arsenic to PSA levels.

talk_title: Spatially organized CXCL3 ligand immune hubs as biomarkers of immunotherapy response and premalignant progression
affiliation: Gladstone Institutes, University of California, San Francisco, Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; oral dysplasia (premalignant)
moa_summary: Spatial transcriptomics reveals CXCL3‑rich immune hubs at the tumor epithelium‑stroma interface, where interferon‑γ‑stimulated myeloid cells and CXCL13‑positive exhausted CD8 T cells co‑localize, facilitating T‑cell infiltration and predicting immunotherapy responsiveness; the same architecture in premalignant oral lesions marks lesions at high risk of malignant transformation.
key_findings: CXCL3 ligand hubs are enriched at the epithelial‑stromal interface in mismatch‑repair deficient colorectal cancers and are absent in proficient tumors. | CD8⁺ CXCL3⁺ T cells infiltrate the cancer epithelium within these hubs, while myeloid cells remain stromal. | In NSCLC, CXCL3 hub presence predicts response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy; a stem‑immunity subtype (TCF7⁺) further refines prediction. | In oral dysplasia, early lesions with high CXCL3‑positive CD8 T‑cell infiltration progress to cancer, whereas non‑progressors lack this feature. | CXCL3 hubs are distinct from tertiary lymphoid structures and do not contain CXCR5⁺ follicular helper cells.

talk_title: Hematopoietic loss of Y chromosome and its impact on mortality and disease
tumor_types: pan-cancer; cardiovascular disease; idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis; Alzheimer disease
moa_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome in hematopoietic cells creates mosaicism that drives increased fibrosis via TGF‑beta signaling and impairs immune surveillance, leading to higher mortality and cancer risk; PD‑1 blockade can reverse LOY‑induced phenotypes in mouse models.
key_findings: Approximately 20% of men in UK Biobank have >5% LOY in blood at baseline, increasing linearly with age. | LOY mice generated by CRISPR exhibit reduced lifespan and increased cardiac fibrosis, reversible by TGF‑beta inhibition. | LOY monocytes display upregulated immune‑checkpoint receptors; PD‑1 inhibition reverses LOY‑induced phenotypes in mice. | Loss of Y is present in all studied men at the single‑cell level, with up to 80% mosaicism in some individuals. | LOY is associated with increased mortality (≈2‑fold) and higher incidence of solid and hematologic cancers.

talk_title: Y chromosome loss as a driver of aggressive bladder cancer and response to checkpoint blockade
moa_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome in bladder cancer creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment with exhausted T cells and M2 macrophages, but paradoxically renders tumors more responsive to PD‑1/PD‑L1 checkpoint blockade, as demonstrated in mouse BBN models and human cohorts.
key_findings: Bladder tumors with LOY have poorer baseline prognosis but respond better to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors. | Isogenic LOY and Y‑positive bladder cancer cell lines show similar in vitro growth but LOY tumors grow faster in immunocompetent mice. | LOY tumors exhibit increased CD8+ T‑cell exhaustion and M2 macrophage infiltration. | CRISPR‑mediated Y chromosome deletion recapitulates enhanced checkpoint inhibitor response. | In IMvigor210, LOY signature stratifies patients with improved survival (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.38‑0.82).

talk_title: Comprehensive analysis of Y chromosome loss across adult and pediatric cancers and therapeutic implications
tumor_types: bladder cancer; neuroblastoma; uveal melanoma; prostate cancer; mesothelioma; sarcoma; pediatric tumors
moa_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome occurs via whole‑chromosome loss or structural rearrangements, often co‑occurring with p53 mutations or X‑linked tumor suppressor loss, leading to altered gene dosage (e.g., DDX3X/EIF1AX dependencies) and an immunosuppressive microenvironment that correlates with poorer outcomes across multiple adult and pediatric cancers.
key_findings: Y chromosome loss is present in 30‑40% of adult male tumors and ~18% of pediatric tumors. | Complete loss of Y is the most frequent Y alteration; structural variants involving Y occur in 25‑30% of sarcomas and bladder cancers. | In uveal melanoma, LOY is clonal and associated with poor prognosis, whereas in lung squamous carcinoma LOY is subclonal and linked to p53 mutations. | Loss of Y co‑occurs with X‑linked tumor suppressor mutations (KDM6A, KDM5C), suggesting a two‑hit mechanism. | DepMap analysis reveals LOY cell lines are uniquely dependent on X‑linked paralogs DDX3X and EIF1AX.
primary_population: Male patients with LOY in uveal melanoma, prostate cancer, mesothelioma

talk_title: Clinical outcomes of HIF‑2α inhibition in von Hippel‑Lindau disease
tumor_types: renal cell carcinoma; cerebellar hemangioblastoma; pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; retinal hemangioblastoma
moa_summary: Belzutifan binds the PAS‑B pocket of HIF‑2α, allosterically preventing heterodimerization with HIF‑1β, thereby suppressing HIF‑2‑driven transcription of angiogenic and proliferative genes, which leads to shrinkage of VHL‑associated lesions across kidney, CNS, pancreas, and retina.
key_findings: In a single‑arm phase 2 study of 61 VHL patients, belzutifan achieved a 70% ORR in renal RCC lesions, including 11% CRs. | CNS hemangioblastomas responded in 50% of cases (≥30% shrinkage), a dramatic improvement over prior VEGFR TKIs (<4% response). | Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors showed a 90% ORR, with most lesions achieving near‑complete shrinkage. | Treatment reduced the annual number of surgical procedures from ~20 per patient to <5, indicating a major quality‑of‑life impact. | Anemia was the most common on‑target toxicity (93% any grade, 13% grade ≥ 3) but was manageable with ESA support. | No significant difference in response was observed between VHL deletion versus point‑mutation genotypes.
primary_population: VHL patients with measurable renal RCC

talk_title: HIF‑2α inhibition across sporadic RCC settings: phase 3 and combination data
affiliation: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Geomedical Oncology
moa_summary: Belzutifan allosterically blocks HIF‑2α/HIF‑1β dimerization, suppressing HIF‑2‑driven transcription; when combined with VEGFR TKIs (lenvatinib) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) it adds durable disease control beyond what TKIs alone achieve, producing a tail of long‑term progression‑free survivors.
key_findings: In the phase 3 LightSpark‑005 trial, belzutifan monotherapy achieved an ORR of 23% with a 34% primary progression rate, comparable to everolimus but with a distinct safety profile. | Combination of lenvatinib + belzutifan (LightSpark‑011) improved median PFS by ~4 months versus cabozantinib (HR 0.70) and yielded ~50% of patients progression‑free at 2 years. | Adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab (LightSpark‑022) reduced risk of recurrence (DFS HR 0.72) versus pembrolizumab alone in high‑risk resected RCC. | Anemia (93% any grade) and early hypoxia are on‑target toxicities; most occur within the first 1–2 months and are manageable with ESA support. | Serum IPAG reduction correlates with deeper tumor responses, suggesting a pharmacodynamic biomarker for HIF‑2 inhibition. | The frontline triplet trial (LightSpark‑012) failed to meet PFS/OS endpoints, highlighting unknown biology limiting HIF‑2α inhibition in first‑line disease.

talk_title: Drosophila and mouse models reveal autophagy‑driven nutrient mobilization and SLC7A9‑dependent ferroptosis suppression in cachexia
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; renal cell carcinoma
moa_summary: Loss of autophagy in host tissues reduces systemic release of cysteine, limiting tumor access to this nutrient; tumor‑derived SLC7A9 (subermesa ortholog) imports cysteine to sustain glutathione synthesis and prevent ferroptosis, thereby supporting malignant growth in a cachectic environment.
key_findings: Autophagy‑deficient Drosophila hosts are protected from organ wasting and tumor growth, indicating host autophagy fuels tumor progression. | Stable‑isotope tracing shows >70% of tumor biomass derives from nutrients mobilized by host autophagy rather than dietary intake. | Genetic screen identified the fly transporter subermesa, orthologous to mammalian SLC7A9, as uniquely required for malignant tumor growth without affecting benign RasV12 clones. | SLC7A9 knockdown in pancreatic cancer cells reduces glutathione levels, induces ferroptosis markers, and impairs tumor growth in vitro and in xenograft models, whereas SLC7A11 knockdown has a milder effect. | Rescue experiments with N‑acetylcysteine or glutathione restore tumor growth only when SLC7A9 is functional, confirming cysteine import via SLC7A9 is critical for ferroptosis suppression.

talk_title: VHL loss drives HIF‑2α‑dependent PTHrP secretion and cachexia in clear‑cell renal carcinoma
moa_summary: Loss of VHL stabilizes HIF‑2α, which directly binds the PTHLH promoter and drives its transcription and translation; secreted PTHrP acts on peripheral tissues to induce hypercalcemia and cachexia, while HIF‑2α inhibition suppresses PTHrP production, normalizing calcium and improving body weight independent of tumor burden.
key_findings: Orthotopic OSRC2 tumors cause severe weight loss and hypercalcemia in mice, independent of tumor size. | Pharmacologic HIF‑2α inhibition (BT‑2399) prevents cachexia and normalizes calcium without affecting tumor growth. | Proteomic secretome analysis identified PTHLH as the only factor down‑regulated by HIF‑2α inhibition in cachexia‑inducing OSRC2 cells. | ChIP‑seq and transcriptional assays confirm HIF‑2α directly activates PTHLH expression. | CRISPR knockout of PTHLH in OSRC2 abolishes cachexia and hypercalcemia in mice; doxycycline‑inducible rescue restores the phenotype. | In a retrospective RCC patient cohort, baseline PTHrP levels correlate with higher calcium and lower muscle mass. | Treatment with FDA‑approved HIF‑2α inhibitor belzutifan (and investigational NKT2152) reduces plasma PTHrP, lowers calcium, and increases BMI, regardless of tumor response.

talk_title: Whole genome duplication as a pan-cancer predictor of metastasis
moa_summary: Whole genome duplication, inferred from copy-number profiles, marks tumors with increased genomic instability that independently predicts higher likelihood of future metastatic events and greater metastatic burden across cancer types, regardless of TP53 status.
key_findings: WGD is present in ~22% of early-stage pan-cancer tumors and varies by cancer type (10% glioma to >30% esophageal/gastric/colorectal). | Primary tumors with WGD have a significantly higher probability of developing metastasis compared to non‑WGD tumors. | WGD+ tumors exhibit higher metastatic burden (≥2 metastatic sites) within the first year after diagnosis. | WGD predicts increased risk of brain metastasis (2‑2.5x higher) across pan‑cancer and NSCLC cohorts. | The association between WGD and metastasis persists in both TP53‑wildtype and TP53‑mutant groups, indicating independence from TP53 status.

talk_title: Tissue‑based genomic instability score (GIS) for HRD prediction in breast, ovarian and pancreatic cancer
tumor_types: breast cancer; ovarian cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: An ensemble model integrates SNV‑derived COSMIC signatures (SBS3, SBS39) and CNV‑based scar metrics to generate a probabilistic GIS that predicts homologous recombination deficiency, enabling broader identification of patients who may benefit from PARP inhibitor therapy beyond BRCA mutation testing.
key_findings: GIS model achieved AUC 0.98 in a 164‑sample test set. | Sensitivity ranged from 78%‑96% across breast, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers; specificity ≥93% across non‑HRD cohorts. | GIS detected HRD in ~1/3 of breast, 45% of ovarian, and 14% of pancreatic cancers, extending beyond BRCA1/2 pathogenic mutations. | Only 2‑3% of GIS‑positive ovarian/breast cases were explained by non‑BRCA HRR gene alterations, indicating added value of the signature. | GIS is available on both tissue and liquid Guardant360 assays, supporting longitudinal monitoring.

talk_title: Integrated multi‑omic analysis of microvascular invasion in resectable hepatocellular carcinoma
moa_summary: A transcriptomic signature derived from microvascular invasion (MVI) tumors identifies cell‑cycle activation, metabolic reprogramming, and EMT‑related transcription factors; high MVI scores predict aggressive biology, poorer immunotherapy response, and highlight TOP2A as a therapeutic vulnerability treatable with doxorubicin.
key_findings: An MVI‑associated gene signature robustly predicts microvascular invasion across five independent HCC cohorts. | High MVI scores associate with increased cell‑cycle pathway activity (G2M, E2F, MYC) and reduced hepatic metabolic function. | Patients with high MVI scores have inferior outcomes on atezolizumab‑based therapy (HR 0.56). | Serum protein panel (six markers) combined with albumin/bilirubin yields AUC 0.856 for non‑invasive MVI detection. | TOP2A emerged as a top dependency; doxorubicin showed strongest in‑vitro efficacy in MVI‑high models.

talk_title: Spatial immune profiling of colorectal adenomas predicts recurrence and progression to cancer
moa_summary: Spatial transcriptomics reveals that loss of mucosal immune surveillance (IgA/M plasma cells, microfold cells) and barrier proteins (MUC2, KRT20) together with increased stem‑cell‑like epithelial states drive adenoma recurrence and malignant transformation.
key_findings: Progressive polyps show marked reduction of IgA/M plasma cells and macrophages, indicating compromised mucosal immunity. | Loss of barrier genes (MUC2, KRT20) and microfold cells correlates with higher recurrence risk. | Stem‑cell‑like transcriptional programs (TUFT cells, claudin‑10+) are enriched in polyps that progress to cancer. | Stromal compartments of progressive polyps display reduced PPAR signaling and DNA repair (HR, base excision). | Shared immune deficits are observed between polyps that recur (with or without cancer) suggesting a common early risk signature.
primary_metric: Immune cell reduction in progressive polyps
primary_value: Significant decrease (p<0.05) in IgA plasma cells, IgM plasma cells, macrophages
primary_population: polyps progressing to cancer vs non‑recurring

talk_title: Whole‑exome HRD scoring predicts PARP inhibitor benefit in ovarian cancer
moa_summary: A whole‑exome derived HRD scar score (integrating LOH, TAI, LST) identifies tumors with homologous recombination deficiency; HRD‑High status predicts superior progression‑free survival with PARP inhibitor maintenance compared with anti‑angiogenic therapy.
key_findings: Two‑thirds of the cohort were HRD‑High (score >50). | HRD‑High correlated strongly with BRCA pathogenic alterations. | In first‑line maintenance, PARP inhibitors improved PFS over bevacizumab in HRD‑High patients (HR 0.56). | In second‑line maintenance, HRD‑High remained the dominant predictor of PARPi benefit, whereas BRCA status lost significance. | Whole‑exome HRD scoring provides independent prognostic information beyond BRCA mutation.
primary_metric: PFS HR for PARPi vs bevacizumab (HRD‑High, first‑line)

talk_title: CA19‑9 for early detection of pancreatic cancer in new‑onset diabetes
moa_summary: Elevated serum CA19‑9 levels rise up to two years before clinical diagnosis of pancreatic cancer; longitudinal monitoring improves lead time by ~6 months and captures cases missed by a single threshold.
key_findings: CA19‑9 alone identified 83% of pancreatic cancers in a new‑onset diabetes cohort with >97% specificity. | A longitudinal algorithm incorporating CA19‑9 velocity increased AUC by ~6% and added ~6 months lead time. | One missed case was captured by the longitudinal rise despite a negative single threshold. | PPV in this high‑risk NOD population was ~70%, substantially higher than in the general population.

talk_title: Spatial biology and AI‑guided image analysis for early melanoma detection
affiliation: University of Queensland / QIMR Berghofer Medical Research Institute
moa_summary: Spatial transcriptomics deconvolution identifies distinct melanocyte transcriptional states; a melanoma‑specific module (purple) correlates with malignant lesions, while a nevus‑specific module (red) marks benign nevi, enabling AI models to predict gene expression from H&E and guide diagnostic decisions.
key_findings: Two melanocyte cell states were discovered: red (nevus‑associated) and purple (melanoma‑associated). | Module scores derived from these states accurately differentiate benign nevi from thin melanoma. | BRAF V600E mutation signal was higher in benign nevi than in thin melanoma. | AI model trained on spatial data can predict SOX10 and S100B expression directly from H&E images with high correlation. | In ambiguous lesions, high melanoma module scores aligned with expert recommendation for excision.
primary_value: Not quantified; clear separation between benign and malignant lesions in validation set

talk_title: ACT Wonder Squared S: Advancing Clinical Trials Working Through Outreach Navigation and Digitally Enabled Referral and Recruitment Strategies
affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center and Research Institute
moa_summary: Implemented a cluster‑stratified, multilevel intervention across 14 priority zones to increase trial referral and enrollment, combining community education sessions, a TrialConnect portal for community physicians, and digital tools (portfolio profiler, eligibility calculator, recruitment dashboard) to align trial availability with cancer burden and streamline referral workflows.
key_findings: Delivered 36 community education sessions reaching ~500 participants, with post‑intervention increases in trial familiarity, intention, and knowledge. | Portfolio profiler and recruitment dashboard were the most frequently used digital tools among internal staff; eligibility calculator had the highest visit count. | 80% of users who accessed a digital tool returned for additional use. | TrialConnect portal had low external physician uptake despite extensive marketing. | Patient decision‑aid tool (Choices DA) was accessed by 25% of eligible patients, with higher usage among White and Hispanic users.

talk_title: Support Trial (ECCOACRN): Community Oncology Practice Sites Clinical Trial Referral Initiative
moa_summary: Conducted a hybrid type‑1 cluster‑randomized rollout across seven NCORP community oncology sites, using stakeholder‑driven tools (web‑based health literacy resources, trial navigators) to improve referral of Black and Hispanic patients, demonstrating feasibility of enrolling underrepresented populations into therapeutic trials.
key_findings: Across sites, 48 of 161 referred Black/Hispanic patients at Baptist site consented (30% conversion). | Carle site: 59 of 193 referred consented (30%). | Geisinger: 24 of 40 referred consented (60%). | Montefiere: 9 of 19 referred consented (47%). | Provider surveys showed 90% reported referring minority patients, yet only ~20% of patients recalled being referred.

talk_title: ME Alex: Tailored AI‑Driven Communication Intervention to Facilitate Cancer Clinical Trial Referrals
moa_summary: Developed an AI‑generated virtual community health educator platform (ME Alex) that delivers plain‑language trial information via video and chat, personalizing messaging based on demographic and linguistic cues to improve trial awareness and referral among underserved patients.
key_findings: Over 1,500 users engaged with the platform to date. | Participants rated content clarity and engagement between 80–90 on a 0‑100 scale. | Higher usage observed among White and Hispanic users; lower among Asian and unknown ethnicity groups. | Iterative feedback led to incorporation of voice‑cloned AI avatars and a chat function for real‑time Q&A.

talk_title: Enabling a Cross‑Site Network (CUS2CT) Through a Learning Collaborative Data Coordination and Evaluation
moa_summary: Established a centralized data coordination hub (DEC) for the CP2CT network, standardizing common data elements, REDCap submission, and governance structures to enable consistent evaluation and rapid dissemination of multilevel trial‑access interventions across four U01 sites.
key_findings: Implemented a unified REDCap platform with common data elements aligned to NCI standards across all network sites. | Created three working groups (data, program evaluation, learning collaborative) and regular steering committee meetings to ensure alignment. | Developed a logic model and standardized outcome metrics now available for external researchers.

talk_title: Accrual to Clinical Trials (ACT) at Ohio State University: Baseline Assessment and Multilevel Stepped‑Wedge Intervention
affiliation: Ohio State University Comprehensive Cancer Center
moa_summary: Performed a baseline audit of referral pathways across OSU oncology clinics, identified multilevel barriers, and launched a stepped‑wedge, group‑randomized intervention targeting patient, provider, system, and community levels with outreach, EMR prompts, navigation, and education to improve trial referral and enrollment.
key_findings: Baseline data showed only 5% of screened patients ultimately consented to a trial. | Key barriers identified: low health literacy, provider time constraints, strict eligibility criteria, IRB delays, and lack of centralized referral contacts. | Proposed interventions include trial point‑of‑contact numbers, EMR alerts, patient navigators, and community education. | Hypothesized that tailored multilevel interventions could raise overall consent rates from 5% toward 8–10%.
primary_value: 40% of 2,000 patients had a trial for their disease
primary_population: All oncology patients screened at OSU clinics

talk_title: Zinc transporter ZIP4 drives pancreatic cancer cachexia via muscle wasting signaling cascade
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Surgery
moa_summary: Overexpression of the zinc transporter ZIP4 in pancreatic tumor cells increases intracellular zinc, activating the KRAB transcription factor, which up‑regulates Rab27b‑mediated secretion of Hsp70‑90 proteins that bind muscle cell receptors, triggering p38 MAPK, eBPβ, UBR2 and ATGL1 signaling and resulting in skeletal muscle atrophy and systemic cachexia.
key_findings: ZIP4 is markedly up‑regulated in human pancreatic tumors versus benign tissue. | ZIP4 knock‑down in mice extends median survival from 15 to 30 days (ASPC model) and from 57 to 92 days (BxPC3 model). | ZIP4‑silenced mice retain body weight and show rescued muscle fiber size and grip strength despite persistent primary tumor burden. | Mechanistically, ZIP4‑driven zinc influx activates KRAB, inducing Rab27b‑mediated Hsp70‑90 release that engages muscle P38 MAPK signaling and atrophy pathways.

talk_title: Dynamic phenotyping of cancer cachexia using longitudinal weight, imaging, and symptom data
affiliation: Kaiser Permanente Northern California, Department of Oncology
tumor_types: lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Longitudinal electronic health record data and serial CT imaging were used to define dynamic cachexia phenotypes (pre‑cachexia, active cachexia, recovery) and to link body‑composition clusters with distinct inflammatory biomarker signatures, revealing subgroups with divergent survival and symptom burden.
key_findings: Applying a sliding‑window BMI trajectory algorithm to ~6,000 advanced cancer patients identified dynamic cachexia episodes, including pre‑cachexia, recovery, and recurrent weight loss. | Hierarchical clustering of CT‑derived body composition changes revealed five phenotypes (early adipose remodeling, moderate wasting, severe wasting, splenomegaly, mixed), each with distinct inflammatory profiles (elevated GDF15, IL‑6, IL‑8). | Patients in severe and splenomegaly clusters had the shortest overall survival, independent of cancer type. | One‑quarter of weight‑stable patients harbored high‑risk imaging phenotypes, suggesting subclinical cachexia detectable before weight loss. | Two symptom‑based latent classes emerged: a high‑symptom burden group with greater fatigue, depression, low activity, and higher cortisol, low leptin, and a low‑symptom group resembling non‑cachectic controls.

talk_title: Targeted Cancer Interception Vaccines and p53 Reactivation Strategies
affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Philadelphia, PA, USA, Director of the Cancer Prevention Interception Targeted Agent Discovery Program
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; lung adenocarcinoma; breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; multiple solid tumors
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; translational; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Clapper presented mRNA‑based vaccines targeting EGFR L858R and KRAS G12C mutations found in premalignant lung ground‑glass opacities, demonstrating CD8+ T‑cell activation and reduced tumor formation in mouse models. She also described the small‑molecule p53 reactivator REM‑491, which restores transcriptional activity of multiple hotspot p53 mutants (R175H, R273) in vitro (EC50 ~40 nM) and suppresses tumor growth in xenograft models without observable toxicity.
key_findings: In a cohort of Lynch syndrome individuals, 85% maintained a positive IFN‑γ ELISpot response one year after two doses of the 209‑peptide NU209 vaccine. | Whole‑exome sequencing of 200 ground‑glass lung nodules revealed 25% harbor EGFR L858R and 40% KRAS G12C mutations. | EGFR L858R mRNA vaccine achieved >90% lipid nanoparticle encapsulation efficiency and induced robust CD8+ T‑cell responses in mice. | Mice vaccinated with EGFR L858R mRNA vaccine showed a dramatic reduction in tumor formation compared with empty nanoparticle controls. | REM‑491 induced Nox and Puma transcription in TP53‑mutant Cal‑33 cells but not in WT or p53‑null cells, confirming mutant‑specific activity. | REM‑491 achieved EC50 ≈40 nM for mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization in TP53 R175H cells and lower EC50 in heterozygous R273 cells. | In vivo, REM‑491 administered daily for 17 days reduced tumor volume and weight in TP53 R175H breast cancer xenografts without weight loss or biochemical toxicity, and MTD >400 mg/kg was not reached.

talk_title: Precision Cancer Prevention: Risk Assessment, Implementation, and Public Health Strategies
affiliation: Gustave Roussy, Paris, France, Director of the Interception Personalized Cancer Prevention Program
tumor_types: breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; lung cancer; liver cancer; colorectal cancer
topic_categories: clinical_trial; biomarker_development; early_detection; risk_reduction; implementation_science; digital_health; AI_ML
moa_summary: Delaloge outlined a public‑health‑driven precision prevention framework that integrates individual risk assessment (clinical scores, breast density, polygenic risk scores) with AI‑enhanced imaging, digital tools, and coordinated care pathways to deliver risk‑adapted screening, surveillance, and risk‑reduction interventions across multiple cancer types.
key_findings: In the MIPEPS trial, 36% of 28,000 women were classified as low risk and 33% as high risk, with a 5‑year invasive breast cancer risk of 1.67% in the high‑risk group. | Turnaround time for the PEARS risk score was a median of 7 weeks, demonstrating feasibility of large‑scale academic risk profiling. | The US Wisdom trial (non‑inferiority) met its primary endpoint of Stage 2B breast cancer incidence, supporting risk‑adapted screening. | The LIBER letrozole prevention trial suffered low accrual (18% acceptance) and 75% 5‑year adherence, resulting in an underpowered study with no observed risk reduction. | The French interception pilot program achieved a 1‑point improvement in the WCRF lifestyle score in 41% of participants and high adherence to recommended screening.
primary_value: Low risk 36%, High risk 33% of participants

talk_title: Metabolic vulnerabilities of Nrf2-activated cancer cells and cysteine availability in vivo
tumor_types: lung adenocarcinoma; liver cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Gina demonstrated that Nrf2 activation rewires cysteine and glutathione metabolism, creating dependencies on cystine uptake, glutamine metabolism, and NADPH production; she showed tissue-specific cysteine sourcing via transsulfuration in liver versus reliance on extracellular cystine in lung tumors, and highlighted how microenvironmental nutrient gradients shape these vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Liver tumors retain transsulfuration activity, while KRAS-driven lung tumors lack cysteine synthesis and depend on extracellular cystine. | Interstitial fluid of lung tumors contains ~40 µM reduced cysteine and ~1 mM reduced glutathione, indicating a shift to cysteine uptake. | Blocking cystine uptake with GGSTOP impairs tumor growth in vivo, but technical issues limited chronic dosing. | Nrf2-active cells become glutamate‑limited and are sensitized to glutaminase inhibition (CB‑839) in standard RPMI but not in tumor‑derived sTIF media. | sTIF media, mimicking interstitial fluid, rescues cystine‑starved cells and abolishes CB‑839 sensitivity, revealing context‑dependent metabolic vulnerabilities.

talk_title: Mitochondrial protein degradation drives heme‑mediated CD8 T‑cell exhaustion
moa_summary: Ho showed that chronic antigen exposure in tumors leads to accumulation of depolarized mitochondria in CD8 T cells; blocked mitophagy forces proteasomal degradation of mitochondrial proteins, releasing heme that binds and degrades BACH2, relieving repression of BLIMP1 and driving terminal exhaustion.
key_findings: MDR‑low CD8 T cells in tumors have high mitochondrial mass but low membrane potential and upregulated proteasome genes. | Proteasome inhibition (MG132) restores BACH2 and reduces BLIMP1, partially reversing exhaustion markers. | Heme released from degraded mitochondrial hemoproteins increases in exhausted T cells and correlates with exhaustion severity in vivo. | Exogenous heme treatment in vitro drives PD‑1 up‑regulation, TCF1 loss, and cytokine production decline. | BACH2 mutant resistant to heme binding preserves stemness markers even in tumor‑derived damaged‑mitochondria T cells.

talk_title: TIM‑4/AMPK‑TREM1 axis controls mitochondrial metabolism and immunosuppression in tumor‑associated macrophages
tumor_types: melanoma; lung carcinoma; ovarian cancer; liver cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; macrophage_biology; metabolism; epigenetics; basic_science; translational
moa_summary: Huang identified that tumor‑derived signals up‑regulate TIM4, which activates AMPK, suppresses TREM1 expression, and enhances mitochondrial complex II activity and glutamine‑derived α‑KG production in tumor‑associated macrophages; the resulting α‑KG fuels JMJD3‑mediated H3K27 demethylation, driving an immunosuppressive M2‑like phenotype.
key_findings: Tumor‑associated macrophages show ~70% reduction of TREM1 protein compared with splenic macrophages. | TIM4 deficiency or blockade restores TREM1 expression, reduces mitochondrial complex II activity, and diminishes α‑KG production. | Myeloid‑specific TREM1 deletion hyperactivates mitochondrial respiration, increases immunosuppressive markers, accelerates tumor growth, and shortens mouse survival. | AMPK activation downstream of TIM4 suppresses TREM1; AMPK knockdown rescues TREM1 and lowers mitochondrial activity. | GMG3 loss in myeloid cells reduces tumor size, TAM numbers, and immunosuppressive markers while enhancing CD8 T‑cell infiltration. | Combined TIM4 antibody and GMG3 inhibitor (GSKJ4) synergistically shrink tumors, decrease TAM immunosuppression, and improve survival in mouse melanoma.

talk_title: Cancer risk and genotype‑phenotype correlations in telomere biology disorders
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Clinical Genetics Branch, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; AML; MDS; pulmonary fibrosis; liver disease
moa_summary: Germline mutations in telomere maintenance genes (e.g., TINF2, RTEL1, TERT) cause critically short or dysfunctional telomeres, leading to stem‑cell failure and a strong selective pressure for clonal hematopoiesis; specific somatic mutations (TP53 loss, MDM4 overexpression, U2AF1) further drive malignant transformation, resulting in markedly elevated risks of head‑and‑neck SCC, AML, and MDS.
key_findings: Median overall survival of the entire TBD cohort was 52 years, but carriers of biallelic or X‑linked/TINF2 variants had median survival only in the 30s. | Overall cancer risk was ~3‑fold higher than the general population, rising to ~4‑fold before transplant. | Head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma risk was ~50‑fold higher (median onset age 14.7 years) in TINF2 mutation carriers. | AML risk was ~50‑fold and MDS risk ~500‑fold higher than expected in age‑sex matched controls. | Clonal hematopoiesis with non‑cancer‑associated mutations (POT1, TERT promoter, PPM1D) correlated with better prognosis, whereas TP53 loss, MDM4 overexpression, or U2AF1 mutations predicted cancer development. | Telomere length measured by flow cytometry reliably distinguishes TBD patients from the general population and aids genotype‑phenotype counseling.

talk_title: Genotype‑specific risk assessment and screening strategies for Lynch syndrome
affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Medicine, GI Cancer Genetics and Prevention Program
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer; gastric cancer; hepatobiliary cancer; pancreatic cancer; urothelial cancer
topic_categories: clinical_trial; biomarker_development; screening; genetics; population_screening; health_equity
moa_summary: Germline pathogenic variants in mismatch‑repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) cause microsatellite instability, markedly increasing risk of colorectal, endometrial and other cancers; genotype‑specific modeling shows earlier onset for MLH1/MSH2 and later, lower‑penetrance disease for MSH6/PMS2, informing age‑adjusted colonoscopy intervals and the potential integration of non‑invasive FIT screening to reduce colonoscopy burden.
key_findings: Lynch syndrome prevalence is ~1/350 in the US, with ~63 % of carriers lacking any personal or family cancer history that would trigger clinical suspicion. | MSH6 and PMS2 carriers constitute the majority of pathogenic variants in population‑based cohorts, yet they have later onset and lower absolute cancer risks compared with MLH1/MSH2. | Genotype‑specific colonoscopy recommendations (e.g., start at 25 y for MLH1/MSH2 annually, at 35 y for MSH6 every 2 y, at 40 y for PMS2 every 3 y) improve risk‑benefit balance and reduce colonoscopy burden. | Modeling shows that integrating biennial FIT with extended colonoscopy intervals (e.g., every 4 y) for PMS2 carriers maintains cancer prevention while improving quality‑adjusted life years and cost‑effectiveness. | Aspirin chemoprevention at 161 mg daily is recommended for all carriers, based on emerging evidence, despite limited data on genotype‑specific efficacy.
primary_metric: prevalence of Lynch syndrome in unselected population

talk_title: Precision modeling and targeted therapy testing in Fanconi anemia‑associated head and neck cancer
