affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Otolaryngology‑Head & Neck Surgery
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; esophageal cancer; vulvar cancer; liver cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: Patient‑derived Fanconi anemia head‑and‑neck cancer xenografts retain the underlying DNA‑repair deficiency and display heterogeneous driver alterations; EGFR overexpression predicts response to cetuximab and dacomitinib, PIK3CA E545K predicts sensitivity to alpelisib (with additive effect when combined with EGFR blockade), and high BCL2/BCLXL expression confers venetoclax sensitivity, providing a precision‑medicine framework for this ultra‑rare population.
key_findings: Two of three Fanconi anemia HNSCC PDX models were the most platinum‑sensitive among >30 sporadic HNSCC models tested. | Model #1 (high EGFR/HER2/HER3) showed exquisite sensitivity to cetuximab and dacomitinib, with dose‑dependent tumor regression. | Model #3 (PIK3CA E545K) was resistant to cetuximab but responded to alpelisib; the combination of alpelisib plus cetuximab produced greater tumor shrinkage than either agent alone. | Model #2 (high BCL2/BCLXL) was resistant to EGFR inhibitors but displayed marked sensitivity to the BCL2 inhibitor venetoclax, suggesting a novel therapeutic avenue. | Heterogeneity across the three PDXs underscores the need for genotype‑guided therapy selection rather than a one‑size‑fits‑all approach in Fanconi anemia‑associated HNSCC.

talk_title: Tobacco use trends and regulatory landscape
moa_summary: The speaker presented epidemiologic data on declining cigarette use, stable cigar use, and rising e‑cigarette use, highlighting the impact of FDA regulatory pathways (PMTA, MRTP) and the need for balanced risk‑benefit assessment of novel nicotine products.
key_findings: U.S. cigarette prevalence is trending downward toward the Healthy People 2030 target of 5% | Cigar use rates have remained flat over recent years | E‑cigarette use has steadily increased since market entry in 2009, both in the U.S. and globally | Youth cigarette use spiked after the introduction of Juul in 2014‑2015 before declining with subsequent regulations
primary_value: decreasing toward Healthy People 2030 goal of 5%

talk_title: Lung cancer screening impact and policy gaps
affiliation: University of Washington, Fred Hutch Cancer Research Center
moa_summary: Low‑dose CT screening of high‑risk smokers reduces lung cancer mortality by 20% (NLST) and improves 5‑year survival dramatically; however, current policy limits uptake to ~19% of eligible individuals, suggesting that simplifying criteria and expanding eligibility could save tens of thousands of lives.
key_findings: NLST demonstrated a 20% mortality reduction with low‑dose CT in high‑risk smokers | Only 19% of eligible U.S. adults are currently screened, yet this still yields ~15,000 lives saved over five years | Modeling suggests expanding criteria could increase lives saved to ~90,000 in five years | Simplifying smoking metrics (duration vs pack‑years) improves equitable identification of high‑risk individuals | Current upper age limit of 80 and 15‑year smoking cessation rule exclude many who could benefit
primary_population: high‑risk smokers screened with low‑dose CT

talk_title: Screening test implementation and follow‑up gaps
affiliation: Ohio State University, Division of Cancer Prevention and Control
tumor_types: breast cancer; cervical cancer; colorectal cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: The presenter highlighted gaps between screening test availability and completion of the screening process, showing low follow‑up rates for abnormal results across breast, cervical, colorectal, and lung cancer screening, especially in underserved populations, and argued for policy changes to support navigation and coverage.
key_findings: U.S. screening uptake is high for breast (80%) but low for lung cancer (14%) | Timely follow‑up after abnormal screens ranges from 60% (cervical) to 66% (mammography) and is lower for lung (48‑58%) and FIT/FOBT (18‑57%) | Out‑of‑pocket costs for follow‑up (e.g., $800‑$1,600 for abnormal mammogram) hinder completion | Medicaid expansion improves screening rates and cancer survival in rural, high‑poverty areas | Implementation barriers, not just test availability, limit effective screening worldwide

talk_title: NCI cancer prevention research pipeline and population impact
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Prevention
moa_summary: The speaker outlined NCI's structured pipeline from target discovery (CAPiT, natural products, immunoprevention) through PREVENT contracts to early‑phase trials (CPCTnet) and large population trials (NCOR), emphasizing the need for biomarkers, prevention agents, and policy support to translate discovery into population benefit.
key_findings: NCI supports a continuum from target discovery (CAPiT, natural products) to PREVENT contracts bridging discovery to clinical trials | Early‑phase prevention trials are coordinated through CPCTnet and NCOR | Biomarker development is a priority for lung cancer risk stratification and broader MCED applications | Policy and funding gaps hinder long‑term prevention studies due to long timelines and limited ROI for industry

talk_title: Measuring cancer evolution with mathematical modeling
affiliation: Computational Biology Research Center, Human Technopole, Milan
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; breast cancer; leukemia; melanoma; pan-cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; spatial_biology; basic_science; platform_technology; other
moa_summary: Sottoriva presented population genetics models that use bulk variant allele frequency distributions to infer clonal dynamics, mutation rates, and selection coefficients in tumors, enabling prediction of evolutionary trajectories and treatment response.
key_findings: Derived analytical relationship (1/F^2) between subclonal mutation frequency and mutation rate in expanding tumor populations. | Demonstrated that positive selection shifts subclonal driver mutations to higher frequencies, distinguishable from neutral drift. | Implemented Bayesian and machine‑learning deconvolution methods to estimate mutation rates and selection coefficients from bulk sequencing data. | Extended models to spatially resolved data, showing sampling bias can obscure selection events.

talk_title: Evolutionary‑informed adaptive therapy and virtual clinical trial design
tumor_types: prostate cancer; melanoma; basal cell carcinoma
moa_summary: Anderson described adaptive therapy, an on‑off dosing strategy that exploits fitness costs of resistance, using PSA dynamics to trigger treatment pauses, thereby prolonging control of resistant prostate cancer and other solid tumors.
key_findings: Adaptive therapy using a 50% PSA reduction rule extended median progression‑free survival to 33 months versus 14 months with continuous dosing. | Patients received <50% of the cumulative drug dose while maintaining disease control. | Virtual trial simulations with calibrated digital twins accurately predicted the adaptive trial outcome before enrollment. | Stratifying patients by first‑cycle PSA dynamics (high/medium/low thresholds) could further improve adaptive therapy efficacy.

talk_title: Metabolic quiescence and ATP synthase reversal as a vulnerability in enzalutamide‑resistant prostate cancer
moa_summary: The presenter identified that enzalutamide‑resistant prostate cancer cells enter a metabolically quiescent state with reversed ATP synthase activity driven by loss of ATPIF1, creating a depolarized mitochondrial membrane that can be selectively targeted with the mitochondria‑directed cation MitoQ, leading to tumor regression in vivo.
key_findings: Resistant cells show reduced basal and maximal respiration across multiple substrates. | Mitochondrial membrane potential is depolarized in resistant cells due to reverse ATP synthase activity. | ATPIF1 expression is decreased in resistant cells and in patient CTCs from non‑responders. | Pharmacologic inhibition of p38 restores ATPIF1 levels and sensitizes resistant cells to enzalutamide. | MitoQ treatment selectively kills resistant cells in vitro and causes tumor regression in resistant xenograft models.

session_title: Cancer Vaccines: The Next Immunotherapy Breakthrough
talk_title: Personalized Neoantigen Vaccines in Glioblastoma and the Impact of Tumor Intrinsic Programs
moa_summary: The speaker investigated personalized peptide vaccines in glioblastoma, finding that the mesenchymal transcriptional subtype is a key predictor of response due to higher HLA class I expression and T cell infiltration. The study explored the timing of anti-PD-1 therapy, suggesting that pretreatment with pembrolizumab followed by vaccine may be superior to vaccine followed by pembrolizumab. Additionally, Temozolomide was found to improve outcomes by depleting circulating Tregs and promoting antigen-specific T cell responses.
key_findings: Mesenchymal subtype in GBM is associated with higher HLA class I expression and better response to ICB monotherapy. | Dexamethasone treatment suppresses the ability of patients to mount circulating T cell responses following vaccination. | In a 40-patient trial, the cohort receiving pembrolizumab before the vaccine showed better outcomes than those receiving pembrolizumab after the vaccine. | Temozolomide-treated patients showed improved overall survival and decreased Treg upregulation following neoantigen vaccination. | Vaccine-reactive T cell clonotypes were detectable in post-treatment tumors and were predominantly CD4+ cells. | Spatial analysis demonstrated co-localization of neoantigen-specific T cells (e.g., NSUN5) with their corresponding antigens.
primary_value: well in excess of historic control

session_title: Cancer Vaccines: The Next Immunotherapy Breakthrough
talk_title: Mechanisms by which mRNA Vaccines Augment Response to Immune Checkpoint Blockade
moa_summary: The speaker proposes that mRNA vaccines, even those not targeting tumor antigens (like the COVID-19 vaccine), can sensitize tumors to ICI by triggering an MDA5-mediated surge in interferon alpha. This systemic interferon alpha activates myeloid cells (DCs and macrophages), which then prime anti-tumor T cells that infiltrate the tumor and secrete interferon gamma, leading to compensatory PD-L1 upregulation and subsequent sensitivity to PD-1 blockade.
key_findings: Retrospective pan-tumor analysis showed patients receiving the COVID mRNA vaccine within 30 days prior to ICI had significantly longer survival. | In mouse models, the COVID vaccine sensitized B16-F0 and Lewis lung carcinoma to ICI via an interferon alpha-dependent mechanism. | Blocking interferon alpha completely abrogated the benefit of the COVID vaccine in mouse models. | The mRNA-LNP complex activates the MDA5 receptor, bypassing the TLR7 silencing provided by N1-methylpseudouridine. | Human data showed a 30% increase in tumor PD-L1 expression in patients vaccinated prior to biopsy. | A prospective Phase II/III trial is being planned to test this universal priming effect.
primary_population: NSCLC patients receiving COVID vaccine within 100 days of ICI

session_title: Cancer Vaccines: The Next Immunotherapy Breakthrough
talk_title: Optimizing Peptide Vaccines and Adoptive Cell Therapy through Stem-like T Cell Generation
moa_summary: The speaker developed a nanoparticle peptide vaccine platform using a Toll 7/8 agonist and a cathepsin-cleavable linker to improve antigen solubility and cross-priming. IV administration of this vaccine induces stem-like CD8 T cells (TCF7+) and systemic type I interferon, which remodels the TME by depleting CHIL3+ monocytes. Furthermore, using a PI3K delta inhibitor during TIL expansion preserves T cell stemness, which, when combined with vaccination, leads to superior tumor control compared to effector-like cells.
key_findings: IV administration of the nanoparticle vaccine induced stem-like CD8 T cells and systemic type I interferon, whereas subcutaneous administration induced effector cells and provided no protection. | Protection from IV vaccination was completely abrogated by depleting type I interferon, which specifically reduced a CHIL3+ monocyte population in the TME. | PI3K delta inhibition during T cell expansion preserved stem-like characteristics (TCF1+, CCR7+, CD62L+), which were significantly more effective in tumor regression when combined with vaccination. | Combination of stem-like ACT and vaccination led to massive immune infiltration and shifted the tumor transcriptome toward an interferon-gamma reactive state. | Blocking either interferon gamma or the interferon alpha/beta receptor completely abrogated the protection provided by stem-like ACT + vaccine.

session_title: Cancer Vaccines: The Next Immunotherapy Breakthrough
talk_title: Inducing De Novo CD8 and CD4 T Cell Immunity in Pancreatic Cancer
moa_summary: The speaker developed a personalized RNA vaccine (autogene cevumarán) for PDAC, targeting high-quality neoantigens. The vaccine induces de novo, long-lived CD8+ and CD4+ Th1 effector T cells. These responses correlate with delayed recurrence and improved overall survival, with CD4+ cells appearing transiently in the blood but expanding in recurrent tumors and supporting CD8+ longevity.
key_findings: Personalized RNA vaccines induced neoantigen-specific T cells in 50% of unselected PDAC patients. | Vaccine-induced CD8 T cells showed exceptional longevity, with a median estimated lifespan of 7.7 years. | At 6-year follow-up, vaccine responders had significantly better overall survival (7/8 alive) compared to non-responders (2/8 alive). | 97.5% of induced CD8 and 100% of induced CD4 clones were absent in pre-vaccination tissues, proving de novo induction. | Induced CD4 T cells transition to a Th1 effector-like phenotype (granzyme+, T-bet+) and are clonally expanded in recurrent tumors. | Cross-reactivity to the wild-type epitope correlates with enhanced T cell longevity in vivo.

session_title: CAR-T Therapy: Transforming the Landscape of Adult and Pediatric Blood Cancers
talk_title: Advancing BCMA and Novel Targets in Multiple Myeloma CAR T Therapy
affiliation: The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Lymphoma and Myeloma
moa_summary: BCMA‑directed CAR T cells (ide‑cel, cilta‑cel) engage the TNFRSF17 antigen on >95% of myeloma cells, delivering potent cytotoxicity and inducing deep MRD‑negative remissions; next‑generation CARs add dual‑epitope binding or tandem targeting of GPRC5D to overcome antigen escape and improve durability, while novel manufacturing (fast off‑rate D‑domain CARs, allogeneic CRISPR‑edited products) aim to reduce CRS/ICANS and broaden access.
key_findings: Ide‑cel Phase 1 in heavily pre‑treated MM achieved 72% ORR and median PFS 11.1 months, surpassing historical expectations. | KarMMa‑3 Phase 3 showed CAR‑T median PFS 13.3 months versus 4.4 months with daratumumab‑based triplet (HR ≈ 0.33). | Cilta‑cel produced 97‑98% ORR with 33% of patients remaining disease‑free at 5 years, indicating functional cure potential. | GPRC5D‑targeted CAR T yielded 87% ORR (94% in BCMA‑naïve patients) and PFS up to 24.3 months, demonstrating efficacy after BCMA failure. | Fast‑off‑rate D‑domain CAR (Anita Cell) reported 96% ORR and 74% stringent CR with a favorable safety profile in early data. | Allogeneic CRISPR‑edited BCMA CAR (CB‑011) achieved 92% ORR, 75% ≥CR, and 91% MRD negativity, though persistence was limited to ~3 months. | In‑vivo lentiviral vector delivery of anti‑BCMA CAR achieved CAR expansion without lymphodepletion in four heavily pre‑treated patients, suggesting a path to off‑the‑shelf therapy.

session_title: CAR-T Therapy: Transforming the Landscape of Adult and Pediatric Blood Cancers
talk_title: Emerging Multi‑Antigen CAR T Strategies in Lymphoma and Leukemia
affiliation: Stanford University, Division of Blood and Marrow Transplantation and Cellular Therapy
tumor_types: large B-cell lymphoma; mantle cell lymphoma; follicular lymphoma; marginal zone lymphoma; ALL; CLL
moa_summary: Multi‑antigen CAR T constructs (tandem, bicistronic, trispecific) co‑express CARs against CD19, CD20, CD22 or CD79B, enabling simultaneous recognition of distinct B‑cell markers to prevent antigen loss; rapid manufacturing platforms reduce vein‑to‑infusion time, while higher upfront dosing and armored IL‑18 CARs aim to boost expansion and overcome an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.
key_findings: Zantacel (CD19/CD20 tandem) achieved 73% ORR and ~50% CR in heavily pre‑treated large B‑cell lymphoma with only 4% grade 3 ICANS. | Kite‑363 bicistronic CD19/CD20 CAR showed ~90% ORR and ~80% CR in primary refractory lymphoma, with reduced neurotoxicity compared to axicel. | J&J tandem CD19/CD20 CAR reported >90% ORR and 75‑80% CR across high‑risk cohorts, with low severe ICANS rates. | Cargo CD22 CAR in post‑CD19 relapse patients yielded 70% ORR and 53% CR overall, and 100% CR in follicular lymphoma cohort, without grade 3+ CRS/ICANS. | Higher upfront CAR dose (double infusion) in high‑LDH patients produced 93% ORR and 80% CR with manageable toxicity. | Armored IL‑18‑secreting CD19 CAR in CAR‑naïve patients achieved 81% ORR and 50% CR, albeit with increased grade 3 CRS resembling HLH. | Rapid manufacturing (YTB‑323, Kite‑753) delivering low‑dose CARs in frontline high‑IPI DLBCL showed ~90% ORR and 74% CR, supporting earlier use.

talk_title: Advances and challenges in autologous CAR T manufacturing and scalability
affiliation: City of Hope, Professor and Deputy Director of the CAR T-cell Program
tumor_types: brain tumors; solid tumors; hematologic malignancies; autoimmune diseases
moa_summary: Brown highlighted that rapid, automated manufacturing (1-3 day processes) and point‑of‑care production can preserve T‑cell fitness and reduce cost, while maintaining the potency of autologous CAR T cells for brain and solid tumors. She described collaborations using Solaris' Cell Shuttle to enable dual‑CAR, pooled approaches and emphasized decentralized manufacturing to improve access.
key_findings: City of Hope has treated 500 patients across 16 phase‑1 autologous CAR T trials. | Manufacturing capacity ranges from 55 to 85 patient‑specific products per year. | Rapid manufacturing platforms (1‑3 days) can shorten time to infusion from ~30 days to ~14 days. | Automated systems (Miltenyi Prodigy, Lonza Cocoon, Ori Biotech Airo, Solaris Cell Shuttle) reduce hands‑on labor and enable point‑of‑care production. | A pooled CAR approach targeting multiple GBM antigens is being developed with Solaris.
primary_population: all autologous CAR T trial participants

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR T cell platform: manufacturing, persistence strategies, and MRD‑guided trials
tumor_types: large B‑cell lymphoma; renal cell carcinoma; multiple myeloma
moa_summary: Roberts described Allogene's allogeneic CAR T platform that uses TRAC and CD52 gene edits to eliminate TCR and render cells resistant to alemtuzumab (CD52‑targeting) lymphodepletion, enabling selective host T‑cell depletion and enhanced CAR T expansion. He introduced the CD70‑targeting “dagger” CAR that cis‑masks CD70 to prevent fratricide and leverages host allo‑reactivity for superior expansion and persistence, demonstrated in RCC trials.
key_findings: Allogene's celma‑cel (CD19 CAR with TRAC and CD52 knockout) achieved a 67% ORR in relapsed/refractory LBCL, with most responses being complete. | No high‑grade CRS, ICANS, or GVHD observed across >200 treated patients. | In the ALPHA III MRD‑positive LBCL trial, 60% of celma‑cel patients cleared ctDNA versus 16.7% on observation (p<0.01). | CD70‑targeting dagger CAR (ALO329) yielded a 31% durable ORR in CD70‑high RCC with unprecedented Cmax and AUC28 expansion. | Higher lymphodepletion intensity (e.g., alemtuzumab exposure) correlated with greater CAR T expansion (AUC28) in phase 1 LBCL studies. | Manufacturing capacity of 30,000‑60,000 doses per year at <$10‑20k per dose, enabling community‑based delivery.

talk_title: In vivo CAR T engineering using GeneWriter RNA‑LNP platform
moa_summary: Diaco introduced Tessera's GeneWriter platform, an RNA‑based non‑LTR retrotransposon system packaged in targeted LNPs that delivers a template RNA encoding a CAR and a GeneWriter mRNA encoding the editing protein, enabling site‑random genomic integration of the CAR without viral vectors. In vitro, GeneWriters achieve 3‑25% CAR‑positive T cells; in vivo mouse studies reach ~25‑30% CAR‑positive T cells with tumor clearance and B‑cell aplasia at doses as low as 0.03 MPK.
key_findings: Five GeneWriter variants yielded 3‑25% CAR expression in human T cells in vitro. | GeneWriter integration shows a near‑random genomic distribution, unlike lentiviral vectors which favor active genes. | In NSG tumor models, LNP‑delivered CD19 GeneWriter achieved ~30% CAR‑positive T cells by day 13 and complete tumor eradication. | In a humanized mouse model, CD20 GeneWriter at 0.3 MPK produced ~20% CAR‑positive T cells and rapid B‑cell depletion; lower dose (0.03 MPK) still achieved delayed but measurable CAR expression and B‑cell aplasia. | GeneWriter integration pattern suggests lower bias toward essential genes, potentially reducing insertional mutagenesis risk.

talk_title: CAR-PRISM trial of cilta-cel in high-risk smoldering multiple myeloma
moa_summary: Cilta-cel, a BCMA-directed CAR T-cell product, is infused after lymphodepletion in high-risk smoldering myeloma patients, leading to rapid CAR T expansion, potent tumor cell killing, and universal MRD negativity without induction therapy, demonstrating a curative intent in an early disease setting.
key_findings: All 20 treated patients achieved MRD negativity at a sensitivity of 10^-6 (100% MRD-negative rate). | Overall response rate was 100% with 90% achieving complete or stringent complete responses. | No dose-limiting toxicities or grade ≥3 CRS/ICANS were observed; CRS was limited to grade 1–2 in all patients. | Non-ICANS neurotoxicities occurred in 35% of patients, mostly low-grade and reversible. | Median follow-up of 15.3 months showed no disease progression; median PFS and OS not reached.

talk_title: First-in-human dose escalation of multi-chain KIR CAR targeting mesothelin
moa_summary: The multi-chain KIR CAR separates the antigen-binding SS1 scFv (targeting mesothelin) from the DAP12 intracellular signaling domain, providing an on/off switch that activates T cells only upon mesothelin engagement, reducing tonic signaling and exhaustion compared with traditional single-chain CARs.
key_findings: Multi-chain KIR CAR showed minimal tonic signaling and lower exhaustion marker expression versus conventional single-chain CARs in vitro. | In mouse xenograft models, the KIR CAR achieved significant tumor regression with lower peripheral CAR T-cell persistence. | In the first-in-human dose escalation (9 patients), no dose-limiting toxicities, CRS (grade 1‑2) in 33%, and no ICANS were observed. | Disease stabilization was achieved in 55% of patients across dose levels, with one partial response at the highest dose. | CAR T-cell expansion in peripheral blood increased with dose but was not proportional, suggesting a plateau effect.

talk_title: FAP‑targeted 4‑1BB ligand trimer amplifies T‑cell engager in MSS colorectal cancer
affiliation: University Clinic of Navarra, Spain; University of Oxford, UK
moa_summary: Civisatuximab engages CD3 on T cells and CEA on tumor cells, providing signal 1; the FAP‑targeted trimeric 4‑1BB ligand binds fibroblast activation protein in the tumor stroma, delivering conditional 4‑1BB co‑stimulation (signal 2) only where FAP is present, thereby enhancing T‑cell activation, proliferation, and antitumor efficacy in MSS colorectal cancer.
key_findings: Combination yielded an overall response rate of 13% (20% at highest 4‑1BB ligand dose) in heavily pretreated MSS colorectal cancer. | Disease control rate reached 50%, exceeding historical monotherapy with civisatuximab. | CRS was mostly grade 1‑2; grade ≥3 CRS occurred in 2% of patients, indicating manageable safety. | Pharmacodynamic analyses showed increased peripheral IFN‑γ, sCD25, and IL‑6, and enhanced intratumoral CD8+ T‑cell density, especially in fibrotic stromal regions. | Higher tumor FAP and CEA expression trended with better tumor shrinkage, suggesting potential biomarkers.

talk_title: Phase 1/2 mRNA‑4359 vaccine plus pembrolizumab in first‑line advanced melanoma
moa_summary: Lipid‑nanoparticle mRNA‑4359 encodes PD‑L1 and IDO1; after intramuscular injection, dendritic cells translate and present these antigens, priming PD‑L1‑ and IDO1‑specific CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T cells that infiltrate tumors, target PD‑L1⁺/IDO1⁺ tumor and suppressor cells, and synergize with pembrolizumab to overcome checkpoint resistance.
key_findings: Objective response rate of 83% (2 CR, 8 PR) with a 92% disease control rate in 12 first‑line melanoma patients. | No vaccine‑related grade ≥3 adverse events; safety profile consistent with other mRNA vaccines. | All evaluable patients showed increased PD‑L1 and IDO1‑specific T‑cell responses by ELISpot. | De novo peripheral TCR clonal expansion observed irrespective of clinical response. | Responses occurred regardless of baseline PD‑L1 expression or prior adjuvant therapy.

talk_title: Immune‑stimulating ADCs with TLR7 agonist payloads and dual payload strategies
tumor_types: breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer; lung cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; ADC; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: The presented work develops immune‑stimulating ADCs (ISACs) that deliver a permeable TLR7 agonist (E104) via a legumain‑cleavable linker, using Fc‑silent antibodies to reduce Fcγ‑mediated uptake and cytokine release; dual‑payload ADCs combine the TLR7 agonist with MMAF to achieve synergistic tumor growth delay in mouse models.
key_findings: Fc‑silent, legumain‑cleavable TLR7‑ADC showed potent macrophage activation in co‑culture assays with minimal cytokine release compared to Fc‑active controls. | In HER2‑positive xenografts, the Fc‑silent ADC achieved tumor regression at 10 MPK Q4D×3, whereas the Fc‑active isotype control caused only modest delay and systemic cytokine release. | Trop‑2‑targeted ISAC demonstrated efficacy at 3 MPK in immune‑competent mice with no activity from isotype or naked antibody controls. | Dual‑payload ADC (MMAF + E104) produced significant tumor growth delay and regression in xenograft models, outperforming each single‑payload ADC, without observable weight loss or behavioral toxicity. | Anti‑Trop‑2 antibody responses were detected in mice after dosing, indicating immunogenicity despite Fc silencing.

talk_title: PI3K/mTOR inhibitor payloads for ADCs targeting B‑cell and HER2 cancers
tumor_types: B‑cell lymphoma; DLBCL; breast cancer; head and neck cancer; pancreatic cancer
topic_categories: ADC; targeted_therapy; PI3K_pathway; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Optimized pan‑PI3K/mTOR inhibitors were conjugated to antibodies (CD79B, HER2, CD71) via stable linkers to create ADCs that deliver potent intracellular kinase inhibition, achieving tumor‑stasis doses as low as 1.5 MPK in lymphoma models and demonstrating synergistic activity with venetoclax through MYC degradation in double‑hit DLBCL.
key_findings: DAR10 ADCs with optimized PI3K/mTOR payloads showed sub‑nanomolar in‑vitro potency across lymphoma cell lines. | In CD79B‑targeted ADCs, a tumor‑stasis dose of ~3 MPK was identified, with regression at 5‑15 MPK, outperforming the free inhibitor (copanlisib). | Combination of the PI3K‑ADC with venetoclax in MYC‑BCL2 double‑hit lymphoma yielded superior tumor suppression and sustained efficacy versus either agent alone. | HER2‑targeted PI3K‑ADC demonstrated bystander killing in mixed HER2‑positive/negative cultures and tumor regression at 9 MPK, with more durable AKT pathway suppression than alpelisib. | Preclinical tolerability studies showed the ADCs were well‑tolerated in rats (up to 150 MPK) and monkeys (up to 100 MPK), with toxicity profiles consistent with on‑target PI3K effects.

talk_title: Degrader Antibody Conjugates (DAC) using SMARCA2/4 targeted protein degraders as payloads
tumor_types: prostate cancer; breast cancer; AML; multiple myeloma; solid tumors
topic_categories: ADC; PROTAC_degrader; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Prelude’s DAC platform conjugates highly potent SMARCA2/4 PROTAC degraders to tumor‑specific antibodies (e.g., anti‑PSMA, anti‑mutant CALR), delivering intracellular degradation of SWI/SNF ATPase subunits, resulting in selective tumor cell killing with reduced systemic toxicity compared to the free degrader.
key_findings: VHL‑based SMARCA2/4 degrader (Degrader‑04) achieved ~300 nM degradation potency; switching to cereblon (Degrader‑06) improved potency to ~40‑90 pM but reduced plasma stability. | DAC‑02 (anti‑PSMA conjugated to Degrader‑04) showed antigen‑specific internalization and SMARCA2/4 degradation in PSMA‑positive LNCaP cells, with robust tumor regression and no significant weight loss, unlike the free degrader. | Cereblon‑based Degrader‑07 achieved >200 h plasma half‑life and picomolar degradation potency, enabling more stable DACs for in‑vivo studies. | Mutant CALR‑targeted DAC demonstrated selective SMARCA2/4 degradation and anti‑proliferative activity in CALR‑mutant cells, with tumor regression in TF1 xenografts at tolerable doses. | Global proteomics confirmed on‑target degradation of SMARCA2/4 with minimal off‑target effects.

talk_title: Heterobifunctional PARP-BET molecules induce PARP2 trapping and synthetic lethality
affiliation: Department of Chemistry, Scripps Research Institute
moa_summary: PSIP1 is a heterobifunctional small molecule that weakly engages PARP1/2 and BRD4 but induces a ternary complex preferentially with PARP2, leading to PARP2 chromatin trapping, inhibition of DNA repair, S‑phase arrest and synthetic lethality in HR‑deficient cells.
key_findings: PSIP1 shows minimal binary engagement of PARP1/2 and BRD4 yet induces potent cytotoxicity comparable to BET inhibition. | Synthetic lethality of PSIP1 is retained in BRCA2‑deficient DLD1 cells and is abolished by stereochemical inversion of the JQ1 moiety. | FRET and co‑IP experiments confirm ternary complex formation between PARP2 and BRD4 that is lost with the inactive enantiomer. | PARP2 knockout confers resistance to PSIP1, whereas PARP1 knockout sensitizes cells, indicating PARP2‑dependent toxicity. | CRISPR screens identify SUMO pathway components (PIAS4, SEMP1) and ABCC1 as selective modifiers of PSIP1 activity.

talk_title: Beyond degradation: induced proximity strategies entering the clinic
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; non‑small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: Daxinrasib is a bifunctional KRAS‑G12C binder that recruits cyclophilin A to create a neo‑protein‑protein interface, enabling high‑affinity inhibition of the previously undruggable KRAS G12C mutant and producing durable tumor regressions in PDAC and NSCLC.
key_findings: Phase 1/2 trial of daxinrasib in KRAS G12C PDAC (n≈40) achieved an ORR of ~50%. | In the larger RASalute‑302 cohort (n=83) the ORR was 35% in second‑line PDAC patients. | Registrational data (RASalute‑303) show a hazard ratio for overall survival of 0.40 versus standard chemotherapy. | Efficacy appears superior in earlier‑line settings and in NSCLC compared with docetaxel.

talk_title: High‑throughput discovery of induced‑proximity molecules using ratiometric transcriptional activation
moa_summary: The platform couples degradation of a transcriptional inhibitor fused to a protein of interest with activation of a reporter gene, enabling sensitive, multiplexed detection of protein degradation or induced protein‑protein interactions across thousands of small‑molecule and target combinations.
key_findings: Ratiometric transcriptional activation detects as little as 10% protein degradation, outperforming HiBiT and direct fluorescence readouts. | Multiplexed screening of 10,000 cereblon‑binding molecules against 120 targets identified selective degraders (e.g., PDE6D) and protein‑dependent toxicities. | RNA‑barcode readout amplifies a 1% fluorescence change to a 15% signal, enabling simultaneous profiling of ~200 proteins per well.

talk_title: Orally bioavailable macrocyclic cyclin inhibitors for cancer
tumor_types: small cell lung cancer; triple-negative breast cancer; mantle cell lymphoma; ER-positive breast cancer
moa_summary: Macrocyclic peptides bind the RXL substrate pocket on cyclin A/B or the cyclin D1–Rb interface, disrupting substrate recruitment and cyclin–CDK complex formation, leading to replication stress, mitotic catastrophe, and selective tumor cell death while sparing normal fibroblasts.
key_findings: Compound three achieved double‑digit nanomolar cellular potency against SCLC lines with improved MDCK permeability. | Compound six reached single‑digit nanomolar biophysical affinity for cyclins A/B, retained selectivity over cyclin E, and showed oral bioavailability (~30% F) in mice. | Oral dosing of compound six (30–100 mg/kg PO) produced robust tumor regressions in CDX SCLC models without body‑weight loss. | The same compound demonstrated tumor regressions in patient‑derived xenograft models of triple‑negative breast cancer. | Isoform‑selective cyclin D1 inhibitor potently inhibited cyclin D1–Rb interaction, blocked proliferation of mantle‑cell lymphoma and ER‑positive breast cancer lines, and outperformed acalabrutinib in a mantle‑cell lymphoma CDX model.
primary_value: observed at 100 mg/kg QD IP in CDX SCLC model
primary_population: small cell lung cancer CDX mice

talk_title: Structure‑guided discovery of DHXX8 RNA‑helicase inhibitors
moa_summary: Compound 54 binds the RNA‑binding tunnel of DHXX8, mimicking a phosphate backbone, and stabilizes the enzyme in an inactive ADP‑bound conformation, blocking RNA translocation and splicing, leading to intron retention of oncogenic transcripts and tumor cell death.
key_findings: Fragment‑based screening identified a carboxylic‑acid fragment that binds the DHXX8 RNA‑binding tunnel and mimics a phosphate group. | Structure‑guided optimization yielded compound 54 with sub‑micromolar biochemical potency and cellular target engagement. | Compound 54 shows good permeability, low clearance, and >24 h exposure above cellular IC50 in PK studies. | In SUM159 xenografts, compound 54 modulated splicing of MCL1 and HSF1, with maximal effect at 6 h for MCL1. | At 30 mg/kg daily, compound 54 produced two complete tumor regressions in HT29 CDX mice, but high doses caused >10 % body‑weight loss, indicating a narrow therapeutic index.
primary_value: significant efficacy at 30 mg/kg and 50 mg/kg daily; two complete regressions at 30 mg/kg

talk_title: Activation lethality: MAPK and WNT pathway activators as cancer therapeutics
tumor_types: melanoma; colorectal cancer; lung cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Small molecules disrupt the ERK–p15 interaction or act as molecular glues to degrade CK1α, thereby releasing negative regulators of MAPK or WNT pathways; this hyper‑activates the pathways beyond tolerable levels in oncogene‑addicted cells, causing mitotic catastrophe or synthetic lethality, while normal cells tolerate the activation.
key_findings: DTX‑067 binds the ERK‑p15 interface; SAR yielded DTX‑MAPK1 with 300‑fold biochemical and 1,000‑fold cellular potency improvement. | DTX‑MAPK1 selectively activates phospho‑ERK in MAPK‑mutant tumor cells with minimal activation in normal hepatocytes and skin. | Oral dosing of DTX‑MAPK1 (60 mg/kg daily) produced strong tumor regressions in BRAF‑mutant melanoma and colorectal models. | CK1α molecular‑glue degrader selectively degrades CK1α, activates WNT signaling, and induces tumor stasis in APC‑mutant CRC xenografts and PDX models. | MAPK activators sensitize KRAS‑inhibitor‑resistant cells and, after resistance to the activator, re‑sensitize tumors to MAPK inhibitors (cross‑sensitization).
primary_value: robust tumor shrinkage in BRAF‑mutant melanoma CDX at 60 mg/kg daily

talk_title: Covalent chemoproteomic approaches to target transcription factors and epigenetic rewiring
affiliation: University of California, Berkeley, Department of Chemistry
moa_summary: Covalent chemoproteomic probes identify hyperreactive cysteines in intrinsically disordered transcription factors (e.g., MYC C203). Stereoselective electrophiles covalently engage these sites, destabilize the protein, and trigger proteasomal degradation, while linked recruiters (e.g., MBD2 inhibitor) enable small‑molecule epigenetic rewiring (TRACER) that recruits HDAC complexes to silence locus‑specific transcription such as ER or AR‑V7. Molecular glues (e.g., ZD186) covalently attach to BCL6 and recruit BRD9, leading to MYC down‑regulation via neomorphic transcriptional complexes.
key_findings: Identified >100,000 ligandable cysteine sites across the human proteome using activity‑based protein profiling. | Stereoselective MYC degrader 236 caused dose‑responsive MYC degradation with EC50 ~2 µM, rescued by proteasome inhibition, and engaged C203 with >100 off‑targets. | Optimized degrader 219A achieved sustained MYC degradation for 24 h, reduced off‑target engagement, and lowered MYC levels by >90% in VCAP cells. | TRACER heterobifunctional molecule linking an estrone agonist to an MBD2 inhibitor suppressed ER transcription >90% at low nanomolar concentrations and maintained inhibition after washout for days. | Molecular glue ZD186 recruited BRD9 to BCL6, resulting in rapid MYC down‑regulation (observed before P21 up‑regulation) and rescue of the phenotype with BRD9 inhibition or knockout. | Covalent destabilizing degraders were also generated for beta‑catenin, AR, and IRF5, demonstrating the generality of the approach for diverse transcription factors.

talk_title: RIP‑Tag platform: tumor‑specific induced proximity targeting essential proteins
moa_summary: RIP‑Tag molecules are heterobifunctional agents that bind a tumor‑specific protein (e.g., AR or ER) and a pan‑essential protein (BRD4). The induced ternary complex sequesters BRD4, causing loss‑of‑function of this essential transcriptional co‑activator selectively in tumor cells, leading to potent cell death independent of the oncogenic driver. Positive cooperativity (>60) between the two ligands drives tumor‑specific activity and a wide therapeutic window.
key_findings: Identified >100,000 ligandable cysteine sites across the proteome, enabling covalent recruitment of novel E3 ligases. | HLD0915 formed a ternary complex with AR and BRD4 at EC50 ≈ 2 nM and inhibited VCAP cell proliferation (GI50 ≈ 3 nM). | In vivo, HLD0915 induced dose‑dependent ternary complex formation and BRD4 loss‑of‑function markers (↓cMyc, ↑HEXIM1) in tumor tissue. | Preclinical efficacy demonstrated tumor regressions in AR‑amplified, AR‑V7+, and AR‑independent prostate cancer xenografts, outperforming enzalutamide. | Phase 1 mCRPC trial (31 patients) showed PSA50 response of 70% and PSA90 of 40% across AR‑amplified, non‑amplified, AR‑mutant, and AR‑V7 sub‑cohorts. | HLD0117 achieved sub‑nanomolar ternary complex formation with ER and BRD4, showed >90% ER transcription suppression, and produced dose‑dependent tumor regressions superior to fulvestrant in MCF7 xenografts. | Both RIP‑Tags retained activity against clinically relevant ER mutations (Y537S, D538G) and AR LBD mutations, indicating resistance‑agnostic potential.

talk_title: Expanding induced proximity: molecular glues, viral E3 ligases and lysosomal targeting chimeras
affiliation: Amgen, Induced Proximity & Small Molecule Discovery
moa_summary: Amgen generated large DNA‑encoded libraries of low‑affinity ligands for >35 E3 ligases (≈350 chemotypes) and screened them for molecular glue activity using high‑throughput proteomics and barcode‑encoded bead assays. Hits such as a VHL‑glue induce selective degradation of caspase‑2 via ternary complex formation. Viral‑derived E6 ligase (E6AP) was repurposed (VIPER‑TAC) to degrade pan‑essential SARS‑1 selectively in HPV‑positive cells, achieving tumor‑specific killing. Lysosomal‑targeting chimeras (LIMTACs) recruit plasma‑membrane KRAS G12D to the lysosomal membrane protein Rnf152, causing relocalization and lysosomal degradation, yielding sustained MAPK pathway inhibition beyond simple kinase inhibition.
key_findings: Built a >500,000‑compound molecular glue library covering 35 newly enabled E3 ligases. | Proteomics screen identified a VHL‑based glue that selectively degrades caspase‑2, confirmed as proteasome‑ and VHL‑dependent with KD ≈70 nM ternary complex. | DNA‑encoded bead assay of 25,000 VHL‑glues uncovered dGem3, which induces transcriptional changes via recruitment of the SMN complex component Gemin3; ternary complex KD ≈70 nM. | VIPER‑TAC using HPV E6/E6AP and a small‑molecule recruiter of SARS‑1 achieved selective degradation of SARS‑1 in HPV‑positive cells, leading to dose‑dependent cell killing absent in HPV‑negative controls. | LIMTAC strategy demonstrated that a bifunctional molecule can relocalize KRAS G12D from the plasma membrane to lysosomal membranes via Rnf152, resulting in KRAS degradation and sustained phospho‑ERK suppression after washout.

talk_title: Applying molecular classifications to therapy and trials in diffuse large B‑cell lymphoma
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Division of Cancer Medicine
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; ADC; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; tumor_microenvironment; spatial_biology; biomarker_development; other
moa_summary: Polatuzumab vedotin delivers a cytotoxic payload to CD79B‑expressing B‑cells, enhancing the efficacy of R‑CHOP and improving PFS (HR 0.73) in frontline DLBCL, especially in ABC and high‑IPI patients. Parallel work leverages gene‑expression and whole‑exome classifiers (COO, DZ‑signature, LymphGen) to stratify patients for targeted regimens such as tafasitamab + lenalidomide + R‑CHOP or chemotherapy‑free SMART combinations, aiming to replace CHOP with biology‑driven, less toxic therapies.
key_findings: Polatuzumab‑R‑CHOP improved PFS versus R‑CHOP (HR 0.73) in a global phase III POLARIX trial, establishing a new frontline standard for DLBCL. | Subgroup analysis showed the greatest PFS benefit in ABC‑type DLBCL, confirming genotype‑guided therapy. | A rapid (median 10‑day) whole‑exome/RNA‑seq pipeline accurately classifies COO, DZ‑signature, and LymphGen subtypes in both newly diagnosed and relapsed patients. | Streamlined eligibility criteria (13 essential vs 37 traditional) enabled faster trial enrollment and maintained efficacy (HR ~0.75) for tafasitamab + lenalidomide + R‑CHOP. | SETD2 haploinsufficiency is enriched in patients of African ancestry; preclinical models demonstrate synthetic lethality with SETD2 inhibition, though clinical development was paused after company acquisition. | Chemotherapy‑free SMART regimens (rituximab + ibrutinib + lenalidomide) achieved favorable PFS in 61 patients with markedly reduced cytopenias and no alopecia.
primary_population: Frontline DLBCL patients with higher IPI

talk_title: Chromatin-associated ENL condensates drive oncogenic transcription
affiliation: University of California, Berkeley, Department of Molecular and Cell Biology
topic_categories: basic_science; chromatin; tumor_microenvironment; transcription; cancer_vaccines; other
moa_summary: Oncogenic mutations in the ENL YEATS domain promote formation of large, low‑dynamics chromatin condensates that concentrate transcriptional elongation machinery at HOXA loci, driving hyper‑activation of oncogenes; disruption of ENL‑histone acetyl binding or RNA‑mediated nucleation impairs condensate formation, reduces transcriptional bursting, and blocks leukemogenesis.
key_findings: ENL hotspot mutations generate large, stable nuclear condensates that colocalize with active HOXA transcription sites. | Condensate formation requires both histone acetyl‑binding via the YEATS domain and RNA interaction; a 3A charge‑neutralizing mutant reduces RNA binding and condensate number by ~30‑40%. | Disruption of condensates (genetic or with a YEATS‑domain inhibitor) displaces ENL from target genes, lowers HOXA transcriptional bursting, and extends survival of mice with aggressive AML by >5 months. | Partial reduction of condensate formation (3A mutant) is sufficient to abolish tumorigenesis in vivo, indicating a threshold effect for oncogenic transcription.

talk_title: Targeting the menin–MLL interaction in AML: clinical advances and resistance mechanisms
topic_categories: clinical_trial; targeted_therapy; epigenetics; combination_therapy; resistance_mechanisms; other
moa_summary: Menin inhibitors disrupt the menin–MLL interaction, causing loss of menin and MLL fusion occupancy at HOXA/MEIS1 loci, leading to transcriptional repression of oncogenic programs; resistance arises via point mutations in the menin binding pocket or stabilizing mutations in MLL or LEDGF that restore complex binding.
key_findings: Revumenib and ziftomenib achieve FDA approval for KMT2A‑rearranged and NPM1‑mutant AML, demonstrating clinical efficacy (ORR 30‑40%). | Base‑editor screens identified recurrent menin‑binding pocket mutations (e.g., M327, G331) that confer 5‑15‑fold IC50 shifts and drive resistance. | Higher dosing of menin inhibitors in PDX models prolongs survival and reduces frequency of binding‑pocket mutations, but selects for alternative resistance via MLL or LEDGF mutations. | MLL‑fusion cells acquire mutations in integrase‑binding motifs (S119L, G123V, G141S) that stabilize the MLL‑menin‑LEDGF complex, bypassing inhibitor disruption.

talk_title: Cell-intrinsic mechanisms and synthetic lethalities of chromosomal instability
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; brain tumor; ovarian cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Chromosomal instability creates aneuploidy‑induced stresses (DNA damage, proteotoxicity, metabolic imbalance) that generate synthetic lethal dependencies such as KIF18A, CDC20, RAF signaling, SCD1, and Aurora B; targeting these dependencies selectively kills CIN‑high tumor cells while sparing diploid normal cells.
key_findings: KIF18A identified as a unique synthetic lethal vulnerability of chromosomally unstable aneuploid tumor cells across multiple independent studies. | CDC20 loss sensitizes CIN‑positive cells to spindle assembly checkpoint inhibition. | Aneuploid cells exhibit basal DNA damage and chronic MAPK (RAF) activation; sub‑lethal RAF inhibition resensitizes them to DNA‑damaging chemotherapy. | Proteasome inhibition is more effective in highly aneuploid tumors when patients are retrospectively stratified by aneuploidy score. | Loss of chromosome 17p (mono‑allelic TP53 loss) creates dependence on SCD1 for fatty‑acid synthesis in brain‑metastatic breast cancer, and SCD1 inhibitors show potent in vitro, ex‑vivo, and in vivo activity. | Aurora B kinase expression is down‑regulated in 17p‑loss tumors, rendering them hypersensitive to alvespib, a selective Aurora B inhibitor. | ARGOS gene RBM14, located near CCND1 amplifications, is toxic when overexpressed and its amplification correlates with worse prognosis.

talk_title: Cell‑extrinsic immune rewiring by chronic STING activation in chromosomal instability
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; lung cancer (metastasis model); mouse mammary tumor models
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; tumor_microenvironment; spatial_biology; combination_therapy; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Chronic chromosome missegregation generates micronuclei that rupture, activating cGAS‑STING; persistent STING signaling shifts from interferon production to an ER‑stress program that promotes metastasis via immune‑suppressive remodeling, while specific STING domains mediate interferon versus ER‑stress outputs.
key_findings: Chronic STING activation in CIN‑high cells abolishes the interferon response and induces a pro‑metastatic ER‑stress transcriptional program. | STING‑dependent metastasis requires an intact immune system; immune‑deficient mice lose the effect. | Stat‑seq saturation mutagenesis mapped distinct loss‑of‑function domains for interferon signaling (cGAMP‑binding and C‑terminal tail) and for ER‑stress signaling (N‑terminal region). | Specific STING variants (e.g., K337) are enriched in lung metastases, indicating gain‑of‑function for metastatic fitness. | STING agonist DMXAA failed in humans because a single amino‑acid difference (position 229) prevents binding; the platform correctly predicted this species‑specific resistance. | Barcoded STING libraries enable on‑target drug resistance mapping, distinguishing on‑target from off‑target effects.

talk_title: Translating chromosomal instability vulnerabilities: KIF18A inhibitors and cGAS‑STING modulation
tumor_types: high-grade serous ovarian cancer; lung adenocarcinoma (LKB1‑mutant); triple‑negative breast cancer; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; immunotherapy; clinical_trial; combination_therapy; translational; biomarker_development
moa_summary: KIF18A inhibition exploits a synthetic lethal interaction with RB‑pathway loss in CIN‑high tumors, while chronic cGAS‑STING signaling in aneuploid cancers desensitizes interferon output; transient cGAS inhibition restores STING protein levels and re‑sensitizes tumors to STING agonists, enabling acute interferon‑driven anti‑tumor immunity.
key_findings: KIF18A inhibitors (VLS‑1488, savolnisib) show clinical activity only in p16‑positive (RB‑deficient) tumors, with partial responses in all p16‑positive cases except one mixed‑histology outlier. | p16 IHC serves as a robust predictive biomarker for KIF18A inhibitor response across multiple tumor types and dose levels. | RB loss drives MAD2 overexpression, creating the synthetic lethal context required for KIF18A inhibitor efficacy. | In chromosomally unstable tumors, chronic cGAS‑STING activation suppresses type‑I interferon via STING degradation and ER‑stress induction, rendering STING agonists ineffective. | Pharmacologic cGAS inhibition (RU521) or genetic cGAS knockout restores STING levels, re‑activates interferon signaling, and markedly enhances response to STING agonists in mouse models. | High cGAS / low STING expression in pretreatment biopsies predicts lack of response to STING agonists in a head‑and‑neck clinical cohort.
primary_value: All PRs (except 1) occurred in p16‑positive tumors

talk_title: Claudin 18.2 antibody therapy clinical data and translational insights
tumor_types: gastric cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; ovarian cancer
moa_summary: Zorvotaximab is an IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds the extracellular loop 1 of claudin‑18.2 and mediates tumor cell killing primarily through ADCC and CDC; cerpituximab adds ADCP activity and can modulate the tumor microenvironment by increasing NK and macrophage infiltration and chemokine release, thereby enhancing T‑cell recruitment.
key_findings: Zorvotaximab monotherapy achieved <10% ORR but 30‑40% disease stabilization in claudin‑18.2 positive gastric cancer. | Randomized FASTO phase 2 showed improved PFS when Zorvotaximab was combined with fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy. | Global phase 3 trials demonstrated an overall survival benefit leading to regulatory approval of the Zorvotaximab‑chemo combination. | Cerpituximab monotherapy induced ADCP and increased tumor‑infiltrating NK cells, macrophages, and chemokines (CXCL9/10) in gastric cancer biopsies. | In pancreatic cancer, cerpituximab plus chemotherapy failed to improve OS despite 28% ORR, suggesting that subcellular localization and KRAS mutation status affect efficacy. | Steroid pre‑medication appeared to improve tolerability of cerpituximab without diminishing ADCP activity. | Claudin expression is heterogeneous and can be lost after chemotherapy in ~40% of patients, highlighting the need for dynamic biomarker monitoring.

talk_title: Translational rationale for claudin‑targeted ADCs and bispecific T‑cell engagers
tumor_types: gastric cancer; breast cancer; multiple myeloma
topic_categories: ADC; bispecific; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; preclinical; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Claudin‑18.2 ADCs deliver high‑potency cytotoxic payloads (e.g., topoisomerase inhibitors) directly to antigen‑positive tumor cells, while bispecific T‑cell engagers (2+1 format) recruit CD3‑positive T cells to form immune synapses independent of HLA, enabling cytotoxicity even at moderate antigen density and providing bystander killing of neighboring claudin‑negative cells.
key_findings: ASP2138 ADC induced potent killing across gastric cancer cell lines with varying claudin‑18.2 density, though higher expression correlated with greater potency. | Claudin‑18.2 bispecific TCE showed cytotoxicity in vitro even at low antigen levels and mediated bystander killing in mixed claudin‑positive/negative organoid cultures. | Pre‑clinical data suggest that lower CD3 affinity bispecifics can retain efficacy while potentially reducing cytokine release syndrome risk. | Clinical data from an Astellas claudin‑18.2 ADC (phase 3) demonstrate activity comparable to HER2‑targeted ADCs, supporting the therapeutic window in gastric cancer. | Heterogeneous claudin expression (≈87% of gastric tumors) underscores the need for strategies such as dual‑antigen targeting or bystander effects to overcome antigen loss.

talk_title: Claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T cell therapy in gastric and pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T cells are engineered to recognize the extracellular loop of claudin‑18.2, leading to antigen‑specific T‑cell activation, cytokine release, and tumor cell lysis; persistence is limited by rapid exhaustion, prompting strategies such as naive T‑cell enrichment, novel lymphodepletion, and repeat infusions to improve durability.
key_findings: Monotherapy claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T achieved a 55% ORR and ≥19% disease control rate in gastric cancer. | Sequential treatment (chemo → PD‑1 → CAR‑T) yielded an 80% ORR and 15‑month median PFS, suggesting benefit of tumor debulking before CAR‑T. | Randomized trial showed a four‑fold higher response rate and a two‑fold OS improvement versus standard therapy, reducing mortality risk by 63%. | High claudin‑18.2 expression, absence of liver/bone metastases, and a TZMK⁺/TPX⁺ immune‑rich microenvironment predicted long‑term responders. | Grade ≥3 gastric mucosal injury occurred in ~10% of patients but was manageable with PPIs and fasting; pulmonary toxicity was rare and linked to CRS. | Repeat CAR‑T infusion showed diminished expansion and persistence, highlighting the need for improved persistence strategies.

talk_title: EHR-based phenotyping of cancer cachexia and identification of anatomical subtypes
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Computational Oncology Lab
tumor_types: melanoma; pancreatic cancer; colon adenocarcinoma; kidney cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Reznik's group defined cachexia windows using longitudinal weight loss criteria in EHR data, aligned these windows with serologic, imaging, and genomic measurements, and identified three CT-based anatomical subtypes (A, B, C) distinguished by organ volume changes and systemic inflammation, with TP53 mutations emerging as a genomic risk factor when modeled as a time-to-event.
key_findings: Implemented a 5% weight loss over 6‑month EHR algorithm to define cachexia windows in >60,000 cancer patients. | Found ~30% prevalence of cachexia episodes in melanoma and high prevalence in pancreatic cancer. | Identified three CT‑based cachexia subtypes: Type A (fat loss + spleen/liver enlargement), Type B (fat loss + organ atrophy and radiodensity increase), Type C (mixed). | Type A subtype associated with worst overall survival across diseases. | TP53 mutation is the most significant genomic correlate of cachexia risk when modeled as a time‑to‑event, validated in an independent cohort. | Type A patients exhibit rising NLR and dropping albumin during cachexia episodes, indicating systemic inflammation.

talk_title: Observational study of cachexia phenotypes using patient-reported outcomes and biomarkers
affiliation: University of Rochester Medical Center, GI Oncology
tumor_types: lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Dunn presented the design and early baseline findings of two prospective observational cohorts (Lotus and UNCOVER) that longitudinally collect patient-reported outcomes, body composition imaging, and blood/tumor biomarkers to identify cachexia phenotypes; preliminary symptom clustering revealed high‑symptom and low‑symptom groups, with the high‑symptom group showing elevated GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 and worse early disease progression.
key_findings: Enrolled ~1,000 patients in Lotus (U Rochester) and ~800 in UNCOVER (Kaiser) to prospectively phenotype cachexia. | Baseline: >50% of patients already have ≥5% weight loss; pancreatic cancer patients show highest prevalence. | Symptom clustering identified three groups: low symptom burden (similar to weight‑stable), high symptom burden (fatigue, anorexia, emotional distress), and intermediate. | High symptom burden cluster associated with higher serum GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 levels. | Patients in high symptom burden cluster had lower physical activity, higher appetite stimulant use, and higher 3‑month disease progression rates. | Preliminary data suggest that symptom clusters may correlate with body‑composition clusters (e.g., low symptom = minimal wasting).
primary_population: advanced lung, pancreatic, colorectal cancer patients

talk_title: Prostaglandin E2–driven cachexia and immunotherapy resistance in lung cancer models
affiliation: New York University School of Medicine, Cancer Immunology Lab
moa_summary: Gonsalves demonstrated that elevated prostaglandin E2 production by tumor‑associated neutrophils and tumor cells drives cachexia and impairs anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy; CRISPR‑mediated knockdown of PTGES or PTGS2 reduces PGE2, delays weight loss in KRAS/LKB1 mouse models and C26 allografts, linking PGE2 to both wasting and immunotherapy resistance, with dysbiotic lung microbiota further amplifying neutrophil activation.
key_findings: In KRAS/LKB1 (KL) lung cancer mice, ~80% develop cachexia with up to 20% weight loss; non‑cachectic littermates retain weight despite similar tumor burden. | Cachectic mice show higher tumor and serum IL‑6 family cytokines and enrichment of arginase‑positive macrophages and prostaglandin‑producing neutrophils. | Prostaglandin E2 levels are markedly increased in cachectic mouse tumors and in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of cachectic lung cancer patients. | CRISPR knockdown of PTGES in KL tumors suppresses PGE2 and significantly delays cachexia onset. | CRISPR knockout of PTGS2 in C26 cachectic cells eliminates tumor PGE2 and protects mice from weight loss. | Lung tumor dysbiosis (higher 16S density) correlates with cachexia and neutrophil infiltration; introducing dysbiotic bacteria to non‑cachectic tumor‑bearing mice induces weight loss. | Clinical cohort analysis shows cachexia reversal after ICI correlates with high PD‑L1 expression and partial responses, whereas continued cachexia associates with progressive disease and early death.

talk_title: Methodological considerations for incorporating real-world data into clinical trial design
affiliation: Brown University, Carl Kawaja and Wendy Holcomb Professor of Public Health, Professor of Biostatistics and Data Science
moa_summary: Hubbard presented a framework for hybrid control trial designs that augment internal control arms with external real-world data, comparing analysis strategies (full pooling, static borrowing, propensity score weighting) via simulations to assess bias, type I error, and power, highlighting propensity weighting as a method that controls bias while improving efficiency.
key_findings: Full pooling of external control data introduces substantial bias in treatment effect estimates. | Static borrowing reduces bias but still shows inflated type I error under strong confounding. | Propensity score weighting eliminates bias and restores type I error to nominal levels while providing a power boost. | Dynamic borrowing yields estimates close to trial-only results with minimal efficiency gain.

talk_title: Real-world data curation from EHRs and applications to clinical trials
affiliation: Flatiron Health, Principal Investigator Elect of the ASCO TAPER study
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; bladder cancer; multiple myeloma; pancreatic adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Meropol described methods for extracting structured variables from EHRs using human abstraction, machine learning, and large language models, presented quality assessment frameworks, and illustrated applications such as external control arm construction for the RESOLVE pancreatic trial and outcome analyses in NSCLC and bladder cancer.
key_findings: LLM-extracted progression dates showed high reliability compared to human abstraction across multiple tumor types. | External control arms built from multiple real-world data sources closely matched the RESOLVE trial control arm after propensity score adjustment. | In bladder cancer, patients receiving cisplatin off-label had better outcomes than those who did not. | In early-stage NSCLC, neoadjuvant immunotherapy improved distant metastasis-free survival, but additional adjuvant therapy did not show further benefit.

talk_title: Prospective real-world data registry embedded in an ECOG-ACRIN phase III trial
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Medical Oncologist, co-chair of ECOG-ACRIN Research Group
moa_summary: O'Dwyer outlined the design and early feasibility results of a prospective real-world data registry (PRECOG 1702) run in parallel with the ECOG-ACRIN phase III trial EA5182, demonstrating high data completeness (>85%) and rapid accrual, and discussed operational challenges such as site CRA engagement, IRB barriers, and EHR documentation gaps.
key_findings: Prospective real-world registry achieved >85% capture of predefined key variables after minimal data cleaning. | Accrual reached target of 25 patients/month after an initial slow start, now active at 40 institutions. | CRAs are critical for data collection; engagement and regular communication improve site performance. | Regulatory challenges include IRB exemptions for patient-reported outcomes and social determinants of health.

talk_title: PACER method reveals TCL1A as a driver of clonal hematopoiesis and leukemia
moa_summary: Jaiswal introduced the PACER (passenger approximated clonal expansion rate) method to infer clone age from passenger mutations, identified TCL1A promoter SNP rs2887399 as a regulator of clonal fitness, and demonstrated that ectopic TCL1A expression drives HSC expansion and myeloid leukemia in transgenic mice, establishing TCL1A as a causal driver of CHIP.
key_findings: PACER can quantify clonal expansion rates from bulk WGS data using passenger mutation counts. | Driver mutation identity accounts for most variance in clone fitness; splicing factor mutations (U2AF1, SRSF2, SF3B1) and JAK2 show highest fitness. | Smoking is the only measured environmental factor positively correlated with clonal expansion rate in humans. | GWAS identified rs2887399 in the TCL1A promoter; the T allele slows expansion of multiple CHIP drivers. | TCL1A is normally silent in HSCs; driver mutations de‑repress TCL1A, leading to HSC fitness advantage. | Transgenic mice with HSC‑specific TCL1A expression develop HSPC expansion and 100% mortality from myeloid leukemia by 12‑13 months. | Carriers of the protective T allele have reduced risk of myeloid malignancies, especially CMML.

talk_title: Phylogenetic and methylation mapping of somatic evolution in blood
affiliation: Cambridge University Hospital & Wellcome Sanger Institute
moa_summary: Nangalia described a combined whole‑genome and whole‑methylome sequencing pipeline for single‑cell colonies, enabling reconstruction of somatic mutation phylogenies and assignment of CpG methylation changes to specific ancestral branches, revealing massive early‑life epiallelic remodeling and that driver mutations both shape and are shaped by these epigenetic landscapes, with some epigenetic changes acting as oncogenic drivers themselves.
key_findings: Developed an automated low‑input pipeline to obtain both WGS and whole‑genome methylomes from ~2 ng DNA per colony. | Mapped ~5,000 CpG‑segment epialleles that change early in embryogenesis (≈10–15 somatic mutations) with ~10,000‑fold higher rate than later life. | Identified ~4,000–5,000 early‑life CpG segments (mostly promoters) that acquire stable methylation states, many showing allele‑specific patterns beyond X‑inactivation. | Demonstrated that CHIP driver mutations (e.g., DNMT3A, TET2, 1q+, DNMT3A Y66F) are associated with increased numbers of epimutations per branch. | Showed that epigenetic silencing of one BRCA1 allele can occur pre‑gastrulation, creating a mosaic that predisposes to BRCA‑like breast cancer. | Found that somatic methylation changes can act as drivers, with certain embryonic epialleles preferentially selected by specific mutations. | Established that methylation trees can resolve polytomies where SNV trees cannot, providing finer temporal resolution of early clonal events.

talk_title: Inflammatory and metabolic consequences of CHIP and therapeutic implications
affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute, Harvard Medical School
tumor_types: myeloid malignancy; cardiovascular disease; chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; liver disease; lung cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; metabolism; clinical_trial; translational; resistance_mechanisms; other
moa_summary: Ebert showed that loss of TET2 in hematopoietic cells reprograms lipid metabolism, leading to accumulation of triglycerides, diacylglycerols and cholesterol esters, which drives heightened IL‑1β‑mediated inflammation in atherosclerosis, COPD, NASH and other age‑related diseases; genetic screens identified ACLY and OGT as key mediators, and pharmacologic inhibition of lipid pathways normalizes the inflammatory phenotype.
key_findings: CHIP carriers have increased risk of atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction, heart failure, arrhythmias, liver disease, kidney disease, COPD, osteoporosis and lung cancer. | TET2 loss leads to lipid droplet accumulation in neutrophils/macrophages, driving IL‑1β‑dependent inflammation. | In vivo CRISPR screen in Tet2‑deficient mice identified ACLY and OGT as essential for the inflammatory phenotype; knockout of these genes restores wild‑type inflammation levels. | TET2 directly binds OGT; loss of TET2 liberates OGT, causing aberrant protein O‑GlcNAcylation and metabolic dysregulation. | A simple risk score incorporating genotype, clone size, RDW, MCV, cytopenias and age stratifies CHIP individuals into low, intermediate and high risk for myeloid malignancy and overall mortality. | High‑risk CHIP (~1% of population) has ~50% overall survival, whereas low‑risk/CHIP‑negative individuals have ~95% survival. | IL‑1β inhibition (e.g., canakinumab) reduces lung cancer incidence, suggesting inflammatory CHIP contributes to early tumorigenesis.

talk_title: What Do We Do Now That We've Machine Learned Everything?
affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center; Synthesize Bio (co‑founder, board member)
moa_summary: Leake outlined the current landscape of AI applications across oncology drug development, emphasizing the distinction between supervised and generative models, the regulatory uncertainty surrounding generative AI, and the need for robust benchmarks, representation, and guardrails to ensure safe, responsible deployment.
key_findings: AI is now embedded in >1,500 FDA regulatory submissions over the past decade, with oncology accounting for ~27% of AI‑containing submissions. | The FDA received 307 AI‑related oncology submissions in 2024 alone, reflecting rapid growth. | Four major impact areas for AI in oncology were identified: care/operations, drug discovery, drug development, and consumer health. | Generative AI models present greater regulatory challenges than supervised models due to broader use cases and lack of clear pathways. | Benchmarking, representation across diverse populations, and external validation were highlighted as critical gaps to address.

talk_title: AI in Pharma R&D: Regulatory Perspectives and Guardrails
affiliation: AstraZeneca, Chief of AI for Science Innovation
moa_summary: Visfilo described AstraZeneca's experience with AI‑enabled pathology devices, the three classes of AI‑driven biomarkers (replication, inference, outcome prediction), and advocated for third‑party benchmarking, clear guardrails, and high levels of evidence to ensure safe clinical deployment.
key_findings: AstraZeneca achieved the first Class 2 FDA clearance for an AI pathology device in 2021 and a breakthrough device designation for an AI‑derived predictive biomarker for an ADC. | Three AI biomarker classes were defined: Class A (task replication), Class B (inferred biomarkers), Class C (prognostic or predictive outcomes). | For Class C2 (predictive of therapeutic response), prospective trial validation is required; explainability is not mandatory if performance is demonstrated. | Emphasis on multi‑stakeholder partnerships (academia, industry, payers, regulators, patients) to set evaluation standards and avoid a “Wild West” scenario.

talk_title: FDA Frameworks for AI in Oncology Drug Development
affiliation: Office of Oncologic Diseases, CDER, FDA; Oncology Center of Excellence AI Program
moa_summary: Puthenvedu presented the FDA’s risk‑based credibility framework for AI models used to support regulatory decisions in drug and biologic development, outlining the seven‑step process, the distinction between AI used for data generation versus decision support, and the 2025 draft guidances on model risk, lifecycle maintenance, and international harmonization.
key_findings: The FDA received >1,000 AI‑containing submissions (CDER + CBER) to date, with oncology representing ~27% of AI submissions. | The draft guidance (Jan 2025) introduces a seven‑step risk‑based credibility assessment, calibrated by model influence and decision consequence. | High‑risk use cases (e.g., AI‑driven inpatient monitoring decisions) require extensive validation, documentation, and early FDA engagement. | AI models that meet the statutory device definition are subject to separate AI‑enabled device guidance (Jan 2025). | Key regulatory principles: methodological transparency, external validation on representative data, alignment with Good Machine Learning Practices, and early sponsor‑FDA communication.

talk_title: DPP4 as a novel checkpoint for CD8 T cells in glioblastoma
moa_summary: DPP4 acts as an immunometabolic checkpoint on CD8 T cells; its inhibition by sitagliptin restores mitochondrial respiration and glycolysis, enhances proliferation, cytotoxic mediator production, and degranulation, partly via up‑regulation of GAD1‑mediated TCA cycle flux, leading to improved anti‑GBM activity.
key_findings: Retrospective analysis of Medicare data showed GBM patients on DPP4 inhibitors had significantly longer survival than matched controls. | CD8 T cells in human GBM express high DPP4, especially terminally exhausted (TOX‑high) subsets. | Pharmacologic or genetic DPP4 inhibition increased CD8 proliferation, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity in vitro. | DPP4 inhibition up‑regulated mitochondrial respiratory capacity and glycolysis, linked to GAD1 activation. | In orthotopic GBM mouse models, sitagliptin prolonged survival, an effect abolished by CD8 depletion. | Global DPP4 knockout mice recapitulated the pharmacologic phenotype, confirming target specificity.

talk_title: Molecular mechanisms of differentiation therapy and arsenic in acute promyelocytic leukemia
moa_summary: ATRA binds the RARα moiety of the PML‑RARα fusion, converting it from a transcriptional repressor to an activator, while arsenic binds a cysteine triad in the PML moiety, inducing SUMO‑dependent ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of PML‑RARα and restoration of PML nuclear bodies, leading to p53 activation, senescence, and terminal differentiation of leukemic promyelocytes.
key_findings: ATRA alone induces differentiation but rarely cures APL; arsenic alone cures ~2/3 of patients; the combination cures virtually all patients. | Both agents directly degrade the PML‑RARα oncoprotein via distinct ubiquitin‑proteasome pathways (UBR5 for ATRA, SUMO‑dependent ligase for arsenic). | Restoration of PML nuclear bodies after treatment correlates with p53 activation and loss of self‑renewal, essential for cure in mouse models. | Mutations in the arsenic‑binding cysteine triad confer resistance, confirming the binding site’s importance. | PML is required for response to conventional chemotherapy in non‑APL leukemias, indicating a broader role in therapy response.

talk_title: Apoptotic priming as a determinant of chemotherapy sensitivity across solid and liquid cancers
tumor_types: acute lymphoblastic leukemia; diffuse large B‑cell lymphoma; follicular lymphoma; testicular germ cell tumor; high‑grade serous ovarian cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; glioblastoma; medulloblastoma; rhabdomyosarcoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cell_death; apoptosis; BH3_profiling; preclinical; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Differential apoptotic priming, measured by BH3 profiling, determines how readily cancer cells undergo mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization upon chemotherapy; hematologic and pediatric CNS cancers are highly primed and thus chemo‑sensitive, whereas most solid tumors are unprimed and require inhibition of multiple BCL‑2 family members to trigger death.
key_findings: Blood cancers and testicular germ cell tumors are intrinsically more primed for apoptosis than most solid tumors, correlating with clinical chemosensitivity. | Baseline BH3 profiling priming scores predict faster CA125 normalization and longer PFS in ovarian cancer patients. | CRISPR knockout of Bax/Bak in HeLa cells reduces priming and confers broad chemoresistance, confirming causality. | Solid tumor cell lines require simultaneous inhibition of multiple pro‑survival BCL‑2 proteins (BCL‑XL, MCL‑1) for cell death, unlike hematologic lines. | Neonatal muscle‑derived rhabdomyosarcomas retain higher Bax/Bim levels, are more primed, and respond better to vincristine; BCL‑XL degrader DT2216 synergizes to improve response.

talk_title: NF1 as a microtubule‑repair factor and predictor of maytansinoid ADC efficacy in HER2‑positive breast cancer
tumor_types: HER2-positive breast cancer; medulloblastoma; rhabdomyosarcoma; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; lung cancer
topic_categories: ADC; microtubule_targeting; biomarker_development; mechanism_of_action; preclinical; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: NF1 binds directly to polymerized microtubules, accelerates tubulin polymerization and promotes self‑repair of intra‑microtubule damage; loss of NF1 impairs this repair, creating more drug‑binding sites for maytansinoid payloads (DM1) on exposed protofilament ends, thereby sensitizing NF1‑deficient cells to T‑DM1 ADCs.
key_findings: NF1 loss does NOT affect Ras signaling in this context but dramatically increases sensitivity to the maytansinoid ADC T‑DM1. | Re‑expression of wild‑type NF1 rescues mitotic timing defects and reduces DM1 sensitivity, confirming specificity. | NF1 directly binds microtubules and accelerates polymerization; in vitro, NF1 counteracts DM1‑induced polymerization block. | NF1‑deficient cells display impaired microtubule self‑repair, leading to increased DM1 binding sites and heightened cytotoxicity. | Clinical cohort analysis shows NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients have significantly longer PFS on T‑DM1.
primary_population: NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients on T‑DM1

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Statistical considerations for early cancer detection test performance
moa_summary: Bob Smith explained that sensitivity and specificity for single-cancer early detection tests differ from multi-cancer tests, highlighted the concept of program sensitivity, and argued that MCEDs have lower aggregate sensitivity but broader public health impact, emphasizing the need for contextual interpretation of performance metrics.
key_findings: Program sensitivity can equalize comparisons between frequent low-cost tests and less frequent high-cost tests. | Sensitivity and specificity can move together when test quality improves, contrary to the assumed inverse relationship. | MCED aggregate sensitivity is lower than single-cancer tests, but modeling suggests annual MCED screening could prevent 17% of cancer deaths. | Stage shift is a blunt endpoint; within-stage tumor size changes can drive survival benefits. | False positive rates for MCEDs are often mischaracterized; a 1% rate reflects the complement of PPV at biopsy.

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Defining target condition and cancer signal origin in MCED tests
moa_summary: Nima Visvade argued that the target condition for MCEDs should be defined by clinically actionable cancer signal origin (CSO), emphasizing biologic parity and diagnostic pathway grouping to improve workup efficiency and clinical utility.
key_findings: Target condition for MCEDs is inseparable from cancer signal origin, which guides diagnostic pathways. | Methylation‑based MCEDs provide accurate CSO predictions that are clinically useful for directing workup. | Biologic families (e.g., HPV‑related tumors) may be more appropriate grouping than strict organ boundaries. | Pathway‑based grouping aligns workup burden with clinical benefit, allowing a single imaging study to evaluate multiple CSOs. | High CSO accuracy transforms MCEDs from vague screening signals to actionable diagnostic starting points.

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Generalizability and implementation considerations for MCED and single‑cancer tests
moa_summary: Asma Shukat highlighted how trial enrichment (age, stage, risk) inflates performance metrics of early detection tests and advocated for age‑sex adjustment, detailed subgroup reporting, and likelihood ratios to improve generalizability to real‑world populations.
key_findings: Trial populations often enrich for older age, higher stage, or known cancers, leading to inflated sensitivity and PPV. | Healthy‑volunteer bias can produce lower prevalence than intended use, underestimating PPV. | Age and sex standardization to the US census modestly shifts sensitivity and specificity, as shown in the PREEMPT CRC cohort. | Granular reporting of performance by cancer type, stage, lesion size, and advanced precursor lesion subcategories is needed but limited by power. | Likelihood ratios and number‑needed‑to‑screen are valuable metrics for clinicians to assess post‑test probability.

talk_title: Consumer wearable data for functional monitoring and outcome prediction in cancer patients
affiliation: University of Pittsburgh and UPMC Hillman Cancer Center
tumor_types: breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer; solid tumor
