moa_summary: The study demonstrates that continuous consumer‑wearable metrics (step count, peak gait cadence, activity fragmentation) can be reliably collected during active cancer treatment and are associated with clinical outcomes such as readmission, fall risk, and overall survival, providing prognostic information beyond clinician‑rated performance status.
key_findings: Patients undergoing chemotherapy wore Fitbits ≥8 h/day on 70% of study days over 90 days, with no decline over time. | Older patients showed higher adherence than younger patients; non‑white participants had significantly lower adherence. | Higher inpatient step counts after cytoreductive surgery (≈1,250 steps/day) were associated with lower 60‑day readmission versus 500 steps/day in readmitted patients. | Peak gait cadence ≥100 steps/min predicted reduced fall risk in older cancer survivors and differentiated fallers in ROC analysis. | In a 2.5‑year follow‑up of 213 chemotherapy patients, peak gait cadence and activity fragmentation remained independent predictors of overall survival after adjusting for age, stage, cancer type, ECOG, and step count. | Clinicians reported increased confidence in decision‑making when provided with wearable‑derived data alongside standard clinical information.

talk_title: Wearable sensors and AI for digital trials across disease domains
moa_summary: By leveraging large‑scale passive wearable data (resting heart rate, activity, sleep) combined with AI algorithms, the presented digital trials demonstrate early detection of infectious disease, risk stratification for pre‑diabetes, and the feasibility of fully remote, multimodal clinical studies across diverse health conditions.
key_findings: A wearable‑based algorithm achieved AUC 0.8 for early COVID‑19 detection in a 40,000‑person cohort. | In the Detect Ahead trial, delivering wearable‑generated alerts did not significantly increase testing adherence, highlighting behavioral barriers to action. | A multimodal classifier integrating wearable, CGM, EHR, saliva, and stool data distinguished normoglycemic from type 2 diabetic individuals with AUC 0.96. | The same classifier generated a continuous risk score that refined risk stratification among pre‑diabetic participants beyond HbA1c alone. | Remote digital trials enrolling up to 1,000 participants were executed without in‑person visits, demonstrating scalability of fully virtual study designs.

talk_title: Integrating wearable sensor data and AI into oncology practice
tumor_types: lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; breast cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Wearable‑derived continuous physiologic streams (steps, heart rate, sleep) combined with AI models can augment traditional oncology assessments, improving prediction of adverse events, treatment toxicity, and health‑care utilization, while highlighting the need for patient adherence and health‑system integration.
key_findings: Only <1% of Medicare cancer patients had billed remote patient monitoring codes, indicating poor system adoption. | AI‑driven coaching achieved a 5% body‑weight reduction in breast cancer patients, but overall adherence to wearable wear time declined over study duration. | Multimodal models integrating wearable, PRO, lab, and imaging data reached AUC 0.86 for predicting urgent care visits, outperforming EHR‑only models. | Physician‑patient performance‑status assessments show high variability; AI analysis of movement videos can provide more objective scores. | Patients receiving subcutaneous PD‑1 therapy at home showed higher compliance with vital‑sign uploads than with step‑count data, underscoring task‑specific adherence challenges.
primary_metric: Remote patient monitoring billing rate in Medicare cancer patients

talk_title: AI‑Guided Discovery and Targeting of Plexin B2 in Circulating Tumor Cell Clusters
moa_summary: AI‑driven ranking (R‑score) identified plexin B2 as a top adhesion molecule driving homotypic and heterotypic CTC cluster formation; neutralizing antibodies block plexin B2, reducing cluster size and number, decreasing lung metastasis in mice, and correlating with improved patient outcomes.
key_findings: R‑score prioritized plexin B2 among 30 adhesion proteins as the top CTC‑cluster driver. | Genetic knockout of plexin B2 in 4T1 cells reduces lung micro‑metastases by ~70% (p<0.001). | Anti‑plexin B2 antibodies decrease both homotypic tumor‑tumor and heterotypic tumor‑myeloid clusters in vitro. | Single‑dose anti‑plexin B2 treatment in BALB/c mice cuts metastatic burden by 60% (p<0.01). | High plexin B2 expression in patient CTC clusters associates with worse distant metastasis‑free survival.

talk_title: SWALL‑1 Controls Epigenetic Methylation, Mitochondrial Metabolism, and Antitumor Immunity in IDH‑Mutant Gliomas
moa_summary: SWELL1 forms the LRRC8A‑C VRAC complex that mediates passive export of the oncometabolite D‑2HG from IDH‑mutant glioma cells; loss of SWELL1 reduces extracellular D‑2HG, enhances cGAS‑STING activation, increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, and suppresses tumor proliferation via hyper‑methylation of MYC/SOX2 pathways and impaired mitochondrial respiration.
key_findings: SWELL1 knockdown decreases extracellular D‑2HG release by ~40% without affecting LDH release. | LRRC8C knockdown reduces D‑2HG release ~25%; LRRC8D knockdown increases release, indicating subunit specificity. | SWELL1‑KO IDH‑mut glioma xenografts show prolonged mouse survival (median 45 vs 30 days, p=0.004). | SWELL1 loss leads to increased microglial CD86⁺ activation and CD8⁺ T‑cell IFN‑γ⁺ frequency. | SWELL1‑KO cells accumulate intracellular D‑2HG (~2‑fold) and display increased H3K27me3 and DNA hyper‑methylation, down‑regulating MYC and SOX2.

talk_title: Lineage Plasticity Drives Antibody‑Drug Conjugate Resistance in Bladder Cancer
moa_summary: Lineage plasticity driven by ASCL1/NEUROD1 reprogramming suppresses NECTIN‑4 expression, rendering enfortumab vedotin ineffective; variant histology tumors up‑regulate alternative surface antigens such as DL3, offering new ADC targets for combination therapy.
key_findings: ASCL1/NEUROD1 overexpression in HT1376 cells reduces NECTIN‑4 mRNA by 70% and abolishes EV‑mediated cytotoxicity. | Spatial transcriptomics shows NECTIN‑4 loss confined to neuroendocrine regions, while DL3 is up‑regulated there. | Patients with NECTIN‑4‑low variant histology have significantly shorter EV treatment duration. | Proposed combination of NECTIN‑4 ADC with DL3‑targeted ADC could address heterogeneous antigen expression.

talk_title: eIF4A1 Inhibition Suppresses AML via Serine Biosynthesis Blockade
moa_summary: Zoledronate inhibits eIF4A1, destabilizing YB‑1‑bound mRNAs such as PHGDH, leading to reduced serine biosynthesis, impaired oxidative phosphorylation, and AML cell death; combined with MCL1 inhibition yields synergistic cytotoxicity.
key_findings: eIF4A1 is the most overexpressed translation initiation factor in AML vs normal hematopoietic cells. | eIF4A1 knockout prolongs survival in immunocompetent AML mouse model (median 60 vs 38 days, p=0.003). | Zoledronate kills AML cell lines with nanomolar IC50 while sparing normal CD34⁺ cells. | PHGDH down‑regulation mediates serine depletion; overexpression rescues growth inhibition. | CRISPR screen identifies YB‑1 and MCL1 as top sensitizers; zoledronate + MCL1 inhibitor shows >2‑fold synergy (CI<0.5).

talk_title: Targeting Rho1/2 to Overcome ADC Resistance in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer
moa_summary: Anti‑RHO1/2 antibodies bind surface oncogenic embryonic proteins re‑expressed in TNBC stem‑like cells, suppressing stemness signatures, NF‑κB signaling, and NRF2‑driven antioxidant response, thereby sensitizing cells to the redox‑modulating agent APR‑246.
key_findings: RHO2+ sorted TNBC cells generate tumors at 5,000 cells; RHO2‑ cells fail to engraft. | Anti‑RHO2 treatment reduces NF‑κB p‑p65 and NQO1 levels in vitro. | Combination of anti‑RHO2 antibody with APR‑246 lowers APR‑246 IC50 by 2.5‑fold in TNBC cell lines. | In vivo, anti‑RHO2 alone modestly slows tumor growth; combined with APR‑246 yields significant tumor regression (p<0.01).

talk_title: Potentiating venetoclax response in AML with the NAMPT inhibitor OT-82
moa_summary: OT-82 inhibits NAMPT, reducing NAD biosynthesis and creating a metabolic vulnerability in venetoclax‑resistant AML cells; combined with venetoclax it restores apoptotic sensitivity and yields durable tumor regressions in diverse AML PDX models.
key_findings: OT-82 showed the lowest LC50 among tested AML drugs across 10 adult AML samples, with a wide therapeutic window versus normal bone marrow cells. | In pediatric AML PDXs (PRR485, AML49), OT-82 alone extended event‑free survival, and the combination with venetoclax further improved survival without added benefit from azacitidine. | Across 16 heterogeneous AML PDXs, the venetoclax + OT‑82 combination produced the most robust and durable responses, achieving maintained complete remissions. | Response was observed irrespective of adverse genomic features (TP53, FLT3, KMT2A rearrangements), suggesting broad applicability.

talk_title: Non‑canonical CAR T cell states associated with durable responses in multiple myeloma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; CAR_T; single_cell; tumor_microenvironment; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Single‑cell multi‑omics of BCMA CAR T therapy revealed that durable responders harbor proliferative, early‑memory CAR T cells and a unique ‘T persistor’ state marked by AP‑1 and NF‑κB activation, low exhaustion, and bone‑marrow residency, which correlates with sustained CAR T persistence and improved clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Durable responders showed enrichment of proliferating and early‑memory CAR T cells early post‑infusion, whereas transient responders displayed higher CD8 exhaustion signatures. | A novel ‘T persistor’ CAR T state emerged at later time points in durable responders, characterized by high AP‑1, NF‑κB, cytokine (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α, IL‑2) expression and CXCR4/CD69 bone‑marrow residency markers. | Clonal tracking demonstrated that T persistor cells arise from multiple early CAR T states, including both CD4 and CD8 subsets. | Effective CAR T therapy reshaped the bone‑marrow microenvironment, reducing immunosuppressive monocyte signatures in durable responders.
primary_value: median >12 mo for durable responders, <12 mo for transient responders
primary_population: multiple myeloma receiving BCMA CAR T

talk_title: KatTCIP molecular glue recruiting P300/CBP to BCL6 induces death in DLBCL
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; molecular_glue; targeted_therapy; epigenetics; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: TCIP3 is a bivalent molecular glue that simultaneously binds the BCL6 BTB domain (via BI‑3812) and the P300/CBP bromodomain (via GNE‑781), inducing a cooperative ternary complex that recruits acetyltransferase activity to BCL6 loci, leading to localized histone and BCL6 acetylation, transcriptional activation of cell‑death genes, and rapid apoptosis of BCL6‑dependent DLBCL cells.
key_findings: TCIP3 forms a cooperative ternary complex between BCL6 and P300/CBP, confirmed by ITC and X‑ray crystallography. | TCIP3 induces potent GFP reporter activation and apoptosis in high‑BCL6 DLBCL lines with an IC50 of 0.8 nM, outperforming BCL6 degraders. | ChIP‑seq shows selective recruitment of p300 and increased H3K27ac at BCL6 target sites without global acetylation loss. | In vivo, TCIP3 depletes germinal‑center B cells and drives near‑complete tumor regression in a DLBCL xenograft model with minimal toxicity.

talk_title: FGFR1 inhibition remodels fibroblast‑derived TGF‑β niche to enhance CAR‑T efficacy in DLBCL
topic_categories: immunotherapy; CAR_T; tumor_microenvironment; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; translational
moa_summary: FGFR1 inhibition in cancer‑associated fibroblasts suppresses TGF‑β production and induces MCP chemokine secretion, leading to recruitment of macrophages and enhanced infiltration of CAR‑T cells; the remodeled microenvironment synergizes with anti‑CD19 CAR‑T to eradicate resistant DLBCL in vivo.
key_findings: FGFR1 inhibition reduced CAF‑derived TGF‑β and simultaneously increased CCL2/7/8 expression, driving macrophage recruitment. | Macrophage depletion partially reversed the anti‑lymphoma effect of FGFR1 inhibition, indicating a contributory role of macrophages. | Combination of FGFR1 inhibitor with anti‑CD19 CAR‑T markedly increased circulating CAR‑T cells and eliminated tumor burden in a resistant DLBCL PDX model. | FGFR1 inhibition did not impair normal hematopoiesis in treated mice, suggesting a therapeutic window.
primary_value: significant reduction with FGFR1 inhibitor + CAR‑T vs CAR‑T alone

talk_title: Condensate dynamics drive adaptive METTL3 inhibitor resistance
tumor_types: acute myeloid leukemia; pancreatic cancer; melanoma
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; epigenetics; RNA_modification; resistance_mechanisms; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: METTL3 inhibition reduces m6A on oncogenic transcripts (MYC, BCL2) but induces adaptive up‑regulation and nuclear condensate formation of the m6A reader YTHDC1; gel‑like YTHDC1 condensates sequester residual m6A‑modified RNAs, restoring translation of survival genes and conferring resistance, which can be overcome by YTHDC1 inhibition or venetoclax co‑treatment.
key_findings: CRISPR screens identified YTHDC1 as a top sensitizer to METTL3 inhibition; its loss synergizes with METTL3 inhibitors in AML and melanoma. | METTL3 inhibition enlarges YTHDC1 nuclear condensates, shifting them to a less liquid, gel‑like state correlated with resistance. | Overexpression of YTHDC1 or PABPN1 disrupts condensate dynamics and confers resistance; YTHDC1 inhibition restores sensitivity. | Combining a YTHDC1 inhibitor or venetoclax with METTL3 inhibition yields strong synergistic anti‑tumor effects with minimal impact on normal CD34+ cells.
primary_value: synergistic reduction with YTHDC1 inhibitor + METTL3 inhibitor

talk_title: Lymphotoxin‑α induces TRAF2 degradation and necroptotic death in AML
moa_summary: Recombinant lymphotoxin‑α engages TNFR1 on AML cells, leading to depletion of the pro‑survival adaptor TRAF2, which shifts TNFR1 signaling toward necroptotic complex formation and cell death, while sparing normal hematopoietic progenitors that retain higher TRAF2 levels.
key_findings: Genetic deletion of LTα in FLT3‑ITD AML mice accelerates disease onset, indicating a tumor‑suppressive role for LTα. | Exogenous LTα induces rapid death of primary AML blasts, including high‑risk TP53‑mutated samples, and promotes differentiation (loss of CD34, gain of CD38). | LTα treatment eradicates leukemia in both syngeneic and immunodeficient PDX models without causing cytopenia or impairing normal hematopoiesis. | TRAF2 is markedly down‑regulated in AML patient samples across genetic subtypes, rendering leukemic cells uniquely sensitive to LTα‑mediated necroptosis.
primary_value: no leukemia development up to 300 days with LTα treatment

talk_title: Integrating multi-omics and novel modalities to map EGFR TKI resistance in NSCLC
affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Oncology
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; biomarker_development; spatial_biology; AI_ML; resistance_mechanisms; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Tan described how sequential EGFR TKIs select for on‑target (e.g., C797S) and off‑target alterations (MET amplification, lineage plasticity) and how multi‑omics (DNA, RNA, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics) can map these states. He showed that ctDNA persistence, MET amplification, and stromal TME signatures predict resistance, and that rational combinations (e.g., EGFR TKI + MET inhibitor, EGFR TKI + chemotherapy, anti‑CD47) can be matched to specific resistance phenotypes.
key_findings: EGFR TKIs achieve high initial ORR (60‑80%) but 50‑60% of patients on frontline third‑gen inhibitors lack any detectable resistance mechanism by NGS. | On‑target resistance mutations (e.g., C797S) occur in 10‑25% of resistant biopsies, while MET amplification is found in 15‑20% and predicts response to MET inhibitors (4/9 partial responses). | Persistent EGFR ctDNA after TKI therapy identifies a high‑risk subgroup with median PFS 5.2 mo vs 9.8 mo (p<0.01). | Spatial transcriptomics revealed CD47 up‑regulation in on‑treatment lesions, suggesting a combinatorial anti‑CD47 strategy. | Stromal‑rich TME signatures (activated neutrophils, CAFs) correlate with 2‑year relapse rates of 48% vs 22% (p=0.004), defining a poor‑risk population for upfront combination trials. | Retrospective analysis of RET inhibitor trials showed ORR >30% and median PFS 18.4 mo, yet phase III trials were terminated due to enrollment challenges.

talk_title: Eco-evolutionary lens for understanding the tumor microenvironment
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Cancer Ecology Center
moa_summary: Pienta presented an eco‑evolutionary framework in which tumors are viewed as ecosystems that undergo niche construction, creating steep fitness gradients (hypoxia, acidosis, immune pruning) that accelerate clonal selection and drive convergent lethal phenotypes such as metastasis and therapy resistance. He emphasized that cytokine‑driven “cancer swamp gas” (e.g., IL‑6, TNF‑α, MMPs) from the microenvironment underlies systemic lethal effects.
key_findings: Convergent evolution in cancer leads to two lethal phenotypes: metastasis and therapy resistance. | Ecological engineering by cancer cells (allogenic and autogenic) remodels the tumor microenvironment, accelerating clonal dominance (e.g., 73% reduction in barcode diversity in vivo vs. in vitro). | Steep fitness gradients from hypoxia, acidosis, and immune pruning create rapid clonal selection, observable across precancerous lesions to metastatic disease. | Cytokine overproduction (IL‑6, TNF‑α, MMPs) from stromal and immune cells constitutes a “miasma” that drives cachexia, thrombosis, and organ failure, accounting for mortality beyond tumor mass. | Integrating evolutionary ecology principles could enable new therapeutic timing and selective pressure strategies to disrupt the tumor ecosystem.

talk_title: AI‑enabled virtual patient modeling and tumor microenvironment analysis
moa_summary: Poon described three AI‑derived capabilities—microscope, crystal ball, and wind tunnel—to decode tumor microenvironment complexity. He highlighted AI‑driven virtual trial simulation (TrialScope) that reproduces KEYNOTE‑10 outcomes, a pathology foundation model (GigaPath) that predicts EGFR mutation from H&E across 41 cancer types, a modality translator (GigaTime) that synthesizes spatial proteomics from routine slides, and a large‑scale virtual patient model (Curiosity) trained on 300 M patients and 100 B medical events that matches supervised baselines on 78 clinical tasks.
key_findings: TrialScope AI can simulate a virtual trial that reproduces the KEYNOTE‑10 pembrolizumab results, demonstrating feasibility of real‑world data‑driven trial emulation. | GigaPath transformer model scales to gigapixel whole‑slide images and learns a pan‑cancer signature across 41 tumor types, enabling EGFR mutation prediction directly from H&E with high accuracy. | GigaTime translates cheap H&E pathology into virtual multiplex immunofluorescence (MIF), generating over 300 000 virtual MIF maps from 14 000 patients and revealing spatial proteomic patterns linked to outcomes. | Curiosity, a 1‑billion‑parameter foundation model trained on 300 M patient journeys (100 B events), matches or exceeds 78 supervised clinical task models without task‑specific fine‑tuning. | AI can automate extraction of eligibility windows from unstructured clinical notes, reducing abstraction cost from hours to seconds and enabling 24/7 trial feasibility monitoring.

talk_title: Single-cell clonal analysis of normal skin and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma evolution
affiliation: Co-PI, Precancer Atlas of Skin Cancer, National Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Shane's work combines single‑cell clonal expansion and bulk sequencing to show that early loss‑of‑function NOTCH and TP53 mutations persist in normal‑appearing keratinocytes without driving overt proliferation, but they create a mutator phenotype that accelerates UV‑induced DNA damage, leading to high‑burden clones that can give rise to actinic keratosis and, when additional driver events (TERT promoter, CDKN2A loss, MAPK activation) occur, to invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.
key_findings: Normal‑appearing keratinocytes can harbor up to eight pathogenic driver mutations (NOTCH1/2, TP53, FAT1, PBRM1) yet remain histologically benign. | Pathogenic‑mutant keratinocytes exhibit a 40‑fold higher mutation burden than neighboring wild‑type cells, indicating a mutator phenotype. | Only ~10% of actinic keratosis lesions adjacent to SCC are genetically related; most represent collision tumors. | Progression to SCC is associated with acquisition of TERT promoter mutations, CDKN2A loss, and MAPK pathway activation (KRAS hotspot). | In a 45,000‑case national cohort, loss‑of‑function mutations in SWI/SNF (ARID2), MAPK activation, and TGF‑β pathway alterations predict metastatic outcome.

talk_title: Spatial and 3D atlases of breast and prostate tumor evolution and bone metastasis
moa_summary: Ding's team integrates 2D and 3D spatial transcriptomics (Visium, Xenium, Codex) with computational pipelines (MORF, Calico ST, Mushroom) to map spatial subclones and their microenvironmental interactions during pre‑cancer to cancer transition in breast and prostate, and to dissect tumor‑osteoclast/osteoblast crosstalk in bone metastases, revealing FGFR2/ACKR3‑mediated homing, AR‑driven programs, and distinct immune niches that may guide combination anti‑angiogenic and macrophage‑targeted therapies.
key_findings: Spatial subclones can be resolved in 2D and 3D; LDHB mutation maps to a distinct subclone in breast tissue. | Bone metastases show tumor‑specific upregulation of ACKR3 (CXCL12 receptor) in breast and AR in prostate, suggesting organ‑specific homing mechanisms. | Prostate bone metastases display increased osteoblast signatures (BMP signaling, SPP1‑CD44), while breast bone metastases show osteoclast activation (PDGF‑β, TGF‑β‑ITGB1). | Both metastasis types exhibit heightened fibroblast (MYCAF) and vascular (VEGF‑A/KDR, FGFR2, JAK1/Notch3‑4) signaling, indicating potential combinatorial anti‑angiogenic targets. | Immune profiling reveals macrophage enrichment toward tumor cores and reduced T‑cell infiltration; breast bone mets have IFN‑γ⁺ macrophages, prostate mets have SPP1⁺ immunosuppressive macrophages.

talk_title: Multi‑omic atlases of multiple myeloma precursors and early immunotherapy interventions
moa_summary: Ghobrial's group integrates whole‑genome, bulk RNA, and single‑cell BCR‑RNA profiling of MGUS, smoldering, and overt multiple myeloma to derive a genomic MM‑like score and a transcriptional MST score that predict progression; spatial proteomics (IMC) of decalcified bone marrow reveals cellular neighborhoods where tumor‑immune interactions (e.g., granzyme B CD8 TEMs, SPP1‑positive macrophages) correlate with outcomes and CAR‑T or bispecific therapy response, supporting early immunotherapy interception.
key_findings: A 14‑gene MM‑like genomic score distinguishes MGUS‑like from myeloma‑like precursor cases. | Six transcriptional signatures, especially beta‑2M, predict progression independent of DNA alterations. | The MST transcriptional score accurately stratifies risk across MGUS, smoldering, and overt MM cohorts. | Spatial IMC identifies tumor‑immune cellular neighborhoods that predict CAR‑T efficacy and cytokine‑release syndrome risk. | Early bispecific antibody (teclistamab) treatment in high‑risk smoldering myeloma yields 100% MRD‑negative responses with favorable safety.

talk_title: Fundamentals of Deep Learning for Digital Pathology
affiliation: University of Chicago, Department of Medical Oncology and Computational Science
moa_summary: Pearson described the architecture of convolutional neural networks and vision transformers applied to whole slide H&E images, and introduced foundation models that learn a language-like embedding from 1.5 million slides (120,000 patients). These embeddings can be fine‑tuned for downstream tasks such as mutation prediction, tumor detection, and segmentation, while highlighting bias sources (e.g., ancestry prediction from TCGA site effects) and mitigation strategies (color normalization, batch correction, synthetic image explainability).
key_findings: Trained a pathology foundation model on 1.5 million H&E slides from 120 k patients, generating a reduced‑dimensional feature vector for each image. | Demonstrated that foundation models can achieve high accuracy on tasks such as ancestry prediction, but identified spurious site‑related biases in TCGA data. | Showed that synthetic GAN‑generated images can be used to train pathologists to recognize molecular patterns (e.g., BRAF‑like vs RAS‑like) improving human prediction performance. | Presented an FDA‑cleared breakthrough‑designated AI tool for Trop2‑ADC companion diagnostics, illustrating clinical translation of digital pathology AI.

talk_title: Implementation of AI Models for Pathology: Prospective Validation and Clinical Deployment
moa_summary: Cambiamalla presented a prospectively validated AI model that infers EGFR mutation status directly from H&E whole‑slide images, allowing the model to act as a non‑exhaustive computational biomarker; by setting high‑sensitivity or high‑specificity thresholds the workflow can safely triage patients away from rapid PCR testing, reducing unnecessary assays by 43% without loss of diagnostic performance.
key_findings: Developed an end‑to‑end EGFR prediction pipeline using a foundation model fine‑tuned on >80,000 H&E slides from MSK Impact. | Internal and external validation showed no performance drop compared with the original research cohort. | Prospective silent trial demonstrated a 43% reduction in rapid EGFR PCR tests while maintaining equivalent PPV and NPV to the gold‑standard workflow. | Independent third‑party validation confirmed robust performance across institutions. | Proposed scaling the silent‑trial framework to multi‑center deployments for additional lung cancer biomarkers and low‑resource settings.
primary_population: lung adenocarcinoma patients undergoing molecular accession

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Spatial Multi‑Omics: From 2D Tissue Maps to 3D Biomarkers
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: Wang described deep‑learning pipelines that align H&E images with spatial transcriptomics and proteomics at single‑cell resolution, register serial sections into 3D tumor maps, and extract spatial niches (e.g., CAF subtypes, macrophage phenotypes, vascular structures, TIS) which can be quantified and linked to clinical outcomes such as immunotherapy response.
key_findings: Generated multimodal 3D maps of human tumors comprising >2 million cells, revealing spatial gradients of CXCL13 and CXCL5 signals. | Identified four distinct CAF spatial organizations that correlate with patient survival. | Discovered two macrophage phenotypes (TIM‑2⁺ immunosuppressive vs CXCL9⁺ immune‑active) with divergent spatial distributions across tumor cores and peripheries. | Developed Omni‑TIS framework that quantifies mature vs immature tumor‑infiltrating stromal niches and predicts response to checkpoint blockade in renal cell carcinoma. | Demonstrated that 3D reconstruction of TIS structures changes perceived volume and may improve biomarker robustness compared with 2D counts.

talk_title: Transcription factor subtypes and DLL3 bispecific T‑cell engager tarlatamab resistance mechanisms in small cell lung cancer
affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute, Lowe Center for Thoracic Oncology
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; biomarker_development; liquid_biopsy
moa_summary: Tarlatamab is a DLL3‑CD3 bispecific T‑cell engager that induces tumor cell killing in DLL3‑high ASCL1 SCLC. Resistance emerges via a transcriptional switch to a NEUROD1 subtype, leading to DLL3 down‑regulation and impaired T‑cell activation, with NEUROD1 overexpression causally driving early resistance in mouse models.
key_findings: Tarlatamab achieved a 35‑40% ORR and 7‑9 month DOR in second‑line SCLC (n=46). | ASCL1 transcriptional subtype correlated with response; 80% of ASCL1 patients responded. | NEUROD1 up‑regulation and DLL3 loss were observed at acquired resistance in both patients and mouse models. | CRISPR‑mediated DLL3 knockout abrogated tarlatamab binding and killing in syngeneic models. | NEUROD1 overexpression in tumor cells caused early resistance to tarlatamab in vivo.

talk_title: Antibody‑drug conjugates targeting DLL3, CES6, B7H3 and other surface antigens in small cell and high‑grade neuroendocrine cancers
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Thoracic Section Chief
tumor_types: small cell lung cancer; high‑grade neuroendocrine cancers
topic_categories: ADC; targeted_therapy; cancer_vaccines; clinical_trial; translational; biomarker_development
moa_summary: ABBV‑706 is an ADC linking a cytotoxic Top1 payload to antibodies against surface antigens DLL3, CES6 or B7H3, delivering DNA‑damage–inducing payloads selectively to SCLC and high‑grade neuroendocrine tumors, with SLFN11 expression enhancing payload efficacy.
key_findings: CES6‑targeted ADC (ABBV‑706) produced a ~60% ORR in relapsed SCLC and high‑grade neuroendocrine cancers (n=127). | Responses were rapid, often within one week of treatment initiation. | SLFN11 positivity correlated with higher response rates, suggesting a predictive biomarker for ADC payload sensitivity. | ADC activity observed across diverse neuroendocrine sites (small cell bladder, cervix, Merkel cell carcinoma). | Preclinical PDX models (≈400) recapitulated clinical efficacy and supported biomarker development.
primary_population: CES6 ADC treated SCLC/high‑grade neuroendocrine patients

talk_title: Neuronal activity drives proliferation and plasticity of small cell lung cancer brain metastases and primary lung tumors
affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Assistant Professor
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; neuroscience; basic_science; translational; combination_therapy; other
moa_summary: Neuronal activity, via glutamatergic and especially depolarizing GABAergic signaling, induces depolarization of SCLC cells, promoting proliferation, invasion, and a transcriptional shift toward a neuroD1‑like phenotype; direct tumor depolarization recapitulates these effects, and anti‑seizure drug levetiracetam suppresses tumor growth in brain metastasis models.
key_findings: Optogenetic activation of premotor cortical circuits increased SCLC brain tumor proliferation and invasion in mice. | SCLC cells receive depolarizing GABAergic currents due to high intracellular chloride, driving growth. | Co‑culture with GABAergic neurons boosted proliferation of all tested SCLC lines; effect blocked by gabazine or tetrodotoxin. | Direct optogenetic depolarization of SCLC cells in vivo accelerated tumor burden, confirming depolarization as the driver. | Vagotomy reduced lung tumor initiation and extended survival in a GEM SCLC model. | Levetiracetam treatment markedly decreased tumor burden and proliferation in multiple SCLC brain metastasis models.

talk_title: Quantitative fluorescence assay for HER2 and Trop-2 to guide ADC selection
affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Department of Pathology and Medicine
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; platform_technology
moa_summary: Reim presented a CLIA‑validated quantitative fluorescence assay that converts HER2 and Trop‑2 protein levels on breast cancer slides into attomoles per mm², enabling continuous, low‑CV measurement. Retrospective analysis showed that higher HER2 abundance predicts longer time to next treatment with trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, while unexpectedly high Trop‑2 levels correlate with poorer response, supporting a selective biomarker approach for ADC choice.
key_findings: Quantitative fluorescence assay (Troplex) achieves inter‑ and intra‑assay CV <10% and is NYS‑DOH approved. | Only ~6% of breast cancer biopsies are true HER2 negatives by the quantitative assay, versus higher rates by conventional IHC. | 71% of IHC HER2‑zero cases have measurable HER2 by the quantitative assay, indicating under‑detection by standard IHC. | Patients with HER2 levels above the median have a median time to next treatment of 12.8 months versus 7.5 months for those below median. | High Trop‑2 expression is associated with shorter time to next treatment and poorer response to T‑DXd, suggesting a negative predictive role.
primary_population: HER2 high (above median) breast cancer patients treated with T-DXd

talk_title: Computational pathology and multimodal AI for ADC response prediction
affiliation: CentraleSupélec, University of Paris‑Saclay, Applied Mathematics
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; AI_ML; computational_biology; spatial_biology; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Bakolopoulou described an unsupervised deep‑learning pipeline that segments IHC or H&E slides, extracts patch representations, clusters them, and links cluster composition to ADC response, revealing that regions enriched for fibroblasts/immune cells correlate with resistance, while tumor‑cell dense regions associate with sensitivity; she also introduced counterfactual‑augmented attention mechanisms to improve interpretability of supervised models.
key_findings: In the DAISY trial, an unsupervised cluster enriched for fibroblast and immune cells was linked to HER2‑ADC resistance. | Applying the same pipeline to ICARUS breast and lung trials identified clusters whose cell density and HER2/DAB intensity predicted response. | A counterfactual‑augmented attention model improved region‑level interpretability without sacrificing predictive performance on TCGA lung and breast datasets. | Integrating H&E‑derived features with IHC data enhanced response prediction compared to using either modality alone. | Foundation models pretrained on large H&E datasets can generate useful representations for ADC response prediction, even with limited trial data.

talk_title: Revisiting the neoantigen approach to cancer immunotherapy
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; CAR_T; TCR_T; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Samuels demonstrated that inhibiting translation fidelity via TYW2 knockout induces programmed ribosomal frameshifts, generating abundant out‑of‑frame peptides that are presented on MHC and elicit strong CD8‑T‑cell responses; chemotherapy can further boost neoantigen presentation, and recurrent driver‑derived neoantigens can be identified through HLA‑matched population databases for vaccine or TCR‑based therapies.
key_findings: Chemotherapy (lymphodepleting) synergizes with KRAS G12V‑specific TCRs by increasing neoantigen presentation. | TYW2 knockout in melanoma cells produces a programmed increase in out‑of‑frame peptides, leading to CD8‑dependent tumor rejection in mice. | Ribosome profiling revealed phenylalanine‑codon pausing and both –1 and +1 frameshifts in TYW2‑KO cells. | Identified >350,000 patients per year with recurrently presented neoantigens using a data‑driven HLA‑matched pipeline. | Bacterial‑derived peptides and translation‑infidelity peptides expand the antigenic repertoire beyond somatic mutations.

talk_title: Pharmacologic inhibition of PMS2 to induce mismatch repair deficiency
topic_categories: DNA_damage_repair; targeted_therapy; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; platform_technology
moa_summary: The small‑molecule 1867 covalently binds cysteine‑73 in the ATPase domain of PMS2, selectively disabling its mismatch‑repair activity; treated cells acquire MSI‑high status, high mutational burden, and become sensitive to PD‑1 blockade, recapitulating the phenotype of genetic PMS2 loss without cytotoxicity.
key_findings: 1867 selectively engages PMS2 cysteine‑73 with >99% specificity among >20,000 cysteines. | Pharmacologic PMS2 inhibition phenocopies genetic knockout, abolishing 6‑guanine‑induced DNA nicks. | Dose‑dependent induction of MSI‑high and mutational signatures (SBS21, SBS15) in vitro. | 1867‑treated tumor cells reject in immunocompetent mice when combined with anti‑PD‑1, but not in immunodeficient hosts. | Combination of 1867 with FOLFOXIRI accelerates MSI conversion and can induce tumor regression without checkpoint blockade.

talk_title: Chemically inducing mismatch repair deficiency to sensitize tumors to PD‑1 blockade
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; melanoma; head‑neck cancer; renal cell carcinoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; DNA_damage_repair; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Temozolomide induces DNA methylation and MSH2 promoter silencing, while cisplatin adds DNA cross‑links; together they rapidly down‑regulate MSH2, generate a mismatch‑repair‑deficient mutational signature rich in frameshift indels, elevate TMB and MSI, and render tumors highly sensitive to PD‑1 blockade in preclinical models and a subset of patients.
key_findings: Temozolomide alone raises TMB but not MSI; cisplatin alone modestly increases frameshifts. | The combination yields a dramatic TMB gain (~125 mut/Mb) and high MSI scores with predominantly subclonal frameshift mutations. | MSH2 promoter methylation drives loss of MSH2 expression after 4–8 weeks of combo treatment. | Combo‑treated cells implanted into immunocompetent mice show delayed growth and complete regression with anti‑PD‑1, unlike single‑agent controls. | In a clinical trial, patients who acquired both high TMB and MSI signatures after therapy had longer survival, though the primary endpoint was not met.

talk_title: Elucidating Broad Cell-Cell Interactions via 3D Super Cell Graphs for Prostate Cancer Risk Stratification
moa_summary: The Skell 3D framework groups phenotypically similar, spatially adjacent cells into super cells, constructs super cell graphs, and extracts interaction features that predict five-year biochemical recurrence in prostate cancer with higher AUC and reduced computational cost compared to standard cell-level graphs.
key_findings: Skell 3D achieved AUC 0.8 for five-year BCR prediction in a 76-patient cohort, outperforming 3D morphology alone (AUC 0.68). | Integration of Skell 3D features increased C-index from 0.64 to 0.61 in survival analysis. | Super cell graphs reduced computation time by ~25-fold relative to standard cell-level graphs. | Ablation studies identified optimal super cell neighbor threshold (15 µm) and interaction distance (20 µm). | Super cell approach showed robustness to simulated Gaussian noise compared with cell-level graphs.

talk_title: AI-Driven Spatial Analysis of 1,100 Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma Patients' H&E Slides Uncovers Three Tumor Subtypes with Distinct Microenvironments and Survival
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: A deep learning model predicts spatial transcriptomic gene expression from H&E slides, enabling clustering of tumors into immune-activated and immune-exhausted spatiotypes; these spatiotypes robustly stratify survival and predict therapy response across multiple head and neck cancer cohorts.
key_findings: Model accurately predicts spatial expression of ~2,800 genes from H&E images, capturing cancer hallmark pathways. | Clustering of predicted spatial transcriptomics identifies two spatiotypes (immune-activated vs immune-exhausted) with distinct survival outcomes. | Immune-exhausted spatiotype shows worse overall, progression-free, and disease-specific survival across TCGA, Hancock, and Taiwan cohorts. | Spatial cluster-based model predicts treatment response with AUC 0.74 and odds ratio ~5 in a 94-patient immunotherapy cohort. | Model performance correlates with number of genes robustly predicted; external validation confirms reproducibility.

talk_title: Pan-Cancer Prediction of Response to Immune Checkpoint Blockade from Histopathology
tumor_types: pan-cancer; melanoma; NSCLC; head and neck; colorectal; others
topic_categories: immunotherapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; AI_ML; biomarker_development
moa_summary: A composite tumor hotness signature (TAHMAC) derived from immune score, inflammation score, and TMB in melanoma is applied pan‑cancer to classify hot vs cold tumors; Path2Omic deep learning infers gene expression from H&E, enabling hotness and ICB response prediction directly from pathology with performance comparable to measured transcriptomics.
key_findings: TAHMAC signature achieved AUC 0.98 for hot/cold classification across 11 diverse cancer cohorts despite being derived from melanoma. | TAHMAC outperformed 26 existing ICB response signatures with AUC 0.74 on 22 transcriptomic cohorts. | Path2Omic accurately inferred ~6,000 genes per cancer type, with TAM genes among the most reliably predicted. | Using inferred expression, tumor hotness prediction reached mean AUC 0.96 across eight TCGA cancers. | ICB response prediction from inferred expression achieved AUC 0.72 on nine external cohorts, surpassing direct slide‑to‑response models.
primary_metric: AUC for tumor hotness prediction (measured expression)

talk_title: A Large Scale Multi-Target Deep Learning Model for Virtual Genomic Profiling in Colorectal Cancer
topic_categories: genomics; AI_ML; diagnostics; clinical_trial; translational; platform_technology
moa_summary: A transformer-based deep learning model processes tiled H&E images to predict whole-exome sequencing mutations (including MSI, BRAF, KRAS) and infer molecular recurrence risk, demonstrating that morphological features can capture pathway-level alterations and correlate with ctDNA-defined outcomes.
key_findings: Single-task models achieved AUC >0.7 for >80% of 463 genes in a 45,000-patient cohort. | Multitask training improved performance for 95% of genes, with ~40% gaining ≥5% AUC. | MSI prediction reached AUC 0.98, BRAF V600E AUC 0.90+, and KRAS AUC 0.85+. | Patients with model-predicted BRAF V600E but wild-type sequencing showed similar poor survival, suggesting pathway-level phenotype capture. | H&E‑based risk model stratified MRD‑negative patients into high/low risk, adding prognostic value beyond ctDNA alone.
primary_metric: AUC for individual gene mutation prediction

talk_title: Peritumoral Collagen Microarchitecture is Differentially Prognostic of Biochemical Recurrence Across Populations in Prostate Cancer
affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology / Emory University, PhD Student
moa_summary: A deep learning collagen segmentation model extracts structural, spatial, and textural features from peritumoral regions of prostatectomy slides; African American‑specific collagen signatures strongly predict biochemical recurrence, whereas European American signatures do not, highlighting population-specific tumor microenvironment differences.
key_findings: Nine collagen features derived from African American training set stratified recurrence risk in the whole test set (HR 4.09, p=0.02). | The same AA features also stratified risk within the AA test subset but failed in the EA test subset. | European American-derived features did not achieve significant prognostic power in any cohort. | Only one feature (Haralick entropy variability) overlapped between AA and EA signatures, indicating distinct collagen architectures. | Texture entropy and GLCM entropy were significantly higher in AA tumors compared to EA.
primary_metric: Hazard ratio for AA-derived collagen signature

talk_title: PathIO: Deep Learning Pathomics Framework for Personalized Immunotherapy Selection and Outcome Prediction in Metastatic NSCLC
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Postdoctoral Fellow
topic_categories: immunotherapy; clinical_trial; AI_ML; biomarker_development; translational; combination_therapy
moa_summary: PathIO uses a pathologist-annotated tissue habitat map from H&E slides, aggregates multi-scale ROI embeddings, and trains a random survival forest to generate a risk score that independently predicts overall and progression-free survival in metastatic NSCLC, correlating with immune cell infiltration and guiding monotherapy versus combination immunotherapy selection.
key_findings: PathIO risk score significantly stratifies OS and PFS across four independent metastatic NSCLC cohorts. | Risk score remains an independent predictor after multivariate adjustment for clinical factors. | Low-risk patients exhibit higher immune cell density and lower tumor cell density on multiplex IF. | In PD-L1‑high patients, low-risk scores predict benefit from ICI monotherapy, whereas high-risk scores suggest added chemotherapy benefit. | Combining PathIO with CT‑based radiomics and clinical variables improves prognostic performance over any single modality.
primary_metric: Hazard ratio for OS (high vs low PathIO risk)

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: From Persistence to Relapse: Evolution of Drug Resistance in Lung Cancer
affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School
moa_summary: Hata demonstrated that drug‑tolerant persister cells in EGFR‑ or ALK‑mutant NSCLC up‑regulate APOBEC3A, leading to increased APOBEC signature mutagenesis, DNA damage, and acquisition of resistance mutations such as EGFR T790M, C797S, or ALK E1210K. Knock‑down of APOBEC3A suppresses mutagenesis and prevents emergence of resistant clones in vitro and in xenografts, implicating therapy‑induced APOBEC activity as a driver of multi‑step resistance evolution.
key_findings: Drug‑tolerant persister NSCLC cells up‑regulate APOBEC3A within 1‑2 days of targeted therapy exposure. | APOBEC3A induction correlates with increased APOBEC signature (SBS2/13) mutations and structural variations in persister clones. | APOBEC3A knock‑down eliminates therapy‑induced APOBEC mutations, reduces double‑strand DNA breaks, and abolishes outgrowth of resistant persister populations in vitro and in vivo. | Patient tumor sequencing reveals enrichment of APOBEC signatures in resistant lesions that were absent pre‑treatment, supporting clinical relevance.

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: Therapeutic Strategies to Address Cancer Cell Heterogeneity and Plasticity
topic_categories: immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; plasticity; combination_therapy; basic_science; translational
moa_summary: Marine introduced a morphology‑based “Origami” platform that quantifies cell surface area and volume to map melanoma cell states. He showed that the trametinib + palbociclib (TP) combination drives a uniform adaptive trajectory toward high‑volume, lysosome‑rich cells; these become vulnerable to nilotinib‑induced ferroptosis. Parallel high‑surface cells can be sensitized by ER‑Golgi inhibitors (e.g., brefeldin A). The quadruple TP + nilotinib + brefeldin A (TPNB) regimen eradicates heterogeneous persister populations in vitro and yields curative responses in mouse models, also enhancing anti‑PD‑1 efficacy via immunogenic cell death.
key_findings: Morphometric profiling (cell surface vs volume) distinguishes four melanoma transcriptional states without omics. | TP (trametinib + palbociclib) induces a uniform high‑volume, lysosome‑rich persister phenotype across 20 cell lines. | High‑volume persisters are selectively killed by nilotinib, a ferroptosis‑sensitizing agent; effect is rescued by ferroptosis inhibitors. | High‑surface persisters are sensitized by ER‑Golgi inhibitors (e.g., brefeldin A), which reduce surface area and enhance TP efficacy. | The quadruple combination TP + nilotinib + brefeldin A (TPNB) eliminates persisters in vitro and produces >1‑year disease control, with curative outcomes in some mouse models. | TPNB also converts refractory tumors to anti‑PD‑1 responders by inducing immunogenic cell death (HMGB1 release, antigen presentation).

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: The Hidden Lives of Cancer Cells: Somatic Evolution, Cellular Plasticity, and the Detection of Residual Disease
affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine and New York Genome Center
topic_categories: spatial_biology; single_cell_multiomics; phylogenetics; liquid_biopsy; biomarker_development; basic_science; translational
moa_summary: Landau presented a phylogenetic framework that reconstructs high‑resolution tumor trees from single‑cell methylome or whole‑genome data, enabling measurement of heritability versus plasticity of cell states. By annotating leaves with transcriptomic phenotypes, he showed that stem‑like, astrocytic, and mesenchymal states in glioblastoma are heritable, while transition probabilities reveal lineage‑specific intermediates. Parallelly, he described whole‑genome ctDNA analysis using AI‑enhanced error suppression and ultra‑deep sequencing to quantify tumor burden (parts‑per‑million) and detect mutational signatures (APOBEC, tobacco) without tumor‑informed panels, providing early MRD detection and treatment monitoring.
key_findings: Methylation‑derived epimutations enable reconstruction of patient‑specific phylogenies from a single tumor sample. | Stem‑like, astrocytic, and mesenchymal GBM states are heritable across cell divisions, as shown by high Moran's I values. | Transition probability analysis reveals a required astrocytic intermediate for stem‑to‑mesenchymal conversion. | In IDH‑mutant gliomas, differentiation follows a unidirectional hierarchy; IDH‑wt GBM shows bidirectional plasticity with dedifferentiation. | Smart PTA scaling to 3,500 cells allows tracing normal esophageal clones back to conception, revealing long‑lived TP53‑mutant clones without pathology. | Whole‑genome ctDNA sequencing with AI error suppression achieves ppm‑level sensitivity, enabling detection of tumor‑derived mutational signatures (APOBEC, tobacco) without a tumor‑informed assay. | Early ctDNA dynamics predict immunotherapy response; steroid use blunts ctDNA‑driven benefit, highlighting clinical utility.

session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_title: MRD Directed Treatment Strategies in Colorectal Cancer
topic_categories: clinical_trial; biomarker_development; liquid_biopsy; early_detection; targeted_therapy; immunotherapy; combination_therapy
moa_summary: Tee reviewed how postoperative ctDNA serves as a highly sensitive MRD biomarker in colorectal cancer, stratifying patients into high‑risk (ctDNA‑positive) and low‑risk (ctDNA‑negative) groups. ctDNA‑guided trials (DYNAMIC, DYNAMIC‑3) demonstrated that chemotherapy can be safely omitted in ctDNA‑negative stage II patients without compromising 5‑yr RFS, and that ctDNA‑positive patients exhibit heterogeneous outcomes based on molecular burden. Persistent ctDNA after adjuvant therapy predicts poor RFS, highlighting a need for escalated or novel therapies, while ctDNA clearance may serve as an early surrogate endpoint for regulatory approval.
key_findings: Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts near‑certain recurrence across all CRC stages. | In DYNAMIC (stage II), ctDNA‑guided management halved chemotherapy use (28%→15%) with non‑inferior 5‑yr RFS (88.3% vs 87.2%). | In DYNAMIC‑3 (stage III), ctDNA‑negative de‑escalation reduced oxaliplatin use (89%→35%) but missed the non‑inferiority margin (−2.8% difference, CI crossed −7.5%). | Higher ctDNA molecular burden (>0.38 mut/mL) identifies a subgroup with 3‑yr RFS of only 23% versus 77% for low burden. | Persistent ctDNA after adjuvant therapy correlates with 3‑yr RFS of 14% versus 79% for cleared patients, suggesting ctDNA clearance as a surrogate efficacy marker. | ALTTEAR trial (TAS‑102) showed no survival benefit despite modest ctDNA clearance, indicating cytotoxic rechallenge alone may be insufficient for ctDNA‑positive disease.

talk_title: Lymphoma Transformers: Spatial Total RNA Sequencing of FFPE Tissue
moa_summary: Fang's group developed a spatial deterministic barcoding (DIBER+) workflow that adds poly‑tails to fragmented RNA in FFPE tissue, enabling unbiased total‑RNA sequencing (coding and non‑coding species) at near single‑cell resolution. The method captures spatial gene expression, chromatin accessibility, histone marks, SNVs, and metabolic activity (via stimulated Raman imaging), allowing reconstruction of clonal phylogeny, detection of microRNA signatures (e.g., miR‑21, miR‑155) and metabolic lipid droplet patterns that correlate with lymphoma transformation.
key_findings: Developed a poly‑tailing strategy that yields higher sensitivity on fragmented FFPE RNA than fresh‑frozen spatial transcriptomics. | Achieved near‑single‑cell resolution spatial maps of coding and >1,800 microRNAs in lymphoma tissue. | Detected de‑novo SNVs and copy‑number alterations from spatial total‑RNA data, enabling clonal phylogeny reconstruction within a single FFPE section. | Identified metabolic lipid‑droplet redistribution (nuclear/perinuclear) associated with transformation to diffuse large B‑cell lymphoma. | Found miR‑155 up‑regulation linked to PI3K/AKT pathway activation, suggesting a potential biomarker for PI3K‑inhibitor response.

talk_title: Spatial Multi‑omics Dissects Immune and Stromal Heterogeneity in Angioimmunoblastic T‑cell Lymphoma
moa_summary: Sakata‑Yanagimoto’s team combined single‑cell RNA‑seq, Xenium spatial transcriptomics, and multiplex digital spatial profiling to map 10 blood‑endothelial cell (BEC) subclusters in AITL, revealing a novel capillary‑activated high‑endothelial venule (cHEV1) that colocalizes with follicular dendritic cells and exhausted TIM‑3⁺ T cells, forming a cHEV‑FDC niche that predicts poor therapeutic response and overall survival.
key_findings: Identified 10 BEC subclusters, with chHEV2 and TIP cells significantly expanded in AITL versus normal lymph nodes. | cHEV1 (capillary‑activated HEV) localizes closest to high‑tumor‑density regions and forms a cHEV‑FDC niche enriched for exhausted TIM‑3⁺ T cells. | Peri‑vascular index of exhausted T cells around cHEV1 is higher in non‑responders to therapy, linking the niche to treatment resistance. | High expression of cHEV and HEV signatures in bulk RNA‑seq cohorts correlates with worse overall survival. | Integrin‑related genes are up‑regulated in AITL‑associated BECs, suggesting a role in niche formation.

talk_title: MMEJ deficiency in Ewing sarcoma creates synthetic lethality with HR inhibition
moa_summary: The EWS-FLI1 fusion protein in Ewing sarcoma disrupts splicing of POLQ exon 25 via loss of the EWSR1 splicing complex, leading to POLQ deficiency and MMEJ impairment. These MMEJ-deficient cells become hyper‑dependent on homologous recombination, rendering them exquisitely sensitive to HR inhibition by CDK12 inhibitors.
key_findings: EWS-FLI1 expression causes exon 25 skipping of POLQ mRNA, abolishing POLQ protein in Ewing sarcoma. | Knockout of EWSR1 or its splicing partners (FKBP1, KHSRP) reproduces POLQ loss and MMEJ deficiency. | Restoration of POLQ rescues MMEJ activity, confirming causality. | Ewing sarcoma cell lines with POLQ deficiency are hypersensitive to CDK12 inhibition, a synthetic lethal interaction. | Exon 25 skipping of POLQ is observed in 100% (36/36) of evaluated patient tumors, providing a potential biomarker.

talk_title: Replication gap biology defines sensitivity to PARP and FEN1 inhibition
moa_summary: BRCA1 loss leads to accumulation of 53BP1 on nascent DNA, mis‑positioning FEN1 and causing persistent replication gaps. PARP1 also positions FEN1; loss of both BRCA1 and PARP1 traps FEN1 at the fork, abolishing its activity and creating synthetic lethality. 53BP1 loss removes both pathways, rendering cells resistant to PARP and FEN1 inhibitors.
key_findings: BRCA1 deficiency causes 53BP1 accumulation on nascent DNA and delayed FEN1 recruitment. | PARP1 loss mimics the BRCA1 phenotype, indicating parallel pathways for FEN1 positioning. | Combined BRCA1 loss and PARP inhibition prevent FEN1 from reaching the replisome, explaining PARP inhibitor sensitivity. | 53BP1 knockout eliminates both PARP‑ and BRCA1‑dependent FEN1 positioning, conferring resistance to both PARP and FEN1 inhibitors. | Replication gap signal measured by nick translation predicts sensitivity to PARP/FEN1 inhibition across multiple models.

talk_title: Clinical landscape of PARP inhibition and combination strategies across tumor types
tumor_types: prostate cancer; breast cancer; ovarian cancer; pancreatic cancer; endometrial cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; gastrointestinal tumors; neuroendocrine tumors; biliary cancer; bladder cancer; liver cancer; gastric cancer
topic_categories: clinical_trial; immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; DNA_damage_repair; biomarker_development
moa_summary: PARP inhibitors exploit synthetic lethality in BRCA1/2‑deficient tumors by trapping PARP on DNA and preventing repair of single‑strand breaks, while combination with immune checkpoint blockade leverages increased mutational load and DNA‑damage‑induced immunogenicity. Emerging data show that ATM or CHEK2 mutations confer variable benefit, and ATR or CDK12 inhibition provides an alternative synthetic lethal strategy in ATM‑ or ARID1A‑deficient cancers.
key_findings: PARP inhibitor plus enzalutamide improves overall survival in metastatic prostate cancer (HR 0.8). | Patients with HRD‑positive prostate cancer derive a larger OS benefit (HR 0.5) compared with HR‑proficient patients. | BRCA2‑mutated prostate cancer shows the greatest PFS and OS improvements; BRCA1 benefit is modest. | ATM‑mutated prostate cancer shows limited or no OS benefit from PARP inhibition alone. | Combination of niraparib with pembrolizumab yields a 24% ORR in platinum‑resistant ovarian cancer, including HRD‑negative patients. | KEYLINK‑007 demonstrates durable responses (>30 months) across multiple tumor types with HRR mutations or HRD scores >16. | ATR inhibition (serolazertib) produces durable responses in ARID1A‑mutant solid tumors, highlighting a new synthetic lethal avenue.

talk_title: Oral endocrine therapy adherence in hormone receptor‑positive breast cancer
affiliation: University of North Carolina Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, Department of Breast Oncology
moa_summary: Long‑term oral endocrine therapy reduces recurrence and mortality in HR+ breast cancer, but suboptimal adherence—especially among younger Black women—diminishes these benefits, as shown by dose‑response relationships between medication possession ratio and survival outcomes.
key_findings: Only ~60% of women remained ≥80% adherent to endocrine therapy over five years in an integrated health system. | Black women under 65 showed lower initiation and lower medication possession ratios compared with White women, especially after chemotherapy. | Distinct adherence trajectories (steady decline vs early decline with recovery) were identified, suggesting heterogeneous patient experiences. | Non‑adherent patients (<95% MPR) had a two‑fold higher risk of relapse after adjusting for prognostic factors. | GETSET trial (N=1,165) accrued and demonstrated high engagement with motivational‑interviewing arms (92% attended ≥1 session).
primary_population: Kaiser breast cancer patients over 5 years

talk_title: Improving adherence to oral therapies in colorectal cancer
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Medical Oncology
topic_categories: clinical_trial; adherence; liquid_biopsy; combination_therapy; precision_medicine; other
moa_summary: Oral agents such as regorafenib, encorafenib, and capecitabine provide modest survival gains in colorectal cancer but are limited by toxicity; tailoring dose intensity via patient‑reported outcomes, pharmacogenomics (DPYD), and rapid ctDNA profiling improves tolerability and enables earlier use of effective oral targeted combos, thereby enhancing adherence and outcomes.
key_findings: Regorafenib 160 mg daily improved OS by 1.4 months versus placebo but caused frequent grade 3+ toxicities. | Personalized dose‑escalation of regorafenib reduced toxicity without compromising survival. | Aligning Lonserf dosing with bevacizumab reduced neutropenia and growth‑factor use while preserving OS. | Frontline encorafenib + cetuximab (BREAKWATER) doubled OS compared with standard chemotherapy in BRAF‑mutated mCRC. | Rapid (<5 days) ctDNA testing at registration enables same‑day identification of BRAF, KRAS, HER2, and DPYD alterations to guide oral therapy selection.

talk_title: Adherence to oral 6‑mercaptopurine in pediatric acute lymphoblastic leukemia
moa_summary: Consistent oral 6‑MP dosing maintains therapeutic leukocyte 6‑TGN levels; sub‑optimal adherence reduces systemic exposure, leading to a two‑fold increase in relapse risk in pediatric ALL, especially among adolescents and lower‑income families.
key_findings: Median adherence was 95% initially but fell to ~90% over six months. | Adolescents (<12 y vs ≥12 y) and low‑income households showed significantly lower adherence. | Patients with adherence <95% had a two‑fold higher relapse risk after adjusting for prognostic factors. | Forgets were the predominant self‑reported barrier (≈80% of missed doses). | A multicomponent intervention (education + web‑based scheduling + text reminders + caregiver supervision) improved adherence by 4% overall and 9.5% among baseline non‑adherers.
primary_value: 95% at start, declining to 90% by 6 months

talk_title: Five‑year update on FDA Project Optimus implementation and case examples
affiliation: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Oncology Center of Excellence
moa_summary: Project Optimus reforms oncology dose selection by shifting from maximum tolerated dose to a totality‑of‑evidence approach that integrates PK/PD, safety, early efficacy, and dose‑response data, using randomized dose comparisons to identify optimized doses before approval.
key_findings: Case examples in advanced lung cancer (ADC, bispecific T‑cell engager, small‑molecule kinase inhibitors) all selected lower doses as the FDA‑labeled recommended dose after randomized dose evaluations. | Randomized dose assessments were feasible even for accelerated‑approval programs, improving safety and benefit‑risk for patients.

talk_title: Randomization backfill and expansion cohorts to enhance Phase 1 trials
moa_summary: Adaptive Bayesian model‑based designs with randomization, backfill, and expansion cohorts enable more efficient dose‑finding by providing early efficacy and safety signals across multiple dose levels and disease subgroups.
key_findings: Randomized dose expansion can augment data at lower dose levels without increasing overall sample size, potentially saving 3–6 patients per trial. | Probabilistic stopping rules based on posterior dose‑toxicity probabilities improve decision‑making compared with traditional 3+3 designs.

talk_title: Incorporating patient‑reported outcomes into dose‑finding trials
moa_summary: PRO‑CTCAE data are integrated with traditional CTCAE grading to define tolerability thresholds (e.g., 35% severe patient‑reported toxicity) and inform dose assignment using a PRO‑augmented continual reassessment method (TIDE‑CRM).
key_findings: A PRO‑based dose‑selection rule (e.g., 35% severe PRO toxicity) can lead to lower recommended doses than clinician‑only DLT thresholds. | Software tools (University of Virginia PRO‑CRM and a custom web app) enable real‑time dose assignment using combined PRO and CTCAE data.

talk_title: Industry perspective on five‑year experience with Project Optimus
moa_summary: Industry implementation of Project Optimus emphasizes early dose optimization using adaptive designs, exposure‑response modeling, and post‑marketing studies to refine doses, aiming to reduce PMR/PMC burdens and improve long‑term tolerability.
key_findings: Historical examples (e.g., gemtuzumab ozogamicin, cabazitaxel, ceritinib, sotorasib) illustrate that lower doses can achieve comparable efficacy with better safety, often after post‑marketing studies. | Project Optimus has spurred increased use of Bayesian adaptive designs, PK/PD integration, and randomized dose comparisons in early development.

talk_title: Epidemiology, risk factors, and therapeutic considerations in early‑onset colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Yang highlighted the rising incidence of early‑onset colorectal cancer, the lack of distinct molecular signatures, and discussed how anatomic location influences treatment sequencing, dose intensification, and radiation decisions, emphasizing the need for fertility, psychosocial support, and research into microbiome, ctDNA‑guided intensification, and lifestyle interventions.
key_findings: Early‑onset CRC incidence is rising ~3% annually, projected 159,000 new cases in 2026 with 16% occurring before age 50. | Mortality is projected at 56,000 in 2026, making early‑onset CRC a leading cause of cancer death in <50‑year-olds. | No unique somatic mutation profile distinguishes early‑onset from average‑onset CRC across four large datasets. | Sexual dysfunction affects 58% of male and 36% of female early‑onset CRC survivors; pain (31%) and anxiety/depression (70%) are also common. | Microbiome signatures (e.g., SBS88) are enriched in patients <40 y and associate with APC mutations, suggesting a potential biomarker for future studies.

talk_title: Precision medicine framework for pediatric and young adult survivorship
topic_categories: survivorship; clinical_trial; biomarker_development; epigenetics; endocrinology; cardiology
moa_summary: Mustafi described how germline variants (e.g., CBR3 GG) modulate anthracycline metabolism, increasing cardiomyopathy risk, and how epigenetic age acceleration links treatment exposure to multisystem late effects, proposing precision survivorship using genetic, epigenetic, and lifestyle data to guide preventive interventions.
key_findings: Anthracycline cardiotoxicity shows a dose‑response; risk escalates >100 mg/m², with CBR3 GG carriers experiencing toxicity at lower doses. | Retinoic acid receptor gamma variants up‑regulate TOP2B, heightening anthracycline susceptibility. | SULT4A1 GG genotype confers marked cardiotoxicity risk at standard anthracycline doses. | Epigenetic age acceleration is significantly higher in survivors and predicts cardiometabolic disease, frailty, and mortality. | Survivors have a >2‑fold higher diabetes incidence; abdominal radiation dose‑dependently damages pancreatic β‑cells, especially in African ancestry with alkylator exposure.

talk_title: Institutional scale genomic profiling and basket trial strategy for young‑onset and rare cancers
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; clinical_trial; platform_technology; genomics; basket_trial; precision_oncology
moa_summary: The speaker described MSK Impact's large‑scale DNA/RNA profiling enabling pan‑cancer identification of actionable alterations (e.g., HER2 amplification, NTRK fusions) and the deployment of basket trials (neratinib, trastuzumab‑deruxtecan) to match rare‑tumor patients with targeted therapies, illustrating variable efficacy across lineages due to mutation type and co‑mutations.
key_findings: MSK Impact has sequenced >109,000 tumors (162,000 samples), including >10,000 patients ≤40 y. | HER2‑amplified acral melanoma responded dramatically to trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, achieving a durable complete response. | Neratinib basket trial showed ~50% ORR in HER2‑mutant breast cancer but no responses in HER2‑mutant bladder cancer, highlighting lineage‑specific drug sensitivity. | RNA‑seq identified high HER2 expression in a lung cancer patient who achieved a complete response to trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, supporting RNA‑based enrollment criteria. | Somatic mosaicism (e.g., ERCC2, ATRX) was detected in young patients with multiple primary tumors, underscoring the need for comprehensive genomic profiling.
primary_metric: overall response rate (breast cancer arm, neratinib)

talk_title: Birth cohort analysis of early-onset cancer incidence across multiple tumor types
affiliation: American Cancer Society, Senior Principal Scientist
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; breast cancer; thyroid cancer; pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; lung cancer; cervical cancer
moa_summary: Using age‑period‑cohort modeling of national cancer registry data, the speaker demonstrated generational shifts in incidence, showing that cohorts born after 1955 have ~50% higher risk of colorectal cancer and similar increases for ER‑positive breast and endometrial cancers, while other cancers show period effects; disparities by education and geography were highlighted.
key_findings: Over 9,000 people under age 50 are diagnosed with cancer worldwide each day, with >3,000 deaths. | People born in 1990 have ~1.5‑fold higher incidence of small intestinal, thyroid, kidney, and pancreatic cancers compared with those born in 1955. | Cohort rate ratio for colorectal cancer indicates a ~50% higher lifetime risk for those born in the 1980s versus the 1950s. | Early‑onset colorectal cancer mortality is higher among men with lower education, with the gap widening over 30 years. | In high‑income countries, early‑onset cancer rates rise while older‑adult rates fall; in Latin America and Asia, rates rise in both age groups. | Lung cancer incidence declines in younger male cohorts but plateaus or rises in younger female cohorts, reflecting tobacco‑control disparities.

talk_title: Metabolic dysfunction and early‑onset colorectal cancer: epidemiology to prevention
affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Gastroenterology and Epidemiology
moa_summary: Large prospective cohorts (NHS1, NHS2, HPFS) were leveraged to link obesity, early‑life adiposity, ultra‑processed food intake, and hyperinsulinemia to increased incidence of early‑onset colorectal adenomas and cancer, and a single‑arm tirzepatide trial is underway to test reversal of metabolic risk.
key_findings: Mid‑life obesity (BMI ≥30) confers a 2‑fold higher risk of early‑onset colorectal cancer compared with non‑obese individuals. | BMI at age 18 in the overweight range is associated with a 1.6‑fold increased risk of early‑onset colorectal cancer. | Maternal weight gain >14 kg during pregnancy is linked to a ~2‑fold higher colorectal cancer risk in offspring. | Highest quintile of ultra‑processed food intake is associated with a 1.5‑fold higher incidence of colorectal adenoma. | Predicted hyperinsulinemia (C‑peptide‑based lifestyle index) shows a 1.3‑fold increased early‑onset adenoma risk, especially in later birth cohorts.

talk_title: Early‑life mutagenesis by colibactin‑producing bacteria drives early‑onset colorectal cancer
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Professor of Molecular Medicine
moa_summary: Whole‑genome sequencing of 1,000 colorectal cancers revealed enrichment of colibactin‑associated signatures (SBS88, ID18) in early‑onset cases; phylogenetic timing shows these mutations arise within the first five years of life, often generating early driver events such as APC mutations, implicating early‑life exposure to pks+ bacteria as a causal factor.
key_findings: Colibactin signatures (SBS88/ID18) are present in ~50% of patients <40 y versus ~17% of patients >70 y. | Early‑onset tumors show a three‑fold enrichment of colibactin signatures compared with late‑onset tumors. | Phylogenetic analysis of normal crypts indicates colibactin exposure occurs within the first 0‑5 years of life. | In >25% of colibactin‑positive early‑onset tumors, the APC driver mutation bears the colibactin signature. | pks colonization is not required to be detectable at tumor sampling, suggesting a past transient exposure.

talk_title: Postpartum breast cancer: involution biology and liver metastasis risk
affiliation: Oregon Health & Sciences University, Professor of Cancer Biology
moa_summary: Weaning‑induced mammary gland involution creates a wound‑healing, immunosuppressive microenvironment that promotes tumor cell shedding and metastasis; concurrent liver involution generates a pre‑metastatic niche that preferentially supports colonization of ER‑positive tumor cells, explaining the high liver metastasis rate in postpartum breast cancer.
key_findings: Women diagnosed with breast cancer within 5 y postpartum have a 2‑3‑fold worse overall survival than nulliparous peers, independent of ER status. | Postpartum tumors show a distinct gene expression signature matching mouse mammary involution (immune suppression, fibroblast activation, ECM deposition). | Postpartum breast cancers are enriched for liver metastases (≈30% of metastatic sites) compared with nulliparous cases. | Weaning‑induced liver involution in mice creates a fibroblast‑rich, immunosuppressive niche that enhances tumor cell engraftment after portal vein injection. | Human liver metastases from postpartum patients often display replacement growth, integrating into existing hepatic architecture without inducing new angiogenesis.

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: Bridging Science and Market Access in Pediatric Oncology
affiliation: Google Ventures, Entrepreneur in Residence; Pediatric Neuro-Oncologist
moa_summary: Blackman presented a macro‑level analysis of pediatric oncology drug development, comparing the 6‑8% overall success rate of drug candidates to the 6‑8% completion rate of the Camino pilgrimage, and highlighted recent acceleration in FDA approvals driven by incentives, while emphasizing the need for faster trial activation and better market‑access pathways for agents such as the DRD2 antagonist dordivaprone in diffuse midline glioma.
key_findings: Pediatric oncology drug development success mirrors the 6‑8% completion rate of the Camino pilgrimage. | Exponential growth in FDA approvals over the past decade, with 8‑9 agents approved per year and 10‑14 new indications/label expansions annually. | Dordivaprone (ONC201) received FDA approval for progressive diffuse midline glioma in patients >1 year, based on a pooled analysis of 376 patients showing a 22% response rate. | Only two pediatric‑only EMA approvals and five FDA approvals were recorded for the 2025 cohort, underscoring persistent regulatory gaps.

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: ADC Landscape and Emerging Small‑Molecule Opportunities in Pediatric Oncology
affiliation: St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital, Senior Vice President for Clinical Research
moa_summary: Fox outlined the current ADC pipeline for children, highlighting successful CD30‑targeted brentuximab vedotin, the limited pediatric activity of folate‑receptor‑α ADCs (e.g., SUTRO‑002 achieving 30% CRs in a small AML cohort), and the challenges of HER2‑directed ADCs in osteosarcoma where low antigen expression limited efficacy, while also introducing emerging targets such as STEAP2 and B7‑H3 for future pediatric ADC development.
key_findings: Only 2 FDA pediatric‑only approvals (EMA 2, FDA 5) despite 712 registered ADC trials worldwide. | For solid‑tumor ADCs, no actively accruing pediatric trials were identified as of April 2024. | SUTRO‑002 treated 10 children with infant AML; 3 achieved complete responses, demonstrating feasibility of pediatric ADCs. | Trastuzumab deruxtecan screened 50 osteosarcoma patients, 9 enrolled; 1 DLT and 1 prolonged stable disease observed, insufficient to proceed to stage 2. | Emerging targets STEAP2 and B7‑H3 are being prioritized for future pediatric ADC programs.

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: Precision Immunotherapy: CAR T Cells and Bispecific Antibodies in Pediatric Cancer
affiliation: Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Associate Professor of Pediatrics
tumor_types: B‑cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia; AML; neuroblastoma; diffuse midline glioma; osteosarcoma; solid tumor
moa_summary: Leach reviewed the rapid clinical impact of CD19‑directed bispecific blinatumomab and CAR‑T (tisagenlecleucel) in pediatric B‑ALL, achieving up to 96% disease‑free survival when moved to frontline, and discussed next‑generation CAR‑T strategies (humanized, dual‑target CD19/CD22, armored constructs) and bispecifics (DLL3‑targeted tarlatamab) aimed at overcoming antigen escape and extending efficacy to solid tumors such as neuroblastoma and diffuse midline glioma.
key_findings: Blinatumomab achieved 27/70 CRs in relapsed/refractory pediatric ALL and improved DFS/OS in the randomized ALL 1331 trial. | Frontline blinatumomab (ALL 1731) raised 5‑year DFS to 96% versus 88% with chemotherapy alone. | Tisagenlecleucel produced an 81% remission rate and ~60% 3‑year OS in heavily pre‑treated pediatric B‑ALL. | Humanized CD19 CAR‑T showed 98% ORR in CAR‑naïve patients, suggesting reduced immunogenicity. | CD22‑CAR trials report 70‑75% ORR, supporting dual‑target strategies to prevent CD19 loss. | GD2‑CAR in neuroblastoma yielded a 66% ORR, with higher activity in low‑burden disease.
primary_metric: blinatumomab CR rate in phase 1/2 R/R ALL

talk_title: Signaling-to-chromatin pathways and histone phosphorylation in lymphoma
affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Department of Immunology
moa_summary: Signal‑induced phosphorylation of histone H3 at serine 28 creates a transient domain that ejects the PRC2 complex, abolishing its allosteric activation and chromatin condensation activity, thereby converting repressive H3K27me3 domains into permissive transcriptional states in germinal‑center B cells and lymphoma.
key_findings: Serine 28 phosphorylation spreads across several hundred kilobase domains in stimulated macrophages and GC B cells, whereas serine 31 phosphorylation is confined to transcription units. | In ABC DLBCL, H3S28ph is constitutively high and overlaps with PRC2‑mediated H3K27me3 at the MYC locus, defining a ‘methyl‑phos switch’. | Biophysical assays show that H3S28ph‑containing nucleosome arrays are poor substrates for EZH2 and resist PRC2‑driven chromatin condensation. | Ex vivo GC B‑cell stimulation reveals progressive loss of H3K27me3 within H3S28ph domains, accompanied by increased chromatin interactivity and H3K36me2 gain. | Similar histone phosphorylation patterns are observed in T cells (TBET locus) and NK cells (IL15RA), and are constitutive in MYD88/CD79B mutant lymphoma PDX models.

talk_title: ELOVL1‑mediated lipid metabolism as a dependency in NRAS‑mutant IDH‑mutant AML
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Department of Medicine
moa_summary: ELOVL1 catalyzes very‑long‑chain fatty‑acid elongation, feeding sphingolipid/ceramide synthesis that sustains lipid‑raft integrity; in NRAS‑mutant IDH‑mutant AML, loss of ELOVL1 disrupts lipid rafts, attenuates NRAS‑driven MAPK signaling, and selectively impairs leukemia cell viability.
