key_findings: CRISPR screens of epigenetic and metabolic libraries identified ELOVL1 as a selective dependency in Tet/IDH mutant AML cells. | ELOVL1 knock‑out depletes NRAS‑mutant AML cells in vitro and in vivo (bone marrow and peripheral blood) but not WT or RAS‑WT AML. | Pharmacologic inhibition of ELOVL1 with a Vertex compound reproduces genetic dependency, causing selective depletion of triple‑mutant cells. | Catalytic‑dead ELOVL1 fails to rescue the phenotype, confirming requirement for enzymatic activity. | Disruption of sphingolipid biosynthesis (SPTLC1, KDSR) phenocopies ELOVL1 loss, whereas peroxisomal fatty‑acid pathways do not. | Lipid‑raft depletion (MBCD) selectively kills NRAS‑mutant AML, and ELOVL1 loss reduces raft abundance and downstream MAPK/PI3K signaling.

talk_title: Epigenetic priming to enhance T‑cell directed therapies in lymphoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; epigenetics; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Epigenetic agents (HAT activator YF2, HDAC3 inhibitor, EZH2 inhibitor, DNMT inhibitor) remodel chromatin to up‑regulate antigen‑presentation genes (MHC‑I/II, CD20) and reverse exhaustion markers, thereby priming lymphoma cells for enhanced recognition and killing by checkpoint blockade, CAR‑T cells, and bispecific antibodies.
key_findings: YF2 increases HLA‑A/B/C and antigen‑processing gene expression, boosting MHC‑I/II surface levels in lymphoma cell lines and mouse B cells. | Combination of YF2 with anti‑PD‑L1 reduces tumor growth and prolongs survival in A20‑bearing mice, with increased tumor MHC‑I and decreased PD‑1 on infiltrating T cells. | HDAC3 inhibition plus PD‑L1 blockade yields 80% CR in primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma and 57–71% CR in Hodgkin lymphoma, with durable responses. | Tazemetostat priming enhances CAR‑T cell infiltration, synapse formation, and cytokine production, achieving 100% ORR and 77% CR in a 13‑patient lymphoma cohort. | Belinostat + tazemetostat restores CD20 (including epitope‑specific variants) on bispecific‑resistant lymphoma cells, increases MHC expression, and drives T‑cell mediated killing in 3‑D spheroid models.
primary_n: n=?? primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma cohort
primary_population: primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma, checkpoint‑naïve

talk_title: Detecting and Modeling Genetic Epistasis in Cancer Using Conditional Selection
tumor_types: pan-cancer; low-grade glioma; endometrial cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Aranzo presented a statistical framework that uses synonymous mutations to build a neutral null model of mutation, compares expected vs observed non‑synonymous counts to estimate driver selection, and then quantifies conditional selection (epistasis) by stratifying tumors on a split gene and re‑estimating selection for a query gene, implemented in the R package cosalliance.
key_findings: Positive and negative epistasis are pervasive, with 25‑50% of driver mutations affected by positive epistasis across cancer types. | Epistasis networks are modular: genes within a module show synergistic interactions, while modules antagonize each other. | Module‑based mutational profiles predict patient survival better than individual gene mutations. | Early mutations in a module can forecast the eventual molecular subtype of a tumor. | Asymmetric epistatic relationships reveal logical ordering of mutations, e.g., TP53 enhances the effect of RB1 in glioblastoma but not vice versa.

talk_title: Quantifying Epistasis and Evolutionary Trajectories in Cancer
moa_summary: Alfaro-Murillo described a continuous‑time Markov chain model that estimates substitution rates for all possible mutational pathways among a set of genes, fits these rates to observed genotype frequencies, and separates mutation from selection using neutral mutation rate estimates (e.g., NDS, SigMutCel) to derive selection coefficients and epistatic interactions.
key_findings: The model can infer four substitution rates for any gene pair, capturing order‑dependent effects. | In colorectal cancer, KRAS and BRAF show strong antagonistic epistasis, while APC and KRAS display weak or non‑significant interaction. | Selection coefficients derived after correcting for mutation rates reveal that many apparent co‑occurrences are driven by high mutation rates rather than true epistasis. | Statistical tests (likelihood ratio) can formally assess the presence of epistasis and compare epistatic effects across populations.

talk_title: Tobacco Smoking and Somatic Selective Epistasis in Lung Adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Jeff showed that smoking alters both mutation rates and selection coefficients of key lung adenocarcinoma drivers; after deconvolution, KRAS, KEAP1, STK11 and SDK11 have higher selection in ever smokers, while EGFR, PI3K and SMAD4 are stronger drivers in never smokers, and complex gene‑by‑gene‑by‑environment epistatic interactions reshape evolutionary trajectories.
key_findings: Smoking increases mutation rates genome‑wide but the magnitude varies by gene (e.g., ALK, GNAS show especially high rates). | After correcting for mutation rates, KRAS selection is significantly higher in ever smokers, while EGFR selection is negligible in smokers. | Pairwise epistasis is common in ever smokers; many driver mutations (e.g., KRAS, KEAP1) antagonize early genes like TP53, indicating early clonal emergence. | Higher‑order interactions (G×G×E) were identified, e.g., KEAP1 selection strongest in smokers without EGFR mutation, weaker in EGFR‑mutant smokers. | Clinical implications: EGFR TKIs achieve higher response rates in never smokers; co‑mutations (e.g., KRAS, KEAP1) predict poorer outcomes in EGFR‑mutant disease.

talk_title: Foundation model of the tumor genome for drug response prediction and evolution analysis
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Computer Science and Engineering
moa_summary: Ideker presented a self‑supervised foundation model (MutationProjector) that learns embeddings of tumor genomes by masking gene alterations and predicting them using graph‑attention networks informed by protein‑protein, TF‑binding, and synthetic‑lethal networks. The low‑dimensional embeddings capture tumor type and combinatorial mutation patterns, which can be used as features for downstream supervised models (e.g., random forest) to predict drug response, including immunotherapy efficacy.
key_findings: The foundation model organizes tumors by type and reveals key single‑gene and pairwise mutation interactions (e.g., RB1‑TP53, FGFR3‑CDKN2A) driving the embedding. | Embedding coordinates improve immunotherapy response prediction in NSCLC (AUC 0.78) versus traditional biomarkers. | Clonal evolution analysis shows a pseudo‑time trajectory correlating with increasing TP53, APC, and CDKN2A alterations across stages. | Scaling the model to 270,000 genomes further improves performance, indicating data‑limited learning.
primary_population: NSCLC patients treated with anti‑PD‑1/PD‑L1 therapy

talk_title: Aneuploidy burden as a predictive biomarker for colorectal cancer risk in inflammatory bowel disease
affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, London, United Kingdom
moa_summary: Graham demonstrated that widespread copy‑number alterations (aneuploidy) arise in inflamed colonic epithelium of IBD patients, forming large clonal expansions that precede dysplasia. Quantifying the aneuploidy burden in dysplastic lesions or in morphologically normal biopsies predicts future colorectal cancer risk, providing a mechanistic link between chronic inflammation‑driven genomic instability and cancer evolution.
key_findings: Aneuploid clones can spread across the entire colon, covering meters of tissue, without visible dysplasia. | Copy‑number burden is significantly higher in lesions that later progress to cancer versus those that do not (p<0.001). | A high aneuploidy burden in low‑grade dysplasia predicts cancer development with ~85% PPV and NPV. | Aneuploidy in non‑dysplastic surveillance biopsies also predicts future cancer, suggesting a field‑effect biomarker. | Margin analysis after endoscopic resection shows that residual aneuploid clones correlate with subsequent cancer, indicating potential for molecular clearance monitoring.

talk_title: Evolutionary strategies to manage therapeutic resistance in cancer
moa_summary: The speaker outlined evolutionary concepts for reducing cancer evolvability (lowering mutation rate, limiting resources, reducing selection pressure) and for changing selective pressures (drug rotation, alternating opposing therapies, adaptive dosing). He highlighted adaptive therapy—dose modulation based on tumor burden—to maintain competition between sensitive and resistant clones, citing preclinical mouse work and a phase‑II adaptive trial in prostate cancer that doubled time to progression.
key_findings: Maximum tolerated dose accelerates selection for resistance; lower or adaptive dosing can prolong control. | Adaptive therapy in metastatic prostate cancer increased median time to progression from 14.3 to 33.5 months and suggested a ~7‑fold fitness cost of resistance. | Preclinical models show that adding benign selective pressures (multiple drugs) slows evolution of any single resistance. | Integrated pest management heuristics (minimum effective dose, monitoring, rotating modes of action) are proposed as a framework for cancer therapy.

talk_title: Extracellular matrix regulation of metastatic dormancy via DDR1-STAT1 signaling
tumor_types: breast cancer; head and neck cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Dormant disseminated tumor cells secrete collagen III, creating a pro‑quiescent ECM that signals through the collagen receptor DDR1 to activate STAT1, maintaining G0 arrest. Proline hydroxylase P4HA2 stabilizes this collagen matrix; its loss reduces hydroxylated collagens, remodels the ECM, activates mitochondria and LDH4A1‑driven metabolism, thereby awakening dormant cells.
key_findings: Dormant tumor cells produce collagen III‑rich ECM that induces nuclear CDK2 biosensor localization, indicating G0 arrest. | Collagen III up‑regulates DDR1 expression, activating STAT1 signaling to maintain dormancy. | P4HA2 is up‑regulated in dormant cells; knockdown restores proliferation and metastasis in lung colonization assays. | Loss of P4HA2 decreases proline‑hydroxylated collagens (III, I, V) in the tumor microenvironment. | P4HA2‑deficient ECM triggers mitochondrial enlargement, increased basal and maximal respiration, and higher ATP production. | LDH4A1 is up‑regulated in awakened cells, driving NADH production; its knockdown blocks awakening despite P4HA2 loss.

talk_title: SPARC-mediated collagen regulation impacts immunity, autoimmunity and tumor progression
tumor_types: breast cancer; acute myeloid leukemia; CLL; autoimmune disease models
moa_summary: SPARC regulates collagen I/IV deposition, shaping ECM scaffolds that control immune cell localization and activation; loss of SPARC reduces collagen barriers, enhances NETosis, alters B‑cell maturation via CD24‑P‑selectin interactions, and promotes myeloid‑derived suppressor cell expansion, thereby influencing autoimmunity, AML aggressiveness, and breast cancer metastasis.
key_findings: SPARC deficiency impairs germinal center formation and B‑cell maturation due to loss of collagen scaffolding and increased CD24‑P‑selectin‑mediated apoptosis. | In SPARC‑KO mice, neutrophil NETosis is enhanced, leading to increased IL‑6 and growth factor release that drives autoimmunity and CLL‑like disease. | High SPARC expression in breast cancer (ECM3 signature) correlates with collagen‑rich, immune‑cold tumors and increased metastasis. | SPARC‑high tumors produce more GM‑CSF, IL‑6, and CXCL12, recruiting myeloid‑derived suppressor cells; COX‑2 inhibition reduces these cytokines. | Knockdown of fatty‑acid desaturase 5 reduces SPARC secretion, decreasing myeloid suppression and metastasis in a SPARC‑high clone.

talk_title: CHIL1‑neutrophil axis drives ECM remodeling, myoepithelial loss and breast cancer metastasis
moa_summary: STAT3‑driven CHIL1 expression in mammary tumor cells recruits neutrophils, inducing NETosis and neutrophil degranulation; neutrophil elastase degrades laminin and collagen IV, dismantling the myoepithelial basement membrane, permitting tumor invasion and metastasis, while NETs create an immune‑excluded niche.
key_findings: CHIL1 overexpression accelerates lung metastasis in Middle T mice within six weeks. | CHIL1 knockout tumors retain an intact CK14+ myoepithelial layer and fail to metastasize. | Neutrophil depletion (anti‑Ly6G) preserves myoepithelial markers and prevents basement membrane degradation. | Pharmacologic inhibition of neutrophil elastase with Alvestat maintains CK14, calponin, and p63 expression and reduces circulating tumor cells. | CHIL1‑driven neutrophil recruitment leads to extensive laminin and collagen IV degradation, driving myoepithelial loss.

talk_title: Fibroblast heterogeneity and therapeutic strategies in pancreatic cancer
affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and Harvard Medical School
moa_summary: Stephanie Duggan reviewed fibroblast heterogeneity in pancreatic cancer, describing myCAF and iCAF subsets driven by TGF‑β and IL‑1β, and summarized failed clinical attempts to deplete fibroblasts or ECM (Smoothened inhibition, pegylated hyaluronidase) which paradoxically worsened survival, highlighting the need for more nuanced targeting.
key_findings: Smoothened inhibition combined with gemcitabine accelerated tumor progression in humans despite preclinical efficacy. | Pegylated hyaluronidase failed to improve overall survival and worsened outcomes when combined with FOLFIRINOX. | TGF‑β blockade depleted myCAFs but did not improve survival in pancreatic cancer trials. | IL‑1β blockade showed no benefit over chemotherapy in pancreatic cancer.
primary_value: no benefit with pegylated hyaluronidase + FOLFIRINOX

talk_title: Stellate‑cell derived Kit ligand as a stromal tumor‑suppressive factor in pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Sherman demonstrated that Kit ligand (KITLG) is expressed by normal pancreatic stellate cells and lost during tumorigenesis; loss of stromal Kit ligand increases inflammation, leukocyte infiltration, and acinar‑to‑ductal metaplasia, thereby promoting tumor initiation, whereas its presence supports normal tissue architecture and restrains tumor progression.
key_findings: Kit ligand is highly expressed in normal pancreatic stellate cells but markedly reduced in pancreatic cancer stroma. | Conditional deletion of Kit ligand in mouse pancreas increases CD45+ leukocyte infiltration and perivascular mesenchymal cells. | Kit ligand loss exacerbates cerulein‑induced pancreatitis and promotes acinar‑to‑ductal metaplasia. | In implantable tumor models, stromal Kit ligand deficiency accelerates tumor growth.

talk_title: Technical implementation and early results of MedGamma fine‑tuning with QLoRA across consortium sites
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Clinical and Translational Informatics Branch
moa_summary: Umut described the federation of MedGamma using QLoRA adapters, where each site fine‑tunes a frozen foundation model on local lung and breast cancer records, then shares low‑rank weight updates via NVIDIA FLARE to produce a more robust multimodal extractor.
key_findings: Three of four sites completed local QLoRA fine‑tuning, showing improved extraction accuracy versus out‑of‑the‑box MedGamma (visualized as orange vs blue bars). | One site demonstrated a statistically significant increase in variable extraction performance after fine‑tuning; other sites reported even larger gains. | Federated aggregation of QLoRA weights is scheduled, with the hypothesis that exposure to four centers’ data will further boost model robustness.

talk_title: Fine‑tuning MedGamma with QLoRA for trial‑matching in lung and breast cancer cohorts
affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center, Department of Machine Learning
moa_summary: Rasool reported that local QLoRA fine‑tuning of MedGamma on ~500 retrospective lung and breast cancer patients per site markedly improved the model’s ability to extract structured trial‑matching variables from pathology reports and clinical notes, and that a forthcoming federated run will test whether pooled updates further enhance performance.
key_findings: Local fine‑tuning at one site increased extraction performance from baseline (blue) to post‑fine‑tuning (orange). | Two additional sites reported even larger improvements after local QLoRA adaptation. | Federated aggregation of QLoRA weights is planned for the week following the conference.

talk_title: Federated deep‑learning model for overall survival prediction in glioblastoma using MRIs
moa_summary: Khanna described a federated pipeline where a pre‑trained Vision Transformer extracts 768‑dimensional embeddings from pre‑operative MRI tumor ROIs, which are pooled and fed to an MLP to predict relative overall‑survival risk; the model was trained across public GBM datasets and a real‑world Moffitt cohort (n=104) using NVIDIA FLARE with FedAvg aggregation.
key_findings: Added 104 real‑world Moffitt GBM patients to three public datasets without centralizing data. | Leave‑one‑site‑out evaluation showed consistent Kaplan‑Meier separation across held‑out cohorts. | Time‑dependent 1‑year AUC and C‑index were comparable to prior centralized studies, though absolute performance was not yet sufficient for clinical decision‑making. | Future plans include incorporating an additional private NCI GBM cohort and expanding to radiology reports, other imaging modalities, and clinical markers.

talk_title: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab stratified by tumor mutation burden and high-risk stage two, three deficient mismatch repair or MSI-high colorectal cancer: The NeoPRISM-CRC study
moa_summary: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab was used in MMRd colorectal cancer to induce pathological complete response. The study integrated ultrasensitive ctDNA monitoring and TCR profiling to identify 'super molecular responders' who clear ctDNA rapidly, suggesting a potential for non-operative management.
key_findings: Nine weeks of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab achieved a 59% pCR rate in high-risk stage II/III MMRd CRC (n=31). | ctDNA was detected in 100% of patients at baseline, with 24% in the ultrasensitive range (<100 ppm). | 100% of 'super molecular responders' (rapid clearance after 1 cycle) achieved pCR. | Post-operative ctDNA surveillance demonstrated 100% specificity and 100% negative predictive value for relapse over a median follow-up of 33 months. | Baseline TCR clonal expansion correlates with pCR, and a combined Bayesian model of TCR and ctDNA kinetics improves response prediction.

talk_title: Early findings from MONITOR-BREAST: ctDNA dynamics during neoadjuvant therapy using an ultrasensitive MRD assay
moa_summary: The study evaluated the association between dense, ultrasensitive ctDNA sampling and pathologic response in neoadjuvant breast cancer therapy. It found that sustained MRD negativity is a strong predictor of pCR, while persistent or intermittent positivity strongly predicts residual disease.
key_findings: 100% specificity: all 47 patients achieving pCR were ctDNA negative post-neoadjuvant therapy. | Sustained MRD negativity (76% of patients) resulted in a 63% pCR rate. | Persistent MRD positivity resulted in 0% pCR and 100% residual disease (all RCB2 or 3). | Frequent sampling identified 64% more patients at risk for residual disease compared to a single post-neoadjuvant time point. | Clearance timing varied by subtype: TNBC cleared within 45 days (90% of patients) vs HR+/HER2- which took 16 weeks (90% of patients).

talk_title: Phase 2 study of adjuvant PD-1 blockade with endocrine therapy in hormone receptor-positive and inflammatory breast cancer
moa_summary: The study tested the combination of pembrolizumab and endocrine therapy in HR+ inflammatory breast cancer (IBC). The hypothesis was that targeting the immunosuppressive microenvironment of IBC would improve DFS, which was observed (71% vs 60% historical).
key_findings: Two-year DFS was 71%, an improvement over the historical benchmark of 60% for HR+ IBC. | ctDNA negativity at the end of treatment was associated with 100% disease-free survival at 24 months. | ctDNA positivity provided a lead time to clinical progression of up to 15 months. | Durable responders showed lower maximum variant allele frequency (VAF) in nodal tissue compared to non-responders. | Deletions in the immunoglobulin heavy locus (IGH) were found in durable responders, suggesting B-cell involvement in the response.

talk_title: Molecular residual disease interception in local regionally advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, the MERIDIAN phase two trial
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: The MERIDIAN trial tests the 'interception' hypothesis: treating MRD-positive HNSCC patients with the PD-1/TIGIT bispecific antibody rivocostimib to clear ctDNA and prevent clinical recurrence. One patient achieved persistent ctDNA clearance and remains disease-free 2 years later.
key_findings: A single patient treated with rivocostimib achieved persistent ctDNA clearance and remains disease-free 2 years post-therapy. | ctDNA detection at 8-12 weeks post-therapy had 50% sensitivity for recurrence, which improved to 73% with longitudinal tracking. | HPV DNA sequencing showed higher sensitivity but lower specificity compared to the RADAR assay in OPC patients. | Combining tumor-informed assays and HPV DNA increased sensitivity to 100% in OPC patients. | The study was paused due to lower-than-expected MRD prevalence (only 6 patients eligible for interception).

talk_title: Unlocking endocrine resistance, co-targeting KAT6 and ER with bifetostat and fulvestrant
moa_summary: Bifetostat inhibits KAT6A/B, shifting the tumor transcriptome toward tumor suppressor programs (e.g., FOXP3, ING4) and away from cell cycle genes (TK1, CYCB1). Combining this with fulvestrant blocks both epigenetic and ER signaling, leading to robust clearance of ESR1 variants associated with endocrine resistance.
key_findings: Bifetostat 5mg + fulvestrant is the recommended dose for Phase 3 based on superior ctDNA clearance and gene expression changes. | Early ctDNA clearance (within 9 weeks) strongly correlates with clinical response (PR and SD). | Patients with undetectable ctDNA at 9 weeks had a significantly longer median PFS (21.9 vs 5.3 months). | The combination effectively clears ESR1 mutations (63% clearance rate), which are key drivers of endocrine resistance.

talk_title: ctDNA Clearance as a Prognostic Biomarker in Extensive Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer: Insights from the LUMINOUS Phase 3B Study
moa_summary: The study used a tumor-agnostic methylation-based assay to monitor ctDNA in ES-SCLC patients treated with durvalumab and platinum-etoposide. The results show that the pattern of ctDNA clearance (sustained vs late vs never) is a powerful prognostic indicator of OS and PFS.
key_findings: Sustained ctDNA clearance was associated with the best outcomes (median PFS 19.3 months vs 6.2 months for non-clearers). | ctDNA re-emergence occurred 55 days before clinical progression in one representative case. | 100% of sustained clearance patients were alive at 12 months, compared to 36% of never-clearers. | The methylation-based assay is viable for SCLC due to the high shedding nature of the tumor. | ctDNA dynamics aligned closely with longitudinal RECIST assessments.

talk_title: Virtual patients with multimodal foundation models and agents
affiliation: EPFL, Computer Science and Life Science, AI Center, Cancer Research Institute
moa_summary: Developed multimodal foundation models that jointly encode histopathology RGB images and spatial proteomics protein panels into a shared representation, enabling virtual patient profiling, in silico treatment forecasting, and AI‑derived biomarker discovery through early‑fusion transformer architectures and reinforcement‑learning‑based sequential acquisition (EchoK).
key_findings: Trained the first multimodal foundation model on 5,003 patients (4,605 publicly released) with paired H&E and spatial proteomics data across 8 cancer types. | Demonstrated that the model can virtually stain unmeasured proteins and predict optimal protein panels, achieving comparable performance to specialized single‑modality models. | Showed that model‑derived signatures stratify responders vs non‑responders in a triple‑negative breast cancer immunotherapy cohort better than existing clinical biomarkers. | Implemented EchoK, a reinforcement‑learning sequential acquisition system that selects a minimal set of proteomic markers (as few as 8) while preserving cell‑typing accuracy.

talk_title: Generative image models and visual question answering for disease dynamics
affiliation: Stanford, Department of Biomedical Data Science
moa_summary: Introduced CellFlux, a flow‑matching based generative framework that learns a continuous velocity field to transform distributions of control cell images into perturbed cell images, and described scaling of the model to >11 M images, joint imaging‑transcriptomics latent representations, and the MicroVQA benchmark for multimodal scientific visual question answering.
key_findings: CellFlux trained on 300 k images achieved higher image‑generation quality than prior baselines; scaling to 11.6 M images further improved performance. | Demonstrated that flow‑matching in latent space (CellFlux v2) enables realistic perturbation simulations and visualizes transformation trajectories. | Created MicroVQA, a benchmark of >1 000 expert‑curated microscopy questions covering higher‑order Bloom’s taxonomy, revealing gaps in current vision‑language models. | Showed that large multimodal LLMs (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro) achieve state‑of‑the‑art scores on MicroVQA but still struggle with fine‑grained visual reasoning.

talk_title: Reliable medical AI agents that plan, evaluate, and verify reasoning
affiliation: ETH Zurich, Assistant Professor of Medical AI
moa_summary: Proposed a framework for medical AI agents that integrate structured knowledge atlases (Myriad QA pairs) and process‑reward verification models to ground and audit each reasoning step, enabling reliable multimodal decision‑making for tasks such as molecular tumor boards and 3D radiology analysis.
key_findings: Constructed Myriad, a 6 M QA pair knowledge base derived from peer‑reviewed literature, improving retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) performance on MedExpertQA. | Developed a process‑reward model that checks individual reasoning steps of a language model against guidelines, reducing hallucinations across multiple downstream medical tasks. | Demonstrated an agent that performs step‑wise 3D chest CT analysis with traceable reasoning, achieving higher fidelity than baseline LLMs.

talk_title: Overview of the Cancer Research Data Commons infrastructure and usage statistics
affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and Information Technology, Chief of Data Ecosystems Branch
moa_summary: The CRDC provides a unified, cloud-based platform that aggregates genomic, proteomic, imaging, clinical, and other data types across seven specialized data commons, enabling standardized access, harmonized metadata, and scalable analysis without requiring data download.
key_findings: CRDC hosts 533 studies covering over 200,000 subjects and 17 petabytes of data. | Since inception, the cloud resource has performed over 1,600 compute years and offers more than 1,200 public analysis tools. | User engagement includes 100,000 monthly users and 47,000 PubMed citations referencing CRDC data.

talk_title: CCDI Data Ecosystem: resources, enrollment, and data integration
affiliation: National Cancer Institute Office of Data Sharing, Branch Chief
moa_summary: CCDI aggregates pediatric cancer clinical, genomic, and imaging data through a unified data model and APIs, enabling cohort building, cross‑resource queries, and rapid molecular characterization (results returned within 21 days) for over 9,000 enrolled patients.
key_findings: CCDI ecosystem currently includes 41 datasets, including Childhood Cancer Survival Study and Pediatric MATCH. | Project Every Child has enrolled >9,000 patients, delivering molecular characterization results within 21 days. | 6,000 patients' molecular data from MCI have been released in cBioPortal, with integrated SNV and methylation widgets. | Over 4,000 pathology images are available in the Imaging Data Commons, with AI‑ready processing by DOE.
primary_population: pediatric and AYA cancer patients on Project Every Child

talk_title: ARPA‑H Biomedical Data Fabric Toolbox integration with CRDC
affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and IT; ARPA‑H Program Officer
moa_summary: The BDF Toolbox develops AI‑assisted tools (e.g., Netrius DataCord for metadata harmonization, Data Gatherer for literature‑linked dataset extraction, AI chatbots for data exploration) that integrate with CRDC APIs to automate data submission, curation, and discovery, reducing manual effort and improving data readiness for analysis.
key_findings: Netrius DataCord AI‑driven curation platform achieved ≥90% reduction in manual curation time in pilot deployments. | Data Gatherer combines rule‑based and LLM methods to extract dataset metadata from publications, enhancing discoverability. | BioInsight AI chatbot enables plain‑language queries across CRDC genomic, proteomic, and imaging resources.
primary_population: real‑world use cases with Netrius tool

talk_title: Obesity as a modifiable risk factor for cancer incidence and outcomes
tumor_types: breast cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; prostate cancer; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: Excess adiposity drives systemic metabolic dysregulation (hyperinsulinemia, hyperleptinemia, inflammation) that increases incidence and worsens outcomes across multiple malignancies; intentional weight loss via bariatric surgery or structured lifestyle programs reverses these metabolic perturbations and reduces cancer risk and recurrence, particularly in breast cancer, by lowering insulin, leptin, and inflammatory markers.
key_findings: Each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI raises post‑menopausal breast cancer risk by ~10% (IARC meta‑analysis). | Obesity at cancer diagnosis is associated with >20% higher breast cancer‑specific mortality across 36 studies. | Bariatric surgery reduces overall cancer incidence by ~40% and obesity‑related cancers (breast ~50% reduction, colorectal ~60% reduction). | In the Breast Cancer Weight Loss Trial, the lifestyle intervention achieved a mean 6% weight loss at 1 yr, with 50% of participants losing ≥5% body weight versus 12% in controls. | Weight loss lowered fasting insulin by 21% and reduced HOMA‑IR by 25% at 6 mo, indicating reversal of insulin resistance. | Metabolic improvements (insulin, leptin, CRP) were observed without yet having invasive disease‑free survival data, which are pending.
primary_metric: breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² BMI increase

talk_title: Preclinical mechanisms of GLP‑1/GIP dual agonist tirzepatide in obesity‑driven breast, colon and endometrial cancer
affiliation: Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, UNC Chapel Hill
tumor_types: breast cancer; colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: Tirzepatide induces ~20% body‑weight loss in diet‑induced obese mice, normalizing hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia and hyperleptinemia; this metabolic reprogramming restores CD8⁺ T‑cell activation and antigen‑presenting cell function, reverses obesity‑driven immunosuppression, and consequently reduces tumor growth in breast, colon and endometrial cancer models, an effect that is abolished by CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion, indicating a T‑cell‑dependent mechanism.
key_findings: Tirzepatide achieved ~20% weight loss in obese C57BL/6 mice, matching human clinical trial outcomes. | Obesity‑driven tumor growth in EO771, MC38 and PY230 models was reduced to lean‑control levels by tirzepatide; pair‑feeding reproduced most of the effect, indicating weight‑loss as the primary driver. | In the LKB1/p53‑deficient endometrial model, tirzepatide reduced tumor burden even in non‑obese mice, suggesting additional metabolic effects. | Obesity suppressed CD8⁺ T‑cell activation (naive markers TCF7, CCR7) and antigen‑presentation gene sets (18/18); tirzepatide fully restored activated phenotypes (granzyme B, PD‑1) and antigen‑presentation scores. | CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion abolished tirzepatide’s anti‑tumor effect, confirming T‑cell dependence. | Tirzepatide did not affect cancer cell proliferation in vitro, consistent with low GLP‑1R/GIPR expression in human cancer cell lines. | Gut microbiome analysis showed tirzepatide enriched butyrate‑producing bacteria, potentially contributing to reduced inflammation.

talk_title: Real‑world evidence of GLP‑1 receptor agonists on cancer risk, incidence, and survivorship
tumor_types: breast cancer; endometrial cancer; ovarian cancer; meningioma; kidney cancer; lung cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Observational analyses of large Medicare and EHR datasets suggest that GLP‑1 receptor agonist exposure is associated with modest reductions in overall and obesity‑related cancer incidence (notably endometrial, ovarian, meningioma) and improved survival among cancer survivors, while a potential increased risk of kidney cancer emerges in type 2 diabetes cohorts, highlighting drug‑specific effects that may be mediated by weight loss, metabolic improvement, and immune modulation observed in preclinical studies.
key_findings: GLP‑1 users had a 17% lower overall cancer incidence (HR 0.83) compared with matched non‑users in the OneFlorida cohort. | Significant risk reductions were observed for endometrial (HR 0.71), ovarian (HR 0.74) and meningioma (HR 0.68) cancers. | In Medicare type 2 diabetes patients, GLP‑1 vs SGLT2 was associated with a 22% increased kidney cancer risk (HR 1.22). | GLP‑1 vs DPP4 showed a borderline 18% increased endometrial cancer risk (HR 1.18) but was not statistically definitive. | Among cancer survivors with diabetes, GLP‑1 exposure improved overall survival (HR 0.78) and breast cancer‑specific survival (HR 0.71) versus DPP4. | Protective effects were consistent across age groups and most pronounced in non‑Hispanic White patients.
primary_metric: overall cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs non‑users, EHR cohort)

talk_title: Real-world variant-level frequencies in NSCLC from liquid biopsy testing
moa_summary: Analyzed >350,000 liquid biopsy NGS and ddPCR tests to quantify variant-level prevalence of actionable NSCLC alterations, demonstrating population-level differences from trial-enriched cohorts and highlighting rapid, non‑invasive molecular profiling across diverse ethnic groups.
key_findings: Analyzed 350,000 NSCLC liquid biopsy tests from 2015‑2025, representing community practice across 20 self-reported ethnicities. | Technical success rate >90% for both NGS and ddPCR platforms. | KRAS G12C was the most frequent SNV; EGFR L858R and exon 19 deletions were also prevalent. | CCDC6‑RET was the most common fusion; ROS1 and ALK fusions were detected at expected frequencies. | MYC copy‑number gain was the most common CNV among the panel. | Observed variant prevalences were modestly lower than literature reports, reflecting real‑world, non‑referral‑biased populations.

talk_title: Histologic landscape and prognostic grading of lung cancer in never smokers
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Integrative Tumor Epidemiology Branch
moa_summary: Characterized histologic patterns of lung cancer in never smokers using the large XELOC Lung cohort and validated the IASLC grading system for non‑mucinous adenocarcinoma, then developed a deep‑learning model on whole‑slide images to predict five‑year overall survival, revealing ancestry‑linked risk patterns.
key_findings: In 844 never‑smoker lung cancers, adenocarcinoma and carcinoid were over‑represented, while squamous and SCLC were under‑represented compared with smoker cohorts. | Combined IASLC grades 1 + 2 showed better overall survival than grade 3 (HR 0.38 in all stages; HR 0.31 in stage I‑II). | Deep‑learning model improved AUC for five‑year OS when added to clinical variables and grading (integrative model AUC > clinical‑only model). | Model identified a subset of grade 3 tumors with low risk, enriched in East Asian ancestry. | Web‑based heat‑map tool enables pathologists to visualize high‑risk histologic regions.

talk_title: Real-world outcomes of trastuzumab deruxtecan vs chemotherapy in HER2‑amplified mCRC
moa_summary: Compared real‑world overall survival of HER2‑amplified microsatellite‑stable metastatic colorectal cancer patients receiving second‑line trastuzumab deruxtecan versus standard chemotherapy, showing a late survival advantage (5‑yr OS 58% vs 49%) despite similar 1‑yr OS and higher rates of ILD and heart failure.
key_findings: Propensity‑matched cohort of 1,870 patients per arm achieved covariate balance (SMD <0.1). | Early survival similar (1‑yr OS 83% vs 85%); long‑term survival diverged favoring trastuzumab deruxtecan (5‑yr OS 58% vs 49%). | Adjusted hazard ratio for death with chemotherapy vs T‑DXd = 1.14 (caution due to early curve crossing). | Higher incidence of all‑grade interstitial lung disease (12%) and heart failure (9%) in the T‑DXd group. | Hematologic toxicities comparable; fatigue and vomiting more frequent with T‑DXd, diarrhea more common with chemotherapy.

talk_title: Cancer risk with ARB vs ACE inhibitor initiation in US veterans
moa_summary: Emulated a target trial using VHA data to compare cancer incidence and diagnosis‑free mortality between ARB and ACE inhibitor initiators, finding a 20‑25% lower cancer risk with ARBs without differences in overall mortality.
key_findings: Cohort of 2.6 M veterans (10% ARB, 90% ACE) followed up to 10 years. | ARB initiation associated with 20‑25% lower overall cancer risk (RR 0.75‑0.80). | No significant difference in diagnosis‑free mortality between groups. | Effect stronger in patients >70 years (≈30% risk reduction). | Consistent risk reduction across major solid‑tumor subtypes.

talk_title: Assisted reproductive technology and risk of female hormone‑related cancers
affiliation: Taipei Medical University & Vanderbilt University Medical Center
tumor_types: breast cancer; ovarian cancer; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: Nationwide propensity‑matched cohort study in Taiwan linking ART procedures to subsequent incidence of breast, ovarian, and endometrial cancers, revealing modest but statistically significant increased risks for breast (HR ≈ 1.12) and ovarian (HR ≈ 1.46) cancers, with no effect on endometrial cancer.
key_findings: Cohort of 105,000 ART users and 105,000 matched non‑ART controls (mean age 47). | Breast cancer events: 1,774 (ART) vs 1,581 (non‑ART). | Ovarian cancer events: 235 (ART) vs 161 (non‑ART). | Endometrial cancer events: 261 (ART) vs 252 (non‑ART). | Adjusted HRs: breast cancer 1.12, ovarian cancer 1.46, endometrial cancer 1.03 (not significant). | Risk persisted after excluding women with endometriosis or PCOS.

talk_title: Real‑world clinical genomics platform for under‑represented populations
affiliation: German Cancer Research Institute (DKFZ) & High STEM
moa_summary: Built a scalable, modular real‑world clinical genomics platform that integrates whole‑genome sequencing, transcriptomics, imaging, and AI‑driven decision support, initially linking German precision‑oncology infrastructure with a prospective Indian patient cohort to generate globally representative multi‑omics data.
key_findings: German WGS pipeline achieved >85% clinically actionable reporting across 20 tumor types. | Pilot Indian cohort demonstrated feasibility of generating WGS, imaging, and pathology data for under‑represented patients. | AI models trained on German data successfully applied to Indian cases for therapy recommendation and risk stratification.

talk_title: Pan‑cancer landscape of alternative lengthening of telomeres via machine‑learning on clinical sequencing
moa_summary: Developed an ensemble classifier that leverages off‑target reads from MSK‑IMPACT panels to predict ALT activity, applying it to 78,000 tumors to map pan‑cancer ALT prevalence, uncover novel genetic associations (e.g., RB1, POT1) and tumor‑type specific prognostic impacts.
key_findings: Classifier achieved ROC‑AUC >0.85 on held‑out validation using C‑circle assay as ground truth. | ALT prevalence recapitulated known high‑ALT cancers (glioma, sarcoma, osteosarcoma) and identified estimates for >30 additional tumor types. | ATRX/DAXX truncations enriched for ALT but many ALT‑positive tumors lacked these alterations. | Missense mutations in RB1 and POT1 associated with ALT activity in wild‑type ATRX/DAXX tumors. | ALT positivity correlated with better OS in glioma but worse OS in sarcoma and osteosarcoma.

session_title: From Risk Awareness to Informed Action Advancing Cancer Communication Across Prevention and Genomic Care
talk_title: Behavioral Insights for Cancer Prevention Messaging and Evaluation of Colorectal Cancer Screening Messages
topic_categories: communication; behavior_change; public_health; social_media; health_communication; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Niederdeppe demonstrated that outcome‑focused, audience‑segmented messaging improves intentions for colorectal cancer screening, especially when messages are tailored to racial subgroups; generic messages increase intent modestly, but race‑congruent messages boost both screening intention and sharing likelihood among Black participants.
key_findings: A wiki‑survey of >2,000 Black and White adults identified distinct top‑ranked messages for each racial group. | In a subsequent RCT (≈2,000 participants), all message conditions increased screening intention versus control. | Black‑preferred messages produced the highest mean intention scores among Black participants, outperforming median‑ranked and White‑preferred messages. | White participants showed no significant increase in screening intention across any message condition. | Message sharing intentions aligned with racial preference: Black participants were more likely to share Black‑preferred messages, and White participants were more likely to share White‑preferred messages.
primary_metric: intent to adhere to screening recommendations
primary_value: higher than control for all messaging conditions
primary_n: n≈1000 Black adults, n≈1000 White adults

session_title: From Risk Awareness to Informed Action Advancing Cancer Communication Across Prevention and Genomic Care
talk_title: Commercial Marketing Techniques for Precision Oncology Communication and the GenePilot Decision Support Tool
affiliation: Rutgers Cancer Institute and Rutgers University
topic_categories: communication; cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; digital_health; AI_ML; patient_engagement; clinical_trial; implementation_science
moa_summary: Kinney applied commercial segmentation, perceptual mapping, and vector message modeling to create GenePilot, a web‑based decision aid that tailors content to mistrust levels and delivers personalized messaging, resulting in reduced decisional conflict and increased self‑efficacy for genetic testing among Black cancer patients.
key_findings: Segmentation identified high‑ vs low‑mistrust clusters among Black patients, each with distinct messaging needs. | Perceptual mapping revealed that high‑mistrust patients prioritized concerns about privacy, insurance exploitation, and genetic discrimination. | GenePilot incorporated persona‑based modules (Fly, Glide, Soar, Navigate) addressing these concerns. | In the RCT (≈100 Black patients), GenePilot significantly lowered decisional conflict and increased self‑efficacy compared with usual care. | Usage analytics showed mandatory progression through all four modules, ensuring exposure to core content.

talk_title: Pre‑cancer evolution of the upper gastrointestinal tract using organoid and single‑cell models
moa_summary: Deep whole‑genome sequencing of microdissected gastric glands revealed age‑related SNV accumulation and a two‑fold SNV/three‑fold indel increase in intestinal metaplasia glands, indicating clonal expansion from a common ancestor. Single‑cell RNA‑seq of organoids shows a spectrum from hybrid gastric‑intestinal phenotypes to advanced intestinal differentiation, driven by epigenetic reprogramming (hypermethylation of gastric genes, demethylation of colon genes) and fetal signature reversion, with chromosome 20 gain linked to matrix‑independent growth.
key_findings: Microdissected metaplastic glands display ~2‑fold higher SNV burden and ~3‑fold higher indel rate than adjacent non‑metaplastic glands. | Mutational signature SBS18 (ROS injury) and increased ID2/ID1 ratio emerge early in metaplasia, resembling MSI tumor signatures. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq defines hybrid gastric‑intestinal cells and a fetal gut signature (TROP2, TACSTD2) in metaplastic organoids. | Chromosome 20 gain is frequent in metaplastic organoids and enables growth without Matrigel, suggesting an early tumor‑initiating event. | Claudin‑18.2 expression declines during metaplastic progression, potentially mediating resistance to CLDN18.2‑targeted therapies.

talk_title: HER2‑directed bispecific antibody zanidatamab in metastatic gastric/GEJ cancer
moa_summary: Zanidatamab is an asymmetric bispecific antibody that simultaneously engages two HER2 epitopes, inducing receptor clustering, cap formation and hexamerization, which markedly enhances FcγR cross‑linking, complement C1q recruitment and thereby drives ADCC, ADCP and complement‑dependent cytotoxicity beyond that achieved by trastuzumab, pertuzumab or their combination.
key_findings: Zanidatamab induces HER2 cap formation and hexamerization not observed with trastuzumab, pertuzumab, or their combination. | In the fourth‑line setting, zanidatamab monotherapy produced durable responses absent with standard HER2‑targeted therapy. | Phase II zanidatamab + chemo yielded a median PFS advantage of 4 months over trastuzumab + chemo (HR 0.65, p < 0.001). | Adding tislelizumab to zanidatamab + chemo further improved OS (HR 0.72, ~7‑month gain) compared with trastuzumab + chemo. | Diarrhea was the most common toxicity in the zanidatamab‑chemo arms, requiring proactive management.

talk_title: Neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy (FLOT + durvalumab) reshapes curative treatment of resectable gastroesophageal cancer
moa_summary: Durvalumab combined with the triplet chemotherapy FLOT delivers sustained neo‑antigen release and checkpoint blockade while the tumor remains in situ, leading to deep tumor regression (MPR/pCR) and enhanced immune priming that enables organ‑preserving strategies in resectable gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma.
key_findings: Neoadjuvant durvalumab + FLOT achieved a 19% pCR rate and 19% MPR rate, markedly higher than historic chemotherapy alone. | Patients with pCR had durable disease‑free survival with median follow‑up >4 years. | Clinical complete response was observed in 83% of early‑cohort patients after six cycles of neoadjuvant therapy. | ctDNA clearance correlated with pathologic response but 38% of ctDNA‑negative patients still recurred, highlighting residual risk. | Chromosomal instability (CIN) signatures predicted poorer immunotherapy benefit, suggesting a need for additional biomarkers.

talk_title: Distinguishing selection in normal tissues from cancer causation
affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Genetics
tumor_types: esophageal squamous cell carcinoma; acute myeloid leukemia; breast cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Axelrod presented a quantitative framework that compares mutation frequencies in cancers (Y) to frequencies in matched normal tissues (X) to compute a carcinogenic effect (odds ratio). Using esophageal SCC, AML and other cancers, she showed that some canonical drivers (e.g., TP53) have high carcinogenic effects, whereas others (e.g., NOTCH1, PIK3CA) appear neutral or even cancer‑inhibiting, indicating that positive selection in normal tissues can distort perceived driver status.
key_findings: Carcinogenic effect is calculated as the odds ratio of cancer vs. normal tissue mutation frequencies. | TP53 shows a carcinogenic effect in the thousands in esophageal SCC, confirming strong driver status. | NOTCH1 mutations are more frequent in normal esophagus than in cancer, suggesting a tumor‑inhibiting role. | PIK3CA, FAD1, EP300 show low carcinogenic effects, indicating limited contribution to malignancy. | In AML, classic drivers (e.g., FLT3) have high carcinogenic effects, while TET2/DNMT3A have low effects.

talk_title: Clonal evolution and promotion in mouse skin carcinogenesis
affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Helen Diller Family Comprehensive Cancer Center
moa_summary: Balmain demonstrated that initiating mutations (e.g., DMBA‑induced HRAS Q61L) persist as latent clones for months, but tumor formation requires a promoting inflammatory stimulus (TPA). Promotion drives clonal expansion of specific RAS mutants, with immune cell infiltration (neutrophils, monocytes) being essential; HRAS and KRAS mutants compete for clonal dominance, and HRAS loss reveals KRAS‑driven tumors.
key_findings: A single DMBA dose creates ~30,000–50,000 mutations, including HRAS Q61L, which remains latent for up to a year. | TPA promotion induces massive clonal expansion of HRAS Q61L, accounting for >90% of tumors. | Spontaneous KRAS G12D/G13R clones expand with TPA but disappear unless HRAS is knocked out. | HRAS knockout mice develop KRAS‑driven tumors, revealing competitive clonal interactions. | Promotion requires innate immune cells; NSG mice (immune‑deficient) develop few tumors.

talk_title: Germline‑somatic associations in cancer using real‑world panel sequencing
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Computational Oncology Service
tumor_types: pan-cancer; breast cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Zhang described a pipeline that imputes genome‑wide germline genotypes from off‑target reads of clinical tumor panels, enabling GWAS of germline variants influencing somatic driver acquisition. A germline locus near MDM4 was linked to higher TP53 mutation rates, likely via increased MDM4 expression reducing p53 activity, and this association persisted after adjusting for mutational burden and smoking.
key_findings: Off‑target reads from panel sequencing can accurately impute common germline variants (R²>0.8). | Pan‑cancer GWAS identified a 1q32/M​DM4 locus associated with TP53 mutations; replicated in Dana‑Farber and TCGA cohorts. | CRISPR deletion of the implicated enhancer reduced MDM4 expression, confirming functional relevance. | The MDM4 risk allele is enriched in stage IV disease and lung metastasis in breast cancer, independent of TP53 status. | Ancestry‑specific germline variant explains part of the higher ERG fusion frequency in European‑ancestry prostate cancer.

talk_title: Cancer driver mutations in normal tissues and their relation to cancer risk
affiliation: Institute for Research in Biomedicine, Barcelona
tumor_types: colon cancer; lung cancer; bladder cancer; blood (AML)
moa_summary: López‑Bigas introduced MutRisk, a model that integrates tissue‑specific mutation rates, trinucleotide signatures, and chromatin context to estimate per‑site mutation probabilities in normal stem cells. Multiplying by stem‑cell pool sizes yields estimates of how many cells harbor specific driver mutations (e.g., ~1.5 million TP53‑mutant lung basal cells in a smoker). The analysis shows that random mutagenesis alone cannot explain observed cancer incidence, highlighting the need for clonal selection driven by promotion and microenvironmental factors.
key_findings: MutRisk predicts highly unequal per‑site mutation probabilities; some TP53 codons are orders of magnitude more likely to mutate. | Estimated numbers of TP53‑mutant stem cells range from ~18 000 in colon to ~1.5 million in smoker lung tissue. | Combinations of driver mutations (e.g., APC + APC + KRAS) are exceedingly rare without clonal selection (<1 per million individuals). | Duplex sequencing of normal bladder reveals strong positive selection for RBM10 truncations and TP53 mutations, with higher DN/DS in males. | TERT promoter mutations show strong selection only in smokers, linking exposure to clonal expansion.

talk_title: Genomic characterization of multiple primary lung cancers in TRACERx
affiliation: Cancer Research UK, Francis Crick Institute, London, UK
moa_summary: Whole-exome sequencing of multiple primary NSCLC tumors revealed a 10% discordance between clinical and genomic relatedness, indicating many cases are misclassified as metastases or primaries, with no distinct genomic features distinguishing multiple primaries.
key_findings: 25% of TRACERx patients have >1 primary tumor, 12% have >1 lung primary | 10% discordance between clinical and genomic relatedness leads to potential mistreatment | Multiple primaries show similar genomic divergence as tumors from different patients | Higher cumulative tobacco exposure associated with multiple lung primaries

talk_title: Mouse Cancer Cell Line Atlas reveals tissue-specific KRAS dosage evolution
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; lung cancer; intestinal cancer
moa_summary: Analysis of 590 mouse cancer cell lines shows that KRAS dosage increase (gain of a second mutant allele) is a common event across KRAS-driven tumors, occurring early in pancreatic cancer but later in lung and intestinal cancers, with tissue-specific dependencies on CDKN2A or Wnt pathway alterations.
key_findings: Two-thirds of KRAS-driven mouse pancreatic cancers acquire a second mutant KRAS copy early in tumor evolution | KRAS dosage increase occurs late (carcinoma stage) in lung and intestinal KRAS-driven cancers | CDKN2A loss is required for KRAS dosage gain in pancreas but not in intestine or lung | Wnt pathway activation precedes KRAS amplification in intestinal tumors

talk_title: Allele-specific KRAS mutation signatures in lung adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Signature analysis of 100k whole-genome lung adenocarcinomas shows smoking-associated SBS4 directly causes KRAS G12C, G12V, and G13C mutations, while clock-like signatures drive other KRAS variants, revealing subtype-specific mutational etiologies.
key_findings: Smoking signature SBS4 directly implicated in KRAS G12C, G12V, G13C mutations | Clock-like signatures SBS5/40 associated with KRAS G12A, G12D, G13D, Q61H mutations | KRAS mutant tumors show higher smoking signature burden than KRAS-wt | Subtype-specific signature contributions suggest smoking does not drive all KRAS variants

talk_title: Functional consequences of ecDNA versus HSR amplification architecture
affiliation: University of North Carolina Chapel Hill, USA
moa_summary: Single-cell multi-omics of ecDNA- versus HSR-amplified cell lines shows that ecDNA dosage correlates tightly with gene expression and protein levels, with nonlinear transcriptional scaling for specific genes (e.g., PVT1), and that ecDNA heterogeneity is actively maintained through biased inheritance and growth advantage.
key_findings: ecDNA-bearing cells display a broad 100‑1000 copy number range per cell, correlating with HER2 protein levels | Gene expression scaling with ecDNA dosage is stronger and more coordinated than in HSR cells | PVT1 transcriptional output and Myc program are highly dependent on ecDNA architecture | Sorted high‑ecDNA and low‑ecDNA populations reconverge to original distribution within 2‑3 generations via biased segregation and growth advantage

talk_title: Single-cell dissection of lung adenocarcinoma to small cell transformation
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, USA
moa_summary: Single-cell RNA and spatial profiling reveal that adenocarcinoma cells transition through a basal, hypoxia‑driven intermediate state before diverging to small‑cell neuroendocrine or squamous lineages, with FOX2B driving neuroendocrine reprogramming and loss of NOTCH signaling.
key_findings: Transformed tumors display greater phenotypic diversity than de novo small cell | A basal intermediate state marked by NF‑κB, JAK‑STAT, hypoxia precedes neuroendocrine transformation | FOXF2B overexpression induces neuroendocrine markers and suppresses EGFR signaling | SDK1 and KEAP1 mutations may define a neuroendocrine‑to‑GI plasticity axis in large‑cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
primary_value: poor, approaching de novo small cell

talk_title: Optical pooled CRISPRi screen identifies regulators of pancreatic cancer plasticity
moa_summary: Lineage‑resolved optical pooled CRISPRi screening of ~900 perturbations in PDAC cells identifies H3K9 methyltransferases SUV39H1/2 as plasticity regulators that increase epithelial‑mesenchymal transitions via heterochromatin loss, distinct from classic EMT drivers.
key_findings: ZEB1 knockdown biases cells toward epithelial state regardless of initial phenotype | GRL2 loss biases toward mesenchymal state | SUV39H1/2 loss enhances bidirectional plasticity, increasing state transitions | Dual‑guide approach enables detection of epistatic interactions (e.g., ZEB1 vs GRL2)

talk_title: Joint single-cell methylome and 3D genome profiling of glioma heterogeneity
affiliation: University of California San Diego / Salk Institute, USA
moa_summary: Joint single-cell methylome and chromatin contact profiling of gliomas reveals asynchronous changes where 3D genome reorganization precedes DNA methylation shifts, identifies mismatched OPC‑like/astrocyte‑like states, and shows ecDNA subclones (e.g., EGFRvIII) associate with distinct methylation programs.
key_findings: IDH‑mutant gliomas share clonal astrocyte‑like cells with 1p/19q codeletion | Mismatched methylation and 3D genome states suggest early chromatin remodeling during cell‑state transitions | ecDNA (EGFR, CDK4) present only in IDH‑wt GBM, with structural variants correlating with methylation subclones | Oncogene‑specific TF motif enrichment (MAPK for EGFR ecDNA, Hedgehog for GLI1 ecDNA) links ecDNA structure to epigenetic reprogramming

talk_title: Gain-of-function molecular glues targeting transcription factors BCL6 and p53
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; molecular_glue; transcriptional_rewiring; combination_therapy; basic_science
moa_summary: Chemically induced proximity glues recruit BRD4, CDK9, or p300 to transcription factors BCL6 or mutant p53, converting them from repressors to activators and triggering a broad pro‑apoptotic transcriptional program; analogous glues tether EWS‑FLI1 to BCL6 loci to rewire gene expression.
key_findings: BCL6‑BRD4 TSIP activates a BCL6‑dependent reporter with EC50 ~5 nM via a hook‑shaped ternary complex. | BRD4 recruitment relocalizes to BCL6 loci, inducing NOXA and other apoptotic genes more potently than BCL6 loss. | In lymphoma xenografts with high BCL6, the TSIP suppresses tumor growth at modest doses, while BCL6‑low models are resistant. | TRAP‑1 (p53‑BRD4 glue) induces p21 and MDM2 expression in p53 Y220C cells, demonstrating gain‑of‑function activation. | dTag‑based glue redirects EWS‑FLI1 to BCL6 sites, upregulating SOX2 and illustrating programmable transcriptional rewiring.

talk_title: Chemical‑genetic convergence of UM171 and KBDBD4 cancer insertions
moa_summary: UM171 binds the HDAC active site and creates a groove in the KBDBD4 kelch domain, acting as a molecular glue that recruits HDAC1/2‑CoREST for ubiquitination; cancer‑derived 2B2C loop insertions in KBDBD4 mimic UM171 by inserting residues into the HDAC pocket, producing neodegradation of CoREST (genetic glue).
key_findings: Cryo‑EM shows UM171 occupies HDAC active site and contacts KBDBD4 2B2C loop. | Cancer insertions (+2 aa) in KBDBD4 2B2C loop drive CoREST degradation without UM171. | Deep mutational scanning of 5,000 KBDBD4 variants identified insertions as the most potent neodegraders. | Structural superposition reveals UM171 arms recapitulate side‑chains of PR and TTYML insertions. | Activation‑lethality phenotype correlates with co‑REST degradation; mutants in lower‑right quadrant of DMS plot are both degradative and cytotoxic.

talk_title: TRIM21 molecular glues targeting the nuclear pore complex in pancreatic cancer
tumor_types: pancreatic adenocarcinoma; head and neck cancer
moa_summary: The small‑molecule PRLX binds the PRY/SPRY domain of TRIM21, enabling recruitment of TRIM21 to the autoproteolytic domain of NUP98; this induces ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of multiple nucleoporins, collapsing nuclear‑cytoplasmic transport, reducing short‑half‑life oncoproteins (MCL1, MYC) and triggering intrinsic apoptosis in TRIM21‑high pancreatic cancer cells.
key_findings: TRIM21 expression is a strong predictive biomarker for PRLX sensitivity across >4,000 compounds screened. | CRISPR KO of TRIM21 completely rescues cells from PRLX‑induced death; overexpression confers sensitivity. | PRLX directly binds TRIM21 PRY/SPRY domain (crystal structure) and recruits TRIM21 to NUP98 APD, leading to NPC ubiquitination and degradation. | Degradation of NPC components reduces cytoplasmic MCL1 and MYC, causing rapid caspase activation. | In KRAS‑mutant pancreatic xenografts, oral JWZ8103 induces tumor regressions with favorable pharmacokinetics. | Selectivity index shows ~23‑fold greater sensitivity in cancer vs normal immortalized cells after 24 h exposure.
primary_value: robust regressions observed after several days of daily dosing

talk_title: Cereblon‑based molecular glue degraders of ZBTB11 to overcome KRAS inhibitor resistance
topic_categories: molecular_glue; targeted_therapy; PROTAC; metabolism; cancer_resistance; platform_technology
moa_summary: A cereblon‑binding IMiD scaffold is linked to a ZBTB11‑binding moiety, forming a ternary complex that recruits cereblon to ZBTB11, leading to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation; loss of ZBTB11 reverses the KRAS‑resistance metabolic program by down‑regulating mitochondrial biogenesis and ribosome genes, restoring sensitivity to KRAS inhibitors without affecting neuronal OXPHOS.
key_findings: JWJ‑0136 degrades ZBTB11 with Dmax ~80 % in resistant cells, dependent on cereblon and proteasome. | Degradation is abolished by a K866A ZBTB11 mutant, confirming on‑target ternary complex formation. | ZBTB11 loss normalizes mitochondrial biogenesis and reduces basal/maximal OCR in resistant cells. | Combination of JWJ‑0136 with KRAS inhibitors restores drug sensitivity across multiple KRAS‑mutant resistant lines and organoids. | Neuronal assays show no significant impact on mitochondrial health or viability, indicating a favorable therapeutic window.

session_title: Harnessing the Power of the Immune System: Turning Cold Tumors Hot
talk_title: Mechanisms of Myeloid-Mediated Resistance to Anti-PD-1 Therapy
tumor_types: melanoma; head and neck cancer; gastric cancer
moa_summary: The speaker proposes that 'cold' tumors are dominated by suppressive M2-like macrophages that prevent T cell function. He identifies PKC delta and GPNMB as key negative regulators of the antitumor response; loss of these targets shifts macrophage phenotype from suppressive to inflammatory, thereby enhancing spontaneous tumor control and anti-PD-1 efficacy.
key_findings: FMT from responder patients into germ-free mice restored anti-PD-L1 efficacy, a phenotype retained generationally in offspring. | Single-cell RNA-seq of FMT mice showed that responder microbiota induced an inflammatory macrophage gene signature and flipped granulocytes away from an MDSC-like phenotype. | Conditional deletion of PKC delta in hematopoietic cells led to spontaneous tumor control and markedly improved anti-PD-1 efficacy. | GPNMB is dominantly expressed in SPP1+ macrophages; its knockout resulted in substantial spontaneous tumor control in B16-SIY and orthotopic breast cancer models. | GPNMB-mediated suppression is driven by tissue-resident macrophages rather than bone marrow-derived cells, as Lysozyme M-Cre deletion showed a phenotype while Vav1-Cre did not.

session_title: Harnessing the Power of the Immune System: Turning Cold Tumors Hot
talk_title: Genetic Screens and Heterotypic Clusters for Overcoming Immunotherapy Resistance
tumor_types: melanoma; colon cancer; NSCLC; sarcoma; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: The speaker used genome-wide CRISPR screens to identify TRAF2 and RNF31 as survival-arm TNF pathway components that, when inactivated, sensitize tumor cells to T cell killing. Additionally, he developed a pipeline to isolate T cell-tumor heterotypic clusters, which are enriched for tumor-reactive, clonally expanded T cells with superior killing capacity.
key_findings: Inactivation of TRAF2 or RNF31 tips the balance from pro-survival to pro-death, leading to tumor regression in immune-proficient mouse models. | DEP5 knockout in primary T cells boosts global translation and proliferation, leading to significant tumor control in adoptive transfer models. | CD8 T cell clusters comprise ~1.4% of all cells in melanoma lymph node metastases, with ~12% of total CD8 T cells residing in clusters. | T cells isolated from clusters show a 9-fold increase in melanoma killing capacity in vitro across 11 patients. | MGT2 was identified as a top hit in proximity labeling and cluster screens; its knockout in T cells enhances tumor killing in vitro and in vivo.

session_title: Harnessing the Power of the Immune System: Turning Cold Tumors Hot
talk_title: Myeloid-Mediated Immune Suppression in Prostate Cancer and Bone Metastasis
moa_summary: The speaker identifies two distinct myeloid-mediated resistance mechanisms in prostate cancer: SPP1-high TAMs in primary/soft tissue tumors that suppress via the CD73-adenosine pathway, and IL-1beta-high mature neutrophils in bone metastases that suppress via CXCR2-mediated recruitment and IL-1beta signaling.
key_findings: Sipuleucel-T induces T cells that are 'excluded' at the invasive margin, limiting clinical efficacy. | SPP1-high TAMs produce adenosine via CD73; blocking the A2A receptor with zafirlukast combined with anti-PD-1 showed clinical responses in 20% of CRPC patients. | Bone metastases are uniquely enriched for IL-1beta-high mature neutrophils, which are functionally immunosuppressive in vitro and in vivo. | Inhibition of CXCR1/2 or IL-1beta receptor rescued the efficacy of combination checkpoint inhibition in bone metastasis mouse models.
primary_population: CRPC patients with high SPP1 TAMs

talk_title: Functional genomic and drug screening approaches for precision medicine in pediatric acute leukemia
tumor_types: acute lymphoblastic leukemia; acute myeloid leukemia
topic_categories: immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Functional genomic CRISPR screens identified SHMT1/2 of the one‑carbon folate pathway as essential for T‑ALL growth, and JAK1/2 as dependencies in GLIS2‑rearranged AML. Small‑molecule inhibition (RZ2994, ruxolitinib) and an FLT1‑targeted ADC (STRO‑02) demonstrated tumor burden reduction in mouse models, while compassionate‑use STRO‑02 achieved >40% MRD‑negative complete remissions in pediatric AML patients.
key_findings: CRISPR screens revealed SHMT1/2 as selective vulnerabilities in T‑ALL, validated by RZ2994 treatment that reduced bone‑marrow and spleen disease in mice. | GLIS2‑rearranged AML models depend on JAK1/2; ruxolitinib inhibited leukemia growth and modestly prolonged mouse survival. | Resistance to JAK inhibition was mediated by loss of RASA2, which activated MAPK signaling and conferred ruxolitinib resistance in CRISPR screens. | FLT1 is highly expressed in GLIS2 AML; the FLT1‑ADC STRO‑02 eradicated leukemia in mouse xenografts and induced MRD‑negative CR in >40% of 40 pediatric patients on compassionate use. | FLT1‑CAR‑T cells are being developed as a follow‑up strategy for FLT1‑positive AML and osteosarcoma.

talk_title: BTK inhibitor resistance in CLL and therapeutic sequencing strategies
affiliation: Ohio State University Comprehensive Cancer Center
topic_categories: immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; clinical_trial; combination_therapy; resistance_mechanisms; translational
moa_summary: Covalent BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) bind C481 and are highly effective in CLL, but resistance arises via BTK C481S, T474I, L528W, and PLCγ2 activating mutations. Non‑covalent BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib retains activity against C481S but selects for alternative BTK mutations (T474I, L528W). BTK degraders (Bexarotenoag, BGB16673) overcome many kinase‑domain mutations, achieving median PFS ~22 months in heavily pre‑treated CLL, though A428D emerges as a potential universal resistance mutation.
key_findings: Covalent BTK inhibitors achieve median frontline PFS of 8.9 years (ibrutinib) and 62–75.8% PFS at 5–6 years (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib). | BTK C481S and PLCγ2 hotspot mutations are present in 81% of lymphocytosis‑type relapses after covalent BTK inhibitors. | Non‑covalent BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib yields >80% ORR with median PFS 19.6 months in relapsed CLL, but selects for BTK T474I and L528W mutations. | BTK degraders (Bexarotenoag, BGB16673) produce median PFS of 22.1 months and 65.9% PFS at 16 months respectively in patients resistant to both covalent and non‑covalent BTK inhibitors. | A428D BTK mutation emerges after degrader therapy and may confer cross‑resistance to all current BTK inhibitors and degraders.

talk_title: High‑throughput approaches to identify new targets and sequencing strategies in multiple myeloma
moa_summary: RAS‑mutant myeloma cells exhibit elevated macropinocytosis, a nutrient‑scavenging pathway. A small monobody conjugated to a MMAE payload is preferentially internalized via macropinocytosis in KRAS/NRAS‑mutant cells, delivering cytotoxic drug selectively and reducing tumor growth in mouse xenografts without affecting wild‑type myeloma.
key_findings: KRAS/NRAS‑mutant myeloma cells display markedly increased macropinocytosis compared with wild‑type cells. | A nanobody‑MMAE conjugate is selectively internalized by RAS‑mutant myeloma via macropinocytosis, leading to tumor shrinkage in mouse models. | No significant off‑target toxicity was observed in wild‑type myeloma or normal tissues in preclinical studies. | The approach is potentially applicable to other RAS‑driven cancers (pancreatic, colorectal, lung).

session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Single-Dose HPV Vaccination Efficacy and Immunogenicity
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Intramural Research Program, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics
moa_summary: A single dose of prophylactic HPV L1 virus‑like particle vaccine induces durable neutralizing antibodies that are four‑fold lower than three‑dose levels yet provide comparable protection against incident HPV 16/18 infection, demonstrating non‑inferiority to the standard two‑dose schedule in large randomized trials.
key_findings: One‑dose HPV vaccination showed durable antibody levels up to 11 years post‑vaccination with only a four‑fold reduction versus three‑dose levels. | ESCUDO randomized trial (≈20,000 girls) met co‑primary non‑inferiority endpoints for both Cervarix and Gardasil 9, demonstrating >80% efficacy for one‑dose versus two‑dose schedules. | Global HPV vaccine uptake in 2020 was only 20% overall and ~9% in low‑resource settings, but one‑dose schedules are now adopted in ~90 countries, raising one‑dose coverage to 63%. | Meta‑analyses of single‑dose trials (Kenya, Tanzania, India) confirm high efficacy against virologic and histologic endpoints across vaccine platforms.

session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Results from the NCI SHIP Trial: Self‑Collected HPV Testing Accuracy
moa_summary: Self‑collected vaginal HPV samples processed with the FDA‑approved Onclarity assay demonstrate >92% positive percent agreement with clinician‑collected samples and high sensitivity for CIN 2/3 detection, supporting regulatory approval of home‑based HPV testing.
key_findings: The SHIP trial enrolled 2,088 participants across 24 US sites, with 495 completing the SO1 protocol. | Self‑collected vaginal samples achieved 89.2% sensitivity for CIN 3+ (vs 96.9% for clinician samples) and 83.7% for CIN 2+ (vs 90.7%). | Positive percent agreement between self‑collection and clinician collection was 92% for overall high‑risk HPV, 94% for HPV‑16, and ~89% for other types. | The FDA granted the first pre‑market approval for a self‑collection HPV assay (BD/Waters Onclarity) based on these data. | The trial population was diverse (23.6% Hispanic, 37.6% Black) and included 42% previously vaccinated, supporting generalizability to underserved groups.

session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Implementation of HPV Self‑Sampling in Safety‑Net Settings
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Associate Professor of Behavioral Science
moa_summary: Deploying mailed or clinic‑based HPV self‑collection kits, combined with patient navigation, markedly increases screening participation among underserved women, demonstrating that implementation strategy—not assay choice—is the primary driver of reach.
key_findings: Mailed self‑collection kits in the PRESTESS trial doubled screening participation (47% vs 21% usual care) among under‑screened safety‑net patients. | Adding patient navigation to mailed kits further improved uptake (41% vs 21%). | Meta‑analyses show self‑sampling yields 2‑2.5‑fold higher participation compared with clinician‑collected screening in underserved populations. | Implementation context (mail, door‑to‑door, clinic integration) drives effectiveness more than the specific HPV assay used. | Cost‑effectiveness analyses indicate self‑sampling is highly sensitive to implementation design, with navigation and high return rates essential for value.

talk_title: Organoid-guided randomized trial of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel versus FOLFIRINOX in pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Patient‑derived pancreatic organoids are cultured from core biopsies, pharmacotyped with gemcitabine, nab‑paclitaxel, and FOLFIRINOX components, and the ex vivo drug sensitivity correlates with patient outcomes; a transcriptional signature derived from sensitive organoids can predict response without the need for culture, enabling functional precision oncology.
key_findings: Organoid establishment success was ~50% across 186 biopsies, with failure associated with markedly longer patient survival. | Ex vivo gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel sensitivity in PDOs predicted better clinical outcomes compared with FOLFIRINOX in the randomized trial. | A transcriptional oxaliplatin‑response signature derived from hypersensitive organoids stratified patients receiving FOLFIRINOX and correlated with overall survival. | Pan‑RAS inhibitor showed heterogeneous activity across KRAS‑mutant and wild‑type organoids, highlighting the need for functional testing beyond genotype. | High‑content microscopy reduced assay turnaround to <30 days while maintaining strong correlation with traditional ATP‑based readouts.
primary_value: gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel superior to FOLFIRINOX (hazard ratio not specified)

talk_title: Functional ex vivo testing and cell‑state‑guided therapy in AML
moa_summary: High‑throughput ex vivo drug screens of primary AML blasts reveal that differentiation state, rather than mutation alone, dictates sensitivity; stem‑like blasts are venetoclax‑sensitive, while monocytic blasts resist venetoclax but are vulnerable to CDK4/6 inhibition, enabling rational venetoclax + palbociclib combinations.
key_findings: Cell state signatures (stem‑like vs monocytic) explain response to >70% of 120 tested drugs. | Venetoclax is highly effective against stem‑like AML but ineffective against monocytic AML. | Panobinostat shows the opposite pattern, being active in differentiated AML. | Venetoclax + palbociclib demonstrates superior ex vivo activity and synergy, especially in RAS‑mutant or monocytic‑like samples. | The pharma‑type composite ex vivo signature predicts clinical response to venetoclax + HMA with near‑perfect accuracy in newly diagnosed AML.

talk_title: Functional Precision Oncology: ChemoID‑guided therapy improves outcomes in recurrent glioblastoma and platinum‑resistant ovarian cancer
moa_summary: ChemoID isolates bulk tumor cells and patient‑derived cancer stem cells, tests a panel of chemotherapies and combinations ex vivo, and reports percent cell kill for each compartment; selecting agents that achieve ≥50% kill in both compartments guides therapy, leading to significant OS and ORR improvements in randomized glioblastoma and ovarian cancer trials.
key_findings: ChemoID‑guided therapy extended median OS by 4.5 months in recurrent glioblastoma (12 vs 7.5 months). | In ovarian cancer, objective response rate increased from 5% to 55% with ChemoID guidance. | Median PFS improved from 3 months to 11 months in the ChemoID arm for ovarian cancer. | A ≥50% cell‑kill threshold in both bulk and cancer stem cell compartments predicted clinical response. | The assay achieved CLIA/CAP accreditation and received FDA breakthrough device designation for brain tumors.

talk_title: Continuum framework for inherited cancer risk and reduced penetrance BRCA variants
tumor_types: breast cancer; ovarian cancer; prostate cancer; uterine sarcoma
moa_summary: Dr. Pal presented clinical case series illustrating that certain BRCA1/2 variants classified as pathogenic in ClinVar are actually reduced penetrance (hypomorphic) alleles, leading to variable cancer phenotypes across families and highlighting the need for a continuum risk framework.
key_findings: Identified 16 consensus reduced penetrance BRCA1/2 variants across Myriad and Ambry labs, with multiple families carrying the same variant but showing divergent cancer histories. | Described a family with an 84‑year‑old metastatic breast cancer patient and two unaffected BRCA2‑positive daughters, illustrating late‑onset disease despite pathogenic classification. | Showed a 61‑year‑old woman with both ER‑positive and triple‑negative breast cancers carrying a BRCA2 VUS that was classified as pathogenic by six labs and VUS by two, underscoring discordant interpretations. | Highlighted that ClinVar entries often label such variants as pathogenic but note hypomorphic potential in buried comments, leading to counseling challenges. | Emphasized that reduced penetrance variants can still warrant standard risk‑reducing interventions (e.g., breast MRI) while allowing shared decision‑making for prophylactic surgery.

talk_title: Functional analysis framework for reduced penetrance pathogenic variants
tumor_types: breast cancer; ovarian cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Dr. Monteiro described a high‑throughput multiplexed assay (MAVE) pipeline that quantifies protein activity for BRCA1/2 variants, using a VARcall mixture model to translate activity scores into pathogenicity probabilities and to identify intermediate‑activity hypomorphic alleles as candidate reduced penetrance pathogenic variants.
