key_findings: Developed a VARcall two‑component mixture model that separates neutral from pathogenic activity distributions and flags intermediate‑activity variants as potential reduced penetrance pathogenic variants. | Demonstrated that known hypomorphic BRCA1 variants (e.g., R1699W, V1736A) fall in the intermediate activity zone and correlate with atypical family segregation patterns. | Showed that large‑scale functional screens (e.g., from Shendure and Couch labs) can identify a subset of missense variants with activity scores between clearly benign and clearly pathogenic, providing a pool of candidate reduced penetrance variants. | Proposed a calibration strategy where a few functionally validated reduced penetrance variants are used to set activity thresholds for genome‑wide classification.

talk_title: Clinical management challenges for reduced penetrance pathogenic variants in hereditary cancer syndromes
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; gastric cancer; duodenal cancer; endometrial cancer; uterine cancer
moa_summary: Dr. Catona reviewed phenotype‑directed management for reduced penetrance APC variants in attenuated FAP and gene‑specific surveillance strategies for reduced penetrance MSH6 variants in Lynch syndrome, highlighting the need for variant‑specific risk calibration and flexible screening intervals.
key_findings: Attenuated FAP caused by 5' APC variants can be managed using polyp burden (Spiegelman score) to dictate duodenal and colorectal surveillance intervals, avoiding premature colectomy. | A specific MSH6 missense variant present in ~4% of MSH6 carriers showed no statistically increased risk for colorectal, ovarian, or uterine cancer compared with population risk, suggesting a reduced penetrance phenotype. | Proposed extending colonoscopy intervals to the maximum guideline‑allowed three years for carriers of the low‑risk MSH6 variant and deferring prophylactic hysterectomy to age 50 or possibly omitting it. | Emphasized that gene‑specific guidelines (e.g., earlier colonoscopy for MLH1/MSH2 vs. later for PMS2) remain essential, but variant‑specific data are needed to refine recommendations.

talk_title: Immunotherapy-induced extracellular matrix remodeling and BMP1-targeted ADC
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; lung carcinoma
moa_summary: Anti-PD-1 therapy activates CD8 T cells that secrete BMP1, which activates LOX and remodels the extracellular matrix, creating a physical and immunosuppressive barrier that limits T cell infiltration; inhibition of BMP1 with a small‑molecule or BMP1‑ADC restores ECM permissiveness and enhances anti‑tumor immunity.
key_findings: Anti‑PD‑1 treatment induces dense collagen I/III deposition in 4T1 and Lewis lung tumors via CD8 T‑cell‑derived BMP1. | SCID mice lacking T cells do not show ECM remodeling after anti‑PD‑1, which is rescued by adoptive transfer of T cells. | BMP1 expression is up‑regulated in CD8 T cells and plasma after anti‑PD‑1 in mice and in head‑and‑neck SCC patients. | Combination of anti‑PD‑1 with the BMP1 inhibitor UK‑383367 restores tumor sensitivity and reduces ECM density. | A BMP1‑ADC (antibody‑drug conjugate delivering the BMP1 inhibitor) retards tumor growth in multiple models.

talk_title: Aging‑driven arginase‑1 mediated NK cell suppression promotes metastatic colonization
moa_summary: Aging increases ARG1 expression in lung and liver myeloid cells and fibroblasts, depleting arginine and suppressing NK cell proliferation and cytotoxicity; pharmacologic inhibition of ARG1 restores NK cell numbers and function, reducing age‑associated metastatic outgrowth of melanoma.
key_findings: Aged lung and liver exhibit markedly higher ARG1 expression in CD45+ myeloid cells and fibroblasts. | Recombinant ARG1 administration to young mice reactivates dormant melanoma cells and increases liver colonization. | ARG1 treatment selectively reduces NK cell infiltration and activation in metastatic sites. | ARG1 inhibition with CB‑1158 in aged mice significantly decreases liver metastasis and restores NK cell frequency and activation markers (decreased PD‑1, increased DNAM‑1). | ARG1 high expression in TCGA melanoma patients correlates with poorer overall survival and is enriched in patients >55 years.
primary_value: significantly increased in aged lung tissue

talk_title: Multimodal Treatment Strategies and Spatial Niche in Gastric Cancer Peritoneal Metastasis
affiliation: Yonsei Cancer Center, Department of Medical Oncology, South Korea
moa_summary: Spatial transcriptomics of matched primary and peritoneal gastric cancer lesions revealed a fibroblast‑rich, dense extracellular matrix niche that physically shields tumor cells from immune effector cells and limits drug delivery. FGFR2 amplification and collagen/ECM‑related pathways are up‑regulated in peritoneal deposits, while immune‑checkpoint activation is reduced, suggesting that CAF‑mediated barrier formation drives immune evasion and therapeutic resistance in gastric cancer peritoneal metastasis.
key_findings: Peritoneal metastasis occurred in 70.8% of stage IV gastric cancer patients (n=3,200) and was associated with markedly poorer overall survival compared with lymph‑node‑only disease. | HER2‑negative gastric cancers showed a higher peritoneal metastasis rate (≈75%) than HER2‑positive cases (≈50%). | FGFR2 amplification was significantly enriched in peritoneal metastasis tissues versus primary tumors, suggesting a potential therapeutic target. | Spatial profiling identified four CAF subtypes (remodeling, immune‑controlling, mesothelial, stress‑related) with remodeling and immune‑controlling CAFs markedly increased in peritoneal lesions. | Peritoneal lesions exhibited higher fibroblast, endothelial, macrophage, and T‑cell infiltration but reduced T‑cell activation and granzyme expression, indicating an immunosuppressive microenvironment. | Antigen‑presentation genes (e.g., cloudin) were down‑regulated in peritoneal metastases, implying reduced efficacy of cloudin‑targeted ADCs in this compartment.
primary_n: n=3,200 stage IV gastric cancer patients
primary_population: overall stage IV gastric cancer cohort

talk_title: Molecular Heterogeneity in Colorectal Peritoneal Metastasis
affiliation: Yale University, Department of Medicine, GI Oncology
moa_summary: Colorectal peritoneal metastases are highly enriched for the mesenchymal CMS4 subtype, characterized by TGF‑β activation, stromal infiltration, and EMT. Multi‑omic profiling shows that CMS4 tumors retain the same driver mutations as primaries but undergo transcriptional reprogramming and distinct microenvironmental remodeling, leading to resistance to standard HIPEC agents. Targeting stromal components such as moesin or PAI‑1 may overcome this resistance.
key_findings: CMS4 is significantly over‑represented in colorectal peritoneal metastases compared with primary tumors and liver metastases. | Within CMS4, three molecular subgroups (RAS‑mutated mucinous, inflamed, and others) exhibit distinct RAS/TP53 mutation patterns and microenvironment compositions. | Genomic landscapes (TP53, APC, KRAS) are largely conserved between primary and peritoneal lesions, indicating polyclonal seeding rather than subclonal divergence. | CMS4 peritoneal metastases display poor prognosis (HR 0.56 vs non‑CMS4) and are less responsive to oxaliplatin‑based HIPEC, but show greater sensitivity to mitomycin C in vitro. | Targeting stromal proteins moesin or PAI‑1 with monoclonal antibodies reduced tumor burden in preclinical peritoneal models, suggesting a novel therapeutic avenue.
primary_metric: Overall survival difference by CMS subtype
primary_population: CMS4 vs non‑CMS4 colorectal peritoneal metastasis

talk_title: Mutational Signatures and Cellular Phenotypes in Ovarian Peritoneal Metastasis
affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Department of Oncology
topic_categories: DNA_damage_repair; immunotherapy; spatial_biology; tumor_microenvironment; basic_science; clinical_trial
moa_summary: Whole‑genome signatures separate HR‑deficient (BRCA1/2‑like) from HR‑proficient ovarian tumors. HR‑deficient peritoneal metastases display heightened interferon/JAK‑STAT signaling, increased antigen presentation, and frequent clonal loss of HLA (6p LOH), leading to T‑cell dysfunction. Genome doubling is universal, generating ongoing chromosome missegregation and micronuclei; however, genome‑doubled cells down‑regulate cGAS‑STING signaling to tolerate instability, shaping immune evasion in the peritoneal niche.
key_findings: ≈50% of high‑grade serous ovarian cancers are HR‑deficient, identifiable by tandem‑duplication (BRCA1‑like) or interstitial deletion (BRCA2‑like) signatures. | HR‑deficient tumors up‑regulate type‑I interferon and JAK‑STAT pathways and show higher expression of antigen‑presentation machinery compared with HR‑proficient (FBI) tumors. | Clonal 6p LOH (HLA loss) is enriched in BRCA1‑like HRD‑DUP tumors and correlates with increased T‑cell dysfunction. | All tumors contain genome‑doubled cells; genome doubling drives extensive cell‑specific aneuploidies and micronucleus formation. | cGAS‑positive micronuclei are abundant in diploid cells but inversely correlated with cGAS‑STING signaling in genome‑doubled cells, suggesting adaptive suppression of innate immunity. | Multiplex IF reveals significantly more PD‑1/PD‑L1 proximal interactions in HRD‑DUP tumors, indicating a distinct immune checkpoint landscape.
primary_value: HR‑deficient patients have better OS than HR‑proficient

talk_title: Modeling immune metabolism and cancer using spatial metabolomics and organoid systems
affiliation: Cancer Research UK, University of Cambridge, Department of Oncology
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; melanoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; metabolism; spatial_biology; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: SANSOM demonstrated that spatially resolved DESI metabolomics can map tumor metabolic phenotypes, revealing glutamine depletion and SLC7A5 upregulation in APC KRAS colorectal tumors, and that genetic deletion of SLC7A5 suppresses tumor growth and alters MAPK/mTOR signaling, highlighting metabolic dependencies that can be targeted with KRAS inhibitors or rapamycin.
key_findings: DESI imaging identified glutamine depletion and high SLC7A5 expression in APC KRAS colorectal tumor epithelium versus normal tissue. | Genetic deletion of SLC7A5 in APC KRAS mouse models caused accumulation of glutamine, glutamate, and alanine and resulted in the strongest tumor suppression observed in colorectal cancer models. | SLC7A5 loss reduced phospho‑S6 signaling, sensitizing tumors to rapamycin and MAPK inhibition. | KRAS G12D inhibitor treatment eliminated phospho‑ERK signaling but did not eradicate tumors; however, it rapidly altered neutrophil, lymphocyte, and monocyte counts and remodeled macrophage and CAF spatial organization. | In pancreatic KPC mice, SLC7A5 deletion slowed tumor genesis, shifted tumors toward a more homogeneous squamous subtype, and increased immune infiltration.

talk_title: Citrate carrier SLC25A1 drives T cell exhaustion and can be targeted to improve immunotherapy
affiliation: University of Pittsburgh, Department of Immunology, UPMC Hillman Cancer Center
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; metabolism; basic_science; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Delgoff showed that chronic TCR stimulation and hypoxia upregulate the mitochondrial citrate carrier SLC25A1, shunting citrate to the cytosol where it fuels de novo lipogenesis and protein acetylation, driving lipid droplet accumulation and hyperacetylation that enforce terminal T cell exhaustion; CRISPR knockout of SLC25A1 restores mitochondrial respiration, reduces lipid droplets, enhances polyfunctionality, and improves tumor control, synergizing with PD‑1 blockade.
key_findings: Chronic TCR stimulation plus hypoxia uniquely upregulates SLC25A1 in terminally exhausted CD8+ T cells. | SLC25A1 mediates export of mitochondrial citrate, leading to cytosolic acetyl‑CoA accumulation, lipid droplet formation, and widespread protein acetylation. | CRISPR knockout of SLC25A1 increases mitochondrial respiratory capacity, glycolysis, and reduces lipid droplet burden in exhausted T cells. | SLC25A1‑deficient T cells exhibit lower PD‑1, TIM‑3, and CD39 expression, retain IL‑2 production, and display superior polyfunctionality in tumors. | Adoptive transfer of SLC25A1‑KO T cells yields greater tumor regression and synergizes with anti‑PD‑1 therapy in mouse models.

talk_title: Reprogramming NK cell metabolism for enhanced adoptive cell therapy
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Immunology
tumor_types: AML; renal cell carcinoma; pancreatic adenocarcinoma; triple‑negative breast cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cell_therapy; metabolism; NK_cells; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Madar described multiple metabolic engineering strategies for NK cell ACT, including CRISPR knockout of the cytokine‑inducible checkpoint CIS and TGF‑β receptor II to boost mTOR and glycolytic pathways, and overexpression of the adenoviral E4ORF1 protein to activate PI3K/AKT/mTOR and increase citrate‑derived biosynthesis, resulting in enhanced mitochondrial mass, oxidative phosphorylation, and cytotoxicity across solid and hematologic tumor models.
key_findings: NK cells cultured in lipid‑rich APEL media acquire higher mitochondrial mass, elongated morphology, and increased spare respiratory capacity versus standard media. | CIS knockout NK cells display upregulated mTOR signaling, glycolysis, and improved survival in aggressive Raji lymphoma mouse models. | TGFBR2‑KO CD70 CAR NK cells demonstrate superior anti‑AML activity in preclinical models and have entered a Phase 1/2 trial (NCT‑pending). | E4ORF1 overexpression in NK cells enhances glycolysis, OXPHOS, fatty‑acid oxidation, and glutaminolysis, leading to robust killing of multiple solid tumor lines even under nutrient starvation. | AMPK activity is required for the E4ORF1‑mediated metabolic boost; inhibition of AMPK abrogates the enhanced cytotoxic phenotype.

talk_title: STK-12 IL-2 agonist plus pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC
moa_summary: STK-12 is a recombinant IL-2 partial agonist that selectively expands antigen-experienced CD8+ T cells while sparing NK and Treg cells, enhancing IFNγ and IP-10 production and clonal TCR expansion, thereby improving response rates when combined with pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC.
key_findings: Overall response rate of 50% and disease control rate of 97% in a high‑risk PD‑L1 negative NSCLC cohort (n=39). | In STK11/KEAP1/SMARCA4 altered subgroup, ORR increased to 61% with 100% disease control. | STK‑12 induced robust expansion of 4‑1BB+ CD8 T cells and >10‑fold clonal expansion of pre‑existing TCR clones. | Minimal classic IL‑2 toxicities observed; most common AE was reversible maculopapular rash in ~50% of patients.

talk_title: Tertiary lymphoid structures predict response to durvalumab, trastuzumab, pertuzumab in HER2‑enriched early breast cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; biomarker_development; spatial_biology; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Durvalumab combined with HER2‑targeted antibodies induces formation of tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) composed of Tfh‑like CD4+ cells, mature dendritic cells, and proliferating B cells; these TLS niches correlate with enhanced CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and higher pathologic complete response in HER2‑enriched breast cancer.
key_findings: TLS‑associated immune module (Tfh‑like CD4+, LAMB3+ mature DCs, cycling B cells) is enriched in pretreatment tumors of patients achieving PCR. | Spatial analysis shows co‑localization of these cell types within 20‑µm niches, defining TLS structures. | Higher TLS niche fraction predicts PCR (p<0.01) and correlates with increased intratumoral CD8+ T cells post‑treatment. | CellChat indicates CD4+ Tfh cells activate B cells via IL‑2, CD40, and ICAM pathways within TLS.

talk_title: Inhibiting nonsense‑mediated decay (SMG1) to enhance antitumor immunity
moa_summary: SMG1 inhibition blocks nonsense‑mediated decay, stabilizing frameshift‑derived PTC transcripts that encode highly immunogenic neoantigens; these are presented on MHC‑I, leading to CD8+ T‑cell activation and tumor control, synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: SMG1 inhibition increased PD‑1+ CD8 T‑cell activation in ex vivo tumor fragments without affecting adjacent normal tissue. | In Lewis lung carcinoma, SMG1 inhibitor monotherapy matched anti‑PD‑1 efficacy; combination yielded superior tumor control. | Immunopeptidomics identified six neoantigen peptides up‑regulated only after NMD inhibition; four were immunogenic in vitro. | MHC‑I blockade abolished T‑cell activation induced by SMG1 inhibition, confirming antigen‑dependent mechanism.

talk_title: High‑fiber dietary intervention improves immunotherapy outcomes in melanoma
moa_summary: A high‑fiber, whole‑food diet reshapes the gut microbiome toward response‑associated taxa, lowers secondary/primary bile acid ratios, reduces immunosuppressive classical monocytes, and enhances intratumoral CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte infiltration, thereby improving checkpoint inhibitor efficacy in melanoma.
key_findings: High‑fiber diet did not alter overall alpha/beta diversity but increased patient‑specific enrichment of known response‑associated taxa. | Serum bile acid profiling showed a significant decrease in secondary/primary bile acid ratio in the high‑fiber arm. | Classical monocyte immunosuppressive cluster decreased over time only in high‑fiber patients. | Digital spatial profiling revealed greater CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte density post‑intervention in responders receiving high‑fiber diet.
primary_value: Numerically higher in high‑fiber arm (exact % not disclosed)

talk_title: IL‑17‑producing TH17 cells drive neurotoxicity in immune‑related adverse events of the CNS
moa_summary: A POLR6‑positive CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell subset expands in the CSF of ICI‑treated patients, secreting IL‑17 and other inflammatory mediators that drive neuroinflammation; blockade of IL‑17A mitigates PET‑detected inflammation and improves neurologic outcomes.
key_findings: Identified a disease‑specific POLR6+ CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell cluster producing IL‑17, MCP1, CCL11, granzyme B, etc. | Higher POLR6+ cell burden correlated with greater PET‑measured neuroinflammation and worse cognitive scores. | Clonal T‑cell expansions persisted up to 12 months, indicating ongoing antigen‑driven response, with NMDA‑R1 as a dominant neuroantigen. | Retrospective anti‑IL‑17A treatment was associated with reduced neuroinflammation and improved neurologic function without affecting cancer outcomes.

talk_title: NAT10‑MYC loop drives autophagy‑dependent MHC‑I downregulation and immunotherapy resistance in MSS colorectal cancer
moa_summary: NAT10 acetylates RNA (ac4C) stabilizing autophagy genes (ATG3, ATG5, Beclin‑1), leading to increased autophagic flux that degrades MHC‑I molecules; a MYC‑NAT10 positive feedback loop sustains this program, causing immune evasion in MSS CRC, which can be reversed by NAT10 inhibition to restore MHC‑I and sensitize tumors to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: NAT10 is overexpressed in MSS CRC and predicts worse overall survival. | NAT10 knockout increases CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and tumor regression in mice. | Combination of NAT10 inhibition with anti‑PD‑1 yields complete tumor regression in 80% of mice. | MYC transcriptionally up‑regulates NAT10; NAT10‑mediated ac4C stabilizes MYC mRNA, forming a feed‑forward loop.

talk_title: Versican proteolysis restores tumor matrix signaling to reverse checkpoint resistance
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; lung cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Proteolytic cleavage of versican generates bioactive fragments that promote formation of immunotriads (CD4+ Tfh‑like cells, CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells) within the tumor stroma, enhancing CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and synergizing with checkpoint blockade to overcome immune exclusion.
key_findings: Versican overexpression in MC38 tumors induced immunotriad formation and cured >70% of mice with anti‑PD‑1 alone. | In VCAN‑high LSC tumors, versican plus dual checkpoint blockade (PD‑1 + CTLA‑4) was required for tumor control. | Exogenous versican delivered via LNP mRNA or recombinant protein showed monotherapy activity in VCAN‑low models and synergized with ICB. | Efficacy depended on cDC1 dendritic cells and CD8+ T cells; depletion abrogated therapeutic benefit.

talk_title: Implementation planning for lung cancer screening in HIV care settings
moa_summary: Dr. Islam described a pre-implementation research package (R01) that uses multilevel epidemiologic, causal inference, and qualitative methods to characterize LDCT lung cancer screening uptake, barriers, and patient decision-making among people with HIV, with the goal of informing tailored implementation strategies.
key_findings: Among ~7,000 HIV+ lung cancer patients, 75% were diagnosed at metastatic stage. | People with HIV had a 15% increased risk of metastatic lung cancer diagnosis compared to non‑HIV patients. | Socioeconomic factors (ZIP‑code education, income, care location) predicted higher probability of metastatic diagnosis. | The R01 aims to (1) describe LDCT screening patterns, (2) estimate direct/indirect effects of social determinants, and (3) explore patient decision‑making via qualitative interviews.

talk_title: Participatory approaches to prioritize colorectal cancer screening for patients with diabetes in FQHCs
affiliation: Rutgers Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, Associate Professor
moa_summary: Dr. O'Malley used implementation mapping with a multi‑stakeholder planning group to identify barriers and select four prioritized strategies (champion identification, low‑literacy educational materials, quality monitoring systems, and implementation meetings) to improve colorectal cancer screening among diabetic patients in safety‑net clinics.
key_findings: Implementation planning group generated 36 potential strategies, refined to top five themes (patient education/communication, clinical flow). | Top four selected strategies: identify/prep champions; develop low‑literacy educational materials; organize quality monitoring (UDS metrics); conduct implementation meetings. | Pilot clinic A achieved 77% screening among diabetic patients (higher among uncontrolled diabetes), using champion‑driven Cologuard outreach. | Pilot clinic B, with lower baseline screening (28%), improved to 60% using adapted postcards, QR‑code videos, and staff training. | Uncontrolled diabetes patients comprised the largest unscreened subgroup in both clinics.

talk_title: Sustaining and scaling HPV vaccination implementation in safety‑net settings
affiliation: University of Southern California, Professor, Department of Population and Public Health Sciences
moa_summary: Dr. Tsui presented a multi‑year R37/R01 project that implemented audit‑and‑feedback, provider communication training (Announcement Approach), and co‑designed staff workflow tools to increase HPV vaccine initiation, demonstrated sustained provider practices and identified contextual factors for scaling to other FQHC systems.
key_findings: After 12‑month implementation, HPV vaccine initiation increased significantly at three pilot clinics, driven by higher uptake at ages 9‑10. | 18‑month follow‑up showed sustained provider discussion of vaccination at age 9 (88% of staff) and increased confidence addressing parental hesitancy (69%). | CSAT scores indicated staff found the strategies easy to use, integrable into workflow, and clinically meaningful (median 5‑7/7). | Turnover of clinic staff remained a major barrier to sustainment; half of staff changed within 24 months. | Scale‑out to AltaMed, Chinatown Service Center, and Riverside San Bernardino IHS required adaptation of training modality (in‑person, virtual, hybrid) and cultural tailoring of materials.

talk_title: The Role of pks+ E. coli in Intestinal Stem Cell Plasticity and Early Onset of Colorectal Cancer
topic_categories: microbiome; cancer_vaccines; basic_science; translational; genomics; stem_cell_biology; mutation_signature
moa_summary: pks+ E. coli produces colibactin, a genotoxin that creates AT-rich DNA lesions (SBS88) and mutates APC splice sites in young patients; colibactin also binds AT-rich motifs, displacing CDX2 and repressing differentiation programs, thereby promoting a shift of epithelial cells toward an LGR5+ intestinal stem cell state, enhancing regenerative capacity and tumor initiation in the young colon.
key_findings: Developed a targeted-exome assay to detect the colibactin SBS88 signature in >80,000 clinical tumors, finding enrichment in colorectal, esophageal, bladder, and head/neck cancers. | Colibactin signature-positive tumors occur in significantly younger patients and are more often located in the rectosigmoid region. | Identified a specific APC splice-site mutation occurring in the AT-rich colibactin context, enriched in young-onset colorectal cancers, suggesting colibactin-driven initiation. | Inverted-polarity organoid model exposed to pks+ E. coli recapitulated colibactin mutational signatures and induced upregulation of LGR5 and ASCL2 stem cell markers. | pks+ E. coli exposure increased functional stem cell capacity (organoid formation) and proliferation compared to Δpks controls. | Thermal proteomics and ATAC-seq revealed colibactin-induced repression of AT-rich transcription factor motifs, notably CDX2, leading to loss of differentiation and promotion of stemness. | Young colon organoids showed greater regenerative response to colibactin-induced injury than aged organoids, linking age-dependent stem cell capacity to early-onset tumorigenesis.

talk_title: Towards New Cancer Medicines with Degraders of Chromatin
moa_summary: Danette presented a framework that quantifies degrader kinetics by measuring degradation rate versus Dmax, defining an inflection point (the "rate cliff") that predicts complete target loss across multiple chromatin regulators; fast degraders with high rate efficiency translate to robust in vivo pharmacodynamics independent of DC50.
key_findings: A universal rate‑versus‑Dmax relationship was observed across diverse degraders (BRD9, EP300, CBP) regardless of target or cell line. | The rate inflection point (0.7–1.8) predicts ≥85–90% Dmax; compounds below this "cliff" show target recovery. | AUC of rate‑versus‑concentration curves distinguishes compounds with identical DC50 but up to five‑fold differences in maximal degradation rate. | Faster degraders exhibit higher ubiquitination levels and form more stable ternary complexes with slower koff rates measured by SPR. | In vivo PK/PD mapping of CBP degraders demonstrates that dosing at Cmax and trough samples the maximal degradation rate, leading to robust target loss in mouse models.

talk_title: Designing Effective Degrader Therapeutics: What Early Clinical Experience Has Taught Us
moa_summary: Bexipridil is a BTK degrader that induces proteasomal removal of BTK, achieving sub‑stoichiometric target clearance; this eliminates residual kinase activity and overcomes a broad spectrum of BTK resistance mutations, providing deeper and more durable responses than BTK inhibitors.
key_findings: 83% overall response rate in a heavily pre‑treated CLL population, including patients who progressed on the approved BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib. | Median duration of response reached 20 months, exceeding the ~12‑month DOR reported for the best current inhibitor. | Degrader retained activity against all known BTK resistance mutations, whereas inhibitors showed gaps in coverage. | Higher dose (600 mg) improved progression‑free survival without increasing adverse‑event frequency, indicating a wide therapeutic index. | Comprehensive proteomics showed BTK as one of the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target degradation. | No QT prolongation observed in healthy volunteers or patients, differentiating this degrader from earlier molecules.
primary_population: heavily pretreated CLL, including BTK‑inhibitor failures

talk_title: First‑in‑Class Dual AR/ARV7 Molecular Glue Degraders
moa_summary: Compounds 15 and 64 act as molecular glues that bind the N‑terminal domain of AR/ARV7 and recruit a Cullin‑RING ligase complex (DDB1‑containing) to induce proteasomal degradation of both isoforms, thereby suppressing AR‑driven transcription and overcoming enzalutamide resistance.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 170,000 compounds identified 13 hits; lead chemotype 7907 degraded both AR and ARV7 via proteasome. | Medicinal chemistry optimization yielded analogs 15 and 64 with sub‑micromolar EC50s and rapid (≤4 h) degradation of both proteins. | Compound 15 suppressed AR‑driven transcription in 22RV1 cells, outperforming enzalutamide and ARV110 in RNA‑seq AR‑score analysis. | In vivo, oral dosing of compounds 15 and 64 markedly inhibited growth of ARV7‑positive 22RV1 xenografts, achieving greater tumor regression than enzalutamide or ARV110. | Mechanistic studies mapped activity to the AR N‑terminal TAL1 and AF1 subdomains; degradation required Cullin‑RING ligase activity (blocked by MLN4924) and was partially reversed by DDB1 knockout. | Global proteomics showed AR and ARV7 among the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target effects; compounds were well tolerated with no weight loss.

talk_title: Role of Sex and Ancestry in Inflammaging, Lymphomagenesis and Its Precision Therapy
affiliation: Jose Carreras Leukemia Research Center, Barcelona
moa_summary: Sex- and ancestry-specific mutations (SPEN, Notch2, ATM, MGA, SETD2) reprogram B-cell differentiation toward aged, auto-reactive AIBCs, driving aggressive DLBCL phenotypes; female BN2 DLBCLs exhibit X-chromosome instability and heightened TLR7 signaling, rendering them sensitive to IRAK inhibition, while African ancestry DLBCLs display senescence-associated secretory phenotypes linked to SETD2 loss, suggesting precision therapeutic windows.
key_findings: SPEN truncation co-occurs with Notch2 truncation in BN2 DLBCLs and predicts inferior survival in a 4,500-patient cohort. | Female BN2 DLBCL mice develop X‑chromosome hypomethylation, increased TLR7 expression, and are more sensitive to IRAK inhibitors than males. | African ancestry DLBCLs harbor frequent ATM, MGA, and SETD2 mutations (>50%) and show elevated senescence (GLB1) and exhausted CD4 T‑cell infiltrates. | SETD2‑heterozygous DLBCL cells are hypersensitive to a clinical SETD2 inhibitor, offering a potential ancestry‑specific therapy. | IRAK inhibition reduces tumor growth in female BN2 DLBCL xenografts, supporting a sex‑specific precision therapy approach.
primary_population: BN2 DLBCL with SPEN+Notch2 double mutation

talk_title: Age-Associated Molecular Landscapes and Therapeutic Responses in Breast Cancer
affiliation: Brigham Hospital and Harvard Medical School
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science; spatial_biology
moa_summary: Aging reprograms breast cancer transcriptomes: older ER‑positive tumors show reduced immune and metabolic pathways, while older triple‑negative tumors acquire an inflamed, EMT‑driven phenotype; age‑selected subclones (e.g., barcode 25) become resistant to paclitaxel, but are sensitized to HDAC inhibition combined with checkpoint blockade, revealing age‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Older breast cancer patients exhibit distinct molecular landscapes: ER+ tumors lose immune/metabolic activity, while TNBCs gain an inflamed EMT phenotype. | In mouse models, aged mice show slower NET1 tumor growth but reduced response to paclitaxel + anti‑PD‑L1, despite higher CD8 infiltration and Treg expansion. | Barcode 25 subclone is enriched by age and paclitaxel, displays intrinsic paclitaxel resistance, and can be eliminated by HDAC inhibition. | Panobinostat combined with anti‑PD‑L1 dramatically extends survival in aged mice, whereas the same combination is less effective in young mice without chemotherapy.

talk_title: Targeting GDF15‑Mediated Immunosuppression to Overcome Age‑Driven Immunotherapy Resistance in Pancreatic Cancer
moa_summary: Aging elevates systemic and tumor‑derived GDF15, which suppresses CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and impairs vaccine‑induced neoantigen responses in PDAC; neutralizing GDF15 restores T‑cell priming and synergizes with KRAS peptide vaccination and checkpoint blockade to achieve tumor regression in aged mouse models.
key_findings: Aged mice develop larger orthotopic KPC tumors and exhibit markedly reduced CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration compared with young mice. | Circulating GDF15 is low in young mice but rises sharply in aged mice and further increases after tumor implantation. | Genetic or antibody‑mediated GDF15 neutralization halves tumor size in aged mice and restores CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration; effect is lost after T‑cell depletion. | GDF15 neutralization enhances KRAS mutant peptide vaccine‑induced IFN‑γ T‑cell responses in aged mice. | In a PDAC GVAX trial, high tumor GDF15 expression correlates with ~2‑fold worse overall survival, independent of age.
primary_population: aged KPC mice treated with anti‑GDF15

talk_title: Evasion of Hepatic ILC1 Immunosurveillance Drives Breast Cancer Liver Metastasis
moa_summary: Hepatic ILC1s mediate immune surveillance of breast cancer liver metastasis via TRAIL-DR5 signaling; loss of DR5 or depletion of ILC1s enhances liver tumor burden, while GLP-1 agonist restores ILC1 numbers and reduces metastasis.
key_findings: High-fat diet mice develop increased breast cancer liver metastasis compared to control diet. | RAG2 gamma knockout mice show increased liver and lung tumor burden, implicating innate lymphocytes in surveillance. | Selective depletion of conventional NK cells increases lung but not liver metastasis, indicating liver ILC1s are essential for hepatic surveillance. | ILC1s mediate tumor killing via TRAIL binding to DR5 on cancer cells; DR5 loss increases liver metastasis. | GLP-1 receptor agonist restores hepatic ILC1 numbers in steatotic mice and reduces liver tumor burden.

talk_title: Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Inhibition Mitigates Tumor Necrosis and Metastasis in Colorectal Cancer
affiliation: Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory / Surgical Oncology
moa_summary: NETs accumulate in necrotic regions of colorectal tumors, driving hypoxia, EMT, and metastasis; pharmacologic (disulfiram) or genetic (PAD4 KO) inhibition of NET formation reduces necrosis, EMT signaling, and liver metastases, and enhances chemotherapy efficacy.
key_findings: PAD4 knockout mice show decreased tumor necrosis and fewer liver metastases compared to wild-type. | Disulfiram treatment reduces NETs, necrosis, and liver metastasis burden in AKPS organoid mice. | NET inhibition combined with FOLFOX chemotherapy further decreases liver metastasis compared to chemotherapy alone. | Necrotic tumor regions exhibit higher EMT markers (vimentin, SNAI1) and inflammatory pathways. | Mice with NET inhibition display increased neutrophil activation markers (citH3) reduction.

talk_title: Metabolic Reuse of Tumor-Derived Signals Coordinates Dendritic Cell Function
moa_summary: Tumor-derived polyamines enhance dendritic cell uptake via RAC1 activation, while tumor-derived cholesterol fuels dendritic cell maturation through NPC1-mediated transport and lipid nanodomain formation; AXL deletion amplifies cholesterol accumulation, boosting maturation and antitumor immunity.
key_findings: ODC1-deficient tumor debris is less efficiently taken up by dendritic cells in vitro and in vivo. | Blocking polyamine transport (AMTX) impairs dendritic cell uptake of tumor debris. | Polyamine deficiency reduces RAC1 expression and activation in dendritic cells. | Inhibition of NPC1 or statin treatment diminishes CCR7 and other maturation markers on dendritic cells. | AXL knockout dendritic cells accumulate more cholesterol, lipid nanodomains, and display heightened maturation and improved tumor control.

talk_title: Spatial Rewiring of IL-1β Network Limits Therapeutic Efficiency in Advanced Non‑Squamous Carcinoma
moa_summary: Anti‑IL1β therapy is effective when administered at pre‑cancer stages by disconnecting IL1β‑IL1R1 signaling in epithelial cells; in advanced disease the network shifts to fibroblast‑centric signaling, limiting efficacy despite increased IL1β levels.
key_findings: IL1β pathway activity rises from normal to pre‑cancer to invasive adenocarcinoma in both human and mouse samples. | Anti‑IL1β antibody suppresses tumor growth when given at pre‑cancer stage but not at advanced stage in two mouse models. | Anti‑IL1β treatment paradoxically increases IL1β plasma levels in mice and humans. | Spatial analysis shows pre‑cancer blockade reduces IL1R1‑positive epithelial cells within the IL1β network, whereas advanced stage blockade reduces IL1R1‑positive fibroblasts. | Location‑aware cell‑cell interaction analysis reveals that IL1β‑IL1R1 contacts are disrupted in pre‑cancer but persist in advanced disease.

talk_title: Gut Commensal Dysbiosis Modulates Lung Microenvironment to Promote Breast Tumor Metastasis via Mast Cell‑Dependent Axis
moa_summary: Gut dysbiosis elevates mast cell activation in the lung, leading to IL‑6 secretion that activates STAT3 in fibroblasts and endothelial cells, promoting ECM stiffening, angiogenesis, and M2 macrophage polarization, thereby creating a permissive niche for breast cancer lung metastasis.
key_findings: Antibiotic‑induced gut dysbiosis accelerates death of mice after tail‑vein breast cancer cell injection. | Lung mast cell density inversely correlates with survival in dysbiotic mice. | Ketotifen (mast cell stabilizer) improves survival of dysbiotic mice, implicating mast cells in metastasis promotion. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq reveals dysbiotic lung mast cells upregulate IL‑6 and degranulation genes. | Fibroblasts and endothelial cells in dysbiotic lungs show STAT3‑driven fibrotic and angiogenic transcriptional programs. | Macrophages shift toward M2 phenotype with increased CCL2 and MMP‑related genes in dysbiotic lungs.

talk_title: Mild Interferon‑γ Signaling Regulates Immune Suppression and Fibrotic TME in Pancreatic Cancer Liver Metastasis
moa_summary: Mild IFN‑γ signaling through IFNγR2 on macrophages sustains antigen‑presentation (CD80/CD86/MHC‑II) and limits immunosuppressive markers (ARG1, CD206, SPP1); loss of this signaling drives SPP1‑high macrophages, fibrosis, and T‑cell dysfunction in pancreatic cancer liver metastasis.
key_findings: Systemic IFN‑γ neutralization increases tumor number and burden in liver metastasis model. | Myeloid‑specific IFNγR2 KO mice exhibit larger tumor burden and higher TAM numbers. | KO TAMs show decreased CD80/CD86/MHC‑II and increased ARG1/CD206/SPP1 expression. | T‑cell proliferation (Ki67) and cytokine production (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α) are reduced in KO mice. | Bulk RNA‑seq reveals down‑regulation of IFN‑γ response and up‑regulation of fibrosis pathways in KO tumors. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq identifies expansion of SPP1‑high macrophages with ECM and TGF‑β signatures in KO.

talk_title: Targeting Chronic Inflammation in Cancer and Fibrosis with Engineered uPAR‑CAR T Cells
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; lung adenocarcinoma; squamous lung cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: uPAR‑CAR T cells recognize and eliminate uPAR‑positive tumor cells, cancer‑associated fibroblasts, and immunosuppressive myeloid cells, thereby dismantling fibrotic stroma, enhancing T‑cell infiltration, and preventing antigen escape.
key_findings: uPAR is co‑expressed on tumor cells, CAFs, and myeloid suppressor cells in >10 solid tumor types. | Single dose of uPAR‑CAR T yields durable tumor regression and protects against rechallenge in multiple xenograft models. | Cisplatin treatment up‑regulates uPAR on ovarian cancer cells, sensitizing them to uPAR‑CAR T killing. | uPAR‑CAR T reduces collagen deposition and fibrosis, leading to increased CAR T cell infiltration. | In humanized mice, uPAR‑CAR T shows antitumor activity without significant depletion of myeloid or granulocyte compartments.

session_title: Innovations in Translating Social and Structural Determinants of Health Across Population, Community, and Basic Cancer Research
talk_title: Novel Spatiotemporal Approaches for Built Environment Physical Disorder Measurement
affiliation: Division of Cancer Prevention and Control, Ohio State University College of Medicine
moa_summary: Placek described a workflow that audits Google Street View images to quantify neighborhood physical disorder, creates a composite disinvestment score with high inter‑rater reliability, fits a spatial semivariogram and applies kriging to interpolate exposure values for individual cohort members, and links these exposures to breast cancer survival outcomes, revealing that higher disinvestment predicts shorter survival for stage 2/3 disease.
key_findings: Audited 23,000 street‑view locations with standardized protocol achieving high kappa agreement, demonstrating reliable measurement of physical disorder. | Internal consistency analysis (Cronbach's alpha) justified collapsing multiple disorder items into a single composite disinvestment score. | Spatial kriging based on the fitted semivariogram provided continuous disinvestment estimates for cohort participants lacking exact address‑level data. | In the breast cancer cohort, each unit increase in the disinvestment score was associated with reduced survival time for stage 2 and stage 3 patients in accelerated failure‑time models. | The methodology enables rapid, reproducible, and scalable exposure assessment for built‑environment research across large urban areas.

session_title: Innovations in Translating Social and Structural Determinants of Health Across Population, Community, and Basic Cancer Research
talk_title: Integrating Social Determinants of Health with Cancer Registry Data to Identify Geographic Drivers of Lung and Prostate Cancer
affiliation: Cancer Prevention and Control Program, Fox Chase Cancer Center; Co‑Director, Center for Biostatistics and Epidemiology, Temple University
tumor_types: lung cancer; prostate cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: Lynch integrated publicly available SSDOH datasets (census, EPA, zoning, etc.) with state cancer registry records, used SaTScan to detect census‑tract clusters of excess lung cancer incidence, and applied multivariable logistic regression to identify smoking, air pollution, traffic density, and old housing as independent drivers; similar neighborhood‑wide association studies linked specific SSDOH variables (e.g., public‑transport use, housing quality) to aggressive prostate cancer, demonstrating that macro‑level social and environmental exposures can be quantified and associated with cancer incidence and outcomes.
key_findings: Identified multiple census‑tract clusters in Pennsylvania with significantly higher lung cancer incidence after age, sex, and race adjustment. | In multivariate models, smoking prevalence, ambient air‑pollution levels, traffic density, and proportion of old housing (asbestos proxy) were each significantly associated with cluster status (p<0.05). | Philadelphia‑specific analysis revealed high‑mortality neighborhoods with greater tobacco outlet density and elevated environmental exposures. | Neighborhood‑wide association study of >14,000 Census variables pinpointed five SSDOH factors (e.g., public‑transport use, housing disinvestment) linked to aggressive prostate cancer in Black men and 17 factors in White men. | Combining three top SSDOH variables into an exposure score markedly improved prostate cancer risk prediction beyond PSA alone (probability ≈30% vs 8% for high vs low exposure scores). | Community‑outreach pilots were launched in identified high‑burden areas, including smoking‑cessation programs and air‑quality monitoring collaborations.

talk_title: Preclinical development of EFTX G12V, a first‑in‑class EGFR‑directed KRAS G12V selective siRNA
moa_summary: EFTX G12V is an EGFR‑targeted, chemically modified siRNA that selectively silences KRAS G12V while sparing wild‑type KRAS, achieving high tumor delivery, sustained KRAS knockdown, downstream ERK inhibition, and tumor regression in KRAS G12V‑driven lung and colon models, including immunocompetent settings with CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: EGFR‑directed siRNA achieved ~90% tumor cell uptake after 2 h in xenograft mice. | Selective KRAS G12V silencing at RNA and protein levels with no effect on wild‑type KRAS. | Single‑agent EFTX G12V induced significant tumor growth control and complete responses in KRAS G12V lung (H727) and colon models. | Sustained tumor tissue accumulation and KRAS protein knockdown observed up to 96 h after dosing. | Immunocompetent MPK195 model showed comparable anti‑tumor efficacy and robust CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.

talk_title: Targeting ORC1A to sensitize KRAS‑mutant colorectal cancer to KRAS inhibitors
affiliation: University of Southern California, Lin Jiang Lab
moa_summary: ORC1A inhibition by olaparib synergizes with KRAS G12C inhibitors by suppressing MCL1 stability via GSK3β activation, leading to proteasomal degradation of MCL1 and caspase‑dependent apoptosis in KRAS‑mutant colorectal cancer models.
key_findings: ORC1A is up‑regulated in KRAS‑mutant CRC and induced after KRAS inhibitor treatment. | Combining olaparib with KRAS inhibitors reduces KRAS inhibitor IC50 by ~10‑fold in SLIC14‑63 cells. | Synergy is driven by marked down‑regulation of MCL1 via GSK3β‑mediated proteasomal degradation. | MCL1 knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition phenocopies ORC1A inhibition, confirming MCL1 as functional driver. | Dual inhibition leads to robust tumor regression in KRAS G12C CRC PDX models without overt toxicity.

talk_title: The Kinase Library: a motif‑based platform to infer kinase activity from phosphoproteomics
affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Boston Children’s Hospital (co‑founder of Maride Therapeutics)
moa_summary: The Kinase Library uses combinatorial peptide motifs to define kinase substrate specificity across the human kinome; by scoring phosphosite sequences from quantitative phosphoproteomics, it infers kinase activity changes in any perturbation, enabling mechanistic insights and biomarker discovery without prior interaction data.
key_findings: Motif‑based scoring accurately identified BRAF/ERK activity dynamics after BRAF inhibitor treatment in melanoma cells. | DNA‑damage kinases (ATM/ATR) were temporally resolved after UV exposure, matching known kinetics. | The platform can predict upstream kinases for >95% of phosphosites, vastly expanding annotation beyond PhosphoSitePlus. | Open‑source Python package and web interface released for community use.

talk_title: CHRNA3‑B4 targeted CAR‑T cells for neuroblastoma and neuroendocrine tumors
tumor_types: neuroblastoma; retinoblastoma; small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: DISCO binders recognize the heteropentameric CHRNA3‑CHRNB4 complex on neuroblastoma cells; engineered second‑generation CD28‑CAR T cells bearing these binders selectively activate and kill double‑positive tumor cells, leading to tumor regression and survival benefit in neuroblastoma and retinoblastoma models.
key_findings: ImmunoTar pipeline identified CHRNA3/B4 as a neuroblastoma‑specific surface complex. | DISCO binders specifically bind cells co‑expressing CHRNA3 and CHRNB4, not single‑positive or negative cells. | DISCO CAR T cells mediate potent in‑vitro killing of CHRNA3/B4‑positive neuroblastoma lines. | Single and double‑dose DISCO CAR T treatment induced tumor regression and prolonged survival in neuroblastoma PDX models. | Intravitreal DISCO CAR T delivery eradicated retinoblastoma tumors in mice, preserving ocular integrity.
primary_value: significant regression in 453X and 44X PDX models

talk_title: REST‑driven Notch activation confers chemoresistance and immunotherapy sensitivity in small‑cell lung cancer
moa_summary: REST overexpression suppresses neuroendocrine differentiation, activates Notch signaling, and up‑regulates MHC‑I antigen presentation, leading to chemoresistance but heightened sensitivity to PD‑1 blockade via increased CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: CRISPRa screen identified REST as a top chemoresistance hit in SCLC PDX. | REST overexpression in PDX abrogates cisplatin/topotecan‑induced tumor regression. | REST drives Notch activation (↑ NICD1/3, Hes1) which mediates resistance; gamma‑secretase inhibition restores chemosensitivity. | REST‑high tumors display increased MHC‑I, CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, and respond to anti‑PD‑1 therapy. | Single‑cell analysis revealed a REST‑associated tumor cell cluster enriched for Notch signaling and CX3CL1 expression.
primary_value: minimal in REST‑overexpressing PDX vs robust in control

talk_title: Age‑associated ferroptosis vulnerability in melanoma via WNT5A‑driven phenotype switching
affiliation: Johns Hopkins School of Public Health
moa_summary: Aged dermal fibroblasts secrete factors that induce melanoma phenotype switching (↓MITF, ↑WNT5A), leading to down‑regulation of GPX4 and other ferroptosis defenses; this creates a lipid‑peroxidation‑rich environment that renders aged melanoma cells hypersensitive to ferroptosis inducers such as IKE or RSL3.
key_findings: Aged skin fibroblast conditioned media sensitizes melanoma cells to GPX4 inhibition. | WNT5A expression inversely correlates with MITF and directly with GPX4 down‑regulation. | Aged mice (24 mo) exhibit higher tumor lipid peroxidation and respond to IKE with tumor growth suppression. | Lipid‑peroxidation inhibitor rescues IKE efficacy, confirming ferroptosis as the mechanism. | WNT5A spatially co‑localizes with 4‑HNE in both mouse and human melanoma samples.
primary_value: significant reduction in 24‑month‑old mice treated with IKE

talk_title: Theranostics and Precision Imaging for Prostate Cancer
affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Department of Radiology
moa_summary: Pomper described small-molecule PSMA-targeted radioligands labeled with fluorine-18 for PET imaging and with beta or alpha-emitting radionuclides for therapy; internalization of the ligand provides prolonged tumor residence and DNA damage via emitted particles, enabling both precise diagnosis and targeted radiotherapy.
key_findings: Fluorine-18 labeled PSMA small molecule provides high-contrast PET images of tiny prostate lesions, including bone marrow metastases. | PSMA-617 radioligand therapy yields PSA 50% declines superior to cabazitaxel with milder side effects. | Alpha-emitting astatine-labeled PSMA agents enable intracellular targeting and potential precision dosimetry. | FAP-targeted radiopharmaceutical detected pancreatic cancer lesions missed by CT/MR, suggesting utility for hard-to-image tumors.

talk_title: The Revolution of Antibody Drug Conjugates From Early Concept to Next Generation
tumor_types: breast cancer; ovarian cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Colombo presented next‑generation ADCs that balance payload potency, linker stability, and antibody affinity; examples include a moderate‑potency Top1 payload conjugated to a high‑internalizing FRα antibody (ZW291) and a pan‑RAS inhibitor payload designed for ADC delivery, achieving tumor‑specific drug exposure and bystander killing while minimizing normal‑tissue toxicity.
key_findings: ZW291 achieved >50% ORR in FRα‑positive patients despite using a non‑maximally potent Top1 payload. | Dual‑binding (avidity) approach enabled selective killing of cells with >10,000 receptors/cell, improving therapeutic window. | Pan‑RAS ADC showed tumor regression at 1 mg/kg in CDX models with sustained target inhibition for 14 days, while sparing normal tissue. | Linker stability critically influences ADC PK: moderate‑stable linkers can still yield long half‑life (e.g., T‑DXd), whereas unstable linkers produce rapid payload release (e.g., sacituzumab govitecan). | Bispecific ADCs (e.g., EGFR/HER3) demonstrate activity independent of single‑target expression, suggesting broader applicability.

talk_title: Protein Engineering Driving T‑Cell Engager Innovation
affiliation: Amgen, Vice President of Research and Development
tumor_types: small cell lung cancer; prostate cancer; pancreatic cancer; colorectal cancer; gastric cancer
moa_summary: Coxon described engineered bispecific T‑cell engagers that bind CD3 and tumor antigens (DLL3, STIP1, CDH3/Mesothelin) with a silent Fc for half‑life extension; avidity‑based dual binding (zalyuritamic) and logic‑gated dual‑targeting (AMG 305) improve selectivity and therapeutic window, while combination with PD‑L1 blockade or IL‑2 muteins aims to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.
key_findings: Talatamab achieved median OS 13.6 mo vs 8.3 mo chemotherapy in SCLC (Phase 3). | Frontline talatamab + atezolizumab increased median OS to 25.3 mo, maintaining manageable safety. | Zalyuritamic’s bivalent STIP1 binding yields activity only on cells with >10 k receptors/cell, sparing normal tissue. | AMG 305 shows potent killing of CDH3/mesothelin double‑positive cells with minimal toxicity in preclinical models.

talk_title: TIL Therapy of Solid Cancers: Features and Clinical Activity
affiliation: Netherlands Cancer Institute, Center for Cell Therapy
moa_summary: Hannan detailed autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy: surgical tumor harvest, ex‑vivo expansion with high‑dose IL‑2, lymphodepleting chemotherapy, and reinfusion; single‑cell and TCR dropout analyses identified CD25‑high, tumor‑reactive CD8 “Goldilocks” cells as key predictors of clinical response.
key_findings: TIL therapy improved PFS and ORR versus ipilimumab in PD‑1‑refractory melanoma (HR significant). | Responders harbored abundant CD25+ tumor‑reactive CD8 TILs with IL‑2/STAT5 signaling signatures. | Dropout screens revealed responders recognized more neoantigens and tumor‑associated antigens, while non‑responders showed viral antigen bystander activity. | In real‑world data (n≈100), anti‑PD‑1 pretreated patients achieved 45% ORR, comparable to trial results; prior ipilimumab+nivolumab patients had lower ORR (30%).

talk_title: Digital pathology models AEON and Paladin for tumor subtyping and molecular alteration inference
affiliation: Computational Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; urothelial carcinoma; breast cancer; glioma
moa_summary: AEON uses a knowledge graph–derived representation to infer 124 detailed tumor subtypes directly from H&E whole‑slide images, while Paladin conditions on each subtype to predict the presence of specific molecular alterations, demonstrating that digital pathology can capture phenotype‑genotype relationships for a subset of alterations (~4%).
key_findings: AEON achieved an average one‑vs‑rest AUROC of 0.996 across 124 tumor subtypes in the internal MSK test set. | Multimodal integration of AEON with the GDENS genomic classifier improved performance in both primary and metastatic validation cohorts. | Paladin correctly identified 9 of 11 ovarian granulosa cell tumors and outperformed DeepHistology on most molecular alteration predictions. | Only ~4% of examined molecular alterations showed a strong phenotype‑genotype signal on H&E, confirming limited but actionable predictive value. | Digital pathology aided variant‑of‑unknown‑significance annotation, low‑purity sample inference, and phenocopy detection.

talk_title: Multi‑omic model for glioblastoma recurrence prediction centered on histopathology
moa_summary: A pathology‑foundation model (UniHeal) is fine‑tuned to predict GBM recurrence time from H&E tiles, using MRI radiomics and clinical covariates as inputs, while spatial omics (Xenium, Codex) provide explainability by linking predictive tiles to gene‑program and immune‑cell signatures.
key_findings: Preliminary cohort of 19 GBM patients shows that short‑recurrence tumors exhibit disrupted tissue architecture and necrosis, whereas long‑recurrence tumors retain normal segmentation. | UniHeal embeddings cluster tiles into biologically meaningful groups (e.g., necrotic vs. vascular regions). | Spatial alignment of Xenium and Codex data reveals that longer recurrence is associated with enhanced immune activity, while short recurrence shows immunosuppressive signatures. | A K‑means spatial domain analysis identified an oligodendrocyte‑enriched niche (cluster 8) with increased immune engagement in long‑recurrence tumors.

talk_title: Unified single‑cell and spatial atlas of prostate cancer progression
moa_summary: Integration of >700 k single‑cell profiles across localized, metastatic, and neuroendocrine prostate cancer defines epithelial, immune, and stromal state trajectories, revealing SP1‑macrophage and mCAF‑centered immunosuppressive niches that drive progression and correlate with poor survival.
key_findings: SP1‑macrophage expansion is associated with lymph‑node and bone metastasis and predicts worse overall survival. | mCAF enrichment co‑localizes with SP1‑macrophage in a stromal niche (CN7) linked to poor outcomes. | Anti‑SP1 treatment in bone‑metastasis mouse models reduced tumor burden, pain, and prolonged survival. | NEPC emergence aligns with specific epithelial lineage‑positive states and distinct transcription factor programs.

talk_title: Deep learning profiling of regulatory somatic variants across clonal cancer evolution
moa_summary: A sequence‑based deep learning model (SAIE) predicts the regulatory impact of somatic variants by comparing reference and mutant genome embeddings across 40 learned sequence classes, enabling patient‑level regulatory disruption scores that correlate with progression‑free interval independent of mutational burden.
key_findings: High regulatory disruption scores are associated with significantly worse progression‑free survival across TCGA pan‑cancer cohorts. | Severity of regulatory impact, not the number of regulatory variants, drives the prognostic signal. | Clonal versus subclonal disruption severity provides complementary prognostic information. | Six patient clusters based on disruption profiles show distinct pathway enrichments (e.g., immune vs. cell‑cycle).

talk_title: Spatial multi‑omics integration to guide glioblastoma surgery and engineered macrophage therapy
affiliation: Department of Neurosurgery, MD Anderson Cancer Center
moa_summary: Integrated spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, and mass‑spec imaging of glioblastoma core and edge identifies mesenchymal‑like and AC‑like cell states linked to TGF‑β–driven immunosuppression; engineered macrophages lacking TGF‑β receptors are designed to resist this suppression and improve therapeutic efficacy.
key_findings: Mesenchymal‑like cell states are enriched in hypoxic tumor cores, while AC‑like cells produce TGF‑β2 that activates tumor‑associated macrophages. | SP1‑macrophage and mCAF co‑localization defines an immunosuppressive niche associated with poor survival. | MasSpec Pen can detect metabolic signatures of proliferating (Ki‑67+) tumor cells intra‑operatively, potentially guiding resection margins. | CRISPR‑KO of TGF‑β receptors in engineered macrophages reduces tumor burden and pain in a glioblastoma bone‑metastasis mouse model.

talk_title: ADT Norm: non‑parametric batch correction for single‑cell proteomics across technologies
affiliation: Pharmacology and Computational Biology, MD Anderson Cancer Center
moa_summary: ADT Norm identifies negative and positive peaks in antibody‑derived tag (ADT) distributions using functional data analysis and aligns these landmarks across batches, removing technical variation while preserving biological cell‑type signals for downstream integration of CITE‑seq, CyTOF, and other single‑cell proteomics platforms.
key_findings: ADT Norm aligns background and signal peaks across heterogeneous CITE‑seq batches, achieving superior batch mixing and cell‑type separation compared with SeR normalization. | The method quantifies antibody staining quality, flagging markers with poor signal‑to‑background separation. | An AI‑agent interface enables natural‑language driven parameter tuning for large‑scale multi‑technology integration.

talk_title: Foundation‑model‑driven MHC‑II peptide binding prediction with data‑efficient graph neural network
moa_summary: AlphaFold3 generates structural representations of peptide‑MHC‑II complexes, ESM2 provides sequence embeddings, and a graph neural network trained on low‑volume PepSeq binding data predicts peptide affinity, achieving state‑of‑the‑art performance with only ~2% of traditional training data.
key_findings: The GNN model trained on 2% of the data matches or exceeds NetMHC‑II Pan performance on DRB1 alleles. | Zero‑shot AlphaFold3 scoring (Fisher score) provides moderate predictive power, but the GNN substantially improves AUC. | Orientation errors in AlphaFold3 predictions for HLA‑DP alleles reduce performance, suggesting a need for orientation‑aware modeling. | Training NetMHC‑II Pan on the same limited dataset yields a 10% lower AUC, confirming the data efficiency of the new approach.

talk_title: Spatial metabolomics and microdevice delivery to map intratumoral drug responses
tumor_types: prostate cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; breast cancer
moa_summary: Implantable microdevices release drugs locally; MALDI imaging visualizes spatial drug distribution and metabolic changes. In KRAS‑mutant tumors, inhibition of macropinocytosis with EIPA reduces intracellular amino acids, confirming nutrient acquisition via protein uptake. Metabolomic signatures (purine catabolites, lipid profiles) correlate with immune cell neighborhoods, enabling prediction of immune states from metabolites alone.
key_findings: EIPA delivery via microdevice decreased tumor amino‑acid levels in KRAS‑mutant xenografts, confirming macropinocytosis‑driven nutrient uptake. | DQ‑BSA fluorescence showed lysosomal processing of extracellular protein in KRAS‑mutant but not KRAS‑wildtype cells. | Spatial metabolomics identified two macrophage metabolic states: purine‑rich (pro‑inflammatory) versus lipid‑rich (pro‑tumor). | Logistic regression on metabolite data predicted immune neighborhoods with ROC AUC >0.85, suggesting metabolites can serve as surrogate immune biomarkers. | Microdevice can co‑deliver diverse agents (small molecules, base editors, antisense oligos, ADCs) with release kinetics tuned by PEG molecular weight.

talk_title: Implantable microdevice platform for intratumoral pharmacodynamics and precision treatment selection
tumor_types: glioblastoma; cutaneous T‑cell lymphoma; head and neck cancer; prostate cancer; bladder cancer; pan‑cancer (solid tumors)
topic_categories: combination_therapy; biomarker_development; spatial_biology; clinical_trial; translational; drug_delivery; immunotherapy
moa_summary: The grain‑of‑rice sized implantable microdevice contains ~20 reservoirs delivering micro‑doses of drugs directly into the tumor for 3–5 days. Spatial gradients of drug concentration are measured by MALDI‑MS or fluorescence, while parallel readouts (IHC, spatial transcriptomics, CyTOF) capture pharmacodynamic effects on tumor cells, immune subsets, and signaling pathways, enabling rapid prediction of systemic response and mechanistic biomarker discovery.
key_findings: Implantable microdevices were placed in >350 patients across 13 trials with no serious adverse events. | Local drug concentration gradients correlated with apoptosis (cleaved caspase‑3) and proliferation inhibition (PCNA) in GBM xenografts. | Spatial transcriptomics confirmed on‑target pathway inhibition for osimertinib (EGFR), lapatinib (HER2/EGFR), everolimus (mTOR), and abemaciclib (cell‑cycle). | In a pilot GBM cohort, microdevice‑derived biomarkers predicted clinical temozolomide response in 5/5 patients, outperforming MGMT methylation status. | Interferon response programs were strongly associated with high‑efficacy temozolomide zones, suggesting a role for innate immunity in alkylator sensitivity. | Combination testing on the device (e.g., palbociclib + anti‑CD4) identified synergistic immune remodeling that translated to superior efficacy in preclinical models. | The platform enables rapid, patient‑specific drug selection and early human PD data for investigational agents, accelerating IND‑enabling studies.

talk_title: Granulocyte reprogramming in gastric metaplasia and phosphodiesterase inhibition as neoadjuvant therapy
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Helicobacter infection induces type I interferon production by plasmacytoid dendritic cells, which together with Hedgehog signaling up‑regulates SLFN4 in granulocytic neutrophils, converting them into suppressive MDSCs that consume L‑arginine via arginase‑1 and maintain low cyclic GMP through PDE6 activity. Inhibition of PDE6 with tadalafil or sildenafil raises intracellular cGMP, activates PKG, and triggers apoptosis of these MDSCs, restoring CD8 T‑cell function and enhancing response to standard FLOT chemotherapy.
key_findings: Glee1 knockout mice retain normal gastric architecture despite Helicobacter infection, implicating Glee1‑positive myeloid cells in metaplasia. | SLFN4 expression marks granulocytic MDSCs; knockdown of SLFN4 increases apoptosis and reduces T‑cell suppression in vitro. | IL1B overexpression plus MNU yields gastric tumors in 45‑90% of mice; sildenafil treatment reduces tumor size and SLFN4‑positive cell infiltration. | In a human neoadjuvant window trial (n≈14), 2 weeks of tadalafil 20 mg daily decreased SLFN12L‑positive granulocytic MDSC frequency in tumor biopsies. | Subsequent FLOT chemotherapy after tadalafil further eliminated MDSCs and exhausted T‑cells while expanding cytotoxic CD8 T‑cells. | Bulk RNA‑seq of sorted SLFN4‑positive cells identified strong up‑regulation of PDE6, providing a mechanistic link to cyclic GMP metabolism.

talk_title: Mechanistic insights into KRAS activation and novel direct KRAS inhibitors
affiliation: Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research
moa_summary: McCormick described a covalent inhibitor that targets a histidine-95 residue unique to KRAS, allowing binding to the GTP‑bound state and shifting the switch‑one region to an inactive state‑one conformation. He also presented a PI3K‑alpha “breaker” that blocks the Ras‑PI3K interaction, increasing affinity from ~20 µM to ~6 nM and preventing downstream signaling.
key_findings: SHP2 phosphatase activity is dispensable for RAS activation, indicating a non‑catalytic role in SOS‑mediated signaling. | A covalent inhibitor targeting KRAS histidine‑95 can engage both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound KRAS, overcoming the limitation of off‑state inhibitors. | Binding of the inhibitor to the switch‑two pocket forces switch‑one into an unproductive state‑one conformation, blocking effector interactions. | The PI3K‑alpha breaker increases KRAS‑PI3K binding affinity to low‑nanomolar range, enabling selective disruption of KRAS‑driven PI3K signaling. | Structural studies reveal distinct conformations of RAF activation complexes that may be exploitable for next‑generation RAF inhibitors.

talk_title: Clinical activity of the KRAS G12D inhibitor 3034 combined with EGFR blockade
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; endometrial cancer; NSCLC
moa_summary: Pitt presented the allosteric KRAS inhibitor 3034, which binds the switch‑two pocket and forces switch‑one into an inactive conformation, thereby blocking effector signaling of both G12D and G12V mutants. When combined with EGFR inhibition (cetuximab), the regimen suppresses wild‑type KRAS amplification and upstream RTK feedback, leading to deep tumor regressions and >90% ctDNA clearance in KRAS‑mutant colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cancers.
key_findings: 3034 monotherapy slowed tumor growth in KRAS G12D HPAC xenografts but did not induce regression. | Combination of 3034 with cetuximab produced tumor regressions in the majority of mice and deep partial responses in patients. | ≥90% reduction in KRAS G12D ctDNA correlated with radiographic responses across colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cohorts. | No increase in cetuximab‑related rash or Grade ≥3 adverse events observed with the combination. | Acquired resistance manifested as KRAS wild‑type amplification after ~300 days of therapy, highlighting a limitation of the current inhibitor’s coverage of wild‑type KRAS.
primary_population: KRAS G12D mutant across CRC, PDAC, endometrial

talk_title: Preclinical and early clinical evaluation of a pan‑KRAS on/off state inhibitor (3034)
tumor_types: endometrial cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; NSCLC
moa_summary: The oral pan‑KRAS inhibitor 3034 binds both the inactive and active states of KRAS, suppressing downstream MAPK signaling and shifting the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation while reducing immunosuppressive signals, leading to tumor growth inhibition and shrinkage that can be enhanced by EGFR blockade.
key_findings: Endometrioid endometrial cancer patients showed robust and durable responses despite co‑occurring PI3K pathway alterations. | Combination of 3034 with cetuximab prevented outgrowth observed with monotherapy in preclinical models. | Preclinical mouse studies demonstrated tumor shrinkage at low oral doses (10‑30 mg) with reduced gastrointestinal adverse events compared with earlier KRAS inhibitors.

talk_title: Clinical development of multi‑selective KRAS tricomplex inhibitors (Deruxon Rasib) and combination strategies
tumor_types: NSCLC; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; colorectal cancer
topic_categories: targeted_therapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; immunotherapy
moa_summary: Deruxon Rasib forms a tricomplex with cyclophilin A and KRAS, sterically blocking effector binding and stimulating GTP hydrolysis, thereby shutting down MAPK and PI3K signaling across multiple KRAS mutants; this dual mechanism yields tumor regression and induces immune activation without significant liver toxicity.
key_findings: Resolute‑302 met its primary and secondary endpoints with a disease control rate of 95% in KRAS mutant patients. | PFS and OS curves showed lower confidence‑interval bounds exceeding those of standard second‑line chemotherapy, supporting a survival benefit. | Preclinical models demonstrated durable tumor regression and prevention of relapse when Deruxon Rasib was combined with covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor (Aleron) or G12D inhibitor (Zoldon). | Combination of Deruxon Rasib with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel improved durability of response in pancreatic cancer models, informing the frontline RESOLUTE‑303 trial design. | Resistance mechanisms identified include amplification of the mutant KRAS allele (≈60% of resistant cases) and non‑genomic adaptations (~40%).

talk_title: KEAP1-NRF2 mediated resistance to KRAS inhibition and glutamine metabolism targeting
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; non-small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: Loss of KEAP1 stabilizes NRF2, reprogramming transcription and glutamine metabolism to confer resistance to KRAS G12D and pan‑RAS inhibitors; co‑inhibition of glutamine metabolism with DRP104 restores sensitivity in vitro and in vivo.
key_findings: CRISPR screens identified KEAP1 knockout as a top driver of resistance to KRAS G12D (MRTX1133) and pan‑RAS (RMC7977) inhibitors. | KEAP1 KO cells show NRF2 and NQO1 up‑regulation and reduced sensitivity to KRAS inhibitors in both pancreatic and NSCLC models. | A glutamine antagonist (DRP104) combined with KRAS inhibitors suppresses tumor growth in three resistant in vivo models. | Patient and PDX datasets reveal higher expression of a KEAP1‑deficiency gene signature in KRAS inhibitor‑resistant cases.

talk_title: Selective ULK1/2 inhibitor 2088 synergizes with KRAS pathway blockade
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; melanoma
moa_summary: The ULK1/2 inhibitor 2088 blocks autophagy initiation, enhancing the efficacy of KRAS pathway inhibitors (MEK or KRAS G12C inhibitors) by preventing drug‑tolerant persister cell emergence without clonal selection.
key_findings: 2088 shows higher cellular affinity than existing ULK1 inhibitors in NanoBRET assays. | Combination of 2088 with trametinib (MEK inhibitor) or sotorasib (KRAS G12C inhibitor) yields strong synergy in NSCLC H2030 cells. | In vivo, 2088 plus sotorasib significantly improves tumor growth inhibition compared to monotherapy in Calu‑1 xenografts. | DNA barcode tracking indicates the combination delays clonal outgrowth and reduces drug‑tolerant persister populations.

talk_title: Modeling acquired resistance to pan‑KRAS inhibitor RMC‑6236 in pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: KRAS Y64 mutations disrupt binding of the pan‑KRAS molecular glue RMC‑6236, conferring resistance; resistance can be overcome by MEK inhibition, suggesting downstream MAPK blockade as a viable combination.
key_findings: Y64D mutation abolishes RMC‑6236 binding to KRAS and prevents tri‑complex formation with RAF. | KRAS Y64D engineered cell lines show >50‑fold increase in IC50 to RMC‑6236 compared with wild‑type. | In xenografts, Y64D tumors exhibit markedly reduced response to RMC‑6236 at both low and high doses. | MEK inhibitor restores sensitivity in Y64D mutant models, indicating downstream MAPK blockade can bypass resistance.

talk_title: Combining RAS inhibitors with ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor BBI 825 to prevent ecDNA‑driven resistance
moa_summary: Inhibition of ribonucleotide reductase with BBI 825 limits nucleotide pools, suppressing ecDNA‑driven KRAS amplification and delaying or preventing resistance to KRAS inhibitors in colorectal cancer models.
key_findings: Combination of BBI 825 with adagrasib or sotorasib completely prevented resistance emergence in ecDNA‑positive RW7213 cells. | BBI 825 suppressed KRAS amplification in resistant subclones and reduced MEK amplification in deraxurib‑treated cells. | In RW7213 xenografts, BBI 825 plus adagrasib markedly delayed tumor growth and lowered KRAS amplification frequency. | In CLR142 PDX, addition of BBI 825 enhanced efficacy of sotorasib plus cetuximab and reduced c‑MYC expression.

talk_title: JAK‑STAT pathway inhibition sensitizes KRAS G12D mutant pancreatic cancer to KRAS inhibitors
affiliation: Ruijin Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine
moa_summary: KRKRAS G12D inhibition induces acute STAT3 activation via IL‑6 feedback; STAT3 inhibition restores sensitivity and synergizes with KRAS inhibitors in resistant pancreatic cancer models.
key_findings: Acquired KRAS G12D inhibitor‑resistant PDAC cells display up‑regulated JAK1/STAT3 signaling. | An oral STAT3 inhibitor selectively kills KRAS‑resistant cells and synergizes with KRAS G12D inhibitor in vitro. | Combination of KRAS inhibitor and STAT3 inhibitor leads to durable tumor regressions in KPC CDX models, overcoming relapse seen with KRAS inhibitor alone. | JAK1 inhibitor tofacitinib also blocks phospho‑STAT3 and enhances KRAS inhibitor efficacy.
primary_population: advanced PDAC patients receiving HRS‑4642 + gemcitabine + pardusol

talk_title: VEGFR2 blockade overcomes KRAS G12D inhibitor resistance driven by PI3Kγ activation
moa_summary: Acquired resistance to KRAS G12D inhibition activates a KRAS‑PI3Kγ‑AKT‑SP1 axis that drives VEGF‑A secretion and autocrine VEGFR2 signaling; VEGFR2 blockade with DC101 restores sensitivity.
key_findings: Resistant PDOs show increased VEGF‑A expression and phospho‑VEGFR2 activation. | CRISPR‑mediated VEGFR2 knockout or DC101 treatment sensitizes resistant cells to MRTX1133. | PI3Kγ inhibition reduces VEGF‑A/VEGFR2 signaling and re‑sensitizes resistant models. | Combination of MRTX1133 with DC101 markedly suppresses tumor growth in multiple resistant xenograft models.

talk_title: KRAS G12C inhibition induces chromosomal instability creating a vulnerability to Aurora kinase A inhibition
moa_summary: KRAS G12C inhibition induces DNA damage and micronuclei formation in a subset of NSCLC cells, activating ATR/ATM pathways that stabilize cyclin B1; Aurora kinase A inhibition forces mitotic catastrophe in these cells.
key_findings: KRAS G12C inhibitor LY3499446 induces variable DNA damage and micronuclei across NSCLC lines. | Aurora kinase A inhibitor LSN3321213 shows the strongest synergy with KRAS G12C inhibition, especially in micronuclei‑high cells. | Combination treatment leads to prolonged G2/M arrest and mitotic catastrophe, markedly reducing colony formation. | In vivo, low‑dose AURKA inhibition combined with KRAS G12C inhibitor produces tumor regression and extends relapse‑free survival.

talk_title: TAP2 downregulation drives immune evasion and can be therapeutically reversed
affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Department of Oncology
tumor_types: lung cancer; colorectal cancer; brain metastases
topic_categories: immunotherapy; tumor_microenvironment; spatial_biology; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Downregulation of TAP2 in lung cancer cells reduces antigen presentation modestly (10‑15% peptide loss) but more critically impairs interferon‑γ signaling via upregulation of the inhibitor SOX1 and suppression of chemokines (CXCL10, CCL5, CXCL11), leading to T‑cell exclusion. IL‑4 produced by tumor‑associated myeloid cells epigenetically silences TAP2, an effect reversible by IL‑4R blockade or epigenetic drugs (HDAC inhibitors, taxanes). Restoring TAP2 re‑sensitizes cells to cytokine signaling and tumor‑antigen specific killing. Separately, secreted factors such as PLA2G10 act as active T‑cell excluders, limiting lymphocyte migration and promoting tumor growth; blocking these molecules restores T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: 24% of treatment‑naïve lung cancers show cancer‑cell selective TAP2 downregulation; only 6% show TAP1 loss. | Low TAP2 expression predicts an 80% increased risk of death (HR 1.8) after immunotherapy, with no effect on chemotherapy outcomes. | TAP2 knockout reduces tumor‑antigen specific CD8 T‑cell killing more than TAP1 loss; re‑expression of TAP2 rescues killing. | TAP2 loss leads to a 10‑15% reduction in total HLA‑bound peptides but causes a >50% decrease in interferon‑γ‑induced gene expression (e.g., IDO1, PD‑L1) via SOX1 up‑regulation. | IL‑4 secreted by CD11b+ myeloid cells epigenetically closes the TAP2 promoter, an effect not reversed by exogenous IFN‑γ/TNF‑α. | HDAC inhibitors (vorinostat), taxanes, and dasatinib up‑regulate TAP2 without increasing PD‑L1, restoring antigen presentation and T‑cell killing in vitro. | Secreted phospholipase PLA2G10 acts as a T‑cell excluder; overexpression in mouse models accelerates tumor growth and reduces CD8 infiltration, while anti‑PLA2G10 antibody restores T‑cell entry. | Spatial analysis of 250 lung cancers identifies ~20% with a dominant immune‑excluded phenotype, which correlates with poorer immunotherapy survival.
primary_population: low TAP2 lung cancer patients treated with immunotherapy

talk_title: Chromatin redistribution of Rb reveals estrogen‑response activation by CDK4/6 inhibition
