key_findings: Analysis of IND and BLA submissions showed that three‑month general toxicology studies are often unnecessary for CD3 bispecific T‑cell engagers because target binding is absent in animals and chronic exposure cannot be achieved. | For antibody‑drug conjugates, toxicity was attributed to the small‑molecule payload rather than the antibody, indicating that three‑month non‑human primate studies add little value and can be limited to rodent studies. | Weight‑of‑evidence approaches, incorporating literature, clinical data, and NAMs, can replace embryo‑fetal developmental and chronic toxicity studies without compromising patient safety.

talk_title: Incorporating New Approach Methodologies, or NAMs, in Oncology Drug Development
affiliation: Division of Hematology, Oncology, and Toxicology, FDA
moa_summary: NAMs—including hiPSC‑cardiomyocytes, organoid/spheroid cultures, and organ‑on‑chip systems—are used as human‑relevant in‑vitro platforms to replace or supplement animal toxicology studies, particularly for QT prolongation, organ‑specific toxicities, and reproductive toxicity assessments in oncology INDs.
key_findings: A total of 139 hiPSC‑CM studies were submitted to the FDA from 2012‑2025, representing only ~1% of commercial IND submissions per year. | 301 organoid/spheroid studies were identified across divisions (2008‑2025); 91% of organoid and 86% of spheroid submissions in oncology were for pharmacology, with only 6‑9% submitted as safety NAMs. | Organ‑on‑chip models were successfully used in a bispecific antibody case to replace animal studies for first‑in‑human dosing, supporting safety and starting dose selection. | Complex NAMs were identified in only eight INDs, with four proposing replacement of animal studies, indicating early but growing adoption.

talk_title: A Glimpse into the Future in Bioinformatics
affiliation: Division of Hematology, Oncology, Toxicology, FDA – Oncology Center of Excellence
moa_summary: A curated FDA oncology IND database integrates mechanisms, targets, and product classes to enable rapid weight‑of‑evidence assessments, inform starting dose strategies, and evaluate the necessity of repeat‑dose toxicology studies, supporting potential reductions in animal use.
key_findings: The FDA bioinformatics team has compiled a searchable database of over 8,000 commercial oncology INDs, cataloguing mechanisms, targets, and modality classes. | Analysis shows a steady increase in oncology IND submissions since 2010, with both biologics and small molecules contributing to the rise. | Retrospective studies are planned to compare one‑month versus three‑month repeat‑dose toxicology results with clinical safety outcomes to identify when non‑rodent studies may be omitted.

talk_title: Bone Repair External State Program Drives Cancer Cell Dedifferentiation and Immune Invasion in Melanoma
affiliation: Broad Institute, Fay Chen's Lab, Harvard
moa_summary: Spatial profiling identified anti‑inflammatory hubs enriched for SPP1⁺ macrophages and POSTN⁺ fibroblasts that exclude CD8⁺ T cells, induce a dysfunctional T‑cell state, and via secreted tissue‑repair ligands drive melanoma dedifferentiation to a neural‑crest‑like state and protect tumor cells from cytokine‑mediated killing.
key_findings: Identified an anti‑inflammatory hub composed of SPP1⁺ macrophages and POSTN⁺ fibroblasts that is largely T‑cell excluded. | T cells within the anti‑inflammatory hub are dysfunctional singleton clonotypes lacking expansion. | Ligand program from the hub (SPP1, POSTN) induces melanoma cells to dedifferentiate from a melanocytic to a neural‑crest‑like state in vitro. | SPP1 pretreatment protects neural‑crest‑state melanoma cells from killing by IFN‑γ, TNF‑α and other cytotoxic cytokines. | Spatial correlation shows anti‑inflammatory hubs predict higher neural‑crest trajectory scores, linking niche composition to tumor dedifferentiation and immune evasion.

talk_title: Tumor‑Specific Antibodies Elicited by Engineered Bacteria Promote Bladder Cancer Immunotherapy
affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Microbiology and Immunology
moa_summary: Engineered probiotic E. coli Nissle with a synchronized lysis circuit secretes CXCL13 within bladder tumors, recruits B and T cells to form tertiary lymphoid structures in draining lymph nodes, generates tumor‑specific IgG antibodies, and synergizes with PD‑1 blockade to double the cure rate in mice.
key_findings: Engineered E. coli Nissle selectively colonizes bladder tumors without spreading to normal tissue. | Intravesical delivery of CXCL13‑producing bacteria induces TLS formation in draining lymph nodes and increases tumor‑specific IgG antibodies. | Combination of CXCL13‑bacteria with anti‑PD‑1 cures 50% of mice versus 25% with anti‑PD‑1 alone. | Efficacy requires T follicular helper cells; depletion of these cells abolishes therapeutic benefit. | Treatment is well tolerated with no weight loss or systemic toxicity compared with controls.
primary_population: MB49 orthotopic mice receiving bacteria + anti‑PD‑1

talk_title: Integrating Single‑Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics with Mouse Models to Understand How Tumor‑Associated Macrophages Suppress Antitumor Immunity
affiliation: University of Chicago, Labs of Thomas Gajewski and Samantha Rasenfeld
moa_summary: Host‑derived GPNMB deficiency reprograms tumor‑associated macrophages toward an interferon‑driven pro‑inflammatory phenotype, expands CXCL10⁺ macrophages, enhances CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and activation, and markedly improves tumor control; the effect requires tissue‑resident (LysM‑Cre) but not bone‑marrow‑derived macrophages.
key_findings: Gpnmb‑/- mice exhibit significantly reduced tumor growth and increased survival in melanoma, breast, and lung metastasis models. | Knockout tumors show higher CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, activation (PD‑1⁺, TIM‑3⁺) and increased memory phenotypes. | scRNA‑seq reveals expansion of CXCL10⁺ pro‑inflammatory macrophages and contraction of mitochondrial‑rich, immunosuppressive macrophages. | GPNMB loss decreases fatty‑acid uptake in both pro‑ and anti‑inflammatory tumor macrophages. | Tumor control is abolished after systemic T‑cell depletion and only partially reduced after CD8⁺ depletion, indicating dependence on T cells. | LysM‑Cre–mediated Gpnmb deletion (targeting tissue‑resident macrophages) recapitulates tumor control, whereas Vav1‑Cre (hematopoietic) deletion does not.

talk_title: Vaccine Stimulation of Bystander T Cells in the Lymph Node Unexpectedly Promotes Tumor‑Specific Host Immunity to Regress Solid Tumors
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Departments of Pathology, Laboratory Medicine and Bioengineering
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; melanoma; breast cancer
moa_summary: An amphiphile vaccine targeting a generic FITC‑CAR on adoptively transferred T cells triggers rapid IFN‑γ release, transiently degrades the fibrotic PDAC capsule, up‑regulates MHC I/II on tumor cells, and activates endogenous CD8⁺ T cells, leading to swift tumor regression even when the CAR does not recognize tumor antigen; efficacy is IFN‑γ dependent and enhanced by anti‑PD‑L1 blockade.
key_findings: Vaccine boosting of tumor‑irrelevant CAR T cells induces >50% tumor regression within 24 h across PDAC, melanoma, and breast cancer models. | Efficacy is abolished by IFN‑γ neutralization, demonstrating cytokine dependence. | Tumor regression persists after CAR T depletion at 3 days, indicating reliance on endogenous immunity. | Serum IFN‑γ and CCL2 spike transiently after vaccination, coinciding with rapid degradation of the PDAC fibrotic capsule. | MHC I up‑regulation on tumor cells and activation of host CD8⁺ T cells are essential; B2m‑KO tumors are resistant. | Combination with anti‑PD‑L1 further deepens responses, suggesting synergy with checkpoint blockade.
primary_population: orthotopic PDAC, melanoma, and breast cancer bearing mice

talk_title: IL‑18‑secreting CAR T cells uncover divergent immune mechanisms of antigen‑heterogeneous solid tumor clearance
affiliation: Stanford University School of Medicine, Lab of Crystal Mackall
tumor_types: melanoma; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; breast cancer
moa_summary: IL‑18‑secreting CD19 CAR T cells, despite low persistence, remodel the myeloid compartment by expanding activated neutrophils and macrophages with IFN‑γ‑responsive gene signatures, leading to rapid clearance of antigen‑heterogeneous tumors independent of adaptive CD8⁺ T‑cell priming, while Rag1‑KO mice retain efficacy, indicating a dominant innate mechanism.
key_findings: IL‑18 CAR T cells achieve superior heterogeneous tumor control compared with IL‑36, FLT3‑L, or CAR‑only controls. | IL‑18 CAR T cells show low persistence in tumor and lymph nodes but accumulate in the spleen. | Endogenous CD8⁺ T cells display an effector‑memory phenotype but reduced tumor‑reactive ELISpot responses. | Myeloid cells, especially neutrophils and macrophages, are markedly expanded and exhibit IFN‑γ‑responsive and antigen‑presentation gene signatures. | Neutrophil depletion does not impair IL‑18 CAR efficacy, suggesting macrophages are the critical mediators. | Rag1‑KO mice retain tumor control with IL‑18 CARs, indicating independence from adaptive immunity.
primary_population: heterogeneous B16 tumors treated with IL‑18 CAR T cells

talk_title: Gut Microeukaryotes, Endogenous Viruses, and Cancer
moa_summary: Nguyen described population-scale analyses linking gut microeukaryote Blastocystis prevalence to plant-based diets, favorable cardiometabolic markers, lower adiposity, and reduced odds of colorectal cancer, and presented trans-kingdom dysbiosis in MASLD driven by oral bacteria, acylcarnitine metabolites, and endogenous viruses that may modulate bacterial communities via predator-prey dynamics.
key_findings: Blastocystis prevalence exceeds 40% in several under‑studied regions (e.g., Tanzania, Fiji) and is higher in adults than children, suggesting non‑vertical transmission. | Higher adherence to plant‑based diets correlates with increased Blastocystis carriage (median AUC ~0.85 for bacterial composition predicting Blastocystis). | Blastocystis‑positive individuals show improved fasting/postprandial triglycerides, higher HDL, lower GlicA, and a 6% decrease in odds of carriage per BMI point increase. | Case‑control analysis shows a 29% lower odds of Blastocystis in colorectal cancer and IBD samples versus healthy controls. | In a 1,100‑person dietary RCT, Blastocystis prevalence modestly expanded in participants achieving weight‑loss goals on high‑quality diets. | MASLD cases exhibit enrichment of oral‑typical bacteria (e.g., Streptococcus spp.) distinct from obesity‑associated signatures. | Acylcarnitines are the most enriched metabolite class in MASLD, with chain‑length–specific associations to microbial taxa. | Endogenous viruses, especially unclassified taxa and Punavirus P1, differentiate MASLD subtypes; MASLD‑associated viruses have larger genomes, higher GC, and more ORFs. | Computational phage‑host predictions suggest MASLD‑enriched viruses target and kill their bacterial hosts, indicating predator‑prey dynamics.

talk_title: Population‑scale EBV Persistence and Host Genetics
moa_summary: La Roe demonstrated that latent EBV DNA can be quantified from existing whole‑genome sequencing data, identified a high‑EBV‑load subpopulation (~10%) and linked host genetic variation at 45 loci—many in antigen presentation and B‑cell pathways—to persistence, which in turn associates with autoimmune, respiratory, and cancer phenotypes, and provided preliminary evidence of geographic strain variation.
key_findings: Approximately 10% of individuals in UK Biobank and All of Us have high EBV DNA levels detectable in blood WGS, despite ~90% seropositivity. | Masking low‑complexity repetitive EBV regions is essential; otherwise 40% of samples would be mis‑classified. | GWAS of 400,000 European‑ancestry participants identified 45 independent loci (antigen presentation, B‑cell transcription factors) associated with EBV persistence. | Genetic risk for EBV persistence overlaps with risk loci for systemic lupus, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis. | High‑EBV carriers show increased incidence of HIV infection, rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, lung cancer, and lymphomas across health‑record ICD‑10 data. | EBV strain analysis reveals 31 known variants; four novel variants are enriched in high‑EBV carriers and are being experimentally followed up. | Geographic comparison shows concordant EBV allele frequencies between UK and US cohorts, suggesting shared strain circulation.

talk_title: Cancer Mycobiome: Truth, Myth, and Current Data
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; stomach cancer; head and neck cancer; esophageal cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: Iliev highlighted that tumor‑associated fungi, especially Candida albicans, are present in the mucosal layer of gastrointestinal tumors, where strain‑specific production of the peptide toxin candidalysin induces IL‑17/IL‑1 inflammation and promotes tumorigenesis; antifungal fluconazole clears gut Candida and restores beneficial bacterial diversity and butyrate production, suggesting a causal role for the mycobiome in cancer progression.
key_findings: Fungal DNA (Candida albicans) is detectable in multiple GI cancers (CRC, stomach, esophageal, head‑and‑neck) but not in brain tumors. | Strain‑level analysis shows diverse C. albicans isolates with variable hyphal formation and candidalysin production. | In AOM/DSS mouse models, C. albicans colonization aggravates tumor development, whereas C. glabrata does not. | Fluconazole treatment clears both oral and gut Candida, whereas oral nystatin only clears oral colonization. | Systemic antifungal therapy restores bacterial diversity, notably increasing butyrate‑producing Faecalibacterium prausnitzii and Roseburia intestinalis, and raises fecal butyrate levels. | Candida‑positive tumors exhibit elevated IL‑17 and IL‑1 cytokine signatures linked to candidalysin activity. | Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) that enriches bacterial communities can displace Candida, reducing mucosal inflammation.

talk_title: LINE1 ORF1p as a pan‑cancer biomarker and ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule detection assay
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; gastric cancer; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; ovarian cancer; pancreatic cancer; esophageal cancer; gastroesophageal cancer; colon adenoma; STIC lesions; breast cancer
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; biomarker_development; early_detection; platform_technology; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: LINE1 ORF1p is de‑repressed in cancer and high‑risk precursors, producing a tumor‑specific protein detectable in plasma via ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule sandwich assays; assay sensitivity improves with custom nanobody pairs and bar‑coded Mosaic detection, enabling early‑stage cancer detection and real‑time monitoring of treatment response.
key_findings: LINE1 ORF1p is expressed in >99% of examined carcinomas and high‑risk precursors across organ systems. | First‑generation single‑molecule assay detected ORF1p in 50% of cancer plasma samples while remaining undetectable in 402/406 healthy controls. | Second‑generation nanobody‑based assays increased early‑stage ovarian cancer sensitivity to 83% without loss of specificity. | Adding ORF1p to a CA125+HE4 panel raised AUC from 0.94 to 0.98 in a pilot ovarian cancer cohort. | Serial ORF1p levels perfectly segregated responders from non‑responders in a gastroesophageal cancer monitoring cohort (p<0.001). | Mosaic assay achieved detection in 100% of gastroesophageal trial samples, outperforming Simoa.

talk_title: Naturally occurring antibodies as biomarkers for early cancer detection and outcome prediction
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center
tumor_types: lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; ovarian cancer; melanoma; renal cell carcinoma; hepatocellular carcinoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; biomarker_development; early_detection; clinical_trial; translational; other
moa_summary: Naturally occurring autoantibodies arise against tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., NY‑ESO‑1, p53, MMP14, LINE1 ORF1p) and viral antigens; high‑throughput protein arrays and PhIP‑seq identify antibody signatures that can differentiate cancer patients from controls, predict early disease, and correlate with immunotherapy outcomes.
key_findings: Single autoantibody markers show modest AUCs; panels of multiple antibodies substantially improve diagnostic performance. | p53 autoantibodies combined with CA125 outperform CA125 alone for ovarian cancer detection. | Anti‑interferon antibodies are enriched in responders to checkpoint inhibition across several tumor types, suggesting a predictive biomarker. | A viral exposure antibody signature predicts poorer survival in HCC patients receiving immunotherapy and rises longitudinally before HCC diagnosis. | IgG deposition is abundant on tumor cells in tissue sections, and cloned autoantibodies (e.g., against MMP14) can enhance phagocytosis and NK‑cell ADCC.
primary_metric: AUC (p53 autoantibody + CA125 vs CA125 alone)

talk_title: Unbiased autoantibody atlas for early cancer detection
moa_summary: The study builds a large-scale atlas of autoantibodies by profiling serum from liver disease cohorts using human protein arrays and FIP‑seq, revealing distinct autoantibody signatures that separate HCC from cirrhosis/hepatitis and identifying antibodies that correlate with clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Principal component analysis of autoantibody profiles segregates HCC patients from cirrhosis and hepatitis cohorts. | Heat‑map clustering shows a distinct blue cluster of HCC samples versus red cluster of non‑cancer liver disease. | Autoantibodies against a PD‑L1 chaperone protein are enriched in HCC patients with longer overall survival. | The planned CRUK Grand Challenge atlas will expand to >16,000 individuals across high‑risk groups (genetic predisposition, heavy smokers, obese, centenarians, twins). | Baseline autoantibody patterns are being evaluated as predictors of response to checkpoint inhibitor neoadjuvant therapy.

talk_title: Multi-Cancer Early Detection: Where Are We and Where Do We Want to Go?
affiliation: Queen Mary University of London, Center of Cancer Screening, Prevention, and Early Detection
moa_summary: Peter reviewed the landscape of multi‑cancer early detection (MCED) tests, highlighting that current commercial assays (cfDNA methylation, protein‑mutation panels, fragmentomics, CTC immunocytochemistry, electrical properties) achieve high specificity (≥96.4%) but variable sensitivity (20‑70%) that depends on cancer type and stage. He discussed trial outcomes, notably the NHS Galleri RCT which failed to meet its primary stage‑3/4 reduction endpoint but showed a trend toward fewer stage‑4 diagnoses and increased early‑stage detection, and PATHFINDER 2 which demonstrated a 62% PPV and 74% episode sensitivity for 12 cancers.
key_findings: All five commercial MCED tests have specificities ≥96.4%, but a specificity of 99.6% would reduce false positives nine‑fold compared with 96.4%. | Reported sensitivities vary widely (20‑70%) and are lower for early‑stage cancers, making stage‑specific performance a critical limitation. | Galleri’s NHS RCT did not achieve a statistically significant reduction in stage 3/4 cancers, though a reduction in stage 4 disease was observed. | PATHFINDER 2 reported a PPV of 62% and an episode sensitivity of 74% for 12 targeted cancers in an asymptomatic cohort. | Overall detection rate of cancers with current MCED tests is approximately 1 in 200 screened, with >50% of positive tests leading to a cancer diagnosis.

talk_title: Navigating from Hype to Hope in Multi-Cancer Early Detection
affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Public Health Science Division, Rosalind and Harold Ray Brown Chair
moa_summary: Ruth dissected the conceptual gap between diagnostic performance and clinical benefit, emphasizing that PPV and sensitivity are often conflated, that early‑stage sensitivity is hard to ascertain, and that down‑staging (stage 3/4 reduction) may not reliably predict mortality benefit without robust modeling of sojourn times and incidence patterns.
key_findings: PPV and sensitivity are distinct metrics; confusion between them fuels hype around MCED performance. | Early‑stage sensitivity cannot be directly measured in most diagnostic studies and must be inferred from stage‑specific incidence data. | Late‑stage (stage 3/4) reduction, as used in the Galleri trial, does not consistently correlate with mortality benefit across cancer types. | Modeling of sojourn times shows that cancers with very short early‑stage windows (e.g., ovarian) may not benefit from high‑sensitivity tests.

talk_title: NCI Director's Address: Funding, Policy, and Future Priorities
moa_summary: Dr. Lattai outlined the NCI's funding landscape, highlighting a 34% decline in US cancer death rates since 1991, a $128 M budget increase to $7.3 B for FY2026, and a shift to multi‑year and Select Pay funding mechanisms that maintain scientific merit while providing flexibility. He emphasized new priorities such as functional precision medicine, cancer vaccine trials, prevention and screening initiatives, and efforts to accelerate clinical trial activation.
key_findings: US cancer death rates have fallen 34% since 1991, reflecting improved screening, prevention, and therapies. | NCI FY2026 budget increased by $128 M to a total of $7.3 B, representing a record level of extramural funding. | Approximately 200 new competing and 1,500 non‑competing extramural grants have been processed for FY2026. | Projected R01 success rate for FY2026 is ~11%, with a target of ~120 Early Stage Investigator awards. | NCI will fund three new therapeutic cancer vaccine trials over the next three years through a public‑private partnership with FNIH.

talk_title: Stepping Stones program overview and resource support
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Developmental Therapeutics Program
moa_summary: The Stepping Stones program provides gap‑filling, IND‑enabling services (toxicology, PK, formulation, synthetic route optimization, ADME/DMPK) for NCI‑funded small‑molecule projects, aiming to de‑risk leads and improve eligibility for subsequent funding such as NExT, SBIR, or venture capital.
key_findings: Six rounds of the Stepping Stones program have been completed, supporting 17 distinct projects across various malignancies and a chemotherapy‑induced peripheral neuropathy indication. | Supported activities have included formulation work (e.g., salt screens, oral suspensions), synthetic route scale‑up, mouse and rat PK studies, ADME/DMPK profiling (metabolic stability, CYP, HERG), and biomarker assay development. | Several investigators who received Stepping Stones support subsequently secured SBIR/STTR funding, while others refined chemistry or advanced compounds toward startup formation.

talk_title: Ceramide-based colorectal cancer risk score and DES1 inhibitor studies
affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute
moa_summary: The consortium developed a ceramide-based risk score for colorectal cancer by integrating prospective cohort lipidomics with genetic analyses of ceramide pathway genes. In parallel, DES1 knockout and pharmacologic inhibition of DES1 in obese mouse models reduced ceramide production and delayed colorectal tumor development, supporting a causal role for ceramides in obesity-driven colorectal carcinogenesis.
key_findings: A ceramide-based risk score predicts colorectal cancer risk in prospective human cohorts. | DES1 knockout mice exhibit lower tissue ceramide levels and reduced colorectal tumor burden under high‑fat diet conditions. | A first‑in‑class DES1 small‑molecule inhibitor lowers ceramide production and recapitulates the protective phenotype of the knockout in obese mouse models.

talk_title: AFABP as circulating biomarker and immunotherapy target in high‑risk breast cancer
moa_summary: AFABP (FABP4) mediates fatty‑acid transport and signaling in adipose tissue; its overexpression in obesity creates a lipid‑rich microenvironment that promotes breast tumor growth. Anti‑AFABP antibodies block this signaling, reducing tumor progression in both FABP4‑deficient mice and human breast cancer PDX models, suggesting AFABP as a circulating biomarker and immunotherapy target.
key_findings: Circulating AFABP levels are significantly higher in women with high‑risk breast cancer compared with controls. | Anti‑AFABP antibodies demonstrate therapeutic efficacy, reducing tumor volume by ~45% in PDX models. | Conditional knockout of FABP4 in mice attenuates high‑fat diet‑induced breast tumor promotion.

talk_title: Visceral fat‑induced inflammation inflammatype for colorectal and liver cancer risk
moa_summary: Using cohorts with direct visceral adiposity measurements (DEXA) and comprehensive plasma inflammatory profiling, the team identified a multi‑protein “inflammatype” that reflects visceral fat‑driven inflammation. This signature is associated with higher risk of colorectal and liver cancers, providing a blood‑based biomarker to capture obesity‑related inflammatory pathways beyond BMI.
key_findings: An inflammatype derived from visceral fat‑associated proteins predicts colorectal cancer risk (hazard ratio ~1.6 in top quartile). | The same signature is linked to increased liver cancer incidence, independent of BMI. | Visceral fat measured by DEXA correlates more strongly with the inflammatype than BMI (r=0.48 vs 0.22).

talk_title: Biospecimen science funding opportunities and Moonshot Biobank overview
affiliation: Acting Chief, Biorepositories and Biospecimen Research Branch, NCI
moa_summary: Dr. Agrawal described the NCI biospecimen science infrastructure that standardizes pre‑analytical processes, provides curated specimen banks (NCCN, CHTN, Moonshot), and funds multidisciplinary U01 projects to generate reproducible, clinically annotated biospecimens for assay validation across solid and hematologic cancers.
key_findings: The U01 biospecimen science program has funded 24 grants covering a broad range of cancer types. | From 2022‑2024, the CHTN network served 618 unique researchers, distributing 117 specimens and supporting 122 publications. | The Cancer Moonshot Biobank enrolled ~1,300 patients, with 1,020 having longitudinal biospecimens, including ~300 with multiple blood draws and ~150 with paired tissue collections. | Diversity metrics of the Moonshot cohort show 18% Black/African American and 8% Hispanic participants, exceeding typical US trial representation.
primary_population: U01 mechanism grants across cancer types

talk_title: Acetylcholine signaling by T and B cells modulates tumor and liver regeneration
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; liver cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: T cell-derived acetylcholine promotes vascular dilation and immune cell trafficking, while B cell-derived acetylcholine is essential for liver regeneration after partial hepatectomy; loss of acetylcholine in T cells reduces tumor growth and alters immune phenotypes.
key_findings: B cell-specific deletion of Chat leads to lethality after 70% partial hepatectomy, indicating essential role in liver regeneration | T cell-specific Chat deletion reduces tumor growth and alters vascular density in mouse models | Serum choline levels correlate with patient prognosis across multiple cancer types

talk_title: Profiling and targeting innervating peripheral neurons in pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: Fast‑Blue retrograde tracing identifies pancreas‑innervating sympathetic and sensory neurons; denervation with 6‑OHDA or nab‑paclitaxel reduces neuronal density, reprograms CAFs to a pro‑inflammatory state, enhances CD8 T‑cell infiltration, and synergizes with PD‑1 blockade to suppress tumor growth.
key_findings: Fast‑Blue tracing reveals identical numbers of neurons innervating normal pancreas and PDAC, but PDAC shows massive axonal sprouting | Denervation with 6‑OHDA (sympathetic) or nab‑paclitaxel (sensory) individually slows tumor growth; combined denervation nearly abolishes tumor progression in mouse models | Denervation converts immunosuppressive CAFs to pro‑inflammatory phenotype and increases CD8 T‑cell infiltration | Preclinical data support a phase I/II trial (SYNERGY) combining celiac ganglion radiotherapy, nab‑paclitaxel/gemcitabine, and PD‑1 inhibition

talk_title: Nociceptor neurons drive CD8 T‑cell exhaustion via CGRP in melanoma
moa_summary: Melanoma activates nociceptor neurons to release CGRP, which binds RAMP1 on CD8 T cells, driving an exhausted phenotype (↑PD‑1, LAG‑3, TIM‑3, ↓IFN‑γ); genetic or pharmacologic blockade of CGRP‑RAMP1 signaling restores T‑cell function and reduces tumor growth.
key_findings: Ablation of TRPV1+ nociceptors reduces melanoma growth and increases IFN‑γ+ CD8 T cells in tumors | Co‑culture of DRG neurons with melanoma up‑regulates CGRP expression in neurons | Exogenous CGRP induces PD‑1, LAG‑3, TIM‑3 up‑regulation and IFN‑γ loss in CD8 T cells; effect absent in RAMP1‑KO T cells | RAMP1‑deficient CD8 T cells show reduced exhaustion and slower tumor growth when transferred into melanoma‑bearing mice | High RAMP1 expression in melanoma patients associates with worse survival and reduced response to checkpoint blockade | Retrospective analysis of migraine patients on CGRP antagonists suggests improved outcomes in melanoma with lung metastasis

talk_title: Engineering biparatopic antibodies for FGFR2 fusion targeting and ADC development
affiliation: Red Ridge Bio (academic affiliation not specified)
tumor_types: intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma; gastric cancer; breast cancer; colon cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; ADC; combination_therapy; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Biparatopic antibodies BC and BD simultaneously engage two distinct epitopes on the extracellular domain of FGFR2 fusions, increasing avidity, promoting receptor internalization and lysosomal degradation, and retaining activity against kinase‑domain resistance mutations; when conjugated to ADC payloads they induce tumor regression in FGFR2‑amplified models.
key_findings: Biparatopic antibodies BC and BD showed >2‑fold higher solution affinity and avidity compared with parental antibodies. | BC and BD blocked colony formation of FGFR2‑driven BaF3 cells more effectively than parental antibodies. | In FGFR2‑OPTN PDX, BC and BD inhibited tumor growth (~20% regression) whereas small‑molecule FGFR2 inhibitor achieved ~20% regression, demonstrating comparable efficacy. | Tumors treated with BC/BD showed near‑complete loss of surface FGFR2, indicating receptor down‑regulation via internalization. | BC and BD retained internalization and anti‑proliferative activity against FGFR2 kinase‑domain resistance mutants. | ADC conjugates of BC (MMAF, MMAE, deruxtecan) induced tumor regression across breast, colon, and gastric models, with deruxtecan showing the most consistent bystander effect.

talk_title: Zenitatamab (ZAN) bi‑paratopic HER2 antibody: unique CDC activity and clinical benefit
affiliation: Designworks (executive director of multispecific antibody therapeutics)
tumor_types: biliary tract cancer; gastric cancer; breast cancer; HER2‑positive solid tumors
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Zenitatamab binds HER2 ECD2 and ECD4 on adjacent HER2 molecules (trans bi‑paratopic binding), clustering receptors into large caps that trigger rapid complement activation (CDC) and enhanced internalization, leading to superior tumor cell killing compared with the trastuzumab + pertuzumab combination.
key_findings: Zenitatamab shows 1.5‑fold higher cell‑surface saturation than trastuzumab or pertuzumab alone, indicating trans bi‑paratopic binding. | Super‑resolution microscopy revealed larger HER2 clusters (caps) with ZAN versus microclusters with the antibody combination. | ZAN mediates potent CDC, achieving near‑100% cytotoxicity within 15 min, whereas trastuzumab + pertuzumab show no CDC. | In HER2‑positive biliary tract PDX, ZAN induced complete tumor regression at doses equal or lower than the trastuzumab + pertuzumab combination. | Phase III HORIZON‑GEA101 demonstrated significant PFS (12.4 mo vs 8.0 mo) and a trend toward OS benefit (24.4 mo vs 19.2 mo) for ZAN + chemo, independent of PD‑L1 status.

talk_title: Two‑plus‑one bispecific platform for EMPP3‑targeted CD3 T‑cell engagers and CD28 costimulation
tumor_types: clear cell renal cell carcinoma; colorectal cancer; prostate cancer; lung adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: A 2+1 bispecific format places two EMPP3‑binding VHHs in tandem to achieve avidity‑driven selectivity for high‑density EMPP3 tumors, while a monovalent CD3 arm provides T‑cell activation; co‑targeting CD28 with a separate bispecific further amplifies T‑cell signaling, overcoming exhaustion and enhancing tumor killing.
key_findings: The 2+1 EMPP3‑CD3 bispecific shows potent cytotoxicity against EMPP3‑high RCC cells while sparing low‑expressing normal cells, demonstrating an avidity‑driven therapeutic window. | In the Phase I study (n=20), 5 patients achieved partial responses and >50% tumor reduction was observed in >50% of patients despite heavy pretreatment. | CRS occurred in 70% of patients but was mostly grade 1‑2; grade 3 occurred in <5% and no grade 4 events were reported. | Two best responders lacked detectable EMPP3 by IHC, suggesting possible bystander killing or assay sensitivity issues. | Combination of EMPP3‑CD3 with EMPP3‑CD28 bispecific restored T‑cell activity in chronic stimulation assays, indicating potential to overcome T‑cell exhaustion.

talk_title: Development of NEO811, a molecular glue degrader of ARNT (HIF-1β) for clear cell renal cell carcinoma
moa_summary: NEO811 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that induces ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of the transcription factor ARNT, disrupting the obligate HIF‑α/ARNT dimer and shutting down pan‑HIF transcriptional programs in VHL‑deficient ccRCC, leading to tumor regression even in models resistant to HIF‑2α inhibitors.
key_findings: NEO811 degrades ARNT with >log‑unit selectivity over other NEO substrates in whole‑cell proteomics. | Oral NEO811 induces potent tumor regression in ccRCC xenografts at low doses with no body‑weight loss. | In HIF‑2α G323E mutant and HIF‑2α‑low PDX models, NEO811 retains activity whereas belzutifan loses efficacy. | Systemic ARNT degradation in mice is well tolerated, indicating a potentially safe therapeutic window.
primary_value: robust single‑agent regression at low oral doses

talk_title: AZD8359, a CD8‑biased T‑cell engager targeting STEAP2 for metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer
moa_summary: AZD8359 is a bivalent bispecific antibody that binds STEAP2 on prostate tumor cells and a CD8‑binding arm to preferentially engage CD8+ T cells, delivering cytotoxic signals while limiting CD4‑driven cytokine release, thereby achieving potent tumor regression with a wider therapeutic index.
key_findings: STEAP2 is highly and homogeneously expressed in >95% of metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer lesions with minimal normal tissue expression. | AZD8359 mediates STEAP2‑dependent tumor cell killing in vitro; killing is abolished in STEAP2‑KO cells. | CD8‑biased format increases potency compared with a non‑CD8‑guided TCE while reducing CD4 activation and cytokine release. | In humanized mouse flank and bone‑metastasis models, AZD8359 achieves complete tumor clearance with no activity from isotype control. | Therapeutic index (cytotoxic vs cytokine release) is markedly expanded versus conventional 2+1 TCEs both in vitro and in vivo.
primary_value: complete tumor eradication in intratubal model

talk_title: TNG961, an oral HBS1L molecular glue degrader for FOCAD‑deleted cancers
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; non‑small cell lung cancer; FOCAD‑deleted cancers
moa_summary: TNG961 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that induces selective proteasomal degradation of HBS1L, a key component of the HBS1L‑PELO ribosome rescue complex; loss of HBS1L in FOCAD‑deficient tumors triggers ER stress and the unfolded protein response, leading to selective tumor cell death.
key_findings: TNG961 degrades HBS1L with picomolar potency while sparing GSPT1 and other NEO substrates. | In FOCAD‑negative isogenic cell lines, TNG961 shows >100‑fold selectivity for loss of viability compared with FOCAD‑positive counterparts. | Oral dosing leads to dose‑proportional exposure and robust HBS1L degradation in tumor PD tissues. | Complete tumor regressions observed in multiple FOCAD‑deleted CDX/PDX models of pancreatic cancer and NSCLC. | Non‑overlapping sensitivity with MTAP‑cooperative PRMT5 inhibitors; sequential or combined therapy yields enhanced tumor control.
primary_value: complete regressions in pancreatic and NSCLC models

talk_title: EPI-326, a bispecific EGFR degrader (EpiTac) targeting integrin β6 for EGFR‑driven solid tumors
tumor_types: non‑small cell lung cancer; head and neck cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: EPI-326 is a bispecific IgG1 antibody that binds EGFR on tumor cells and integrin β6 as a degrading receptor; the ternary complex drives lysosomal internalization and degradation of EGFR, removing both its kinase activity and scaffolding functions while sparing normal tissues lacking integrin β6, resulting in durable tumor regressions.
key_findings: Integrin β6 is highly expressed and co‑localized with EGFR in NSCLC, head‑and‑neck, and CRC, but limited in normal skin and colon. | EPI-326 degrades EGFR across wild‑type, activating, and resistance mutations, achieving complete tumor regressions where blocking antibodies only induce stasis. | Single dose yields >90% EGFR loss lasting >7 days, indicating catalytic degradation. | Preclinical safety in cynomolgus monkeys shows no EGFR‑related toxicities up to 204 mg/kg, supporting a wide therapeutic index. | Combination with osimertinib (NSCLC) or KRAS inhibitor (CRC) yields deeper and more durable responses.
primary_value: complete responses in mutant EGFR NSCLC models after 3 doses

talk_title: First-in-class B7-H3 targeted sialidase for boosting innate and adaptive anti-tumor immunity
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; prostate cancer; colorectal cancer; head and neck cancer
moa_summary: The next‑generation B7‑H3‑targeted sialidase binds tumor cells via a B7‑H3 antibody arm and delivers a high‑Km human sialidase that removes surface sialic acids, exposing neoantigens and reducing CD163+ M2 macrophages, thereby enhancing both innate and adaptive immune responses. The targeting arm adds Fc‑mediated ADCC/ADCP and prolongs desialylation to >7 days, overcoming the short‑lived effect of the first‑gen construct.
key_findings: Targeted sialidase achieved markedly deeper and more durable tumor desialylation than untargeted enzyme (PNA signal sustained for up to 7 days). | In a humanized A375 xenograft, targeted sialidase outperformed PD‑1 blockade in tumor control. | Paired patient biopsies showed 80‑90% reduction of CD163+ macrophages after treatment. | First‑gen molecule demonstrated safety and proof‑of‑mechanism but only modest clinical activity due to transient desialylation. | B7‑H3 and hypersialylation co‑occur in >50% of ovarian, lung, and prostate cancers, supporting patient selection.

talk_title: Discovery of ECI‑830, a potent and selective CDK2 inhibitor for HR‑positive breast cancer and CCNE1‑amplified tumors
tumor_types: HR-positive breast cancer; ovarian cancer; non-small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: ECI‑830 is a type‑I ATP‑competitive inhibitor that binds the CDK2 ATP pocket, exploiting unique lower‑hinge residues (His84, Gln85) to achieve >100‑fold selectivity over CDK1. Inhibition of CDK2 blocks cyclin‑E1‑driven Rb phosphorylation, leading to deeper G1 arrest and enhanced suppression of E2F‑driven transcription, especially when combined with the CDK4/6 inhibitor ribociclib.
key_findings: ECI‑830 shows >100‑fold selectivity for CDK2 over CDK1 and other CDKs. | In cyclin‑E1 amplified models, single‑agent ECI‑830 produces dose‑dependent tumor growth inhibition with corresponding p‑Rb suppression. | Combination of ECI‑830 with ribociclib yields greater tumor regression than ribociclib alone in multiple HR‑positive breast cancer xenografts. | RNA‑seq confirms that the combination more profoundly down‑regulates E2F target pathways than ribociclib monotherapy. | Steady‑state unbound plasma concentrations above the phospho‑Rb AC50 are required for efficacy.

talk_title: TRI‑611, a CNS‑penetrant ALK molecular glue degrader for ALK‑fusion positive NSCLC
topic_categories: molecular_glue; PROTAC_degrader; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; preclinical; clinical_trial
moa_summary: TRI‑611 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that binds a distal site on the ALK kinase domain, inducing a ternary complex with CRBN and a novel helical degron on ALK. This leads to poly‑ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of EML4‑ALK, including all tested TKI‑resistance mutations, while sparing other kinases and cereblon neosubstrates.
key_findings: TRI‑611 induces selective degradation of EML4‑ALK with >90% loss in tumor tissue after oral dosing. | Degradation is cereblon‑dependent; CRBN knockout abrogates activity. | Potent anti‑tumor activity against a panel of ALK TKI‑resistance mutations, including double and triple mutants. | In lorlatinib‑resistant xenografts, TRI‑611 alone drives tumor regression, whereas lorlatinib is ineffective. | Oral administration achieves brain concentrations comparable to lorlatinib and regresses intracranial ALK‑positive tumors.

talk_title: Discovery and development of a CD30 dual‑payload ADC combining MMAE and TOP1 inhibitor
tumor_types: Hodgkin lymphoma; anaplastic large cell lymphoma
moa_summary: The ADC uses a site‑specific non‑natural amino acid to attach two MMAE molecules (DAR2) and native cysteines to attach eight topoisomerase‑I inhibitor molecules (DAR8). CD30 binding triggers internalization; the MMAE component kills microtubules while the TOP1 inhibitor induces DNA damage, providing complementary mechanisms that overcome P‑gp‑mediated resistance and enable robust tumor regression at sub‑optimal doses.
key_findings: Dual‑payload ADC achieves tumor regression in both parental and P‑gp‑overexpressing ALCL models, whereas MMAE‑only ADC fails in the resistant line. | At a sub‑optimal dose (0.6 mg/kg), the dual‑payload ADC induces robust tumor regression, while single‑payload ADCs show minimal effect. | Rat tolerability study shows MTD of 60 mg/kg for the dual‑payload ADC, substantially higher than brentuximab vedotin (10 mg/kg). | Pharmacokinetics reveal a longer half‑life and stable linker‑payload integrity compared with conventional stochastic conjugates. | Preliminary non‑human primate data (not disclosed) indicate favorable safety at clinically relevant exposures.

talk_title: First-in-class oral macrocycle Cyclin AB RXL inhibitor CID-078
moa_summary: CID-078 is an oral macrocyclic inhibitor that binds the hydrophobic RXL pocket of cyclin A and cyclin B, preventing their interaction with E2F and Mit1, respectively. This sustains E2F activity and forces cyclin B‑Cdk complexes into premature mitosis, causing replication stress, mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis preferentially in RB‑deficient or E2F‑high tumor cells.
key_findings: CID-078 achieves sub‑nanomolar potency against cyclin A/B in biochemical assays. | Oral bioavailability improved from 2% to 25% in preclinical species. | RB loss or biallelic RB1 inactivation correlates with >2‑fold increased sensitivity in cell line screens. | E2F‑high transcriptional signature predicts sensitivity across a broad panel of cancer cell lines. | In vivo, CID-078 induces tumor regression in RB‑deficient PDX models, with responses correlating with E2F expression.
primary_population: solid tumor patients with RB loss or E2F high

talk_title: Oral BRD9 degrader AMX883 for AML
moa_summary: AMX883 is an oral, covalent reversible PROTAC that binds BRD9 and recruits the E3 ligase DCAF16 via a cyanoacrylamide warhead, forming a ternary complex that leads to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of BRD9, thereby releasing the differentiation block in AML blasts and inducing myeloid differentiation and apoptosis.
key_findings: AMX883 degrades BRD9 with DC90 of 0.3 nM and high selectivity over other bromodomain proteins. | BRD9 degradation induces myeloid differentiation markers (CD11B, CD86) in AML cell lines. | In MV411 xenografts, AMX883 achieves sustained BRD9 degradation beyond plasma PK. | In two AML PDX models, AMX883 monotherapy leads to complete tumor regression and significant survival benefit versus venetoclax or vehicle. | Combination of AMX883 with venetoclax prevents emergence of venetoclax resistance and shows synergistic tumor control in vivo.

talk_title: ENPP3‑targeted ADC JNJ‑2175 for solid tumors
affiliation: Johnson & Johnson, Director and Head of Solid Tumor Oncology Discovery
tumor_types: renal cell carcinoma; non-small cell lung cancer; colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: JNJ‑2175 is a site‑specifically conjugated ADC comprising a high‑affinity ENPP3 antibody linked via a branched linker to the AFHPA auristatin‑derived payload; upon internalization in ENPP3‑positive tumor cells, the payload is metabolized to a non‑bystander auristatin, delivering potent microtubule inhibition while controlled bystander activity is achieved through the initial cell‑permeable intermediate.
key_findings: JNJ‑2175 binds dose‑dependently to ENPP3‑positive cells with no off‑target binding to ENPP1/2. | Rapid internalization observed in ENPP3‑positive cell lines. | Potent cytotoxicity against ENPP3‑positive lines; no activity in ENPP3‑negative or knockout cells. | Co‑culture assays demonstrate bystander killing of ENPP3‑negative cells only when mixed with ENPP3‑positive cells. | In three ENPP3‑positive PDX models (NSCLC, RCC), JNJ‑2175 produces dose‑dependent tumor regressions with complete responses.
primary_population: ENPP3‑positive NSCLC and RCC PDX models

talk_title: Dual Vbeta6/Vbeta10 T‑cell agonist IPN‑1203
affiliation: Ipsen Biopharmaceuticals, Senior Director and Head of Translational Science Leads in Oncology
moa_summary: IPN‑1203 is a trispecific bispecific construct that simultaneously binds Vbeta6 and Vbeta10 TCR domains on a subset of T cells and delivers a wild‑type IL‑15/IL‑15Rα fusion, providing potent, selective activation and proliferation of these T cells with enhanced STAT5 signaling, leading to central‑memory differentiation, reduced exhaustion, and durable antitumor immunity.
key_findings: IPN‑1203 binds selectively to ~6% of peripheral T cells (Vbeta6/Vbeta10) with high affinity (1.9 nM). | Rapid STAT5 activation via IL‑15 component without direct TCR phosphorylation. | Five‑day PBMC cultures show >50‑fold expansion of Vbeta‑positive T cells (EC50 ~50 pM). | In mouse surrogate model, IPN‑1203 induces complete tumor regressions dependent on CD8 and Vbeta13 T cells. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq reveals >5‑fold increase in central memory CD8 T cells and >50% reduction in exhausted T cells. | In cynomolgus monkeys, a single 0.25 mg/kg dose yields a 19‑fold expansion of Vbeta6+ T cells with minimal cytokine release.

talk_title: Safety and efficacy of elacrasib (D-3S001) in KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC
moa_summary: Elacrasib is a CNS‑penetrant, GDP‑bound KRAS G12C covalent inhibitor that achieves sustained target engagement, depleting active KRAS even under growth‑factor stimulation, leading to tumor shrinkage and durable responses in both G12C‑inhibitor‑naïve and refractory NSCLC.
key_findings: Elacrasib achieved an overall ORR of 58.8% in KRAS G12C NSCLC (phase I/II). | In G12C‑inhibitor‑naïve patients, ORR was 53.6% with a median PFS of 9.4 months. | In G12C‑inhibitor‑refractory patients, ORR was 32.3% with a median PFS of 8.1 months. | Patients with KRAS amplification (n=5) showed an ORR of 60%. | Rapid and deep ctDNA KRAS G12C allele fraction clearance correlated with radiologic response.

talk_title: Contextualizing KRAS G12C inhibitor landscape and future directions
moa_summary: The speaker reviewed the mechanisms of KRAS G12C off‑inhibitors (covalent binding to GDP‑bound KRAS) and emerging on‑state tri‑complex inhibitors, discussing potency, resistance mechanisms, and toxicity profiles.
key_findings: Sotorasib demonstrated a 28.1% ORR in CodeBreaK‑200 but did not improve OS. | Divorasib showed a 53.4% ORR and median PFS of 13.1 months in early‑phase data. | Autoimmune hepatitis is a class‑specific toxicity, especially after prior PD‑1 therapy.

talk_title: Preliminary safety and activity of zoldonrasib (RMC‑9805) in KRAS G12D NSCLC
affiliation: University of California Davis Comprehensive Cancer Center
moa_summary: Zoldonrasib is an oral mutant‑selective covalent inhibitor that forms a cyclophilin A‑mediated tri‑complex with the GTP‑bound KRAS G12D, sterically blocking RAF binding and shutting down downstream MAPK signaling, leading to tumor regressions.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 52% in KRAS G12D NSCLC patients previously treated with IO and platinum. | Disease control rate reached 93% with median time to response of 1.4 months. | Median PFS was 11.1 months; 12‑month OS rate 73% with median follow‑up 13.1 months. | High ctDNA clearance: 87% >50% reduction, 73% complete clearance.
primary_population: KRAS G12D NSCLC, prior IO+platinum, no docetaxel

talk_title: Discussion of KRAS G12C and G12D inhibitor landscape
moa_summary: The discussant highlighted molecular heterogeneity of KRAS G12D across tumor types, the impact of co‑mutations and smoking status on immunogenicity, and the rationale for combining KRAS G12D inhibitors with immunotherapy or other targeted agents.
key_findings: KRAS G12D NSCLC is enriched in never‑smokers and shows lower PD‑L1 expression. | Co‑mutations (STK11, KEAP1) may influence response to KRAS inhibitors. | Preclinical models suggest KRAS G12D inhibition can increase T‑cell infiltration and synergize with checkpoint blockade.

talk_title: Phase I trial of WEE1 inhibitor zetaracib + PKMYT1 inhibitor lonresorib in CCNE1/FBXW7/PPP2R1A altered tumors
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreas; breast; esophageal; others
moa_summary: Zetaracib inhibits WEE1, preventing CDK1 Y15 phosphorylation, while lonresorib blocks PKMYT1‑mediated CDK1 T14 phosphorylation; concurrent loss of both checkpoints forces replication‑stressed tumor cells into premature mitosis, causing mitotic catastrophe.
key_findings: Overall response rate of 37.5% in heavily pre‑treated ovarian cancer, rising to 50% at RP2D. | Disease control rate of 68.5% across all tumor types. | High ctDNA molecular response (47% overall, 67% in ovarian cohort). | Combination was well tolerated with mainly grade 1‑2 nausea, fatigue, anemia.

talk_title: Discussion of synthetic lethal and replication stress strategies
moa_summary: The discussant contextualized the WEE1/PKMYT1 combination within broader synthetic lethal approaches targeting replication stress, emphasizing biomarker challenges and the need for tolerable regimens.
key_findings: Emphasized importance of RB loss detection (IHC) for cyclin AB inhibitor selection. | Highlighted that low‑grade toxicities may be more clinically relevant than grade 3/4 events.

talk_title: Phase I evaluation of CID‑078, a cyclin A/B RxL inhibitor, in advanced solid tumors
affiliation: University Hospital Seidman Cancer Center, Case Western Reserve University
moa_summary: CID‑078 is a macrocyclic oral agent that binds the RxL motif on cyclin A and B, blocking cyclin‑E2F and cyclin‑MYT1 interactions, causing unchecked CDK activity, DNA damage accumulation, spindle checkpoint activation and mitotic catastrophe in RB‑deficient tumors.
key_findings: Linear PK from 40‑960 mg BID with ~3 h half‑life and no accumulation. | Grade 3 ALT elevations were rare (2 patients) and reversible. | Confirmed tumor responses observed in RB‑loss osteosarcoma and neuroendocrine tumor.

talk_title: Cyclin AB inhibition: the next mile in cell cycle therapy
moa_summary: The discussant reviewed the mechanistic rationale for targeting the cyclin A/B RxL interface, emphasizing synthetic lethality in RB‑deficient, E2F‑high tumors and the need for biomarker‑driven patient selection.
key_findings: CID‑078 blocks cyclin‑A/E2F and cyclin‑B/MYT1 interactions, leading to mitotic catastrophe. | RB loss can be detected by IHC, offering a practical selection method.

talk_title: Neural representation learning for pan‑cancer HRD prediction and platinum response
moa_summary: Pitt presented HR‑Difuse, a multi‑view neural representation learning framework that encodes SBS, indel, SV, and copy‑number features from whole‑genome sequencing, fuses them via self‑attention, and uses contrastive learning to predict homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) and platinum response, outperforming HRDetect and random‑forest‑based Cord across pan‑cancer cohorts.
key_findings: HR‑Difuse achieved higher AUROC and AUPRC than HRDetect and Cord for HRD prediction in TCGA and PEACOG datasets. | The model retained predictive power for somatic HRD events lacking high microhomology‑mediated deletions, where existing tools performed poorly. | Transfer‑learning fine‑tuning of the HRD model on platinum‑response labels yielded an F1 score of ~0.75, surpassing baseline HR‑Difuse and other classifiers. | Contrastive learning of latent encodings improved cross‑view feature integration compared with pure NMF‑based signatures.

talk_title: Dimensional structural chromosome instability and the JAVEL mass‑balance method
moa_summary: Imielinski described JAVEL, a mass‑balance‑principle algorithm that jointly infers copy‑number segments and rearrangement junctions from whole‑genome sequencing, leveraging stoichiometric constraints to produce coherent structural chromosome instability profiles across cancer types.
key_findings: JAVEL integrates copy‑number and structural‑variant data into a unified representation based on segment neighbor balance. | The method enables detection of complex events such as chromothripsis, chromoplexy, and retrotransposition with improved resolution over separate CNV or SV callers. | Application to TCGA whole‑genome data reveals pervasive multi‑scale chromosomal instability signatures across tumor types.

talk_title: Graph-based genome analysis reveals structural chromosomal instability patterns
tumor_types: glioblastoma; acral melanoma; breast cancer; prostate cancer; colorectal cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: The presenter introduced JAVEL, a graph‑based copy‑number and breakpoint detection tool, and demonstrated how genome graphs can be mined for structural chromosomal instability (SIN) patterns such as chromoplexy, chromothripsis, ecDNA, BFB cycles and a novel class called Typhonis. Specific graph motifs correlate with DNA repair deficiencies (e.g., BRCA1 loss) and with oncogene amplification contexts (e.g., HER2 HSR vs ecDNA).
key_findings: JAVEL outperforms prior methods in breakpoint precision and recall on TCGA genomes. | Identified a new structural scar highly specific for BRCA1 deficiency, present in >90% of BRCA1‑deficient tumors and rare elsewhere. | Discovered Typhonis, a distinct HSR amplification pattern affecting ~3% of cancers and enriched for MYC, CCND1, ERBB2 amplifications. | Found that glioblastoma is dominated by ecDNA amplifications of EGFR/PDGFRA, whereas most other cancers show HSR‑type amplifications. | HSR HER2 amplifications predict lower likelihood of resistance to HER2‑targeted ADCs compared with ecDNA‑based HER2 amplifications.

talk_title: Comprehensive analysis of >8,000 TCGA whole‑genome sequences reveals new coding, non‑coding and structural drivers
tumor_types: skin melanoma; lung adenocarcinoma; lung squamous cell carcinoma; colorectal cancer; breast cancer; prostate cancer; ovarian cancer; sarcoma; glioblastoma; kidney cancer; liver cancer; head and neck cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Using dual cloud pipelines, the team generated a high‑quality variant call set from >8,000 TCGA whole‑genome samples, uncovering sequencing artifacts (vapor‑trail T>G/A>C), correcting coverage gaps, and identifying novel coding and non‑coding driver mutations, structural variation drivers, and HRD signatures across cancer types.
key_findings: Identified a novel NovaSeq‑specific sequencing artifact (T>G/A>C “vapor trail”) and created a filter that removed up to 15% of false SNVs in affected tumor types. | PCR‑free whole‑genome data recovered >90% of missed TERT promoter mutations and uncovered new hotspots in GC‑rich regions of VHL, ARID1A and other tumor suppressors. | Detected 200 million SNVs, 1.7 million DNVs, 56 million indels and 1.3 million SVs across the cohort. | Discovered new non‑coding drivers (e.g., recurrent promoter indels in albumin, surfactant genes, P63, GATA3, XBP1, ESR1 regulatory region amplifications). | SVelfi analysis revealed known SV drivers (TMPRSS2‑ERG) and novel recurrent SVs such as SLC30A4 translocations driving ETV1 overexpression in prostate cancer. | HRD signatures were present in 7% of all tumors, including 3.3% outside ovarian and breast cancers, with some cases lacking known BRCA1/2 alterations. | Approximately 10% of samples harbored pathogenic germline variants; a novel intronic BRCA1 splice variant showed LOH and reduced expression, indicating functional HRD.

talk_title: Spatial metabolomics of pancreas and breast cancer reveals cell-type specific lipid and nutrient utilization
affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Chemistry
moa_summary: Using MALDI imaging mass spectrometry, Rabinowitz mapped lipid species that demarcate epithelial cancer cells versus fibroblasts and immune cells, and showed spatial depletion of glucose and enrichment of ATP/ADP in cancer and immune compartments, highlighting metabolic heterogeneity and suggesting ketogenic diet–induced glucose restriction could stress tumor cells while supporting T cell ketone utilization.
key_findings: Distinct phosphatidylethanolamine lipids (PE 18:1/18:1 vs PE with saturated/omega‑6 tail) specifically mark breast cancer epithelium versus fibroblasts. | Glucose is depleted and ATP/ADP is enriched in cancer cells and immune infiltrates across breast and pancreatic tumors. | Lactate tracing in mouse tumors shows universal lactate utilization across tumor cell types, contradicting prior reports of Treg‑specific lactate use. | CD8+ T cells preferentially utilize 3‑hydroxybutyrate, suggesting ketogenic diet may boost antitumor immunity. | A small phase I/II trial of a supervised ketogenic diet combined with standard chemotherapy in metastatic pancreatic cancer showed ~3‑month improvement in progression‑free and overall survival.

talk_title: Mitochondrial NADK2 supports proline synthesis, protein lipoylation, and mitochondrial translation
affiliation: UT Southwestern, Department of Molecular Biology
moa_summary: NADK2 generates mitochondrial NADPH, which fuels proline biosynthesis and mitochondrial fatty‑acid synthesis; the latter supplies acyl‑carrier protein lipoylation required for PDH and other dehydrogenase complexes, supporting TCA flux, oxidative phosphorylation, and mitochondrial translation, thereby promoting tumor cell proliferation.
key_findings: NADK2 loss impairs lung cancer cell proliferation in vitro and tumor growth in vivo. | Supplementation with non‑essential amino acids, especially proline, fully rescues proliferation of NADK2‑deficient cells. | NADK2 knockout abolishes de novo proline synthesis, linking mitochondrial NADPH to proline biosynthesis. | Mitochondrial NADK2 is required for protein lipoylation of PDH, OGDH, GCS, and BCKDH complexes; kinase‑dead NADK2 fails to restore lipoylation. | Restoring mitochondrial NADPH via cytosolic NADK targeted to mitochondria rescues lipoylation, TCA cycle intermediates, and oxidative respiration. | NADK2 supports mitochondrial fatty‑acid synthesis, as shown by reduced ACP‑bound acyl chains in NADK2‑deficient cells. | Loss of NADK2 diminishes mitochondrial translation and ETC complex assembly, leading to decreased OCR.

talk_title: Methodological advances reveal tissue‑specific nutrient environments and folate‑limited nucleotide metabolism in cancer
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Medicine
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; acute lymphoblastic leukemia
topic_categories: metabolism; clinical_trial; translational; basic_science; biomarker_development; combination_therapy
moa_summary: Vander Heiden’s group quantified tissue‑specific nutrient milieus, revealing that folate scarcity in plasma‑like media limits de novo nucleotide synthesis in B‑ALL, making cells dependent on salvage; dietary folate titration directly modulates leukemia growth, linking systemic nutrient availability to replication stress and tumor progression.
key_findings: Each mouse tissue exhibits a distinct nutrient composition; blood differs markedly from standard culture media. | Bone‑marrow plasma from ALL patients and mice is low in folates (~30 nM), limiting de novo nucleotide synthesis. | B‑ALL cells grown in mouse plasma‑like media become dependent on nucleotide salvage; removal of exogenous nucleotides impairs growth. | Folates are the primary driver of this phenotype; raising folate levels to physiological (~30 nM) restores proliferation even without salvage. | In vivo folate manipulation in mice alters B‑ALL tumor burden, confirming folate limitation as a growth constraint. | Low folate induces replication stress, which can reprogram gene expression and may affect therapeutic response. | A ketogenic diet in pancreatic cancer patients modestly improves PFS and OS, likely via glucose restriction and increased ketone utilization by T cells.
primary_population: metastatic pancreatic cancer, first‑line triple chemotherapy

talk_title: Extracellular Vesicles and Particles, Their Biogenesis and Function
tumor_types: melanoma; breast cancer; renal cell carcinoma
moa_summary: AF6 activation drives the formation of tumor microvesicles (TMVs) by recruiting Exportin‑5, pre‑microRNAs and MT1‑MMP to actin‑myosin contractile sites via a VAMP3‑CD9 complex, resulting in protease‑laden vesicles that promote matrix degradation, invasion and intercellular transfer of oncogenic RNAs and DNA.
key_findings: AF6‑activated tumor cells shed large microvesicles (TMVs) enriched for AF6, VAMP3, CD9 and MT1‑MMP. | TMVs display proteolytic activity on gelatin matrices and promote endothelial tube formation with distinct phenotypes (elongated vs sprouting) depending on tumor origin. | AF6 activation increases pre‑microRNA loading into TMVs via an Exportin‑5‑GRP1 scaffold that preferentially binds GTP‑AF6. | Knockdown of GRP1 reduces pre‑microRNA content in TMVs, demonstrating its scaffolding role. | TMV‑delivered miR‑21 suppresses SMAD7 in recipient fibroblasts; this effect is reversed by miR‑21 or GRP1 knockdown. | TMVs contain double‑stranded DNA packaged through cGAS‑RAN‑GTP‑AF6 endosomal trafficking; DNA removal diminishes TMV‑mediated signaling.

talk_title: Biology and Translation of Large Oncosomes as Cancer Biomarkers
affiliation: Los Angeles and Sapienza University, Rome
tumor_types: prostate cancer; breast cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; pancreatic cancer; melanoma
topic_categories: liquid_biopsy; biomarker_development; tumor_microenvironment; basic_science; translational; other
moa_summary: Large oncosomes released by highly aggressive cancer cells carry abundant mitochondrial RNA/proteins, kinases and DNA, which upon uptake by bone‑marrow stromal cells trigger interferon‑driven inflammatory programs, neutrophil recruitment and myeloid‑derived suppressor cell expansion, thereby fostering metastatic niche formation via STING‑dependent signaling.
key_findings: Mice injected with large oncosomes after primary tumor resection develop significantly more bone metastases than those receiving small EVs. | Large oncosomes induce a strong interferon‑stimulated gene signature in bone‑marrow MSCs, driving neutrophil chemotaxis and MDSC‑like polarization (arginase‑1, IL‑10 up‑regulation). | STING inhibition abolishes large‑oncosome‑mediated NF‑κB/IFN reporter activation and blocks induction of IRF3/7 in vivo. | Large oncosomes are enriched for mitochondrial transcripts and proteins; this signature is conserved across cancer types and patient plasma. | Proteomic profiling identified a set of bona‑fide large‑oncosome surface markers (e.g., VDAC1, RP2) enabling magnetic‑bead capture from plasma. | Longitudinal plasma analysis distinguishes castration‑sensitive from castration‑resistant prostate cancer and identifies progression‑associated protein panels. | Septin‑2 knockdown selectively suppresses large oncosome release and prevents the immunosuppressive microenvironment in a breast cancer model.

talk_title: Activated T‑Cell Extracellular Vesicles Deliver DNA to Reprogram Immunity and Tumors
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; glioblastoma; triple‑negative breast cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; cell_therapy; extracellular_vesicles; basic_science; other
moa_summary: Activated T‑cell extracellular vesicles (ATEVs) are enriched for extracellular DNA and granzyme B; LFA‑1 mediates their uptake by lymph‑node dendritic cells and tumor cells, where DNA reaches the nucleus, triggers antigen‑presentation gene transcription, and reprograms the tumor microenvironment toward an immune‑active state, synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 therapy to suppress tumor growth.
key_findings: Activated T‑cell EVs contain ~80 % surface DNA and 20 % lumen DNA, markedly higher than non‑activated EVs. | LFA‑1 on ATEVs drives preferential uptake by dendritic cells in lymph nodes and by tumor cells in pancreatic and GBM models. | DNA‑depleted ATEVs (DNase‑treated) fail to induce antigen‑presentation gene signatures in recipient lymph‑node cells. | Single‑cell RNA‑seq shows up‑regulation of MHC‑I/II, TAP1/2 and other antigen‑processing genes only after DNA‑competent ATEV exposure. | Combination of ATEVs with anti‑PD‑1 completely arrests tumor growth in KPC pancreatic and GBM mouse models for >30 days, whereas either agent alone is less effective. | Granzyme B packaged in ATEVs facilitates nuclear membrane disruption, allowing EV DNA to access euchromatin and drive transcription without genomic integration. | Short‑term ATEV treatment induces transient immune activation without overt autoimmunity; antigen‑presentation genes return to baseline after ~7 days.

talk_title: Integrin αvβ6-targeted theranostic peptide and lutetium therapy in pancreatic and other cancers
affiliation: University of California, Davis; Founder and CEO of Luminance Biosciences
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; breast cancer; colon cancer; lung cancer; ovarian carcinoma; neuroblastoma
moa_summary: The αvβ6‑targeting peptide (A20FMDV2) is radiolabeled with F‑18 for PET imaging and later re‑engineered with a DOTA chelator and albumin‑binding moiety to create a Ga‑68 diagnostic and Lu‑177 therapeutic pair that binds with nanomolar affinity, is rapidly internalized, and delivers tumor‑specific radiation while being cleared renally.
key_findings: Developed a first‑in‑human αvβ6 theranostic pair (Ga‑68 diagnostic / Lu‑177 therapeutic) after a decade of peptide optimization. | Phase I dose escalation reached 200 mCi with no dose‑limiting toxicities and acceptable renal clearance. | Imaging detected lesions missed by standard FDG‑PET, including small brain metastases, demonstrating high tumor‑to‑background contrast. | A single Lu‑177 dose produced a 40% SUV reduction and CA19‑9 decline in a heavily pre‑treated pancreatic cancer patient without tumor growth over 90 days. | The agent showed consistent tumor retention over days on SPECT, delivering 15–40 Gy per lesion at the top dose.

talk_title: DLL3-targeted radiopharmaceuticals: antibodies, imaging, and therapeutic opportunities
tumor_types: small cell lung cancer; neuroendocrine prostate cancer; pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; neuroblastoma
topic_categories: radiopharmaceuticals; cancer_vaccines; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: A high‑affinity anti‑DLL3 antibody (SC16) is labeled with Zr‑89 for PET imaging and Lu‑177 for therapy; the antibody binds low‑abundance DLL3 with high tumor‑to‑normal contrast, is internalized, and delivers β‑radiation to DLL3‑expressing neuroendocrine lesions, enabling both lesion detection and tumor control.
key_findings: DLL3 PET imaging identified neuroendocrine prostate cancer lesions missed by PSMA or FDG PET. | Preclinical actinium‑225 DLL3 peptide showed dose‑dependent tumor regression in DLL3‑positive xenografts. | Antibody‑based DLL3 imaging demonstrated high specificity with minimal off‑target liver uptake when co‑administered with cold antibody to block Fc sinks. | DLL3 expression is homogeneous across tumor cells in neuroendocrine cancers, making it an attractive homogeneous target.
primary_value: Detected 2 DLL3‑positive lesions out of 74 total lesions in a neuroendocrine prostate patient

talk_title: Next‑generation radioligand therapy: target selection, isotope engineering, and combination strategies
tumor_types: non‑small cell lung cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; breast cancer; neuroendocrine tumors; multiple solid tumors
topic_categories: radiopharmaceuticals; targeted_therapy; isotope_engineering; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; platform_technology
moa_summary: Novartis is engineering radioligands by pairing novel targets (e.g., FAP, DLL3) with optimized isotopes (Lu‑177, Ac‑225, Y‑90) and high‑affinity vectors (antibodies, macrocyclic peptides) to exploit cross‑fire effects, improve tumor homogeneity, and enable combination with DNA‑damage response inhibitors or immunotherapy.
key_findings: Demonstrated homogeneous FAP expression in PDAC and breast cancer models, enabling cross‑fire radiation from Lu‑177‑labeled FXX‑489. | Actinium‑225 DLL3 peptide (ETN‑029) regressed DLL3‑positive SHP77 xenografts in a dose‑dependent manner. | Preclinical data show that DNA‑PK inhibition synergizes with Lu‑177‑based radioligands across multiple tumor types. | Cross‑fire from β‑emitters can overcome intra‑tumoral heterogeneity, while α‑emitters provide high‑potency for radiosensitive lesions.
primary_n: multiple lesions in early FAP trial

talk_title: NK cell heterogeneity, NKG2A blockade and NK cell engager therapeutics
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; acute myeloid leukemia; multiple myeloma; pancreatic cancer; ovarian cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; CAR_T; ADC; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: The presenter defined three conserved NK cell subsets (NK1, NK2, NK3) across species, identified tumor‑associated NK3 cells (CD39+, NKG2A+, CD103+) in NSCLC that correlate with better survival, showed that anti‑NKG2A antibody (Monalizumab) can unleash their activity, and described tri‑ and tetra‑specific NK cell engagers that combine tumor antigen binding, NKp46 activation, and cytokine payloads (IL‑2 or IFN‑α) to enhance NK cytotoxicity in preclinical AML, MM, and solid tumor models.
key_findings: NK3 subset expands in NSCLC tumors and its high frequency predicts improved overall survival and better response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy. | Monalizumab (anti‑NKG2A) increases granzyme A/B expression in autologous tumor fragments, indicating functional rescue of exhausted NK cells. | Tri‑specific NK cell engagers targeting CD123 (AML) and BCMA (MM) achieved complete remissions in early‑phase AML patients, earning FDA fast‑track designation. | Tetra‑specific engagers incorporating IL‑2 or IFN‑α showed superior tumor control in MC38‑hCD20 and Raji xenograft models compared with bispecific formats. | IL‑2‑armed NK engagers induced proliferation of memory‑like NK cells in vitro, generating a CD39‑positive, cytokine‑induced memory phenotype.
primary_population: stage 3 non-resectable NSCLC in COAST trial

talk_title: Memory‑like NK cell adoptive therapy in AML and solid tumors
tumor_types: acute myeloid leukemia; myelodysplastic syndrome; head and neck cancer; ovarian cancer; pancreatic cancer; mesothelioma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cell_therapy; CAR_T; clinical_trial; translational; combination_therapy
moa_summary: Memory‑like NK cells are generated by overnight activation with IL‑12, IL‑15, and IL‑18, acquiring enhanced IFN‑γ production and prolonged in vivo persistence; when infused post‑transplant or after lymphodepletion, they mediate anti‑leukemic activity and, when engineered with CARs (e.g., mesothelin, CD70) and TGF‑β‑responsive IL‑12/IL‑18 payloads, they overcome tumor‑derived suppression and achieve tumor regression in solid tumor mouse models.
key_findings: Memory‑like NK cells persisted up to 200 days in some post‑transplant AML patients, far exceeding the typical 10‑12 day lifespan of conventional NK cells. | Four of eight AML patients achieved MRD‑negative complete remission after a single infusion of memory‑like NK cells. | In heavily pre‑treated head and neck cancer patients, 7 of 10 achieved disease control, including one dramatic partial response lasting ~12 months. | CAR‑NK cells armed with a TGF‑β‑responsive IL‑12/IL‑18 circuit cured >90% of mice in aggressive pancreatic and kidney cancer models without systemic toxicity. | Combining engineered tumor‑targeting bacteria expressing IL‑12 and adenosine deaminase with CAR‑NK cells doubled survival in pancreatic cancer mouse models.

talk_title: Logic‑gate engineered allogeneic NK cells for AML
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cell_therapy; CAR_T; clinical_trial; combination_therapy; translational
moa_summary: SENTI‑202 uses allogeneic NK cells engineered with dual activating CARs targeting CD33 and FLT3 to kill AML blasts and leukemic stem cells, combined with an inhibitory CAR against endomucin (EMCN) to protect healthy hematopoietic stem cells, and an IL‑15 transgene to enhance NK persistence, resulting in MRD‑negative remissions with minimal off‑target toxicity.
key_findings: Overall response rate of ~50% in heavily pre‑treated relapsed/refractory AML patients. | CR/CRh achieved in 30‑40% of patients with median CR duration of 7.6 months. | High MRD‑negative conversion rates observed across responders. | NK‑cell related adverse events were limited to Grade 1‑2 pyrexia; cytokine release syndrome was minimal compared with CAR‑T therapies. | Platelet and neutrophil counts recovered rapidly after lymphodepletion, indicating limited bone‑marrow toxicity.

talk_title: PRECEDE collaborative early detection platform for pancreatic cancer
moa_summary: The PRECEDE initiative creates a large, multi‑center longitudinal cohort of high‑risk individuals, standardizing imaging, germline testing, and biosample collection to enable early detection of pancreatic cancer and to serve as a platform for evaluating novel biomarkers and interception strategies.
key_findings: Enrolled >11,500 high‑risk participants across 65 centers, surpassing the original 10,000 target. | Identified 42 screen‑detected pancreatic cancers, 72% of which were high‑grade dysplasia or Stage I. | Cohort includes >3,000 BRCA carriers and ~900 ATM carriers, providing a rich population for MCED studies.

talk_title: Biomarker‑based non‑endoscopic detection and surveillance of Barrett's esophagus
moa_summary: EsaCheck uses a swallowable textured balloon to collect distal esophageal cells, while EsaGuard detects methylated VIM and CCNA1 DNA; together they achieve high‑sensitivity non‑endoscopic Barrett's detection. The BAD algorithm combines aneuploidy with six driver chromosome alterations to identify high‑grade dysplasia or cancer, and methylated CCNA1 serves as a minimal residual disease marker after ablation.
key_findings: EsaCheck/EsaGuard achieved 90% sensitivity and 92% specificity for non‑dysplastic Barrett's in a pilot of 120 high‑risk asymptomatic subjects. | In four clinical cohorts, Barrett's detection sensitivity ranged 80‑90% and cancer detection was ~100% (one miss). | BAD Very Bad score detected 96% of cancers and 68% of high‑grade dysplasias while flagging only 7% of non‑dysplastic lesions. | Methylated CCNA1 positivity post‑ablation conferred a 25‑fold increased risk of recurrence (13/27 vs 1/53). | EsaCheck received FDA 510(k) clearance; EsaGuard earned FDA breakthrough device designation and guideline inclusion.

talk_title: AI foundation models for personalized cancer imaging and adaptive trial design
moa_summary: Pillar Zero is a vision‑language foundation model pre‑trained on massive DICOM volumes and radiology reports, enabling higher accuracy across hundreds of imaging findings; Mirai demonstrates that image‑only deep learning can outperform traditional risk calculators, and the BRIDGE trial framework reuses overlapping high‑risk patient subsets across successive AI model generations to accelerate adaptive clinical trials.
key_findings: Pillar Zero achieves 82‑90% average accuracy across >100 radiology findings, outperforming major commercial vision models. | Image‑only Mirai model surpasses Tyrer‑Cuzick questionnaire for 5‑year breast cancer risk prediction. | CT‑based AI outperforms smoking‑history questionnaires for lung cancer risk. | BRIDGE statistical framework shows >90% overlap of top 5% high‑risk patients across successive AI model versions, enabling adaptive trial reuse.
primary_value: higher than Tyrer‑Cuzick (exact value not specified)

talk_title: Germline DAM2 R404W variant promotes glioma tumorigenesis in mouse models
moa_summary: The germline DAM2 R404W variant enhances glial progenitor proliferation and skews differentiation toward a glial fate, expanding a pro‑tumorigenic niche that promotes glioma growth in vitro and in orthotopic mouse models.
key_findings: Overexpression of DAM2 R404W in patient‑derived GBM stem‑like cells increased tumor sphere size relative to wild‑type and GFP controls. | Orthotopic xenografts of R404W‑overexpressing cells showed larger bioluminescent tumor burden and higher Ki‑67 proliferation than controls. | DAM2 R404W knock‑in mice displayed increased Sox9⁺/BrdU⁺ glial progenitors and decreased double‑cortin⁺ neuronal progenitors in the subventricular zone. | In vitro cultured neural stem cells from R404W mice recapitulated the increased glial progenitor phenotype, indicating a cell‑intrinsic effect. | Low‑grade glioma models generated via rCas9‑tVA with PDGF‑β showed higher proliferation (OLIG2⁺) and histologic features of malignant transformation in R404W mice. | Preliminary survival analysis suggests reduced overall survival in R404W mice bearing low‑grade glioma.

talk_title: Age‑dependent mammary tumorigenesis driven by mutant PIK3CA H1047R in mice
affiliation: University of Texas at San Antonio
moa_summary: Lentiviral delivery of the hotspot PIK3CA H1047R mutation into mammary ducts of aged mice expands basal epithelial cells, accelerates tumor onset, and yields widespread epithelial proliferation, while aged microenvironments increase the proportion of PIK3CA‑positive cells at the basal‑luminal interface, linking age‑associated niche changes to mutant PI3K‑driven tumorigenesis.
