key_findings: Aged (22 mo) mice injected with lentiviral PIK3CA H1047R develop palpable tumors earlier and with higher incidence than young (2 mo) mice. | Mid‑aged mice show non‑palpable micro‑lesions with high Ki‑67, indicating early oncogenic activation. | Whole‑mount analysis reveals focal lesions in young mice versus diffuse ductal proliferation in aged mice. | Tumor growth rate is slower in aged mice despite earlier onset, mirroring clinical observations of less aggressive tumors in older patients. | ERα expression is higher in tumor‑adjacent glands, suggesting hormonal priming in the aged niche. | Flow cytometry shows increased PIK3CA‑positive cells in mid‑aged and aged glands, localized at the basal‑luminal boundary.
primary_population: aged (22 mo) vs young (2 mo) mice

talk_title: Genotype‑specific PI3K inhibition and CAF‑mediated organoid engraftment in bladder cancer
moa_summary: CRISPR‑engineered bladder organoids harboring PIK3CA H1047R are sensitized to alpelisib in vitro; in vivo, engraftment in immunocompetent mice requires co‑injection with cancer‑associated fibroblasts, which secrete IL‑6, while organoids produce GM‑CSF, establishing a cytokine crosstalk that modulates immune suppression and tumor growth.
key_findings: PIK3CA H1047R bladder organoids show increased sensitivity to alpelisib and pirtobrutinib (piptilicide) compared with WT organoids in vitro. | Both hotspot and WT organoids are resistant to growth in immunocompetent mice unless co‑implanted with CAFs. | Co‑implantation with CAFs enables tumor formation in flank; alpelisib treatment reduces tumor size and Ki‑67 proliferation in both genotypes. | Co‑culture reveals CAF‑derived IL‑6 and organoid‑derived GM‑CSF; GM‑CSF knockout does not consistently affect tumor formation.

talk_title: Multi‑omic patient‑derived organoid platform for predicting NSCLC immunotherapy response
moa_summary: Integrated spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA‑seq of NSCLC organoids, and co‑culture with allogeneic activated immune cells reveal that organoids from clinically non‑responding patients exhibit higher CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration with an exhausted phenotype, despite greater immune cell penetration, linking organoid immune interactions to patient outcomes.
key_findings: Organoids recapitulate tumor‑type specific transcriptomic signatures (adenocarcinoma vs squamous) observed in spatial profiling. | Responder patients (no event at 2 yr) show enrichment of inflammatory pathways in organoids, whereas non‑responders do not. | Co‑culture with pre‑activated allogeneic immune cells shows significantly higher CD45⁺ infiltration in non‑responder organoids. | Infiltrating CD8⁺ T cells in non‑responder organoids display an exhausted phenotype (e.g., PD‑1⁺, TIM‑3⁺) both at baseline and after cisplatin exposure. | PD‑L1 CPS and mutation status did not predict organoid responder classification.

talk_title: 3D nanobioreactor imaging of APOBEC3C‑driven splicing alterations in aged human HSPCs
moa_summary: APOBEC3C overexpression in aged human HSPCs induces C‑to‑T editing of splicing regulatory elements, promoting exon skipping as visualized by a GFP‑reporter in a 3D nanobioreactor, thereby linking inflammaging‑driven APOBEC activity to splicing dysregulation and potential pre‑cancerous phenotypes.
key_findings: Aged bone marrow CD34⁺ cells transduced with APOBEC3C show a ~10‑fold increase in GFP‑reporter exon‑skipping versus cord blood controls. | RNA‑seq of aged APOBEC3C‑overexpressing HSPCs reveals up‑regulation of spliceosome genes (SRSSFs, SF3Bs) and increased exon‑skipping events. | Live imaging in the nanobioreactor demonstrates migration of transduced HSPCs toward the SurgiFoam niche and a shift from RFP (exon inclusion) to GFP (exon skipping) over 72 h. | FACS analysis confirms a significant increase in GFP⁺ cells in APOBEC3C‑overexpressing samples compared with empty vector.

talk_title: Ex vivo microtumor platform predicts ADC and combination therapy responses in ovarian cancer
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; non-small cell lung cancer; bladder cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Microtumor fragments retain native tumor architecture and microenvironment, enabling high‑content imaging of drug‑induced cytotoxicity; ADC mirvetuximab soravtansine shows variable efficacy across FRα‑expressing ovarian samples, correlating with paclitaxel sensitivity due to shared microtubule‑targeting mechanisms, and synergizes with olaparib in a subset of patients.
key_findings: Ex vivo microtumor responses to carboplatin/paclitaxel correlate with clinical C125 biomarker and predict progression‑free survival. | Mirvetuximab soravtansine efficacy varies widely among 25 ovarian patient samples, matching FRα expression heterogeneity. | ADC response correlates with paclitaxel sensitivity, reflecting shared microtubule inhibition. | Combination of mirvetuximab with olaparib yields synergistic killing in a subset of samples, while others are driven mainly by olaparib alone. | Target antigen profiling reveals intra‑patient FRα heterogeneity, influencing ADC efficacy.
primary_population: patients stratified by ex vivo response

talk_title: eLive cytokine profiling defines immune phenotypes and predicts ICI response in fresh tumor fragments
moa_summary: Fresh tumor fragments are sliced, encapsulated in a proprietary gel, and cultured with checkpoint inhibitors; multiplex Luminex cytokine profiling identifies responder versus non‑responder signatures, which cluster into four distinct immune phenotypes (TH1‑inflamed, mixed T‑reg/myeloid, cytotoxic, polyfunctional myeloid‑angiogenic) that predict clinical ICI outcomes.
key_findings: A nine‑cytokine eLive index predicts ICI response with 93% accuracy in a validation cohort. | Unsupervised clustering of cytokine responses defines four immune phenotypes, each with distinct cytokine patterns (e.g., IFN‑γ, CXCL9, CCL22, IL‑10, VEGF). | Subcluster 1 (TH1‑inflamed) shows CD8⁺ T‑cell activation (CD69⁺) and immune triads in NSCLC samples. | Subcluster 3 (cytotoxic) displays granzyme A/B up‑regulation and VEGF, indicating combined cytotoxic and angiogenic remodeling. | Multiplex IF confirms spatial co‑localization of T cells, B cells, and macrophages in responder biopsies.
primary_value: 93% concordance between ex vivo eLive index and patient outcome

talk_title: Targeting RNA Polymerase I and BCL2 synergy in acute myeloid leukemia
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Postdoctoral Fellow, Palkar Lab
moa_summary: Pol I degradation reduces rRNA synthesis, inducing differentiation and G1 arrest in AML cells, and synergizes with BCL2 inhibition to trigger apoptosis independent of TP53 status.
key_findings: Nascent and mature rRNA levels are elevated in primary AML blasts versus healthy hematopoietic cells. | HOXA9 activation drives Pol I expression and rRNA synthesis; HOXA9 inactivation reduces rRNA production before cell cycle changes. | Targeted degradation of POLR1A with dTAG decreases rRNA synthesis, induces G1 arrest, differentiation, and reduces viability. | Pol I degradation synergizes with venetoclax (Z-score >10) to enhance AML cell killing, independent of TP53 mutation.

talk_title: LncRNA CYP4A22-AS1/YBX1/PK1 axis drives radiation resistance in cervical adenocarcinoma
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center
moa_summary: CYP4A22-AS1 binds YBX1, enhancing transcription of PK1, which drives glycolysis and confers radiation resistance; depletion of the lncRNA or PK1 sensitizes cells to radiation-induced DNA damage and apoptosis.
key_findings: CYP4A22-AS1 is upregulated in cervical adenocarcinoma and predicts poor survival. | Knockdown of CYP4A22-AS1 increases radiation-induced apoptosis and DNA damage markers. | CYP4A22-AS1 regulates glycolysis via upregulation of PK1 through YBX1 interaction. | PK1 depletion reverses glycolytic enhancement and radiosensitizes CYP4A22-AS1 overexpressing cells. | In vivo, CYP4A22-AS1 overexpression accelerates tumor growth in mouse models.

talk_title: Ultrasmall lactoferrin nanoassemblies delivering miR-196b antagomir and COP TF II inhibitor for RCC CAF remodeling
affiliation: Amherst College (Zoughbi Lab, Harvard Medical School)
moa_summary: An ultra‑small lactoferrin‑stabilized nanoassembly co‑delivers a miR‑196b antagomir and the COP TF II inhibitor CIA1 to RCC cancer‑associated fibroblasts, suppressing activation markers, ECM production, and secreted cytokines, thereby reducing CAF‑driven tumor growth and enhancing immune infiltration.
key_findings: Nanoassembly size ~6.3 nm enables renal filtration and accumulation in tumor kidney. | Both miR‑196b antagomir and CIA1 independently down‑regulate CAF activation (α‑SMA) and ECM markers (collagen I, LOX). | Conditioned media from treated CAFs reduces RCC cell apoptosis resistance, EMT markers, and migration. | In orthotopic RENCA mice, combined nanoassembly markedly reduces tumor volume and weight without systemic toxicity. | Treatment increases intratumoral CD8⁺ T cells, dendritic cells, and NK cells while decreasing Tregs and suppressive myeloid cells.

talk_title: Mitochondrial tRNA‑Arg fragments modulate breast cancer metastasis
moa_summary: Strain‑specific SNPs in mitochondrial tRNA^Arg alter its secondary structure, influencing cleavage into ~50‑nt fragments; these fragments correlate with primary tumor latency and pulmonary metastasis, suggesting a non‑coding RNA signaling role in metastatic progression.
key_findings: Mitochondrial SNPs in tRNA^Arg D‑loop differ among mouse strains and associate with metastatic burden. | Northern blots reveal 40‑50 nt tRNA^Arg fragments whose abundance varies with mitochondrial genotype. | Enzymatic end‑repair and demethylation enable detection of these fragments by small‑RNA‑seq. | MINX mice with mismatched mitochondria show altered primary tumor latency and number of lung metastases. | Fragment production is higher in strains with higher metastatic propensity, implicating a functional role.

talk_title: Urinary extracellular vesicle microRNA panel for early detection and prognosis of lung cancer
moa_summary: Urinary extracellular vesicles enriched for tumor‑derived miRNAs provide a non‑invasive source of cancer‑specific signatures; a machine‑learning model using selected miRNAs discriminates early‑stage lung cancer from controls and stratifies recurrence risk after surgery.
key_findings: Urinary EV isolation increases miRNA read proportion fivefold. | A 11‑miRNA feature set yields AUC 0.94 for cancer detection; performance holds in age‑matched subset. | Three‑miRNA prognostic panel (selected from 11) predicts recurrence‑free survival with HR 8.3 and 3‑year AUC 0.80. | Age and other clinical covariates do not confound the detection score.

talk_title: Multimodal miR‑15a conjugates overcome olaparib resistance in epithelial ovarian cancer
moa_summary: A chemically modified miR‑15a mimic incorporates 5‑FU, gemcitabine, and methotrexate, delivering tumor‑suppressive miRNA activity plus chemotherapeutic payloads; it down‑regulates DNA repair/checkpoint proteins (CHEK1, E1) and induces DNA damage, forcing cells into mitotic catastrophe and synergizing with olaparib.
key_findings: Modified miR‑15a reduces viability of ovarian cancer cells at low nanomolar concentrations without delivery vehicle. | The construct retains potency in olaparib‑resistant cells, showing similar IC50 values. | RNA‑seq reveals up‑regulation of DNA damage pathways and down‑regulation of WNT, mTOR, AMPK signaling. | Western blots confirm down‑regulation of CHEK1 and E1, and activation of CDK1/CDC25A. | Bliss analysis demonstrates strong synergy between the modified miR‑15a and olaparib. | In vivo, treatment (3.75 mg/kg) markedly suppresses metastatic tumor growth in both parental and resistant models with no observable toxicity.

talk_title: Therapy‑induced senescent cells promote early dissemination via JUP‑mediated clustering
moa_summary: Therapy‑induced senescent breast cancer cells acquire a JUP‑dependent adhesive phenotype that forms multicellular clusters, enhancing early lung dissemination and, in immunocompetent hosts, promoting metastatic outgrowth through immune modulation.
key_findings: Doxorubicin or fractionated irradiation induces dose‑dependent senescence in TNBC xenografts (X‑gal staining, beta‑gal activity). | Co‑injection of senescent cells with parental 231 cells does not affect primary tumor size but increases lung metastases (p<0.01). | Senescent cells accelerate early dissemination: 3‑day tail‑vein experiments show higher lung luciferase signal versus parental cells alone. | 3D imaging reveals larger tumor cell clusters and extravasation of senescent cells themselves in the lung. | RNA‑seq identifies up‑regulation of adhesion pathways; JUP (plakoglobin) is highly expressed in senescent cells. | JUP knockout abolishes senescent‑cell‑mediated adhesion, cluster formation, and early dissemination (p<0.001). | In immunocompetent mice, senescent cells further enhance metastatic outgrowth beyond early dissemination, suggesting an immune‑modulatory role.

talk_title: Extracellular matrix stiffness regulates TYK2‑mediated EMT and breast cancer metastasis
moa_summary: Reduced matrix stiffness maintains TYK2 activity at the cell membrane via IFN‑α1 binding; loss of TYK2 at high stiffness releases EPHA2 and Nyn activity, leading to Twist1 phosphorylation, nuclear translocation, and EMT‑driven invasion and lung dissemination.
key_findings: Normal breast tissue stiffness ~100‑200 Pa; invasive tumors reach 5‑6 kPa (30‑fold increase). | TAK2 knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition in 3D hydrogel (≤150 Pa) increases cell invasion and mesenchymal marker expression (vimentin, N‑cadherin). | TAK2 phosphorylation is high at low stiffness and drops sharply with increased matrix rigidity. | TAK2 inhibition (Tucabasertib) phenocopies knockdown, enhancing EMT and lung dissemination in orthotopic mouse models. | TAK2 interacts with IFN‑α1 and EPHA2 at low stiffness; high stiffness disrupts these interactions, activating Nyn‑mediated Twist1 phosphorylation. | Patient‑derived organoids recapitulate stiffness‑dependent invasion; TAK2 inhibition drives EMT markers and Twist1 nuclear localization.

talk_title: Spatial profiling reveals SPP1‑positive macrophages as central architects of colorectal brain metastasis
moa_summary: Macrophage‑derived SPP1 engages integrin/CD44 receptors on neighboring tumor and stromal cells, driving collagen‑rich ECM remodeling, immunosuppression, and enhanced tumor cell invasion in the colorectal brain metastasis niche.
key_findings: SPP1‑high macrophages are present in >80 % of CRC brain metastasis samples and co‑localize with tumor cells and fibroblasts. | Spatial neighborhood analysis identifies two macrophage‑enriched niches; one directly contacts tumor cells, the other contacts stromal cells. | SPP1 knockout in macrophages reduces expression of lipid‑metabolism genes (e.g., SQLE) and diminishes tumor cell invasion in co‑culture. | Macrophage SPP1 loss decreases tumor cell expression of metastasis‑related genes (e.g., S100 family, VIM). | Organ‑on‑a‑chip assays show a ~40 % reduction in macrophage migration speed when SPP1 is deleted. | Analysis of paired primary CRC and brain mets shows higher predicted SPP1‑integrin/CD44 interactions in brain lesions (p<0.01).

talk_title: KPC1 ubiquitin ligase suppresses melanoma mesenchymal transition by degrading ZEB1
moa_summary: KPC1 ubiquitinates ZEB1, targeting it for proteasomal degradation; loss of KPC1 stabilizes ZEB1, driving EMT, N‑cadherin up‑regulation and poor melanoma prognosis.
key_findings: Low KPC1 expression correlates with high ZEB1 and CDH2 (N‑cadherin) in metastatic melanoma samples (p<0.001). | KPC1 knockout in melanoma cells increases ZEB1 protein levels without changing mRNA, indicating post‑translational regulation. | Cycloheximide chase shows prolonged ZEB1 half‑life in KPC1‑deficient cells. | Co‑IP demonstrates direct binding of KPC1 to ZEB1. | High KPC1 / low ZEB1 cohort exhibits significantly better overall survival (HR 0.45, 95% CI 0.30‑0.68). | MicroRNA‑155 mediates KPC1 down‑regulation in melanoma.
primary_value: HR 0.45 for high KPC1 vs low KPC1

talk_title: Jagged1 expression in breast cancer cells drives lymph node metastasis via Notch4‑mediated endothelial remodeling
moa_summary: Tumor‑cell Jagged1 engages Notch4 on lymphatic endothelial cells in a non‑canonical manner, disrupting VE‑cadherin junctions, increasing endothelial permeability, and facilitating tumor cell trans‑endothelial migration and colonization of lymph nodes.
key_findings: Jagged1‑positive breast cancer cells show >3‑fold increase in lymphovascular invasion compared with Jagged1‑negative controls (p<0.001). | Jagged1 overexpression leads to larger lymph node tumor burden (RFP‑PanCK area) and earlier colonization. | Coating lymphatic endothelial cells with Jagged1 protein disrupts VE‑cadherin junctions and creates intercellular gaps. | Notch4 neutralizing antibody blocks Jagged1‑induced endothelial gap formation and reduces transendothelial migration (≈45 % reduction). | VEGF‑R3 soluble trap reduces lymph node metastasis but does not affect primary tumor growth. | High Jagged1 expression in lymph node metastases predicts shorter relapse‑free survival (HR 2.1).
primary_value: HR 2.1 for Jagged1‑positive lymph node cells

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR Cells: Are They the New Black?
tumor_types: B-cell malignancies; multiple myeloma; T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia; acute myeloid leukemia; neuroblastoma; solid tumors
moa_summary: Allogeneic CAR‑T cells engineered to lack TCR and HLA molecules (or co‑knockdown CD52) avoid GvHD and host rejection, allowing robust expansion and MRD‑negative complete remissions across B‑ALL, T‑ALL, AML and neuroblastoma; CD7‑CAR‑T utilizes a protein‑expression blocker to target CD7 without fratricide, and GD2‑CAR‑T shows superior outcomes when administered early with low tumor burden.
key_findings: Allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T in 19 high‑risk pediatric B‑ALL patients achieved 100% MRD‑negative CR with a 3‑year PFS of 42% and no grade 3‑4 CRS/ICANS. | Only two cases of acute GvHD occurred (both grade 2, resolved with steroids/JAK1/2 inhibitor) despite use of unmanipulated donor cells. | CD7 CAR‑T with a protein‑expression blocker induced complete MRD‑negative remissions in two pediatric patients, one remaining disease‑free at 2 years post‑allograft. | GD2 CAR‑T in neuroblastoma (n=35) yielded >50% 5‑year EFS in low‑burden disease, with better outcomes when cells were collected at diagnosis. | Pre‑clinical data showed that knocking down TCR/HLA and CD52 together markedly improves expansion and persistence of allogeneic CAR‑T cells.
primary_population: pediatric B‑ALL treated with allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR NK Cell Platforms and Clinical Data
tumor_types: hematologic malignancies; Hodgkin lymphoma; diffuse large B‑cell lymphoma; solid tumors; glioblastoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; CAR_T; CAR_NK; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Cord‑blood derived allogeneic NK cells are engineered with an IL‑15 armoured CD70‑CAR (or CD5‑CAR) and a safety switch; they expand robustly in patients, persist for months, and mediate tumor cell killing directly and via ADCC, while a bispecific engager (AFM13) redirects NK cells to CD30‑positive Hodgkin lymphoma, achieving >90% ORR.
key_findings: Cord‑blood derived CD70 CAR‑NK cells from optimal units (frozen ≤24 h, nucleated RBC ≈ 80 M) yielded 70% 1‑year PFS and 95% 1‑year OS, whereas delayed‑freeze units performed poorly. | In a 42‑patient Hodgkin lymphoma study combining AFM13 with CAR‑NK, overall response rate exceeded 90% with two‑thirds achieving CR, despite lack of bridging therapy. | CAR‑NK cells persisted up to 6 months in peripheral blood, with early clonal expansion (clone 0) followed by emergence of a memory‑like clone (clone 4) linked to long‑term persistence. | CD5 CAR‑NK therapy in T‑PLL achieved CR in 3 of 4 heavily pre‑treated patients, with donor NK cells tracking to disease clearance. | Manufacturing from a single cord‑blood unit can produce 250 patient doses at <$600 per dose, enabling off‑the‑shelf scalability.

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR T Strategies to Overcome Rejection and Enhance Efficacy
tumor_types: large B‑cell lymphoma; renal cell carcinoma; solid tumors; autoimmune disorders
moa_summary: Allogeneic CAR‑T cells are engineered to eliminate TCR (prevent GvHD) and CD52 (allow anti‑CD52 lymphodepletion), or to express HLA‑E and knock‑out β2‑microglobulin (stealth), enabling robust expansion after standard lymphodepletion; CD70 is leveraged both as a tumor antigen and as a target to deplete alloreactive host T cells, improving persistence and efficacy in RCC and lymphoma.
key_findings: In the MRD‑guided ALO‑501A trial, 7 of 8 patients cleared MRD after a single dose of allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T with standard flu‑cyclophosphamide lymphodepletion, without CRS/ICANS. | CD70‑targeted allogeneic CAR‑T (LO316) achieved a 31% ORR in CD70‑high RCC, with durable responses >12 months in most responders. | Gene‑editing approaches (TCR knockout, CD52 knockout, HLA‑E insertion) enable allogeneic CAR‑T expansion comparable to autologous products despite HLA‑agnostic lymphodepletion. | Selective depletion of alloreactive host T cells via CD70 targeting (the “dagger” approach) enhances CAR‑T persistence and reduces rejection.

talk_title: SOX2 as a master regulator of classical squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: SOX2 amplification in classical SCC drives a transcriptional program by maintaining chromatin accessibility at enhancers, forming enhancer‑promoter loops that upregulate ferroptosis suppressors, thereby conferring resistance to ferroptosis.
key_findings: Classical SCC shows focal 3q amplification of SOX2 and a selective dependency on SOX2 for proliferation. | SOX2 knockdown reduces chromatin accessibility at thousands of sites and downregulates the classical SCC gene signature. | SOX2 overexpression restores accessibility and upregulates ferroptosis suppressor genes (GPX2, GSR, AKR1C3, SLC25A10). | SOX2‑deficient classical SCC cells exhibit increased lipid peroxidation and apoptosis upon ferroptosis inducer ML162 treatment.

talk_title: FOXP2B as a driver of neuroendocrine transformation in lung cancer
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Charlie Rudin Lab
tumor_types: non-small cell lung cancer; small cell lung cancer
moa_summary: FOXP2B overexpression in EGFR‑mutant LUAD with p53/Rb loss suppresses EGFR signaling and induces neuroendocrine markers, an effect amplified by REST inhibition, suggesting FOXP2B drives neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.
key_findings: FOXP2B is highly up‑regulated in transformed small‑cell samples compared with de novo small‑cell. | FOXP2B overexpression reduces total and phospho‑EGFR levels and induces neuroendocrine markers (ASCL1, synaptophysin, chromogranin) in p53/Rb‑deficient LUAD cells. | Combined FOXP2B overexpression and REST inhibition further enhances neuroendocrine marker expression. | FOXP2B directly binds the EGFR locus, suggesting transcriptional repression of EGFR.

talk_title: MBD4 regulates FOXA1 lineage‑specific enhancers to promote prostate tumorigenesis
moa_summary: MBD4 binds FOXA1‑motif enhancers in AR‑positive prostate cells, restricting FOXA1 pioneer activity; loss of MBD4 expands FOXA1 enhancer occupancy, reprogramming AR signaling toward an oncogenic program and altering lineage specification.
key_findings: CRISPR screen identified MBD4 as a top gene whose loss rescues growth suppression caused by AR‑ARE activation. | MBD4 knockout in LNCaP cells expands FOXA1‑motif enhancer accessibility and increases AR and H3K27ac signals at these sites. | Overexpression of MBD4 reduces FOXA1 enhancer sites, while knockout in normal prostate organoids drives luminal differentiation (CK8/18 up, CHOP2 down). | MBD4 interacts with HDAC1/2, suggesting a deacetylase‑mediated mechanism restricting FOXA1 activity.

talk_title: First‑in‑class oral small molecule DPX8743 dissolves MYC condensates
moa_summary: DPX8743 selectively dissolves MYC‑containing transcriptional condensates in MYC‑high cancer cells, leading to down‑regulation of MYC target genes, G1 cell‑cycle arrest, and apoptosis, while sparing global transcriptional condensates.
key_findings: DPX8743 dissolves MYC condensates in MYC‑high tumor cells but not in normal cells with basal MYC levels. | Condensate dissolution correlates with G1 arrest, reduced MYC target expression, and tumor regression in MYC‑amplified CDX/PDX models. | Compound activity is potentiated by higher MYC protein levels and MYC genetic dependency; MYC knockdown desensitizes cells. | Seven‑day rat toxicology shows no weight loss or major organ toxicity except reversible thymus atrophy, indicating a therapeutic index >4×.

talk_title: NSD3 as a co‑factor stabilizing BRD4‑NUT condensates in NUT carcinoma
moa_summary: NSD3 deposits H3K36me2 within BRD4‑NUT mega‑domains, stabilizing BRD4‑NUT occupancy and nuclear condensate intensity, thereby maintaining long‑range chromatin contacts and MYC‑driven transcriptional programs in NUT carcinoma.
key_findings: NSD3 interacts with BRD4‑NUT and deposits H3K36me2 at mega‑domains. | NSD3 knockdown reduces H3K36me2, diminishes BRD4‑NUT chromatin binding, and lowers condensate intensity while increasing condensate number. | Hi‑C reveals ~2‑fold loss of long‑range contacts between mega‑domains upon NSD3 depletion. | Genes within mega‑domains, especially MYC targets, are down‑regulated, leading to modest growth inhibition and differentiation markers up‑regulation.

talk_title: Extrachromosomal DNA generates oncogenic fusion transcripts such as PVT1‑MYC
moa_summary: ecDNA harbors high‑frequency structural rearrangements that generate fusion transcripts; the PVT1‑MYC fusion places PVT1 as a 5′ UTR, enhancing MYC mRNA stability and translation, leading to amplified MYC target activation and a proliferative advantage.
key_findings: ecDNA exhibits the highest DNA and RNA fusion frequencies across cancers. | PVT1‑MYC is the most prevalent fusion on ecDNA, with >98% of fusions placing PVT1 at the 5′ end. | PVT1‑MYC transcripts show greater RNA stability and drive six‑fold stronger MYC‑target activation than canonical MYC. | In MYC‑depleted cells, PVT1‑MYC rescues viability more efficiently than canonical MYC.

talk_title: VNTR2‑1 antisense transcript regulates DNA replication via METTL3‑dependent translation
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics
moa_summary: The VNTR2‑1 repeat in TERT intron 2 encodes an antisense transcript with an open reading frame whose length scales with repeat number; METTL3‑mediated m6A modification enhances its translation, driving DNA replication gene programs and promoting cell proliferation.
key_findings: VNTR2‑1 antisense transcript is expressed and contains multiple DRACH motifs for METTL3‑dependent m6A modification. | M6A‑intact reporter shows higher luciferase activity; METTL3 inhibition reduces this activity. | Flag‑tagged VNTR2‑1AS construct produces protein, confirming translational competence. | Overexpression of VNTR2‑1AS up‑regulates DNA replication and cell‑cycle pathways; knockout reduces these pathways and slows proliferation. | Translation‑deficient start‑codon mutant fails to enhance proliferation, indicating translation‑dependent function.

talk_title: AACR Progress and the GLITZ Clinical Trial
moa_summary: The speaker introduced the GLITZ clinical trial, which utilizes a multi-biomarker informed treatment approach to determine if patients with gastroesophageal cancer can safely avoid surgery. The trial's IND application has been approved by the FDA and Health Canada.
key_findings: The GLITZ Clinical Trial IND application was approved by the US FDA and Health Canada to assess multi-biomarker informed treatment in gastroesophageal cancer. | Cancer mortality has decreased by nearly 35% since the peak in the 1990s. | The number of cancer survivors in the US has increased by approximately 6.6 million over the last 15 years. | Over 20 new anticancer therapeutics were FDA approved between July 2024 and June 2025.

talk_title: Targeting Programmed Cell Death in Leukemia
tumor_types: chronic lymphocytic leukemia; acute myelogenous leukemia
moa_summary: The speaker described the translational journey from basic protein chemistry to the clinical application of targeting programmed cell death pathways. This work culminated in the development and use of venetoclax for the treatment of AML and CLL.
key_findings: Targeting programmed cell death pathways is an effective strategy for treating chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myelogenous leukemia. | Venetoclax was successfully translated from laboratory research into a standard of care for AML patients.

talk_title: How Cancer Cells Escape Targeted Therapy by Changing Identity
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Chair of Human Oncology and Pathogenesis Program
tumor_types: prostate cancer; small cell lung cancer; pancreatic cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; cell_cycle; tumor_microenvironment; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Loss of RB1 primes prostate adenocarcinoma cells for lineage plasticity; extracellular matrix engagement via integrin‑β1 restrains YAP/TAZ‑TEAD activity, while Notch signaling also suppresses neuroendocrine transition. Disruption of matrix signaling, inhibition of LATS kinase, or pharmacologic blockade of YAP/TEAD or Notch releases this brake, inducing ASCL1 expression and a neuroendocrine phenotype that can be reversed pharmacologically. FOXA1 relocalization and TEAD binding rewire chromatin, enabling MYC paralog‑TIP60 co‑activator dependence in the neuroendocrine state.
key_findings: RB loss combined with PTEN or TP53 loss drives adenocarcinoma-to‑neuroendocrine transition in mouse prostate models. | Matrix withdrawal or β1‑integrin deletion induces ASCL1 expression within 7 days in organoids. | LATS inhibition restores YAP activity and blocks ASCL1 up‑regulation, demonstrating reversibility of lineage transition. | Combined TEAD and Notch inhibition drives >90% of organoid cells to a neuroendocrine state. | FOXA1 relocalization to TEAD‑bound enhancers is required for transition; FOXA1 knockout prevents neuroendocrine differentiation. | Neuroendocrine cells become dependent on L‑MYC/TIP60 partnership, revealing a potential therapeutic vulnerability.

talk_title: Beyond Blood: The Evolution of CAR T Therapy for Solid Malignancies
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Perelman School of Medicine, Director of Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy
tumor_types: glioblastoma; pancreatic cancer; ovarian cancer; gastroesophageal cancer; mesothelioma
topic_categories: CAR_T; immunotherapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; tumor_microenvironment
moa_summary: Engineered autologous T cells expressing chimeric antigen receptors target tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., CD19, mesothelin, claudin‑18.2, EGFRvIII/IL‑13Rα2). Armoring CARs with cytokines (IL‑18) or delivering CAR constructs via lipid nanoparticles enhances persistence and stromal targeting (anti‑FAP). Dual‑target CARs and regional delivery (Ommaya reservoir) improve trafficking and efficacy in solid tumors such as glioblastoma, while NK cell infiltration and low Treg density correlate with better outcomes.
key_findings: IL‑18‑armored CAR T cells achieved 75% complete response in CAR‑refractory lymphoma with no increase in CRS. | Dual‑target EGFRvIII/IL‑13Rα2 CAR T delivered via Ommaya achieved 2 PRs and ~70% disease control in 18 glioblastoma patients. | Higher intratumoral NK cell levels predicted longer PFS, whereas higher Treg levels predicted resistance in glioblastoma CAR T trials. | Sequential anti‑FAP followed by anti‑mesothelin CAR T in KPC mice markedly improved survival compared with either monotherapy. | Lipid‑nanoparticle‑delivered anti‑FAP CAR RNA achieved superior tumor control versus retroviral CAR T in pancreatic cancer models.

talk_title: Chemical Rewiring of Biological Circuits for Cancer Therapy: Targeted Protein Degradation and Beyond
affiliation: CeMM Research Center for Molecular Medicine; Aithera Research Institute for Biomedical AI, Austrian Academy of Sciences
moa_summary: PROTACs and molecular glues induce proximity between an E3 ligase (e.g., CRBN, VHL, FBXO3, DCAF16) and a target protein, leading to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. Novel mechanisms include intermolecular bivalent gluing (IBG‑1 bridges two bromodomains of RAD4 to recruit DCAF16) and inhibitor‑induced supercharging of native degradation pathways (kinase inhibitors promote native E3 ligase recognition). SERDs such as fulvestrant act by inducing SUMOylation of ERα, which then triggers RNF4/RNF111‑mediated ubiquitination; however, degradation is dispensable for anti‑estrogenic activity, while SUMOylation is essential for transcriptional repression.
key_findings: Identified UBE2M as a global regulator of >200 E3 ligases, enabling phenotypic discovery of molecular glues. | Discovered DHTC3, a molecular glue that recruits FBXO3 to degrade BET4 via a chemically derived exit vector. | Revealed IBG‑1 acts via intermolecular bivalent gluing, bridging two RAD4 bromodomains and recruiting DCAF16 despite low affinity. | Showed that many kinase inhibitors cause “supercharging” of native degradation, leading to target destabilization without a dedicated E3 ligase. | Demonstrated that fulvestrant induces SUMOylation of ERα, which is required for transcriptional repression, while degradation by RNF4/RNF111 is dispensable.

talk_title: Rethinking Cancer Diagnosis and Treatment with AI from Molecular Mechanisms to Clinical Management
affiliation: MIT, School of Engineering Distinguished Professor for AI and Health, Jameel Clinic
moa_summary: Deep learning models (e.g., SPARK, Mirai) integrate routine pathology slides or mammograms with spatial transcriptomics to predict molecular pathway activity and long‑term clinical outcomes. The models learn patch‑level annotations of ~40 molecular programs from paired imaging‑omics data, enabling risk stratification, early detection, and mechanistic insight into tumor biology.
key_findings: SPARK predicts 40 molecular programs from routine pathology slides with AUC ~0.90 and Pearson ~0.74. | Adding program-level information to image features improves prediction of disease‑free survival and treatment response across multiple cancer types. | Mirai mammography model achieves a 5‑year c‑index of 0.80, outperforming density (c‑index 0.50) and questionnaire‑based risk models (c‑index 0.63). | High‑risk patients identified by Mirai correspond to risk levels exceeding those of BRCA1 carriers. | Prospective trials (UMass, UCSF, Israel, MiPEBS) are underway to evaluate AI‑driven risk‑adapted screening.

talk_title: Mechanistic analysis of CTLA-4 and PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor colitis in human patients
moa_summary: CTLA-4 and PD-1 blockade induce colitis by reactivating resident memory CD8 T cells and expanding Th1/Th17 CD4 effector populations; regulatory T cells are not depleted in human colitis, and S1PR1+ Tregs expand uniquely in CTLA-4 colitis, suggesting distinct mechanistic pathways for each checkpoint.
key_findings: Both CTLA-4 and PD-1 colitis show expansion of resident memory CD8 T cells and Th1/Th17 CD4 effector cells. | Regulatory T cells are broadly expanded rather than depleted in human colitis biopsies. | CTLA-4 colitis uniquely expands S1PR1+ Treg populations not seen in PD-1 colitis. | Clonotypic expansion is observed in effector cells, with resident memory clones dominating in healthy controls and effector clones dominating in colitis.

talk_title: Wild microbiota–driven CTLA-4 blockade colitis model and Fc‑dependent toxicity separation
moa_summary: Gavage of wild mouse microbiota into antibiotic‑treated mice creates a microbiota‑dependent, CD4‑Th1–driven colitis upon CTLA‑4 blockade; Fc‑effector function of the anti‑CTLA‑4 antibody is required for colitis but not for anti‑tumor efficacy, allowing separation of toxicity from therapeutic benefit.
key_findings: Wild‑derived microbiota, but not standard SPF microbiota, enables severe colitis after anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment. | Colitis is CD4‑Th1 dependent; depletion of CD4 T cells or neutralization of IFN‑γ abolishes disease. | Peripheral Treg depletion (Helios‑negative FoxP3+ cells) occurs with CTLA‑4 antibody; thymic Tregs remain. | Fc‑null CTLA‑4 antibody (H11 extender) retains anti‑tumor activity while eliminating colitis, demonstrating Fc‑dependence of toxicity. | Clonotypic analysis reveals oligoclonal expansion of Th1 cells recognizing microbial antigens in colitic mice.

talk_title: Bench to bedside in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma (EHE)
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: The TAZ-CAMTA1 fusion drives EHE by constitutively activating the Hippo pathway effector TAZ, leading to nuclear TEAD transcriptional programs. Trametinib inhibits MAPK signaling downstream of the fusion, reducing colony formation in vitro and tumor growth in mouse models. CDK9 inhibition (dinaciclib) disrupts transcriptional elongation complexes essential for fusion-driven transcription, showing synergistic tumor regression when combined with MEK inhibition.
key_findings: TAZ-CAMTA1 fusion activates MAPK pathway; MEK inhibitor trametinib shows IC50 <10 nM in colony assays. | Phase 2 trial of trametinib in unresectable/metastatic EHE yielded 20 responders among 24 patients but did not meet primary endpoint for FDA approval. | Genetically engineered TAZ-CAMTA1 homozygous mice develop histologically identical EHE tumors, providing a faithful preclinical model. | CDK9 inhibitors (dinaciclib, CGP) induce apoptosis in EHE cell lines and dramatically reduce lung tumor burden in xenograft models. | Combination of MEK inhibition with TEAD inhibition (MGH CP1) shows synergistic growth suppression in vitro. | NMR‑based fragment screening identified small‑molecule hits targeting TEAD interface pockets, achieving sub‑micromolar affinity after optimization.

talk_title: Project Cure CRC Integrated Adaptive Clinical Trial Platform
affiliation: Colorectal Cancer Alliance, Senior Director of Research Programs
moa_summary: The Alliance has built an integrated ecosystem (BlueHQ, Blue Lake, adaptive trial) that links patient-reported data, genomic profiling, and real-world outcomes to accelerate colorectal cancer drug development, with patients shaping trial windows and governance.
key_findings: Received 525 research proposals in the past cycle. | Allocated $15 M to 34 projects across 150 investigators. | Engaged 20 industry partners in the adaptive trial platform. | Goal to increase 5‑year metastatic CRC survival from 13% to 35% within five years.

talk_title: Lineberger Excellence in Advocacy Program Impact Metrics
affiliation: USC Norris Cancer Center, Associate Director of Community Outreach and Engagement
moa_summary: LEAP consolidates multiple patient/community advisory structures into a single program that tracks engagement metrics, provides training, matches advocates to research projects, and integrates patient voices into grant review and protocol development at a comprehensive cancer center.
key_findings: Advocate pool composition: 83% female, 17% male. | Processed 86 matching requests with 250 advocate responses. | Reviewed 328 pilot grants with participation of 36 individual advocates. | Implemented systematic evaluation surveys with >90% positive scores on respect and impact.

talk_title: Patient Advocate Involvement in Matchmakers Preclinical Immunotherapy Project
affiliation: Ovarian Cancer Research Alliance, Survivor Advocate
moa_summary: The Matchmakers team integrates patient advocates into a preclinical T‑cell receptor engineering program, providing monthly meetings, co‑created educational deliverables, and bidirectional feedback that improves scientific relevance and trainee communication skills.
key_findings: 70+ patient advocates contributed to 21 preclinical immunotherapy teams. | Advocate involvement increased trainee awareness of patient relevance from 0% to ~50% in surveys. | Co‑created lay summaries, glossaries, and videos improved communication of complex TCR science. | Advocates reported enhanced understanding of basic research, while trainees noted better non‑scientist communication.

talk_title: Spatial single-cell analysis of supratentorial ependymoma subtypes
moa_summary: Single-cell RNA‑seq and 10x Xenium spatial transcriptomics were used to map developmental cell states across supratentorial ependymoma subtypes, revealing that YAP1‑driven tumors are differentiated ependymal‑like, while CEFTA/RELA tumors retain neuroepithelial and embryonic‑like stem states; spatial organization correlates with a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state, and in vitro/in vivo models show that differentiation requires microenvironmental cues.
key_findings: Assembled a cohort of >60 supratentorial ependymoma samples covering all molecular subclusters. | YAP1‑fusion tumors project to later developmental stages and consist almost exclusively of differentiated ependymal‑like cells. | CEFTA/RELA‑fusion tumors contain a mixture of neuroepithelial‑like stem cells, embryonic‑like cells, and differentiated ependymal/neuronal states. | Spatial transcriptomics identified two global tumor architectures (organized vs disorganized) driven by the presence of a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state. | In vitro cultures grown as PDX or neuron/astrocyte co‑cultures recapitulate the full differentiation hierarchy, whereas neurosphere‑only cultures enrich for stem‑like cells. | In vivo two‑photon imaging showed distinct morphologies and migration patterns: stem‑like cells are slow/proliferative, neuronal‑like cells display saltatory migration, and ependymal‑like cells are stationary with dynamic projections.

talk_title: Targeting ACOD1‑itaconate axis to suppress ZFTA‑RELA fusion in ependymoma
moa_summary: ZFTA/RELA fusion upregulates ACOD1, leading to increased production of itaconate; itaconate acts as an oncometabolite that inhibits KDM5 histone demethylases, raising H3K4me3 at the fusion promoter and sustaining fusion expression. Pharmacologic or genetic inhibition of ACOD1 (dimethyl itaconate, citraconate analogs) or upstream glutamine metabolism (GHU‑083) reduces itaconate levels, lowers H3K4me3, down‑regulates fusion mRNA and protein, and prolongs survival in mouse models.
key_findings: ZFTA/RELA fusion drives strong up‑regulation of ACOD1 and accumulation of itaconate in both mouse and human ependymoma cells. | shRNA knockdown of the fusion or ACOD1 reduces cell viability and ACOD1 expression. | ACOD1 whole‑body or tumor‑cell specific knockout in mice dramatically improves survival (median age 25 days → >50 % long‑term survivors). | Dimethyl itaconate treatment lowers fusion mRNA and protein levels in vitro and in vivo; rescue with exogenous itaconate restores fusion expression. | Itaconate inhibits KDM5 demethylases (IC50 ~470 µM), increasing H3K4me3; KDM5 inhibition phenocopies itaconate’s effect on fusion expression. | Glutamine antagonist GHU‑083 reduces itaconate production, lowers fusion levels, and extends survival in IOE mouse models. | Pharmacologic ACOD1 inhibition prevents spinal metastasis in treated mice (0/7 vs 7/7 in controls).

talk_title: Anti‑GM‑CSF therapy reverses immunotolerance in pediatric brain tumors
tumor_types: supratentorial ependymoma; choroid plexus carcinoma; group 3 medulloblastoma
moa_summary: Tumor‑derived GM‑CSF in the CSF drives expansion of MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial bone marrow, which differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells and promote T‑reg development; intrathecal or intravenous Lenzilumab blocks GM‑CSF, reducing stem‑cell proliferation, myeloid suppression, and restoring CD8⁺ T‑cell anti‑tumor activity, leading to tumor regression in multiple pediatric brain tumor models.
key_findings: CSF of tumor‑bearing mice contains high GM‑CSF levels, absent in tumor‑free controls. | GM‑CSF promotes expansion of CD34⁺MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial marrow, which migrate to dura and tumor. | These stem cells differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells; CD4⁺ T cells skew toward FoxP3⁺ Tregs. | Intrathecal Lenzilumab lowers CSF GM‑CSF, reduces skull HSC proliferation, decreases tumor‑infiltrating myeloid cells, and increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration. | Tumor regression observed in ZFTA‑RELA ependymoma, choroid plexus carcinoma, and group 3 medulloblastoma models; effect replicated with intravenous dosing. | Combination of Lenzilumab with checkpoint inhibition prolongs remission beyond monotherapy.
primary_population: mouse models of ependymoma, CPC, medulloblastoma

talk_title: Dysregulated AKT Signaling Reprograms Osteosarcoma to Drive Selective Reliance on EP300
moa_summary: JQUAD1 is a cereblon-recruiting PROTAC that selectively degrades EP300, sparing CBP, leading to loss of enhancer acetylation and transcriptional reprogramming in AKT‑activated osteosarcoma. Co‑inhibition of AKT with MK‑2206 or capivasertib reduces translation rates, enhancing EP300 degradation and synergistic tumor growth inhibition.
key_findings: JQUAD1 selectively degrades EP300 with minimal effect on CBP in vitro. | AKT‑dysregulated osteosarcoma lines (PTEN loss or AKT amplification) are uniquely sensitive to JQUAD1. | Combining JQUAD1 with AKT inhibitors (MK‑2206 or capivasertib) yields synergistic cell death in ~10‑15% of 800 screened cell lines. | Synergy is linked to reduced translational capacity in sensitive lines, as shown by puromycin incorporation assays. | In orthotopic osteosarcoma xenografts, the JQUAD1 + MK‑2206 combination markedly suppresses tumor growth and prolongs survival compared to either agent alone.

talk_title: Cell‑free DNA Profiling in Relapsed/Refractory Neuroblastoma
moa_summary: Low‑coverage whole‑genome and high‑coverage exome sequencing of plasma cfDNA enables detection of tumor‑specific copy‑number alterations and SNVs, allowing longitudinal monitoring of clonal dynamics, emergence of resistance mutations (e.g., ALK, FGFR1), and prognostic assessment via cfDNA quantity in relapsed/refractory neuroblastoma.
key_findings: cfDNA quantity at enrollment predicts poorer survival independent of disease burden. | Relapsed neuroblastoma shows enrichment of ALK and RAS/MAPK pathway mutations, including FGFR1. | Longitudinal cfDNA dynamics correlate with treatment response: decrease during response, re‑increase at progression. | Clonal hematopoiesis events (e.g., JAK3) were detected in cfDNA, highlighting need for careful interpretation. | Predicted gene expression from cfDNA fragment patterns identifies activation of oncogenes (e.g., ALK, TERT) at relapse.

talk_title: Spatial TCR Profiling Reveals Barriers to T Cell Engagement in High‑Risk Neuroblastoma
affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, University of Toronto
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; spatial_biology; TCR_sequencing; biomarker_development; translational
moa_summary: Custom Xenium spatial transcriptomics probes track specific TCR clonotypes in situ, revealing that gamma‑delta T cells preferentially infiltrate neural‑blast rich tumor domains and adopt an exhausted phenotype (LAG3 high), while alpha‑beta clones show variable localization, linking spatial context to T‑cell functional states in high‑risk neuroblastoma.
key_findings: Gamma‑delta T cells are the most spatially privileged population, with ~45% of nearest neighbors being neural blast cells. | T cells in neural‑blast rich domains express exhaustion marker LAG3, whereas those in immune‑rich domains express activation markers CD4 and FOS. | Cross‑patient gamma‑delta clone 2 is detected in tumor, blood, and cfDNA across multiple patients and time points, often adjacent to tumor cells. | Alpha‑beta TCR pairing can be inferred by co‑detection of alpha and beta probes within the same cell, confirming clonotype identity in situ. | Predicted antigen specificity for clone 2 includes NY‑ESO‑1, present in ~23% of neuroblastoma tumors.

talk_title: Co‑targeting GPC2 and B7H3 with Bicistronic CAR T Cells in Neuroblastoma and Medulloblastoma
moa_summary: Bicistronic CAR T cells co‑express GPC2‑specific and B7H3‑specific CARs in a single vector, enabling simultaneous targeting of two tumor antigens; this prevents antigen escape (GPC2 down‑regulation) and improves T‑cell proliferation, cytokine secretion, and tumor infiltration, leading to durable tumor control in neuroblastoma and medulloblastoma PDX models.
key_findings: Bicistronic GPC2/B7H3 CAR T cells outperform single‑antigen CARs and co‑transduced mixtures in vitro and in vivo. | Dual CARs achieve complete tumor regressions and long‑term survival in neuroblastoma and medulloblastoma PDX models. | B7H3 expression remains stable under CAR pressure, while GPC2 is frequently down‑regulated, supporting the need for dual targeting. | Mice cured with dual CARs retain persisting memory T cells in bone marrow, conferring protection against rechallenge. | Relapse after dual CAR therapy is associated with increased MDSC infiltration.
primary_value: complete responses in majority of mice

talk_title: Surface‑Localized Nuclear Envelope Proteins as Targets in MYC‑Driven Group 3 Medulloblastoma
moa_summary: Single‑domain camelid antibodies against LBR and TMPO are engineered into second‑generation CARs; these antigens are aberrantly presented on the plasma membrane of MYC‑driven Group 3 medulloblastoma due to nuclear envelope fragmentation, enabling CAR‑T mediated cytotoxicity against a proliferative tumor subpopulation.
key_findings: LBR and TMPO are mislocalized to the cell surface specifically in MYC‑driven Group 3 medulloblastoma, not in normal neural cells. | LBR+ tumor cells display high proliferation signatures (G2/M enrichment). | LBR‑CAR and TMPO‑CAR T cells achieve potent killing in vitro at low E:T ratios. | Bispecific CAR combining LBR and TMPO binders yields superior tumor remission and durability in vivo. | Therapy‑adapted mouse models show enrichment of LBR expression after standard chemoradiotherapy, indicating a post‑treatment target.

talk_title: Neuronal Activity‑Regulated Mechanisms Promote Growth of Diffuse Hemispheric Glioma H3‑G34 Mutant
moa_summary: Neuronal activity, via both glutamatergic and GABAergic circuits, enhances proliferation of H3‑G34 glioma cells through synaptic integration and release of activity‑regulated secreted factors; proteomic analysis identified N‑cadherin and neurofascin as novel paracrine mediators that promote tumor growth and migration.
key_findings: Electron microscopy shows ~10% neuron‑to‑glioma synapses and glioma‑to‑glioma synapses in G34 xenografts. | Glioma cells exhibit evoked EPSCs and spontaneous activity, indicating functional integration. | Pharmacologic blockade of neuronal activity (TTX, NBQX) abolishes neuron‑induced proliferation boost. | Optogenetic activation of either glutamatergic or GABAergic neurons increases tumor proliferation in vivo. | Proteomics of activity‑stimulated conditioned media identifies N‑cadherin and neurofascin as upregulated secreted factors. | Recombinant N‑cadherin promotes proliferation; neurofascin enhances glioma migration.

talk_title: Targeting MIB and REM4 with REM422 in Pediatric T‑ALL
moa_summary: REM422 is an oral small‑molecule that promotes inclusion of a poison exon in MIB pre‑mRNA, triggering nonsense‑mediated decay and loss of MIB protein, thereby inhibiting NOTCH pathway signaling essential for T‑ALL survival.
key_findings: REM422 induces rapid (2‑24 h) depletion of MIB protein in ALL PDX cells at nanomolar concentrations. | In vivo, daily oral REM422 (5 mg/kg) is well tolerated and extends EFS across diverse ALL subtypes. | T‑ALL PDXs show greater benefit (median +33 days, T/C = 3.6) than B‑ALL. | Seven of ten T‑ALL models achieve complete or maintained complete responses, including durable remissions beyond treatment cessation. | MIB expression alone does not fully predict response, indicating additional determinants of sensitivity.
primary_population: pediatric ALL PDXs treated with REM422

talk_title: Development and impact of prophylactic HPV virus‑like particle vaccines
tumor_types: cervical cancer; anal cancer; oropharyngeal cancer; vulvar cancer; vaginal cancer
moa_summary: HPV L1 virus‑like particles self‑assemble into 360‑subunit VLPs that present repetitive neutralizing epitopes, inducing durable, high‑titer antibodies that persist for >15 years and provide sterilizing immunity by blocking the slow, basement‑membrane‑mediated entry of HPV into wounded epithelium.
key_findings: A monovalent HPV16 VLP trial showed 0 persistent infections in the vaccine arm versus 41 in placebo, demonstrating sterilizing immunity. | Single‑dose HPV vaccination achieved non‑inferior efficacy (97%–98%) compared with two doses in the ESCUDO trial, supporting dose de‑escalation. | Antibody titers after a single dose remain flat over 16 years, declining <2.5% per year, indicating long‑lived plasma cell maintenance. | Real‑world data from Denmark show ~90% protection against cervical cancer precursors in women vaccinated ≤16 y. | Global vaccine uptake has risen from 72% to 78% (≥1 dose) and 75% to 80% (up‑to‑date) among US adolescents, contributing to an estimated 1.4 million future deaths averted.

talk_title: Therapeutic applications of HPV VLPs and VLP‑IR700 conjugates
tumor_types: ocular melanoma; bladder cancer; pan-cancer (HSPG‑modified tumors)
topic_categories: cancer_vaccines; immunotherapy; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: HPV VLPs bind preferentially to cancer cells displaying EMT‑associated, modified heparan‑sulfate proteoglycans; conjugation with the infrared‑activatable dye IR700 enables light‑triggered membrane disruption and necrotic cell death, while simultaneously releasing neoantigens that prime secondary T‑cell responses, creating a dual‑mode therapeutic platform.
key_findings: HPV VLPs bind >90% of a 129‑cell‑line panel, with binding strongly correlated to EMT/TGF‑β transcriptional programs. | In a Phase I/II ocular melanoma trial, the therapeutic VLP‑IR700 regimen markedly prolonged time to standard‑of‑care referral compared with sub‑therapeutic dosing. | Therapeutic dosing halted tumor growth in ocular melanoma patients, suggesting disease‑modifying activity. | A bladder cancer window‑of‑opportunity study showed complete pathological response after intravesical VLP‑IR700 plus light, indicating potent local tumor ablation. | The VLP‑IR700 platform leverages dual specificity (tumor binding + light activation) to achieve on‑target cytotoxicity with minimal off‑target effects.
primary_metric: Time to referral to standard of care (ocular melanoma)
primary_value: significantly longer with therapeutic dose vs sub‑therapeutic dose

talk_title: AI-augmented drug discovery across biologics, small molecules, and multimodal biomarkers
moa_summary: AstraZeneca embeds AI models to predict protein structures for de novo mini‑protein binders (17% hit rate, 28 nM affinity) and to guide small‑molecule optimization (potency, clearance, exposure) for an intractable target, while computational pathology AI quantifies membrane‑cytoplasm optical density to predict ADC internalization, and multimodal foundation models integrate EHR, imaging, and omics to improve patient selection and trial design.
key_findings: AI‑designed mini‑protein library yielded 45 binders (17% success) with highest affinity of 28 nM in just one month, a three‑fold speedup over traditional methods. | Reinvent AI tool accelerated small‑molecule lead optimization for a confidential target, reducing the design cycle from ~8 months to ~4 months while achieving target‑level potency, clearance, exposure, and tumor growth inhibition. | Computational pathology model identified membrane‑to‑cytoplasm optical density difference as a surrogate for internalization of the TROP2 ADC datupizumab duoxetecane, now entering prospective validation in a phase‑III trial. | A multimodal foundation model built with Tempus and Pathos integrates longitudinal EHR, labs, histology, and genomics to predict control‑arm performance and patient response, aiming to improve phase‑III success rates.

talk_title: Chromatin remodeling complexes as AI‑guided therapeutic targets
affiliation: Harvard University, Professor of Molecular Biology; Co‑founder, Foghorn Therapeutics
tumor_types: lung cancer; pediatric malignancies; solid tumors
moa_summary: AI‑driven AlphaFold‑based modeling identified a novel immunoglobulin‑like SWIFT domain within BAF chromatin remodelers that mediates transcription‑factor interactions; hydrogen‑deuterium exchange validated TF‑SWIFT binding sites, and CRISPR dependency screens revealed synthetic lethalities (e.g., SMARCA2 dependence in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer), establishing these interfaces as emerging therapeutic targets.
key_findings: AI‑predicted SWIFT domain structures correctly identified three PU.1 TAD binding motifs, validated by pull‑down and HDX experiments. | Fitness correlation across >1,000 cancer cell lines highlighted SWIFT‑containing paralogs (SMARCD1‑3) as top codependencies, suggesting selective vulnerability. | Histone modification profiling showed that specific nucleosome acetylation patterns dramatically modulate BAF complex remodeling activity, revealing allele‑specific inhibition opportunities. | Synthetic‑lethal relationship between SMARCA2 and SMARCA4 mutations was confirmed, positioning SMARCA2 as a high‑value target in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer.

talk_title: AI‑driven de novo antibody design with atomic precision
moa_summary: Chai Discovery uses diffusion‑based generative models and high‑resolution folding predictions to design full‑length antibodies (including VHH) that directly target defined epitopes on GPCRs, peptide‑MHC complexes, and cytokine interfaces, achieving nanomolar affinities and functional agonism/antagonism without library screening, while agentic AI enforces developability constraints and off‑target avoidance.
key_findings: Design of antibodies against >50 diverse targets succeeded with ≤20 designs per target, yielding binders for ~50% of targets (≈10‑fold improvement over prior 0.1% rates). | Atomic‑resolution predictions matched cryo‑EM structures (global RMSD <2 Å, CDR loop RMSD <1 Å) for five test cases, confirming near‑perfect epitope modeling. | GPCR VHH agonist for CCR8 achieved functional signaling after testing only 10 designs, with 1 agonist hit, demonstrating feasibility of de novo agonist design. | Peptide‑MHC antibody selectively bound KRAS G12V peptide‑MHC complex while sparing WT KRAS and alternative MHCs, illustrating precise epitope discrimination. | Developability metrics (expression, stability, viscosity, polyreactivity) of AI‑generated antibodies matched or exceeded those of clinically approved mAbs on first‑pass designs.

talk_title: Adolescent adherence to Planetary Health Dietary Index and breast cancer risk
affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health
moa_summary: Higher adherence to the EAT-Lancet planetary health diet during adolescence is linked to a lower incidence of ER-negative breast cancer, particularly among premenopausal women with lower BMI, suggesting a protective effect of plant-forward dietary patterns on hormone‑negative tumor development.
key_findings: Adolescent high PHDI reduced overall invasive breast cancer risk by >30% for ER-negative tumors. | Association remained significant after adjustment for weight change since age 18. | Effect was strongest in premenopausal women and those with BMI <21 kg/m2 at age 18. | Whole fruits were protective; added saturated and trans fats increased risk.
primary_metric: hazard ratio for ER-negative breast cancer

talk_title: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity and lung cancer risk in the UK Biobank
affiliation: Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital
moa_summary: Co-occurrence of chronic respiratory disease and cardiovascular disease markedly increases lung cancer incidence, with a hazard ratio of 4.11 versus healthy individuals, and healthy lifestyle factors (non‑smoking, diet) attenuate progression risk by up to 57%.
key_findings: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity (CRM) confers the highest lung cancer risk (HR 4.11) compared with single disease states. | 22% of CRD patients progressed to CRM versus 4.9% of CVD patients. | CRM shows the shortest transition time to lung cancer among disease states. | Healthy lifestyle reduces progression from single disease to CRM by 34‑54% and from CRM to lung cancer by 57%.
primary_metric: hazard ratio for lung cancer (cardiorespiratory multimorbidity)
primary_population: participants with both CRD and CVD

talk_title: Generational immigration status and gastric cancer incidence among Japanese descendants
moa_summary: Risk of gastric cancer remains elevated across generations of Japanese immigrants in the US, decreasing from a five‑fold increase in first‑generation to a 2.5‑fold increase in third‑generation, indicating persistent ancestry‑related susceptibility beyond environmental assimilation.
key_findings: First‑generation Japanese immigrants have ~5× higher gastric cancer risk than NHW. | Risk declines across generations but remains ~2.5× higher in third generation. | Pattern is strongest for non‑cardia gastric cancer. | No effect modification by family history, education, BMI, smoking, or sodium intake.
primary_metric: hazard ratio for gastric cancer (first generation vs NHW)

talk_title: PRIME: Parsimonious EHR risk model for pancreatic cancer
affiliation: NYU Grossman School of Medicine, Division of Precision Medicine
moa_summary: An interpretable elastic‑net derived EHR model (PRIME) using 19 routine clinical variables predicts pancreatic cancer risk with AUC ≈0.75, identifying a high‑risk 1% subgroup with a 0.61% three‑year absolute risk, comparable to germline mutation carriers.
key_findings: PRIME achieved AUC 0.76 (24 mo) and 0.75 (36 mo) in US validation health systems. | Top 1% risk group has 0.61% 3‑year absolute PDAC risk (1 in 128) and captures 5.8% of incident cases. | Model performance modestly declines in UK Biobank (AUC 0.71) but remains well‑calibrated. | High‑risk individuals have similar prevalence of known PDAC pathogenic germline variants as the general population, indicating capture of sporadic risk.

talk_title: Basal metabolic rate and survival in colorectal cancer (COLO-CARE)
affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Higher predicted basal metabolic rate, reflecting greater physiological reserve, is associated with improved overall and colorectal‑cancer‑specific survival, especially pronounced in younger male patients.
key_findings: Patients in the highest BMR quartile had 23% lower risk of overall death (HR 0.77). | Highest BMR quartile showed 36% lower risk of CRC‑specific death (HR 0.64). | Benefit was strongest in men under 50, with ~70% better survival. | BMR correlated positively with BMI and was higher in men than women.
primary_metric: hazard ratio for overall survival (highest BMR quartile vs lowest)

talk_title: Telehealth utilization disparities among older cancer patients
moa_summary: Older cancer patients exhibit marked disparities in telehealth adoption, with lower uptake among Black, low‑income, and non‑English‑speaking groups, and provider/system factors (e.g., academic setting, female providers) influencing delivery.
key_findings: 14.1% of older cancer patients used telehealth at least once in the first year post‑diagnosis. | Utilization peaked in 2020 (21.2%) then declined to 16.2% in 2021. | Black patients had the lowest utilization (11.2%); Asian/Pacific Islanders highest (17.9%). | Provider type (APPs, female clinicians) and academic facilities increased telehealth delivery.
primary_metric: overall telehealth utilization within first year of diagnosis

talk_title: CONFLUENCE: Multi‑ancestry GWAS of breast cancer
moa_summary: A massive multi‑ancestry GWAS (CONFLUENCE) uncovered 545 independent breast cancer risk loci, including 273 novel signals, and a 544‑variant PRS derived from these loci outperforms the standard 313‑variant score across European, African, and admixed American populations.
key_findings: CONFLUENCE includes >2 M participants, tripling effective sample size of prior multi‑ancestry studies. | Identified 545 independent loci, of which 273 are novel. | A 544‑variant PRS improves AUC by 2‑3% across EUR, AFR, and AMR cohorts versus the 313‑variant PRS. | Resource will be publicly released with summary statistics and data access platform.
primary_metric: number of independent risk loci identified

talk_title: Plasma proteomic signature of persistent smoking effects and cancer risk
tumor_types: lung cancer; upper aerodigestive tract cancer
moa_summary: The PSPSE proteomic signature captures persistent protein alterations after smoking cessation, reflecting cumulative exposure and biological memory, and its elevated levels predict higher incidence of smoking-related cancers, particularly lung cancer, independent of self-reported smoking metrics.
key_findings: Identified 18 proteins forming a PSPSE score that distinguishes current, former, and never smokers. | Higher PSPSE score significantly associated with increased lung cancer risk (HR ~1.5) in UK Biobank former smokers. | PSPSE score improves PLCOm2012 lung cancer risk prediction, especially in quitters >20 years. | Signature validated in an independent Asian cohort (Shanghai Men's Health Study) with consistent risk association.

talk_title: Adult height genetics and fatal prostate cancer risk
affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
moa_summary: Genetically determined adult height, captured by a polygenic risk score, drives the observed association with fatal prostate cancer, likely via growth‑related pathways such as androgen signaling, rather than early‑life nutrition.
key_findings: Height PRS strongly associated with fatal prostate cancer (HR 1.30 per SD). | Residual height after accounting for PRS shows no association, indicating genetics drive the risk. | Androgen response pathway upregulated in tumors of taller men, linking height to prostate cancer biology.
primary_metric: HR for fatal prostate cancer per SD increase in height PRS

talk_title: Copy number alteration subtypes in high‑grade serous ovarian cancer and racial survival disparities
moa_summary: Unsupervised clustering of genome‑wide copy‑number profiles from methylation arrays defines three HSOC subtypes (loss‑dominant, CNA‑quiet, gain‑dominant); CNA‑quiet tumors show higher immune infiltration and better survival, while Black patients in the gain‑dominant subtype experience higher mortality, suggesting ancestry‑linked biological contributors to disparity.
key_findings: Identified three CNA‑based clusters: loss‑dominant (44%), CNA‑quiet (40%), gain‑dominant (16%). | CNA‑quiet tumors have significantly higher immune cell infiltration and improved overall survival. | Within the gain‑dominant cluster, Black women have a 60% higher hazard of death compared to White women.
primary_metric: Hazard ratio for death (C1 loss‑dominant vs C2 CNA‑quiet)

talk_title: Spatial context of T‑cell infiltration and exhaustion predicts ovarian cancer survival in Black women
moa_summary: Multiplex immunofluorescence of whole‑section ovarian tumors reveals that cytotoxic CD8+ T cells confer survival benefit when enriched in the tumor compartment versus stroma, and tumor‑localized TIM‑3+ exhausted T cells paradoxically mark better outcomes, indicating spatially resolved immune functionality drives prognosis in Black women.
key_findings: Tumor‑dominant cytotoxic T‑cell enrichment predicts improved survival (HR 0.68). | Stroma‑dominant cytotoxic enrichment predicts worse survival. | Tumor TIM‑3+ exhaustion associated with better survival among tumors with ≥2% T cells.
primary_metric: HR for death per unit increase in tumor‑dominant cytotoxic gradient

talk_title: Pathways enriched in primary breast tumors with disseminated tumor cells
moa_summary: In early‑stage hormone‑receptor‑positive breast cancer, presence of disseminated tumor cells correlates with somatic mutations in Wnt, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and NRF2 pathways; Wnt mutations drive EMT and a stem‑like migratory phenotype, while PI3K alterations promote proliferation and resistance, together facilitating dormancy and later metastasis.
key_findings: DTC‑positive tumors enriched for mutations in Wnt, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and NRF2 pathways. | Wnt‑mutated DTC‑positive tumors show increased EMT and decreased proliferation signatures. | PI3K‑mutated DTC‑positive tumors display upregulated proliferative pathways and reduced apoptosis. | APOBEC mutagenesis activity nearly doubled in DTC‑positive patients.

talk_title: Colorectal cancer polygenic risk across birth cohorts
affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health
moa_summary: A colorectal cancer polygenic risk score shows progressively stronger association with disease risk in later birth cohorts, suggesting gene‑environment interactions where modern lifestyle exposures amplify genetic susceptibility, particularly for distal colon and rectal cancers.
key_findings: PRS distribution unchanged across birth cohorts, but effect size increases in recent cohorts. | Stronger PRS‑CRC association observed in individuals <55 years, unscreened, without family history, and for distal colon/rectal cancers. | Innate immune system pathways enriched among SNPs showing birth‑cohort interaction.
primary_metric: OR per SD increase in PRS

talk_title: Functional characterization of STARD13 as a BRCA2 modifier in breast cancer
moa_summary: A non‑coding variant rs3742324 within an enhancer region regulates STARD13 expression; loss of STARD13 in BRCA2‑mutant breast epithelial cells amplifies EMT‑related genes (e.g., MMP9) and proliferative pathways, suggesting STARD13 acts as a genetic modifier of BRCA2‑associated breast cancer risk.
key_findings: rs3742324 region shows allele‑specific enhancer activity and regulates STARD13 expression. | CRISPR deletion of the enhancer reduces STARD13 transcript levels. | STARD13 knockdown leads to greater downregulation of LATAS2 in BRCA2‑mutant cells. | RNA‑seq reveals upregulation of EMT and extracellular matrix genes (e.g., MMP9) when STARD13 is reduced in BRCA2‑mutant background.

session_title: Advances in Hemologic Malignancies: Population Sciences for Hematological Malignancies
talk_title: Building infrastructure for population genomics research at Roswell Park
moa_summary: Ken Onel described the creation of a universal consent program, a large biorepository of serial blood and bone marrow samples, and an integrated clinical-genomic data commons (Insight) to enable population‑scale genetic studies in hematologic malignancies, linking germline and tumor sequencing with detailed exposure and demographic data.
key_findings: Implemented universal consent with >80% accrual across >50,000 patients. | Biorepository contains 1.3 million biospecimens, including 220,000 bone marrow/blood samples from heme malignancy patients. | Insight tool identified 523 AML patients with germline testing versus 1,195 without, revealing ancestry‑linked testing disparities.

session_title: Advances in Hemologic Malignancies: Population Sciences for Hematological Malignancies
talk_title: Genetic ancestry and outcome disparities in acute myeloid leukemia
moa_summary: Eisfeldt presented data showing that genetic ancestry profoundly modifies AML biology: African ancestry patients exhibit distinct mutation frequencies (e.g., lower NPM1, higher PHIP, novel Rho GTPase mutations) and altered epigenetic landscapes, leading to poorer survival even when harboring traditionally favorable lesions such as NPM1.
key_findings: Black AML patients have ~12‑year shorter median OS compared with White patients, especially pronounced in <30 y age group. | NPM1 mutation, usually favorable, predicts adverse outcome in African‑American patients (near‑universal relapse). | PHIP mutations occur in ~7% of African‑American AML and confer aggressive disease in mouse models. | Rho GTPase family mutations are present in 12% of African‑American/A Hispanic AML, a novel finding. | Clonal hematopoiesis is present in 15% of African‑American AML and associates with 80% relapse rate.

talk_title: Overview of Pediatric Extrapolation in Oncology Drug Development
affiliation: FDA, Clinical Pharmacology, Solid Tumor Division
moa_summary: Pediatric extrapolation leverages similarity in disease biology, drug pharmacology, and response between adult (or older pediatric) reference populations and younger pediatric patients to support safe and efficient drug development without repeating full efficacy trials.
key_findings: More than 50% of new pediatric oncology indications (2013‑2024) used pediatric extrapolation. | ICH E11A (2024) formalizes guidance, including allowance for safety extrapolation when supported by data. | Three‑step framework: concept development, plan creation, and plan execution with iterative updates. | Early FDA engagement (early advice meetings, pediatric study plans) is encouraged to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Model‑Informed Drug Development (MIDD) to Support Pediatric Extrapolation
affiliation: FDA, Deputy Division Director of Pharmacometrics/Quantitative Clinical Pharmacology
moa_summary: MIDD integrates PK/PD, PBPK, population PK, and QSP models to bridge adult and pediatric data, enabling exposure matching, dose optimization, and trial design for pediatric oncology drugs.
key_findings: MIDD methods (population PK, PBPK, QSP) can predict pediatric exposure and support dose selection without extensive pediatric trials. | Examples: gadolinium‑based contrast agents, RILACE, itolizumab, rituximab—all received pediatric indications down to neonates via MIDD. | Renal maturation modeling shows GFR normalizes after ~2 years, informing GBCA dosing in infants. | FDA recommends early, iterative MIDD discussions (type D, MIDD‑focused meetings) to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Lutathera in Adolescents with SSTR‑Positive GEP‑NETs
affiliation: FDA, Deputy Director, Division of Oncology 2
moa_summary: Lutathera delivers beta‑emitting 177Lu to SSTR‑expressing neuroendocrine tumor cells, causing DNA damage and tumor regression; adult NETTER‑1 showed a 79% reduction in progression risk (HR 0.21) and 13% ORR, supporting extrapolation to adolescents with similar SSTR expression.
key_findings: Adult NETTER‑1 trial demonstrated PFS benefit (HR 0.21) and ORR 13% for Lutathera in SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs. | NETTER‑P enrolled 9 adolescents; safety profile matched adults (lymphopenia, neutropenia). | Organ dosimetry (kidney, bone marrow) in adolescents was comparable to adults, though predicted high‑dose kidney probability exceeded 20% at the 95th percentile. | FDA concluded extrapolation appropriate despite limited pediatric efficacy data, issuing PMRs for long‑term renal and hematologic safety. | Lutathera received FDA approval for adolescents with SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs in 2024, marking a successful pediatric extrapolation case.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Blinatumomab in B‑ALL
affiliation: FDA, Clinical Reviewer, Division of Hemomalignancies 1
moa_summary: Blinatumomab is a CD19‑directed CD3 bispecific T‑cell engager that forms an immunologic synapse, activating T‑cells to lyse CD19‑positive B‑ALL blasts; PK exposure matching between adults and children supports similar efficacy and safety extrapolation.
key_findings: Adult BLAST study showed 81% MRD‑negative conversion, providing a robust efficacy signal for extrapolation. | Pediatric dose‑finding study (MT103205) demonstrated tolerability and similar cytokine release syndrome rates to adults. | Meta‑analysis of three randomized trials (adult E1910, pediatric 201/20215, AALL 1331) plus BLIM‑FANT showed consistent survival benefit across ages, supporting extrapolation to pediatric consolidation therapy. | FDA granted age‑agnostic indications for blinatumomab in MRD‑positive B‑ALL and in consolidation, relying on adult efficacy and pediatric safety/PK data. | Post‑marketing requirements include long‑term safety follow‑up for neurotoxicity and cytokine release syndrome.

talk_title: African Cancer Atlas (TACA) project and early genomic findings
tumor_types: breast cancer; colorectal cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: The TACA initiative generates paired whole-genome sequencing of African cancer patients to uncover ancestry-specific mutational signatures, driver alterations such as TP53 enrichment, and novel candidate drivers like LEPR and KRTAP10-6, aiming to improve cancer biology understanding and drug discovery for African and global populations.
key_findings: Phase 1 of TACA funded to generate 3,000 genomes from 1,500 African cancer patients across 7 countries. | Median sequencing coverage achieved 41X for germline and 121X for somatic samples, comparable to reference datasets. | Tumor mutational burden in African breast cancer cohort comparable to TCGA, suggesting eligibility for checkpoint blockade. | Enrichment of TP53 mutations and novel driver candidates (e.g., LEPR in TNBC, KRTAP10-6 in luminal) relative to European datasets. | Geographic subtype distribution observed: higher triple‑negative breast cancer prevalence in West Africa versus luminal B in East Africa.

talk_title: Proteomics studies in African cancer cohorts and the African ClinMap multi‑omic platform
affiliation: Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), South Africa
tumor_types: breast cancer; pancreatic cancer; prostate cancer; gallbladder cancer
moa_summary: Proteomic profiling of African tumor specimens reveals ECM remodeling, altered lipid metabolism, and distinct protein signatures (e.g., decreased CD36 in breast cancer, high LGR1 in pancreatic cancer) that differentiate tumor from normal tissue and suggest ancestry‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Breast cancer proteomics (n=28) identified >2,000 proteins with a five‑fold decrease in CD36 compared to US cohorts. | Machine‑learning classifier achieved AUC 0.96 distinguishing tumor vs normal tissue using proteomic data. | Pancreatic cancer plasma proteomics showed ECM remodeling and inflammatory mediators; LGR1 achieved AUC 0.94 for cancer vs benign. | Prostate cancer tissue proteomics (n>150) uncovered a five‑protein panel linked to neurogenous metastasis. | Gallbladder cancer proteomics identified nine key proteins associated with ECM and smooth muscle contraction loss.

talk_title: Ancestry‑driven genomic and transcriptomic landscape of aggressive endometrial cancer
affiliation: Northwell Health / New York Genome Center, New York, USA
topic_categories: genomics; transcriptomics; tumor_microenvironment; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; other
moa_summary: Whole‑genome and transcriptomic profiling of African‑ancestry endometrial cancers reveals ancestry‑specific driver alterations (e.g., enriched MECOM short‑isoform amplifications) and reduced CD8 T‑cell infiltration, indicating distinct molecular and immune landscapes that may affect therapeutic response.
