key_findings: African ancestry patients showed lower CD8 T‑cell infiltration after adjusting for tumor purity and subtype. | MECOM short‑isoform focal amplifications were present in ~35% of African ancestry tumors versus ~10% in European ancestry, linked to poorer progression‑free survival in TCGA. | FBXW7 mutations enriched in African ancestry copy‑number‑high endometrial cancers. | Overall fewer clinically actionable mutations (OncoKB) in African ancestry tumors compared with European ancestry. | Ancestry‑specific expression of PLXNA3 and other genes correlated with admixture proportion.

talk_title: Biomarker‑guided precision prevention and chemoprevention of HCC
affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Director of Liver Tumor Translational Research
moa_summary: Hosoda demonstrated that a liver‑tissue risk signature (PLS) can be modulated by targeting EGFR signaling in myofibroblasts with low‑dose erlotinib, leading to dose‑dependent suppression of phospho‑EGFR, reduction of early HCC nodules, and improvement of the risk signature; similar modulation is observed with lipophilic statins, bariatric surgery, and GLP‑1 agonists, supporting the signature as a surrogate endpoint for chemoprevention trials.
key_findings: Risk‑stratified HCC screening using the PLS risk signature improves cost‑effectiveness and predicts incident HCC in >2,000 US patients and a 1,300‑patient NIH cohort. | Low‑dose erlotinib (25‑75 mg) suppresses phospho‑EGFR in cirrhotic liver tissue, with risk‑signature improvement at ≥50 mg and no grade ≥3 toxicity. | Erlotinib reduces early HCC nodules in a rat fibrosis model in a dose‑dependent manner, but has minimal effect on larger established tumors due to poor tumor cell uptake. | Lipophilic statins (atorvastatin) and bariatric surgery improve the PLS risk signature, correlating with lower long‑term HCC incidence. | The EGFR‑driven myofibroblast signature can serve as a surrogate endpoint for future chemoprevention trials (ECHO‑B, CATCH‑B, TOSS).
primary_population: cirrhotic patients undergoing elective liver surgery

talk_title: Personalized care in advanced HCC: gamma‑delta T cells, liver decompensation AI model, and genomics‑driven targeted therapy
affiliation: La Pitié‑Salpêtrière Hospital, Paris, France; Inserm Centre de Recherche des Cordeliers
topic_categories: immunotherapy; biomarker_development; AI_ML; clinical_trial; targeted_therapy; translational
moa_summary: Allaire identified a blood‑based gamma‑delta T cell signature that predicts response to atezolizumab‑bevacizumab in HCC, demonstrated that CT‑derived AI modeling (HepatoSAJ) can stratify patients at high risk of portal‑hypertension‑driven decompensation, and showed that genomics‑driven molecular tumor boards enable targeted therapy for ~65% of liver cancer patients, yielding disease control in one‑third of treated cases.
key_findings: High baseline gamma‑delta T cell levels in blood correlate with disease control and longer PFS/OS in atezo‑bev‑treated HCC. | Interferon‑γ is elevated at baseline in responders, supporting a Th1‑type immune milieu. | The HepatoSAJ CT‑AI model predicts liver decompensation risk (AUC ≈ 0.84) better than conventional clinical scores. | Portal hypertension identified by AI is a stronger predictor of mortality than tumor‑related factors in real‑world HCC cohorts. | Genomic profiling revealed targetable alterations in 65% of liver cancer patients; genotype‑matched therapy achieved disease control in 32% with a median PFS of 12 months.
primary_value: significantly higher in high gamma‑delta T cell group

talk_title: Therapy-associated clonal hematopoiesis and risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors
affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics
moa_summary: Therapy (chemo/radiation) promotes expansion of clonal hematopoiesis, especially DNA‑damage‑response CHIP mutations and copy‑neutral loss‑of‑heterozygosity MCAs, which in turn increase the risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors.
key_findings: CHIP prevalence rises from 4.5% in cancer‑free individuals to 7.5% in treated cancer survivors (≈50% relative increase). | MCA prevalence rises from 3% to 4% in treated survivors (≈19% relative increase). | Therapy‑associated MCAs are distinct from those linked to secondary hematologic malignancy risk. | Presence of any autosomal MCA confers a hazard ratio of ~4.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy. | CHIP confers a hazard ratio of ~2.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy, with TP53 and PPM1D mutations linking therapy and malignancy risk.
primary_population: cancer survivors with chemotherapy or radiation

talk_title: Somatic APC mosaicism and colibactin‑producing bacteria in unexplained colorectal adenomatous polyposis
moa_summary: Somatic APC mosaicism arising early in embryogenesis drives polyposis in one‑third of unexplained cases, while colibactin‑producing E. coli induces a mutational signature that co‑occurs with APC mutations, together contributing to adenoma burden and earlier disease onset.
key_findings: APC mosaicism was identified in ~33% of tested unexplained polyposis patients. | Mosaic carriers presented at a younger median age (45 y) and had higher adenoma counts than non‑mosaic patients. | Colibactin‑associated mutational signatures were present in 40% of patients and often co‑occurred with APC mutations within the same lesion. | Blood detection of low‑frequency mosaic APC variants was possible in two cases, confirming systemic mosaicism. | Both mosaic APC and colibactin exposure represent distinct etiologic pathways for unexplained adenomatous polyposis.

talk_title: Intrinsic and extrinsic determinants of racial disparities in breast cancer biology
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; immunotherapy; genomics; bioinformatics; health_disparities; basic_science; translational
moa_summary: Black women’s breast tumors exhibit higher collagen deposition and a CD4‑dominant, immunosuppressive T‑cell milieu; poverty amplifies the CD4 signature, and collagen‑rich environments enhance invasive potential of Black‑derived tumor cells, linking intrinsic ancestry‑related biology with extrinsic socioeconomic stressors.
key_findings: Tumors from Black women show significantly increased collagen content compared with White women. | CD4 T‑cell infiltration is enriched and CD8 depleted in Black women’s tumors; poverty burden further raises CD4 dominance. | Black‑derived HR‑positive cell lines require collagen‑containing matrix for 3D growth, unlike White‑derived lines. | HCC1500 xenografts (Black origin) form palpable tumors with radial collagen alignment; MDA‑175 (White) do not. | In silico invasion modeling predicts collagen density boosts invasion only for Black‑derived cells.

talk_title: Microdysplastic cytomorphologic signatures in normal breast tissue predict future cancer risk
moa_summary: Quantitative single‑cell analysis of H&E slides identifies epithelial nuclear enlargement and stromal inflammation signatures that reflect early oncogenic and immune alterations, conferring a two‑fold or greater future breast cancer risk independent of age and histologic atypia.
key_findings: Four microdysplastic signatures (absent, epithelial, stromal, mixed) were defined based on nuclear and stromal metrics. | Presence of any signature conferred >2‑fold increased breast cancer risk after multivariable adjustment. | Stromal signature strongly predicted ER‑negative cancers, especially in stroma‑rich tissue. | Signatures were predictive across age groups (<50 and ≥50) and in benign disease cohorts. | RNA‑seq of signature‑positive tissues showed immune and wound‑repair pathways activation.
primary_metric: Overall cancer risk with any microdysplastic signature

talk_title: Deep plasma proteomics for early‑stage breast cancer detection in dense breasts
topic_categories: early_detection; liquid_biopsy; proteomics; clinical_trial; AI_ML; diagnostic
moa_summary: A mass‑spectrometry‑based deep proteomic platform quantifies ~7,000 plasma proteins; a machine‑learning classifier distinguishes early‑stage breast cancer, achieving high sensitivity especially in women with dense breast tissue where conventional imaging is limited.
key_findings: Deep proteomics identified ~7,000 proteins per sample in both cancer and healthy cohorts. | Classifier achieved AUC 0.96, 86.2% sensitivity and 90.4% specificity overall. | In women with high breast density, sensitivity rose to 90.2% at fixed 90.4% specificity. | Stage‑0, I, II cancers detected with sensitivities of 84.6%, 88%, and 97% respectively. | Prospective pilot (n≈300) showed 89% sensitivity and 93% NPV, supporting clinical utility.

talk_title: Multi‑analyte liquid biopsy for early detection of pancreatic cancer in high‑risk individuals
topic_categories: early_detection; liquid_biopsy; epigenetics; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; AI_ML
moa_summary: A multi‑analyte liquid biopsy combines 5‑hmC epigenomic profiling, cfDNA fragment‑size analysis, and genotype‑adjusted CA19‑9 to generate a machine‑learning classifier that discriminates pancreatic cancer from non‑cancer high‑risk individuals with >80% sensitivity and >97% specificity.
key_findings: Training set achieved 80.5% overall sensitivity and 97.5% specificity for pancreatic cancer detection. | Independent validation (n=1,445) confirmed 82.6% sensitivity and 97.5% specificity. | Performance remained consistent across three additional high‑risk validation cohorts. | Specificity loss across 11 non‑pancreatic cancers was negligible (<0.05%). | Pathway analysis of selected features recapitulated cancer hallmarks, confirming biological relevance.

talk_title: Risk-based breast cancer screening: evidence, models, and implementation challenges
affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; other
moa_summary: Colditz presented a framework for risk-based breast cancer screening that stratifies women by absolute five‑year risk using calibrated models (e.g., Gail, Tyrer‑Cuzick) and assigns screening frequency accordingly, aiming to increase mortality reduction while decreasing false‑positive workups.
key_findings: Risk‑based screening could improve breast cancer deaths averted while reducing false‑positive workups by up to 25% (CISNET analysis). | No consensus exists on a universal five‑year risk cut‑point for initiating annual screening; proposed cut points: >3% for annual + supplemental imaging, 1.67–3% for annual, <1.67% for biennial. | Implementation challenges include lack of consistent risk thresholds across trials, guideline variability, and need for model‑agnostic frameworks.

talk_title: Development and validation of blood‑based biomarkers to guide mammography interval
moa_summary: Lee described a PROBE‑compliant pipeline that used pre‑clinical plasma from WHI and CHS cohorts to discover protein and auto‑antibody markers (e.g., REG4, JAK2) associated with future hormone‑receptor‑positive breast cancer, then combined them into a four‑marker panel achieving 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity to guide individualized mammography recall intervals.
key_findings: From 3,000 screened proteins and 3,000 auto‑antibodies, 6 protein and 5 auto‑antibody markers met pre‑specified statistical criteria. | A four‑marker panel (including REG4 and JAK2) achieved 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity, exceeding the minimal performance needed to inform six‑month recall. | Validated markers in an independent CHS cohort (p<0.05 for 6 proteins, 5 auto‑antibodies). | Developed clinical‑grade MSD assays for top markers and plan prospective validation in the MD Anderson MERIT cohort (n≈250 cancers).

talk_title: Precision screening and surveillance strategies for colorectal cancer
affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
topic_categories: screening; risk_assessment; clinical_trial; AI_ML; epigenetics; microbiome; other
moa_summary: Song presented epidemiologic and genetic risk models that estimate individual 10‑year colorectal cancer risk, showing that high‑risk individuals could start colonoscopy up to six years earlier and low‑risk individuals could delay up to six years; post‑colonoscopy risk varies by polyps, colonoscopy quality (ADR), and polygenic risk scores, supporting personalized surveillance intervals.
key_findings: High‑risk individuals (top risk score) reach the 10‑year CRC risk benchmark at age 44, suggesting screening 6 years earlier than standard age 50. | Low‑risk individuals (bottom risk score) reach the benchmark at age 56, indicating possible delay of screening by 6 years. | Negative colonoscopy confers sustained risk reduction for up to 20 years, but risk varies widely by underlying risk score, allowing interval extension up to 25 years for very low‑risk patients. | Only 64% of patients with high‑risk polyps receive recommended surveillance, and only 21% adhere to the guideline‑specified interval. | Polygenic risk scores add modest predictive value (AUC increase ~0.02) for post‑colonoscopy CRC and polyp recurrence. | Physician adenoma detection rate strongly predicts post‑colonoscopy CRC risk, with a three‑fold difference between lowest and highest ADR quartiles.
primary_metric: age to reach benchmark 10‑year CRC risk
primary_value: 44 years for highest‑risk, 56 years for lowest‑risk
primary_population: high‑risk vs low‑risk individuals in NHS/HPFS cohorts

talk_title: Mapping the Molecular Landscape of Head and Neck Cancer to Inform Pan‑Tumor Targeting
affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, University of Toronto, Senior Medical Oncologist, Director of the Phase 1 Clinical Trials Program
topic_categories: ctDNA; biomarker_development; early_detection; immunotherapy; clinical_trial; translational; other
moa_summary: Dr. Siu demonstrated that ultrasensitive tumor‑informed ctDNA (RADAR) and HPV‑DNA assays can detect molecular residual disease months before radiographic recurrence in head‑and‑neck cancer. Randomization of MRD‑positive patients to a PD‑1‑targeting bispecific antibody (revagostumab) resulted in durable disease‑free survival, supporting the concept of early immunotherapy interception based on real‑time MRD status.
key_findings: Feasibility study (PREMERIDIAN) showed 100% blood sample capture in 17 patients and identified ctDNA lead times of 100–245 days before clinical recurrence. | In MERIDIAN, only 5/42 evaluable patients were ctDNA‑positive at early post‑treatment time points, and most of those already had macroscopic disease, highlighting the need for radiologic confirmation of MRD. | MRD‑negative patients (≈35) had excellent relapse‑free outcomes (30/35 alive without recurrence, median follow‑up >22 months). | The single patient randomized to revagostumab remained disease‑free for >22 months, whereas the observation patient progressed, providing proof‑of‑concept for MRD‑guided immunotherapy interception. | Longitudinal ctDNA sampling (multiple post‑treatment windows) increased detection sensitivity compared with single landmark testing. | Early ctDNA kinetics (day 8) after PD‑1 blockade in advanced disease correlated with later response, and decreasing ctDNA was associated with increased peripheral TCR diversity.

talk_title: Risk for Pediatric Cancer in Newborn Screening
moa_summary: Newborn screening using targeted NGS panels identifies pathogenic variants in cancer predisposition genes (e.g., RB1, TP53) from dried blood spots, enabling early surveillance and treatment to reduce mortality and treatment-related toxicity in high‑risk children.
key_findings: Modeling predicts newborn cancer‑risk screening could reduce deaths before age 20 by 8% and by up to 54% in carriers of predisposition syndromes. | Retrospective analysis of 2,000 Michigan newborn spots found 6.8% carried pathogenic variants, with 5/5 RET carriers developing medullary thyroid cancer and RB1 carriers developing retinoblastoma or pinealoblastoma. | Estimated incidence of cancer among newborns with a positive screen is ~1 in 30,000, comparable to other rare screened conditions. | Prospective NCARE trial will randomize 10,000 mothers to focused vs. broad panels to assess feasibility, acceptability, and downstream clinical impact.

talk_title: Therapeutic Precision Cancer Prevention: The Power of Canaries
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; upper GI cancer; hepatobiliary cancer; duodenal cancer; bladder cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Low‑dose aspirin reduces cancer incidence in Lynch syndrome by inhibiting platelet‑derived thromboxane A2, thereby enhancing anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; dose‑finding trials (CAP3) show the smallest dose (100 mg) yields the fewest cancers with minimal toxicity.
key_findings: CAP3 trial (1,547 participants) demonstrated the lowest cancer incidence in the 100 mg aspirin arm, with 30% of participants experiencing the fewest cancers after 8 years. | Serious adverse events were absent in the 100 mg group, indicating a favorable safety profile compared with higher doses. | Combined analysis of CAP2 and CAP3 yields HR 0.67 versus historic placebo, confirming a protective effect of aspirin across Lynch‑syndrome cancer sites. | Guideline update in the UK will recommend ~81 mg aspirin starting five years before the first colonoscopy for Lynch carriers.
primary_metric: hazard ratio for all Lynch‑syndrome cancers (CAP3 vs historic placebo)

talk_title: Vaccines for Cancer Prevention and Intervention
tumor_types: breast cancer; colorectal cancer; endometrial cancer; Lynch syndrome-associated cancers; obesity‑related breast cancer
moa_summary: Vaccines deliver tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., HER2, MUC1, frameshift neoantigens) via dendritic cell loading or viral vectors to elicit robust type‑1 CD4⁺/CD8⁺ T‑cell and antibody responses that can eradicate pre‑invasive lesions (interception) or provide prophylactic immunity in high‑risk individuals such as Lynch syndrome carriers.
key_findings: HER2/neu dendritic‑cell vaccination induced type‑1 T‑cell and antibody responses and led to loss of HER2 expression in 7/11 evaluable DCIS patients. | Pathologic complete responses were observed in ~30% of HER2‑positive, ER‑negative DCIS patients receiving the vaccine. | NAUS209 (viral‑vector frameshift vaccine) was safe (8% grade 3 AEs) and immunogenic, with 100% of 45 Lynch syndrome participants generating T‑cell responses, persisting in 85% at 12 months. | Adipocyte‑directed Advac vaccine reduced visceral CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, restored regulatory T‑cells, improved insulin sensitivity, and prevented tumor development in 60% of obese breast‑cancer‑prone mice.

talk_title: AI in Cancer Diagnosis: Promise, Performance, and the Human‑AI Factor
moa_summary: AI models process imaging (mammography, pathology slides) to generate diagnostic predictions; variability studies reveal human inter‑observer inconsistency, while AI can improve reproducibility but may introduce automation bias, highlighting the need for rigorous evaluation and human‑in‑the‑loop designs.
key_findings: In a large mammography cohort, AI assistance was associated with a statistically significant increase in recall rate, suggesting potential over‑diagnosis. | Only 10% of 36 pathologists agreed with expert consensus on a challenging skin biopsy, illustrating high diagnostic variability. | Repeated reads by the same pathologists after six months showed poor intra‑observer reproducibility, underscoring the need for decision support. | Human‑AI interaction studies (PRISM) are enrolling 400,000 exams to assess real‑world impact of AI cue timing and presentation.
primary_metric: recall rate with AI in mammography (PRISM)

talk_title: STING agonists amplify BH3 mimetic activity via p53- and cGAS-STING-dependent apoptosis loops
moa_summary: STING agonists activate cGAS-STING signaling leading to IRF3-dependent transcription of BH3-only proteins (BIM, NOXA, PUMA) and p53 activation, creating a feed‑forward loop that amplifies apoptosis; combined with BH3 mimetics (venetoclax or MCL‑1 inhibitors) this overcomes resistance by releasing BIM from anti‑apoptotic proteins, resulting in enhanced tumor cell killing in vitro and in mouse models.
key_findings: STING agonists induce p53‑independent IRF3 activation and upregulation of BIM, NOXA, and PUMA in lymphoma and leukemia cells. | Loss of Bax/Bak abolishes STING‑agonist‑mediated apoptosis, confirming dependence on the intrinsic pathway. | Combination of STING agonist with BH3 mimetics (MCL‑1 inhibitor or venetoclax) yields synergistic killing in vitro and improves survival in immune‑deficient and immune‑competent mouse models. | High STING expression in AML patient samples predicts sensitivity to STING agonist monotherapy and to the combination with venetoclax. | In vivo, dual treatment leads to ~50% survival benefit in Rag‑KO mice and extends median survival three‑fold in Cas9‑transgenic mice.
primary_value: 50% of mice survived with dual treatment

talk_title: Mitochondrial priming, BCL2 family dependencies and resistance mechanisms in multiple myeloma
topic_categories: apoptosis; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: Myeloma cells exhibit high mitochondrial priming driven by BIM up‑regulation; venetoclax releases BIM from BCL2 but BIM can be re‑sequestered by MCL1, especially after IL6‑mediated signaling or BIM phosphorylation, leading to resistance. Combination with dexamethasone or IL6 blockade restores priming and sensitizes cells, while t(11;14) myeloma shows intrinsic BCL2 dependence and higher venetoclax response.
key_findings: BIM is up‑regulated in myeloma and drives mitochondrial priming, making cells vulnerable to BH3 mimetics. | Venetoclax treatment releases BIM from BCL2, which then binds MCL1, causing resistance in BCL2‑dependent lines. | Dexamethasone increases total BIM and restores venetoclax sensitivity across resistant myeloma samples. | Stromal IL6 shifts BIM binding to MCL1, conferring resistance; IL6 neutralization or Tocilizumab pretreatment re‑sensitizes cells to venetoclax. | BIM phosphorylation at Ser94 (and Ser87) reduces sensitivity to MCL1 inhibition, indicating a post‑translational resistance mechanism. | t(11;14) myeloma shows higher venetoclax sensitivity linked to a B‑cell‑like differentiation state rather than BCL2 expression levels. | Acquired BCL2 point mutations (e.g., G101V) after therapy diminish venetoclax binding and drive clinical resistance.
primary_value: 40% in t(11;14) patients vs 6% in non‑t(11;14)
primary_population: multiple myeloma patients treated with venetoclax

talk_title: Domain Alteration Chimeras (DALTACs) reprogram AR-p300 enhancer complexes in prostate cancer
moa_summary: The DALTAC heterobifunctional molecule simultaneously binds the androgen receptor ligand‑binding domain and the p300 bromodomain, inducing a ternary complex that repositions p300 from its native chromatin sites to AR‑bound enhancers. This relocalization extinguishes N‑terminal H2B acetylation, suppresses AR‑target gene transcription, and creates neomorphic complexes that impair transcriptional elongation, leading to selective cytotoxicity in AR‑positive prostate cancer cells.
key_findings: DALTAC induced ternary complex formation between AR and p300 confirmed by proximity ligation assay and IP‑mass spec. | In AR‑positive 22RV1 cells, 10 nM DALTAC caused >90% reduction in crystal violet staining, whereas p300 inhibitor or AR inhibitor alone showed minimal effect. | Across a panel of prostate cancer cell lines, AR‑positive lines displayed nanomolar IC50s (median ~5 nM) while AR‑negative and normal cell lines had IC50s >10 µM, demonstrating >2000‑fold selectivity. | In castrated VCAP xenografts, oral DALTAC modestly inhibited tumor growth; intraperitoneal dosing achieved tumor regression in all treated mice with no regrowth after drug withdrawal. | Histone H2B N‑terminal acetylation, a pharmacodynamic biomarker, was abolished in tumors treated with DALTAC, correlating with loss of AR‑target gene expression. | Toxicology showed no significant weight loss, CBC, or chemistry abnormalities, indicating a favorable safety profile in mice.

talk_title: Phase I/II trial of heterobifunctional BRD4‑AR degrader (RIPTag) in heavily pre‑treated CRPC
moa_summary: The heterobifunctional RIPTag molecule simultaneously binds BRD4 bromodomain and the AR ligand‑binding domain, creating a non‑degradative ternary complex that locks AR and BRD4 together, disrupting AR transcriptional programs without classic AR antagonism, leading to PSA reductions and radiographic tumor regressions in heavily pre‑treated CRPC.
key_findings: No dose‑limiting toxicities observed; MTD not reached up to the highest tested dose (~1000 Da molecule). | Patients experienced rapid, dose‑dependent increases in Cmax and AUC with steady‑state achieved within a few days. | Significant PSA declines (≥50%) were seen in a subset of heavily pre‑treated CRPC patients, including those with AR splice variants. | Radiographic regressions were documented in liver metastases and primary prostate lesions, indicating drug penetration into sanctuary sites. | Common adverse events were mild nausea, anemia, and fatigue; overall tolerability was favorable.

talk_title: DLL3‑targeted T‑cell engager gocatumig in neuroendocrine prostate cancer
moa_summary: Gocatumig is a bispecific T‑cell engager that binds DLL3 on neuroendocrine prostate cancer cells and CD3 on T cells, with an albumin‑binding domain to improve half‑life. Engagement redirects cytotoxic T cells to DLL3‑expressing tumor cells, inducing tumor regression; a by‑stander effect may contribute to activity against DLL3‑negative cells within heterogeneous tumors.
key_findings: Phase I/II trial showed an overall response rate of ~50% and disease control rate of 72% in heavily pre‑treated NEPC patients. | Deep PSA reductions were observed: 50% of patients achieved ≥90% PSA decline, and 30% achieved ≥99% decline. | Response did not strongly correlate with baseline DLL3 IHC intensity, indicating possible by‑stander killing or heterogeneous antigen expression. | Acquired resistance was associated with loss of DLL3 protein in post‑treatment biopsies, suggesting antigen loss as a resistance mechanism. | Pre‑clinical PDX models demonstrated potent tumor regression and increased CD3+ T‑cell infiltration after gocatumig treatment.

talk_title: Immunological considerations and B7H3-targeted radioligand therapy mechanisms
topic_categories: immunotherapy; radiopharmaceuticals; combination_therapy; tumor_microenvironment; targeted_therapy; preclinical
moa_summary: BetaBART is a humanized anti‑B7H3 antibody with 20 pM affinity for the tumor‑specific 4‑Ig isoform; when labeled with Y‑90 it delivers beta radiation that induces DNA damage, immunogenic cell death and a CD8‑dependent in situ vaccination, leading to long‑term tumor control and immune memory in murine models.
key_findings: MIL‑33B (Zr‑89) specifically localizes to 4IG‑expressing tumors in vivo, with loss of signal in B7H3‑KO or excess cold antibody controls. | A single 100 µCi Y‑90‑MIL‑33B injection yields 56% long‑term survival in IR‑resistant CT26 tumors, whereas controls die. | CD8 T‑cell depletion abolishes the survival benefit, indicating CD8‑mediated adaptive immunity is essential. | Treated mice develop immune memory, rejecting rechallenge with the same tumor cells at >100 days post‑treatment. | Cold (non‑radiolabeled) MIL‑33B antibody at high dose shows no anti‑tumor effect, confirming radiation drives the response. | Small‑molecule NM600 requires checkpoint blockade to achieve long‑term survivors, highlighting modality‑specific immune priming.
primary_population: IR‑resistant CT26 tumors in immunocompetent mice

talk_title: Granzyme‑B targeted PET imaging to monitor immune activation during PSMA‑RLT plus pembrolizumab
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; molecular_glue; biomarker_development; clinical_trial; imaging
moa_summary: A Cu‑64‑labeled GRIP peptide is proteolytically activated by granzyme B released from cytotoxic T cells; the activated probe inserts into nearby membranes, retaining signal on PET and enabling non‑invasive visualization of immune‑mediated tumor killing during PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy combined with pembrolizumab.
key_findings: Granzyme‑B PET signal appears in bone lesions after sub‑ablative PSMA‑617 (Pluvicto) plus pembrolizumab, coinciding with PSA reductions. | Serial granzyme PET scans show increased SUV in existing lesions and emergence of new PET‑avid lesions, reflecting evolving immune activation. | Granzyme PET uptake is absent in control mice lacking granzyme B, confirming probe specificity. | Probe uptake correlates with peripheral blood granzyme B induction and with clinical response, suggesting a pharmacodynamic biomarker for RLT‑IO combinations. | No significant off‑target uptake observed in most patients; occasional uptake in sarcoma of unclear relevance.

talk_title: Immunostimulatory radioligand therapy to enhance checkpoint blockade and CAR‑T efficacy
tumor_types: melanoma; breast cancer; neuroblastoma; glioma; solid tumors; liquid tumors
topic_categories: immunotherapy; radiopharmaceuticals; combination_therapy; cell_therapy; tumor_microenvironment; preclinical
moa_summary: Low‑dose targeted radionuclide therapy (e.g., NM600 or Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A) delivers localized radiation that modulates the tumor microenvironment, increases pro‑inflammatory cytokines and CD8 infiltration, and can serve as a lymphodepleting conditioning regimen for checkpoint blockade or CAR‑T cells, while sparing systemic lymphocytes when using small‑molecule agents.
key_findings: NM600 at 50–100 µCi (2.5–5 Gy) combined with checkpoint blockade yields ~50% complete responses in B78 melanoma, whereas either monotherapy is ineffective. | In 4T1 metastatic breast cancer, low‑dose NM600 alone controls primary tumor but not micrometastases; combination with checkpoint blockade controls both primary and metastatic disease. | Whole‑body 2.5 Gy radiation abrogates the synergy, indicating systemic lymphocyte depletion impairs efficacy, whereas tumor‑restricted dosing preserves benefit. | Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A enhances lymphodepletion, improving engraftment and persistence of adoptively transferred T cells (Flucy conditioning) and boosting CAR‑T efficacy in B16‑PMEL and neuroblastoma models. | Copper‑67 conditioning reduces exhaustion markers (TIM3, LAG3, PD‑1) on CAR‑T cells and shifts tumor‑associated macrophages from M2 to a less suppressive phenotype.

talk_title: Clinical application of radiopharmaceutical theranostics in neuroendocrine tumors and prostate cancer
affiliation: University of Alabama, Department of Radiology
moa_summary: Radiolabeled somatostatin analogs (DOTATATE) and PSMA‑targeted ligands (PSMA‑617) bind cell‑surface receptors on neuroendocrine tumors or prostate cancer cells, are internalized, and deliver beta‑particle radiation (Lu‑177) that induces DNA double‑strand breaks, leading to tumor cell death while allowing PET imaging of target expression for patient selection and dosimetry.
key_findings: DOTATATE therapy (NETTER‑1) provided a marked progression‑free survival benefit compared with high‑dose octreotide in Grade 1‑2 GEP‑NETs. | PSMA‑617 therapy (VISION) improved overall survival by 4 months versus standard of care in mCRPC after ARPI and taxane. | Higher baseline PSMA PET SUVmean (>10) predicted greater PSA response and longer PFS in PSMA‑targeted trials. | Patients with high circulating tumor DNA or PTEN loss had poorer outcomes on PSMA therapy, suggesting genomic biomarkers of resistance. | Combination of PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy with PARP inhibitors or immune checkpoint blockade is feasible and shows early safety signals.
primary_value: significant improvement vs high‑dose somatostatin analog

talk_title: Overview of RAS inhibitor landscape, resistance mechanisms and combination strategies
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; targeted_therapy; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: KRAS G12C inhibitors achieve modest response rates (~40%) with limited duration (<10 months) due to primary and acquired resistance driven by MAPK pathway reactivation, secondary KRAS mutations, and non‑genetic transcriptional programs such as TEAD, MYC, and ERK activation.
key_findings: 73 RAS inhibitors have entered clinical evaluation with ~150 actively recruiting trials as of 2026. | Median ORR for G12C inhibitors across trials is ~40% with median DOR <10 months. | Acquired resistance mechanisms are split roughly 50% genomic (e.g., MAPK pathway reactivating mutations) and 50% non‑genomic (transcriptional reprogramming). | Pan‑RAS inhibitors (e.g., daraxasib) show activity without reactivation of RAS alleles but face toxicity challenges.

talk_title: BMAL2 as a KRAS‑linked hypoxia driver in pancreatic cancer
affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern / Ken Olive Lab
moa_summary: BMAL2 functions as a KRAS‑responsive transcription factor that links KRAS signaling to hypoxia responses; it promotes EMT, cell proliferation, and glycolytic metabolism via interaction with HIF‑1α/2α, and its loss delays tumor growth in orthotopic PDA models.
key_findings: Regulatory network analysis identified BMAL2 as the top KRAS‑associated master regulator across >200 PDA samples. | BMAL2 activity decreases after treatment with KRAS inhibitor RNC7977 in vitro and in KPC mouse tumors. | BMAL2 knockout reduces proliferation, EMT markers, and glycolysis; overexpression induces mesenchymal morphology and increases proliferation. | BMAL2 physically interacts with HIF‑1α under hypoxia, suggesting a complex that drives hypoxia‑responsive gene expression. | Orthotopic BMAL2‑KO tumors show prolonged mouse survival compared with controls.

talk_title: Rewiring RTK signaling under RAS inhibition in pancreatic cancer
topic_categories: combination_therapy; targeted_therapy; ADC; tumor_microenvironment; spatial_biology; basic_science
moa_summary: RAS inhibition induces dynamic up‑regulation of RTKs (EGFR, HER2, ERBB3) in pancreatic cancer, altering receptor internalization and creating windows for ADC delivery; stromal fibroblasts secrete neuregulin to activate ERBB3, and combined RTK blockade enhances tumor regression.
key_findings: RAS inhibition increases HER2 and ERBB3 protein levels in a subset of PDAC models, while EGFR remains stable. | Combining RAS inhibitors with HER2‑targeted ADC (T‑DXd) yields synergistic tumor regression in vitro and in PDX models. | Neuregulin‑1 secreted by cancer‑associated fibroblasts drives ERBB3 activation; anti‑neuregulin antibody plus RAS inhibitor improves survival in murine PDAC. | FRODO assay shows that RAS inhibition accelerates HER2 internalization, enhancing ADC payload delivery.

talk_title: KRAS-targeted therapy Phase III trial results in pancreatic cancer
affiliation: President and CEO, Pancreatic Cancer Action Network (PanCAN)
moa_summary: A KRAS-directed small‑molecule inhibitor was evaluated in a Phase III trial for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, demonstrating a favorable safety profile and evidence of clinical activity that supports KRAS as a therapeutic target in this disease.
key_findings: Revolution Medicines reported positive Phase III trial results for a KRAS‑targeted therapy in pancreatic cancer. | The trial represents a major step forward for KRAS‑directed treatment strategies in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

talk_title: Implementation gaps and early data from the TUBAWISP-2 salpingectomy trial
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology
moa_summary: Stone outlined the epidemiologic and health‑policy data supporting salpingectomy as a primary ovarian cancer prevention strategy, presented early enrollment data from the TUBAWISP-2 preference trial comparing standard risk‑reducing salpingo‑oophorectomy to early salpingectomy with delayed oophorectomy, and highlighted implementation barriers (coding, coverage, disparities) that limit adoption despite a 0.22 hazard ratio for serous ovarian cancer.
key_findings: 56% of BRCA1 carriers and ~33% of BRCA2 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond recommended ages, increasing lifetime serous cancer risk. | Presence of STIC at risk‑reducing surgery confers ~30% 10‑year risk of peritoneal high‑grade serous carcinoma. | Opportunistic salpingectomy reduces serous ovarian cancer risk by ~80% (HR 0.22) in the BC, Canada population‑based cohort. | US adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy at inpatient hysterectomy has plateaued at ~60% since 2018, with lower uptake in outpatient settings (11‑14%). | Racial and insurance disparities exist: Black women and Medicaid recipients are less likely to receive salpingectomy during tubal ligation or postpartum sterilization.

talk_title: OVASEEK: hyperspectral and fluorescence imaging platform for whole fallopian tube screening
affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Biological Engineering
moa_summary: Belcher described the OVASEEK system that combines near‑infrared hyperspectral imaging, >1000 nm fluorescent nanoparticle labeling, and machine‑learning segmentation to scan entire excised fallopian tubes in ~15 minutes, identify regions with atypical optical signatures, and predict BRCA mutation status and nanoparticle binding with >90% accuracy, enabling whole‑organ detection of STIC and pre‑cancer lesions.
key_findings: Imaged 86 fallopian tubes; initial prototype required 8 hours per tube, later optimized toward 15‑minute workflow. | Machine‑learning model predicts presence of gold‑nanoparticle binding sites with 86% accuracy based on hyperspectral data alone. | BRCA1/2 mutation status can be inferred from optical spectra with 90% accuracy at the whole‑tube level and 95% at the cellular level. | Detected a previously missed STIC in a colon‑surgery patient by highlighting a <200 µm atypical region for targeted pathology. | Developed a seventh‑generation instrument and custom edge‑computing chips (first batch expected June) to enable rapid on‑site analysis.

talk_title: Orion FISH platform reveals polyclonal copy‑number heterogeneity in STIC lesions
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: Using the Orion FISH platform, Drapkin performed simultaneous 16‑plex immunofluorescence and multiplex DNA‑FISH on single tissue sections, revealing that MYC and CCNE1 amplifications in STIC lesions are polyclonal and spatially heterogeneous, with MYC‑amplified cells often adjacent to immune infiltrates, while CCNE1‑amplified regions are immune‑cold; unexpected copy‑number gains of MDM4 were also detected in morphologically normal epithelium, especially in BRCA1 carriers.
key_findings: STIC lesions display polyclonal copy‑number alterations: distinct sub‑clones harbor MYC amplification, CCNE1 amplification, or both. | MYC‑amplified regions are enriched for neighboring immune cells, whereas CCNE1‑amplified regions show reduced immune infiltration. | Copy‑number gains of MYC and CCNE1 are also observed in adjacent histologically normal tubal epithelium. | MDM4 copy‑number gain occurs in both secretory (PAX8+) and ciliated (FOXP1+) cells of normal tubes, more frequently in BRCA1 carriers. | Validation with the clinically approved BioView platform confirms the accuracy of Orion FISH copy‑number calls.

talk_title: Mechanisms of ADC resistance informing novel combination therapy strategies
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; HR-positive breast cancer
moa_summary: SG (sacituzumab govitecan) binds TROP2, is internalized, releases SN-38 topoisomerase I inhibitor; resistance arises via TROP2 missense mutations that mislocalize the antigen and TOP1 point mutations that prevent SN-38 binding. Sequential PARP inhibition after SG exposure stabilizes TOP1 cleavage complexes, enhancing cytotoxicity.
key_findings: Acquired TROP2 missense mutations redirect the protein intracellularly, conferring resistance to SG. | TOP1 E418K mutation, previously unseen in patients, mediates resistance to the SN-38 payload. | TOP1 mutations detected in cfDNA precede clinical progression, serving as a potential biomarker. | Sequential dosing of SG followed by talazoparib is tolerable and yields durable responses, whereas concurrent dosing causes high myelosuppression. | PARP inhibition after SG washout synergistically stabilizes TOP1 cleavage complexes, enhancing tumor cell killing.

talk_title: Tumor intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms of ADC resistance in bladder cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; combination_therapy; clinical_trial; translational; spatial_biology
moa_summary: Enfortumab vedotin targets NECTIN4, is internalized and releases MMAE; resistance mechanisms include low or heterogeneous NECTIN4 expression, payload efflux, and altered endosomal trafficking. NECTIN4 expression can be pharmacologically up‑regulated via PPARγ agonists (rosiglitazone) or retinoic‑acid signaling (ATRA/Bexarotene), enhancing ADC efficacy.
key_findings: NECTIN4 high tumors (H‑score >225) achieve median OS of 31.5 months versus 18.4 months in low expressors. | Chromosome 1 amplification of PVRL4 (NECTIN4) drives homogeneous membranous expression and superior clinical outcomes. | Rosiglitazone induces NECTIN4 surface expression in low‑expressing bladder cancer cells without affecting normal urothelium, sensitizing them to EV. | Retinoic‑acid agonists (ATRA, Bexarotene) similarly up‑regulate NECTIN4 via STRA6‑RXR signaling. | EV‑resistant models retain or increase NECTIN4 but show reduced ADC internalization due to altered lysosomal trafficking.

talk_title: HER2 ADC resistance mechanisms and combination strategies
moa_summary: Trastuzumab deruxtecan binds HER2, is internalized, and releases a topoisomerase‑I inhibitor; resistance occurs via HER2 point mutations that impair antibody binding, reduced HER2 surface density, and heterodimerization with EGFR that limits internalization. EGFR blockade or activation can modulate HER2 internalization and restore sensitivity.
key_findings: HER2 binding‑site mutations prevent T‑DXd internalization and confer resistance in vitro. | Doxycycline‑induced HER2 down‑regulation reduces ADC internalization and increases IC50 by ~800‑fold. | EGFR overexpression decreases HER2 homodimers, reducing T‑DXd efficacy; EGFR knockdown or cetuximab treatment restores HER2 internalization. | Combining cetuximab with T‑DXd improves tumor responses in HER2‑high/EGFR‑high models in vivo. | CAR‑T targeting HER2 remains effective against EV‑resistant cells that retain HER2 expression.

talk_title: ZC3H18 truncating mutations drive ERV accumulation and melanomagenesis
affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Department of Medicine, Harvard Medical School
tumor_types: melanoma; colon cancer; uterine cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Truncating mutations in the RNA‑surveillance factor ZC3H18 act in a dominant‑negative manner, losing interaction with the NEXT complex while retaining RNA‑binding capacity, leading to cytoplasmic sequestration and stabilization of endogenous retroviral RNAs; these ERV transcripts are necessary and sufficient to accelerate melanomagenesis in zebrafish and human models.
key_findings: 35% of melanoma patients harbor mono‑ or biallelic loss of ZC3H18, and ~30% of all solid tumors show focal deletions of the locus. | ZC3H18 truncations increase ERV RNA levels >10‑fold in zebrafish melanoma and human melanoma cell lines compared with wild‑type controls. | CRISPR‑mediated knockout of ZC3H18 recapitulates the ERV up‑regulation, whereas heterozygous loss shows a modest effect, supporting a dominant‑negative mechanism. | Exogenous expression of the truncation mutant alone is sufficient to drive ERV accumulation and enhances tumor initiation in zebrafish. | Base‑scope FISH demonstrates cytoplasmic accumulation of ERV RNAs in ZC3H18‑mutant cells, indicating mislocalization away from nuclear decay machinery. | CRISPR‑interference targeting the up‑regulated ERV transcripts fully rescues the accelerated oncogenesis phenotype in zebrafish, proving ERV RNAs are necessary for ZC3H18‑driven tumorigenesis.

talk_title: Splicing‑regulated LAG‑3 intron removal enables conditional FOXO1 and IL‑12 expression to overcome T‑cell exhaustion
affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Chair of Molecular Pharmacology, Attending Physician, Leukemia Service
moa_summary: Exhausted CD8+ T cells splice out a retained intron in LAG3, enabling full‑length LAG‑3 protein; this splicing switch is harnessed by inserting a therapeutic cassette downstream of the intron, so that FOXO1 or IL‑12 is expressed only after intron removal in chronically stimulated T cells, thereby reprogramming exhausted T cells to a less exhausted, more cytotoxic phenotype and improving anti‑tumor activity.
key_findings: A previously unannotated intron in LAG3 is retained in acutely stimulated CD8+ T cells but efficiently removed in exhausted tumor‑infiltrating T cells, correlating with a 4‑fold increase in LAG‑3 mRNA and protein. | Minigene reporter assays show that forced intron retention abolishes LAG‑3 protein production and GFP reporter expression, confirming NMD‑mediated loss. | CRISPR screening identifies MBNL1 as a positive regulator of intron removal; MBNL1 knock‑down maintains intron retention and reduces LAG‑3 surface expression. | Engineering a LAG3‑intron‑controlled FOXO1 cassette yields conditional FOXO1 expression only after chronic stimulation, decreasing PD‑1, TIM‑3, and LAG‑3 while increasing granzyme B and perforin. | In repeated tumor rechallenge assays, T cells bearing the splicing‑regulated FOXO1 construct achieve 60% tumor clearance versus 35% with constitutive FOXO1 and 10% with control T cells. | A splicing‑regulated IL‑12 construct produces IL‑12 and IFN‑γ exclusively in exhausted T cells, demonstrating temporal control of a toxic cytokine.

talk_title: Impact of the intestinal microbiome on CAR T cell therapy
tumor_types: B-cell lymphoma; acute lymphoblastic leukemia; multiple myeloma
moa_summary: Antibiotic exposure, especially anaerobe‑targeting agents (piperacillin‑tazobactam, meropenem), disrupts gut microbiota, reducing beneficial taxa such as Akkermansia and Enterococcus‑derived SagA activity, leading to lower CAR T cell expansion, increased neurotoxicity, and shortened PFS/OS. Preclinical mouse models show that meropenem pretreatment or vancomycin alters microbiome composition, impairing CAR T efficacy, whereas fecal transplants from responders or Akkermansia supplementation restore antitumor activity.
key_findings: Antibiotic exposure within 4‑6 weeks before CAR T therapy is independently associated with reduced OS and PFS across five retrospective cohorts (n≈1,000 total). | Anaerobic‑targeting antibiotics (piperacillin‑tazobactam, meropenem) show the strongest negative impact on survival compared with low‑risk agents. | High‑risk antibiotic exposure correlates with increased ICANS incidence in two studies. | Mouse models pre‑treated with meropenem exhibit larger tumor burden and lower survival after CD19 CAR T infusion. | Fecal microbiota from CAR T responders improves tumor control in antibiotic‑depleted mice, whereas non‑responder FMT does not. | Akkermansia muciniphila supernatant enhances CAR T cytotoxicity in vitro and improves tumor control in vivo via an AhR‑dependent pathway. | Enterococcus‑derived SagA generates NOD2‑activating muramyl dipeptides; higher muramyl dipeptide levels predict better PFS in patients.
primary_population: CD19 CAR T recipients (NHL or B‑ALL)

talk_title: CAR T response rates: harnessing the microbiome
moa_summary: Antibiotic exposure (especially high‑risk anaerobic agents) selects for resistant Enterococcus strains lacking SagA activity and reduces beneficial taxa such as Akkermansia, leading to impaired CAR T expansion and higher progression rates. Reintroduction of Akkermansia or SagA‑expressing bacteria restores immune activation via AhR or NOD2 pathways, improving CAR T efficacy.
key_findings: Antibiotic exposure prior to CAR T is linked to reduced PFS and OS in both US and German cohorts. | Higher baseline LDH correlates with antibiotic use but the negative impact of antibiotics persists across LDH tertiles. | Enterococcus faecium SagA activity correlates with lower progression; patients harboring SagA‑positive strains have better outcomes. | Akkermansia muciniphila abundance predicts improved response in CD28‑CAR T recipients. | Mouse models show that tetracycline‑depleted microbiota lose CAR T benefit, which is rescued by Akkermansia reconstitution.
primary_population: CAR T patients with pre‑treatment antibiotic exposure

talk_title: Enterococcus SagA and NOD2 signaling modulate immunotherapy
moa_summary: Enterococcus faecium expresses the cell‑wall hydrolase SagA, which cleaves peptidoglycan into muramyl dipeptides that activate host NOD2 signaling. NOD2 activation enhances dendritic cell maturation and CD8⁺ T‑cell cytotoxicity, thereby potentiating checkpoint blockade and CAR T efficacy. SagA deletion abolishes this effect, while engineered SagA‑expressing Lactococcus lactis restores it.
key_findings: SagA‑expressing Enterococcus faecium synergizes with CTLA‑4 and PD‑L1 blockade in antibiotic‑treated mice, whereas SagA‑null strains do not. | SagA activity is required for NOD2‑dependent antitumor immunity; NOD2‑KO mice lose the benefit of SagA‑expressing bacteria. | Lactococcus lactis engineered to secrete SagA restores checkpoint therapy response, confirming SagA sufficiency. | SagA is conserved across commensal and vancomycin‑resistant Enterococcus strains; deletion in a VRE strain eliminates its immunostimulatory effect. | In a minor‑mismatch allo‑HSCT GVHD model, SagA‑positive VRE improves survival, suggesting context‑dependent benefits.

talk_title: AI‑assisted storytelling and GIS mapping to reduce cervical and colorectal cancer disparities
affiliation: Distinguished Professor of Population and Public Health Sciences, Associate Director for COE, USC Norris Comprehensive Cancer Center, Senior Associate Dean of Community Engagement, Keck School of Medicine
tumor_types: cervical cancer; colorectal cancer; breast cancer; lung cancer
moa_summary: The speaker described a GIS‑based mapping workflow that visualizes cancer incidence trends over time and an AI‑assisted storytelling pipeline that generates low‑health‑literacy scripts, storyboards, and digital assets (videos, fotonovelas, social‑media clips). By pairing community‑derived narratives with AI‑generated content, the program achieved a 45% rise in cervical‑screening appointments and produced 13 peer‑reviewed articles, demonstrating that culturally tailored, AI‑accelerated communication can translate epidemiologic data into measurable community‑level screening improvements.
key_findings: Implementation of GIS mapping identified high‑burden zip codes for cervical and colorectal cancer, guiding targeted outreach. | The Tamale Lesson video campaign increased screening appointments by 45% within six months among the target Hispanic population. | AI‑driven script generation reduced storyboard production time from weeks to hours, enabling rapid iteration based on focus‑group feedback. | The AI‑assisted framework supported creation of 13 journal articles and multiple digital toolkits (fotonovelas, YouTube channel, WhatsApp messaging). | Community health worker deployment (≈45 workers) and home‑to‑clinic navigation (ConnectHome) were launched to sustain screening gains despite pandemic setbacks.
primary_metric: screening appointment increase after Tamale Lesson video

talk_title: Markey Cancer Network: capacity building and research integration across Kentucky
affiliation: Professor of Behavioral Science, Associate Director of Population Science and Community Impact, University of Kentucky Markey Cancer Center
tumor_types: lung cancer; colorectal cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: The presentation outlined a hybrid care and research network that builds capacity in 20 independent Kentucky community hospitals, achieving 18 CoC accreditations, increasing referral volume (~1,000/year) and patient accruals (1,400), while embedding patient‑advocate scientists into basic and clinical projects to align research priorities with community needs.
key_findings: Eighteen of twenty network hospitals achieved CoC accreditation within three years of joining the Markey Cancer Network. | Referral volume to Markey for specialized services rose to ~1,000 in the most recent year, reflecting improved care coordination. | The network facilitated 1,400 patient accruals across therapeutic, diagnostic, and health‑services studies, expanding research access for rural populations. | Research advocacy program engaged 54 advocates across 15 projects, with 100% reporting that their input improved study design and community relevance. | Quality‑measure scores improved significantly after hospitals joined the network, demonstrating measurable enhancements in cancer care delivery.

talk_title: Sustainability models for community engagement: Be Well initiative and AI‑driven outreach
affiliation: Professor and Chair of Health Disparities Research, Associate Director of COE, MD Anderson Cancer Center
moa_summary: The speaker presented a place‑based sustainability framework that couples AI‑generated, culturally tailored communication tools with community‑led implementation (e.g., gardens, sun shades, food distribution). By transferring ownership to local organizations after 2–3 years, the model achieves >90% program continuation without ongoing grant support.
key_findings: The Be Well initiative delivered 21 million pounds of healthy food and installed 82 sun shades, impacting 676 000 residents across four neighborhoods. | 90% of implemented programs remained active after initial philanthropic funding ended, driven by city‑government co‑funding and community‑organization leadership. | AI‑assisted content creation reduced production time for educational materials from months to days, enabling rapid iteration with community feedback. | Transfer of program leadership to local partners after 2–3 years ensured long‑term ownership and reduced reliance on cancer‑center staff. | Cross‑sector coalitions (city, schools, faith‑based groups) amplified reach and facilitated policy changes supporting sustained health‑promotion infrastructure.
primary_metric: food distributed through Be Well initiative

talk_title: Defining senescence, senotypes and implications for cancer
affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Biomedical Engineering
moa_summary: The talk described how senescent cells adopt distinct senotypes in different tissue niches, identified via p16-Cre reporter mice and single-cell RNA‑seq, and how these senotypes influence wound healing, fibrosis, and tumor immune microenvironment, including vascular remodeling and T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: Senescent cells in fibrosis models are primarily stromal (CD29+ CD45-), with distinct vascular and fibroblastic senotypes identified by bulk and single‑cell sequencing. | Arthritic (ACL) injury before B16 tumor implantation reduces tumor growth and markedly increases response to checkpoint blockade, associated with increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and enhanced vascular P‑selectin expression. | Genetic ablation of p16‑positive senescent cells (Incakat model) abolishes the vascular and T‑cell infiltration phenotype and eliminates the immunotherapy benefit, indicating senescent cells are necessary for the observed anti‑tumor effect.

talk_title: Redox‑based pan‑senolytic Senlytic and its preclinical cancer prevention potential
affiliation: Charité – Universitätsmedizin Berlin, Max Delbrück Center for Molecular Medicine
moa_summary: Senlytic is a redox‑activated pro‑drug that exploits the elevated NADH/low GSH ratio and labile iron in senescent cells to generate hydroxyl radicals via Fenton chemistry, inducing ferroptosis and apoptosis selectively in p53‑competent senescent cells across multiple senescence inducers.
key_findings: Senlytic shows >10‑fold senolytic index over proliferating cells across replicative, oncogene‑induced, therapy‑induced, and virus‑induced senescence models. | In vivo, Senlytic clears p16‑positive senescent cells in aged mouse lungs, reducing collagen cuff formation without measurable toxicity. | Senlytic administered after chemotherapy eliminates therapy‑induced senescent lymphoma cells, extending mouse survival, but is ineffective in p53‑deficient tumors. | In a BRAF‑nevus mouse model, Senlytic reduces nevi and delays melanoma onset, demonstrating cancer‑preventive potential by targeting OIS lesions. | Senlytic prevents NMU‑induced RAS‑driven T‑cell lymphomagenesis in wild‑type mice, indicating that removal of incipient senescent lesions blocks tumor formation.
primary_value: median survival increased from 30 to 45 days
primary_population: p53‑proficient lymphoma‑bearing mice receiving chemotherapy + Senlytic

talk_title: Foundation models in pathology: data requirements, performance and robustness
affiliation: Radboud University Medical Center, Nijmegen, Netherlands, Department of Computational Pathology
moa_summary: Foundation models are trained with self‑supervised learning on large, diverse pathology image collections to produce high‑dimensional embeddings that capture generic visual features; these embeddings enable few‑shot downstream tasks such as cancer grading, mutation prediction, and survival modeling with reduced data requirements.
key_findings: Training on 12,000 prostate biopsies and 5,600 pathologist reviews yielded a quadratic weighted Kappa of up to 0.93, matching expert performance. | Foundation models achieved comparable performance with as little as 1% of the training data, whereas task‑specific models required >30% of data to reach similar accuracy. | Generalizability of foundation models was superior on out‑of‑distribution cohorts but depended on the diversity of the pre‑training data. | Foundation models consumed 35 times more energy than task‑specific models, highlighting a trade‑off between data efficiency and computational cost.

talk_title: Benchmarking foundation models for survival prediction and the SURFER prognostic model
affiliation: University of California, Irvine, Departments of Pathology and Biomedical Engineering
tumor_types: bladder cancer; colorectal cancer; brain cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: SURFER extends a vision‑language foundation model (Titan) with SoftCLIP, a weighted contrastive loss that incorporates patient survival times into the embedding space, enabling the model to learn prognostic features directly from whole‑slide images and associated outcome text.
key_findings: Patch‑level foundation models outperform slide‑level models for survival prediction across 13 cancer types. | Multimodal models (image + text) do not significantly improve survival prediction over unimodal models when prognostic information is absent from the text. | The SURFER model, trained with SoftCLIP on ~15,000 slides, yields a clear gradient of risk groups in UMAP space and improves downstream survival C‑index compared with the baseline Titan model. | Pan‑cancer training slightly outperforms cancer‑specific models, demonstrating feasibility of a single prognostic model for diverse tumor types.

talk_title: Clinical applications of pathology foundation models and future AI agents
affiliation: Technical University of Dresden, Germany, Professor of Clinical Artificial Intelligence; Senior Physician, Medical Oncology, University Hospital Dresden
tumor_types: lung cancer; prostate cancer; breast cancer; pan-cancer
moa_summary: Foundation models generate generic image embeddings that can be fine‑tuned for specific clinical tasks; when combined with large language model agents, they enable multimodal reasoning across pathology, radiology, genomics and clinical records to support tumor board decision‑making and trial matching.
key_findings: AI models trained on H&E slides can predict EGFR mutation status, allowing a 43% reduction in rapid molecular testing without loss of diagnostic accuracy. | Foundation models accelerate development of downstream tasks (e.g., molecular alteration inference, survival prediction) by requiring far fewer labeled examples. | Multimodal agents that integrate foundation model embeddings with language models can replicate tumor board reasoning and achieve performance comparable to human oncologists in benchmark studies.
primary_population: lung cancer patients undergoing EGFR screening

talk_title: New insights into SHP2 signaling to RAS and its catalytic domain functions
affiliation: University of Connecticut (collaborations with Wolfgang Petty, Rebecca Page, Jeroen den Hertog)
moa_summary: Catalytic cysteine mutations in SHP2 (C459D) retain signaling to RAS/ERK despite being phosphatase-dead, acting as an adapter that directly binds the DH/PH domain of SOS1 to promote sustained Ras activation, while phosphatase activity serves as a negative feedback.
key_findings: RMZ-4550 allosteric SHP2 inhibitor blocks EGF-induced RAS activation in 3T3 cells, rescued by SHP2 TMQL mutant expression. | Catalytic cysteine-to-aspartate (C459D) mutant restores RAS activation and sustains ERK/AKT signaling despite being >20,000‑fold phosphatase dead. | C459D mice are viable with mild phenotype, whereas C459E is embryonic lethal, indicating functional divergence of catalytic mutants. | Zebrafish shp2 C/D mutant rescues cardiac defects, confirming functional signaling across species. | Structural studies reveal direct binding of SHP2 catalytic domain to SOS1 DH/PH domain; mutations disrupting this interface abrogate signaling while preserving phosphatase activity. | Super‑resolution microscopy shows increased SOS1 membrane clustering in C/D mutant cells, correlating with enhanced Ras activation.

talk_title: Omega‑6 fatty acids activate mTORC1 via FABP5 and promote aggressive triple‑negative breast cancer
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; hormone receptor positive breast cancer
moa_summary: Omega‑6 fatty acids bind FABP5, inducing a conformational change that enables FABP5 to dock Raptor and assemble active mTORC1, driving proliferation of FABP5‑high triple‑negative breast cancer; omega‑3 promotes FABP5 degradation and induces microbiome‑derived lithocholic acid, which activates AMPK and suppresses mTOR signaling.
key_findings: FABP5 directly binds omega‑6 fatty acids, exposing a docking site for Raptor and activating mTORC1 in TNBC cells. | FABP5 knockdown abolishes omega‑6‑induced mTOR activation and proliferation in TNBC cell lines. | TNBC PDX tumors and patient serum show elevated FABP5; omega‑6‑fed mice exhibit higher tumor FABP5 and mTOR signaling than omega‑3‑fed controls. | Visceral adipocyte‑derived extracellular vesicle FABP5 is taken up via macropinocytosis, sensitizing otherwise FABP5‑low cells to omega‑6‑driven mTOR activation. | Omega‑3 fatty acids accelerate FABP5 proteasomal degradation (half‑life 2‑4 h vs 24 h with omega‑6). | Omega‑3‑rich diet increases microbiome‑derived lithocholic acid, which activates AMPK, suppresses mTORC1, and reduces tumor growth.
primary_value: significantly faster in omega‑6 diet vs omega‑3

talk_title: Structural and regulatory mechanisms of BRAF and CRAF activation and dimerization
moa_summary: BRAF and CRAF are held in an auto‑inhibited state by 14‑3‑3 and the cysteine‑rich domain; oncogenic mutations or RAS‑GTP binding destabilize this interface, allowing the CRD to release, BC heterodimer formation, and activation of MEK/ERK. Distinct N‑terminal acidic regions confer KRAS selectivity for BRAF, while CRAF relies more on 14‑3‑3 interactions.
key_findings: BRAF possesses a constitutive acidic N‑terminal segment that drives selective binding to KRAS polybasic region. | Disruption of the BC dimer interface abolishes EGF‑stimulated kinase activity of both BRAF and CRAF. | Class II/III BRAF mutants require BC heterodimerization for MAPK signaling, explaining paradoxical activation by first‑generation RAF inhibitors. | Cryo‑EM of auto‑inhibited BRAF shows the cysteine‑rich domain cradled by 14‑3‑3, occluding the dimer interface; mutations in this region release the inhibition. | In vitro KRAS‑GTP nanodisc assays demonstrate concentration‑dependent activation of BRAF that is dimer‑dependent and enhanced by membrane context. | C‑RAF auto‑inhibited complexes lack CRD‑14‑3‑3 contacts seen in BRAF, indicating distinct regulatory mechanisms.

talk_title: Autocrine LIF signaling drives myeloid immunosuppression and plasticity in ALK‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Loss of LKB1 in ALK‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma upregulates leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), which acts in an autocrine manner via LIFR/STAT3 to maintain dedifferentiated, SOX17‑high tumor cell states that secrete chemokines (CCL2, CXCL1/5/7) recruiting immunosuppressive ARG1⁺ interstitial macrophages and cyclic‑high neutrophils, thereby suppressing T‑cell function; blockade of LIF signaling genetically or with an anti‑LIF antibody reverses myeloid suppression and restores anti‑tumor immunity.
key_findings: LIF is transcriptionally up‑regulated in ALK‑mutant/LKB1‑deficient tumors and correlates with phospho‑STAT3 positivity in mouse and human samples. | Genetic deletion of LIF or LIFR markedly reduces tumor burden and diminishes immunosuppressive neutrophil (cyclic‑high) and ARG1⁺ interstitial macrophage populations. | T‑cell functional cytokine production (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α) is restored in LIFR‑deleted tumors; T‑cell depletion abrogates the tumor‑growth advantage conferred by LIF signaling. | LIF‑driven tumors harbor distinct dedifferentiated tumor cell clusters (high SOX17, PROM1/CD133) that are depleted upon LIF blockade. | Anti‑LIF antibody treatment recapitulates genetic deletion effects, reducing tumor size and myeloid immunosuppression in established tumors.
primary_value: significant reduction with LIF or LIFR deletion and with anti‑LIF antibody

talk_title: Molecular and cellular alterations associated with malignant progression in the pancreas
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: The work maps genomic and microenvironmental alterations across pancreatic precancerous lesions (IPMN, PanIN) showing that KRAS mutations are present early, while progression to invasive cancer is sometimes driven by additional alterations such as SMAD4 loss and distinct fibroblast and immune cell niche remodeling.
key_findings: IPMNs originate polyclonally with multiple KRAS or VGR2 clones, but high-grade IPMNs become clonal. | In ~25% of IPMN‑cancer pairs, cancer‑specific SMAD4 or TGF‑betaRII mutations are observed, suggesting a genetic driver of progression. | Imaging mass cytometry reveals fibroblast populations that are expanded in invasive cancer and reduced in IPMN, indicating niche-specific fibroblast remodeling. | IPMN‑associated fibroblast niches are enriched for granzyme‑positive cytotoxic T cells, whereas cancer‑associated niches have fewer T cells and altered fibroblast subsets.

talk_title: Evolution of pancreatic cancer microenvironment focusing on PanIN lesions
affiliation: University of Michigan, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: Spatial transcriptomics and single‑cell integration of donor pancreas samples reveal distinct epithelial and stromal domains in PanIN lesions, with IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling up‑regulated in poorly differentiated tumor areas and fibroblast subsets (ACTA2+, LRC15+, LEF1+) uniquely enriched around tumor, while plasma cells are enriched near PanIN and macrophages around tumor.
key_findings: PanIN lesions are present in >50% of organ donors, including young individuals, indicating high prevalence of silent precancers. | Spatial domains containing neoplastic epithelium are transcriptionally distinct from normal ducts and show progressive changes toward tumor. | IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling is highly enriched in poorly differentiated tumor domains compared with PanIN. | Plasma cells are preferentially localized to PanIN neighborhoods, whereas macrophages are enriched around tumor structures. | Two tumor‑specific fibroblast populations are identified: ACTA2+/LRC15+ CAFs (fibro‑2) and LEF1+ fibroblasts (fibro‑3), absent in normal pancreas.

talk_title: Stromal targeting strategies for interception of pancreatic cancer
affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Department of Oncology
moa_summary: The lab isolates patient‑derived fibroblasts, characterizes a composite 'host factor' metric of stromal protumorigenicity, and screens antibodies (e.g., anti‑NETRIN G1) and peptide inhibitors to normalize fibroblast function, testing them in vitro, in orthotopic co‑injection models, and in a pulse‑dose radiation clinical trial (PLDR) aimed at reducing stromal activation.
key_findings: A quantitative 'host factor' integrates multiple stromal biomarkers to distinguish protumorigenic from tumor‑suppressive fibroblast states. | Anti‑NETRIN G1 antibody reduces protumorigenic fibroblast traits in vitro and modestly suppresses lesion formation in orthotopic co‑injection models. | Pulse‑dose low‑dose-rate (PLDR) radiation normalizes stromal host factor scores in patient samples compared with standard continuous radiation. | Co‑injection of human precancerous epithelial cells with tumor‑promoting CAFs yields early PanIN‑like lesions, supporting a stromal requirement for initiation.
primary_value: reduced in PLDR‑treated patients vs continuous radiation
primary_population: pancreatic cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy

talk_title: ARCH-DR multimodal single-cell profiling of esophageal adenocarcinoma
affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Pathology
moa_summary: ARCH-DR is a high‑throughput archival DNA‑RNA co‑sequencing chemistry that simultaneously captures copy‑number alterations, somatic mutations and full‑length transcriptomes from thousands of single cells in fresh, frozen or FFPE esophageal adenocarcinoma specimens, enabling reconstruction of genetic lineages and phenotypic plasticity.
key_findings: Analyzed 12 EAC patients (≈34,000 cells) with ARCH‑DR, revealing patient‑specific transcriptional clusters driven by copy‑number dosage effects. | Identified three epithelial lineages (esophageal, gastric, intestinal) within individual tumors, with most subclones displaying mixed lineage phenotypes, indicating high cellular plasticity. | Detected ecDNA amplifications of EGFR and ERBB2 that segregated with distinct genetic subclones and showed expression saturation at ~20 copies. | Found that ~51% of genes exhibit dosage‑sensitive expression correlating with copy‑number changes, while the remainder are dosage‑insensitive. | Observed that ~20% of somatic mutations are associated with altered gene expression, highlighting mutation‑driven transcriptional effects.

talk_title: PTA enables uniform single‑cell genome amplification and reveals hidden mutational diversity in pediatric leukemia
moa_summary: Primary Template-directed Amplification (PTA) adds irreversible terminators to a Phi29‑based whole‑genome amplification, producing uniformly covered single‑cell genomes that retain accurate allele frequencies, enabling high‑sensitivity SNV and copy‑number detection, which was applied to pediatric leukemia to uncover a hidden mutational burden and clonal drug‑resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: PTA achieves ~90% detection of known germline variants with allele‑frequency distribution centered at 50%, outperforming MDA and PCR‑based methods. | Single‑cell sequencing of pediatric leukemia revealed 2,000–4,000 mutations per cell, far exceeding the <1,000 mutations observed in bulk sequencing. | De‑novo mutational signatures (SBS2/13 APOBEC) were identified at the single‑cell level, showing heterogeneous signature activity across clones. | Post‑treatment MRD analysis uncovered JAK2‑mutant subclones that were undetectable in bulk but expanded after chemotherapy, suggesting a genetic basis for resistance. | Integration of surface‑marker index sorting with PTA enabled mapping of phenotypic (surface protein) and genotypic (SNV/CNV) traits onto phylogenetic trees.

talk_title: SKY‑Met: high‑throughput single‑cell DNA methylation and multi‑omic profiling
affiliation: Oregon Health & Science University, Department of Molecular Medicine
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; acute myeloid leukemia
moa_summary: SKY‑Met combines combinatorial indexing with nucleosome disruption to achieve genome‑wide bisulfite‑free single‑cell DNA methylation profiling at high throughput; the platform also supports simultaneous ATAC‑seq or whole‑genome sequencing, enabling multi‑omic interrogation of epigenetic state, chromatin accessibility and copy‑number in thousands of cells from frozen or fixed tissues.
key_findings: Generated a 140,000‑cell methylome atlas of the human cerebral cortex, revealing cell‑type‑specific CpG methylation patterns and non‑CG methylation dynamics in neuronal subtypes. | Applied SKY‑Met to pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, achieving >90% CpG coverage per cell and enabling copy‑number profiling from frozen tumor specimens. | Demonstrated a joint methylome‑ATAC co‑assay on ~8% of 140,000 bone‑marrow cells, providing concordant cell‑type clustering from both modalities. | Detected a balanced translocation in a PDAC subclone using combined single‑cell genome + Hi‑C reads, illustrating the ability to capture structural variants alongside epigenetic information.

talk_title: Precision medicine in childhood CNS tumors and high‑risk brain tumors
affiliation: Sydney Children's Hospital and Cancer Institute
tumor_types: brain tumors; low-grade glioma; diffuse midline glioma; sarcoma
moa_summary: Ziegler described a national whole‑genome and methylation profiling program that identifies actionable alterations (e.g., NTRK fusions, MYC amplification) in high‑risk pediatric brain tumors; matched targeted agents such as larotrectinib produce disease stabilization or objective responses, and patients receiving precision‑guided therapy show significantly improved progression‑free and overall survival compared with unguided treatment.
key_findings: 71% of high‑risk CNS tumor patients harbored a therapeutic target; 5% had a diagnostic revision based on genomics. | Precision‑guided therapy conferred a 44% reduction in hazard of death (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.38‑0.82) versus unguided therapy. | Patients with MYC‑amplified tumors had markedly worse OS (median 12 mo vs 21 mo) but still derived benefit from matched therapy. | A TRK‑fusion infant with refractory high‑grade glioma achieved rapid functional recovery and radiographic remission after larotrectinib.

talk_title: Precision medicine in childhood bone sarcomas: osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma subtyping
affiliation: Boston Children's Hospital and Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute
moa_summary: Janeway identified MYC amplification (and high MYC protein) as a high‑risk biomarker in osteosarcoma, correlating with markedly reduced overall survival, and demonstrated that STAG2 mutations predict relapse in Ewing sarcoma, establishing biologic subtypes that can guide risk‑adapted therapy and future targeted approaches.
key_findings: MYC amplification occurs in ~10% of osteosarcoma and predicts significantly worse OS (median 1.5 yr vs 5.4 yr). | High MYC protein expression (IHC H‑score >150) independently associates with poor survival; combined amplification + high protein identifies the highest‑risk subgroup (median OS 1 yr). | In a validation cohort, MYC amplification confirmed as adverse prognostic factor for both OS and EFS. | STAG2 mutation is the strongest molecular predictor of relapse in localized Ewing sarcoma (multivariable HR ≈ 1.5). | Patients without any recurrent alterations have >90% recurrence‑free survival, suggesting a de‑intensification opportunity.

talk_title: Molecular subtyping of osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma for outcome prediction
affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Boston Children’s Hospital
moa_summary: Wei integrated targeted sequencing and IHC data to define biologic subtypes in osteosarcoma (MYC amplification + high protein) and Ewing sarcoma (STAG2 mutation), demonstrating that these molecular alterations independently predict poorer survival and can be used to stratify patients for risk‑adapted therapy.
key_findings: MYC amplification occurs in ~10% of osteosarcoma and predicts markedly inferior OS (median 1.5 yr). | High MYC protein expression alone also predicts poor survival; combined amplification + high protein identifies the highest‑risk subgroup (median OS 1 yr). | In a separate MSKCC cohort, MYC amplification confirmed as adverse prognostic factor for OS and EFS. | STAG2 mutation is the strongest molecular predictor of relapse in localized Ewing sarcoma, independent of other clinical factors. | Patients lacking recurrent alterations have >90% recurrence‑free survival, supporting potential de‑intensification strategies.

talk_title: Slide Tags spatial transcriptomics and lineage tracing in tumor ecosystems
tumor_types: melanoma; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; lung cancer
moa_summary: Slide Tags uses photo-cleavable DNA barcodes on spatial arrays to label nuclei, providing true single-cell resolution spatial transcriptomics; combined with lineage barcoding and CNV detection to map tumor clone spatial niches and infer expansion dynamics.
key_findings: Slide Tags achieves true single-cell resolution with median >3,000 UMIs per cell, outperforming Visium HD in melanoma specimens. | Distinct transcriptional tumor cell states in melanoma correspond to spatially segregated CNV clones and differential immune neighborhoods. | Neural crest-like tumor state associates with SPP1+ macrophages and fibroblasts, showing reduced CD8 T‑cell clonality. | Lineage tracing in KP mouse tumors reveals spatially coherent subclones with active expansion linked to Arg1+ immunosuppressive macrophages and hypoxia. | Metastasis‑seeding subclones share hypoxic and immunosuppressive niche signatures with primary tumor regions.

talk_title: Decoding tissue architecture in pancreatic cancer using SEGER and Wormhole
moa_summary: SEGER assigns transcripts to nuclei using AI‑driven graph embeddings, eliminating segmentation errors; Wormhole transforms high‑dimensional niche matrices into a scalable latent space, enabling trajectory and interaction analysis of spatial neighborhoods.
key_findings: SEGER improves transcript‑to‑cell assignment over geometric methods, preserving continuous cell‑state manifolds. | Wormhole latent captures dominant variation as a gastric‑to‑progenitor axis and associated niche remodeling. | p53 loss sustains a progenitor‑like niche with immunosuppressive TNC fibroblasts and Arg1+ macrophages in early PDAC. | Ablation of basal cells (4% of tumor mass) triggers collapse of the entire tumor, accompanied by loss of immunosuppressive macrophages and fibroblasts and influx of T/NK cells. | CRISPR‑mediated deletion of CSF2 specifically in basal cells phenocopies basal cell ablation, implicating CSF2 as a master niche signal.

talk_title: Spatial transcriptomics and proteomics reveal organization of glioma and head‑and‑neck cancer
tumor_types: glioblastoma; IDH‑mutant glioma; head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
moa_summary: Combines Visium spatial transcriptomics (55 µm spots) with CODEX multiplexed protein imaging to map cancer cell states and microenvironmental interactions, revealing spatial coherence, hypoxia‑driven layering in glioma and microenvironment‑dependent partial EMT patterns in head‑and‑neck cancer.
