key_findings: In GBM, mesenchymal‑hypoxia tumor cells form spatially coherent patches; high hypoxia correlates with structured tissue organization. | GBM spatial layers: necrotic/hypoxic core → mesenchymal/hypoxia → inflammatory macrophages → vascular repair → neurodevelopmental tumor cells → normal brain parenchyma. | IDH‑mutant gliomas show less structure overall; low‑grade tumors retain normal white‑gray matter boundaries that act as barriers to malignant cell infiltration. | In head‑and‑neck cancer, partial EMT programs are either edge‑enriched (fibroblast‑derived TGF‑β) or core‑enriched (myeloid‑derived oncostatin M), influencing immune infiltration. | Oncostatin M and TGF‑β both induce partial EMT genes in vitro, but spatial context determines dominant driver in vivo.

talk_title: Spatial Transcriptomics Profiling of Colorectal Cancer Tumors in Alaska Native People: Discovery of Prognostic Biomarkers
moa_summary: This study utilized spatial transcriptomics (GeoMix) to profile colorectal cancer tumors in Alaska Native people, identifying prognostic biomarkers associated with mortality. Key findings include differential gene expression (SPINK4, MUQ2, HBA2) and pathway enrichment (interferon response, EMT) in tumor and stromal compartments, as well as altered cell type abundance (plasma cells, mast cells), correlating with patient survival outcomes.
key_findings: Identified spatially specific markers tied to colorectal cancer-specific mortality in Alaska Native people. | In the tumor center epithelial compartment, downregulation of MILK2 and SPINK4, and interferon alpha and gamma pathways were associated with not dying from colorectal cancer. | In the tumor center, a decrease in plasma cells and an increase in mast cells were observed in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer. | In the invasive margin epithelial compartment, an increase in HBA2 expression was observed in patients who died from colorectal cancer. | Epithelial-mesenchymal transition pathways were enriched in the invasive margin (epithelial and stromal compartments) in patients who died from colorectal cancer.

talk_title: Highly Multiplexed Immunoassays Enable Discovery of Multi-Marker EV Surface Signatures as Novel Biomarkers
moa_summary: This presentation introduces a novel combinatorial immunosequencing platform for highly multiplexed profiling of extracellular vesicle (EV) surface signatures. The technology enables the discovery of multi-marker combinations that can distinguish cell origins and provide prognostic information, demonstrating improved sensitivity over single markers and showing consistent findings across multiple colorectal cancer cohorts.
key_findings: Soluble CEA was undetectable in 16% of stage IV CRC patients, while the same molecule on EV surfaces was detectable. | The combinatorial immunosequencing platform can detect rare EV subpopulations (1 in 10 million) with high sensitivity and a four-log dynamic range. | Three-marker combinations consistently discriminated between epithelial and stromal cell origins in CRC EVs across three independent cohorts. | Several multi-marker EV surface signatures showed FDR significant prognostic value in longitudinal CRC cohorts. | CD73, CD13, and CEA were identified as recurrent components in CRC-secreted EVs with prognostic potential.

talk_title: Differences in Survival Outcomes Among Metastatic Colorectal Cancer Patients Across Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor Two and Three Expression Levels
moa_summary: This study investigated the prognostic and predictive value of FGFR2 and FGFR3 gene expression and FGFR2 SNPs in metastatic colorectal cancer patients from the CALGB SWOG 80405 trial. FGFR2 high expression was associated with worse survival overall and specifically in the cetuximab arm, while FGFR3 high expression and a specific FGFR2 SNP (RS-3135718) were associated with improved survival in the cetuximab arm, suggesting differential roles in response to EGFR inhibition.
key_findings: FGFR2 high expression was prognostic for worse overall survival in mCRC patients (HR 1.28). | FGFR2 high expression was predictive of worse overall survival in the cetuximab arm (HR 1.56). | FGFR3 high expression was predictive of improved overall survival in the cetuximab arm (HR 0.59). | FGFR2 SNP RS-3135718 variant allele was predictive of improved PFS and OS in the cetuximab arm. | FGFR2 inhibition decreased cell viability in CRC cell lines, reiterating its criticality for cell survival.

talk_title: Mapping of Spatial Organization of B Cells and Immune Signaling in Triple Negative Breast Cancer to Characterize Response to Immunotherapy
moa_summary: This study investigated the role of B cells and cytokine signaling in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) response to immunotherapy using imaging mass cytometry (IMC). Findings indicate that B cell densities, specific B cell niches (e.g., B activated niche), and their interactions with other immune cells are significantly associated with immunotherapy response. The spatial organization of B cells and cytokine enrichment within niches appear to be key drivers of treatment efficacy.
key_findings: Several B cell populations (germinal center B, plasma memory, naive B) are associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment. | Specific B cell niches (germinal center, B activated, plasma-rich, plasma-maturing) were identified. | The B activated niche and its interactions with other immune cells are predictive of immunotherapy response. | B cells are more significantly impacted post-immunotherapy compared to other TME cells. | Cytokine enrichment within niches is a persistent feature in immunotherapy responders across treatment time points.

talk_title: AI-Powered Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) Links Pancreatic Precursor Biology to PDAC Survival Phenotypes
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; pancreatic precursor lesions
moa_summary: This study applied AI-powered Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) to analyze pancreatic precursor lesions and PDAC. By integrating high-resolution microscopy with mass spectrometry-based spatial proteomics, the researchers identified early molecular changes in normal ducts associated with cancer development, including immune editing and metabolic reprogramming. The technology also enabled the detection of KRAS mutations directly from proteomic data, linking genotype to phenotype and revealing insights into immune evasion in high-grade PANA.
key_findings: Cancer-associated normal ducts show distinct proteomic profiles compared to incidental normal ducts, including oxidative stress, metabolic reprogramming, and immune editing. | Inflammation and antigen presentation pathways are upregulated early in pancreatic precursor lesions but drop sharply in high-grade PANA, indicating immune evasion. | KRAS hotspot mutations can be identified from proteomic data, enabling genotype-phenotype correlation in precursor lesions. | The study identified a 'cancer field effect' where normal ducts near tumors acquire immune signatures. | DVP identified distinct proteomic differences between spatially adjacent regions within tumors, highlighting tumor heterogeneity.

talk_title: Spatial Immune Multiomics Refines Prognostication in Early-Stage Estrogen Receptor-Positive Breast Cancer
affiliation: Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland
moa_summary: This study utilized spatial multiomics (IHC, spatial transcriptomics, spatial proteomics) on early-stage ER+ breast cancer patients from the TAILORx trial to refine prognostication in the intermediate recurrence score group. Findings reveal that inflamed immune microenvironments are associated with dysfunctional responses, including upregulation of checkpoints and downregulation of ER signaling. High stromal CD8 density was identified as a predictive marker of chemo inferiority, suggesting that immune infiltration patterns can refine treatment decisions.
key_findings: Four TME types and three epithelial types were identified, with immune types not significantly captured by Oncotype DX Recurrence Score. | Inflamed epithelia were characterized by downregulation of ESR1 and suppression of DNA repair pathways. | Inflamed TME and epithelia were prognostic in the intermediate recurrence score group, with chemotherapy showing poorer outcomes. | High stromal CD8 density was a predictive marker of chemo inferiority in the intermediate recurrence score group. | Increasing CD8 density conferred poorer outcome on chemotherapy, adding prognostic information to clinical variables.
primary_population: Intermediate recurrence score ER+ breast cancer

talk_title: Unbiased Phenotyping of CRISPR Edited Tumors with Preserved Map and Subcellular Imaging of the Mouse Whole Transcriptome, 22,000 Plex
moa_summary: This presentation describes the PertiMap platform, which uses CRISPR-edited, barcoded tumors in mice to generate large-scale in vivo perturbation data. This data is then 'humanized' using the MAIRS model, which projects human biology onto mouse H&E images, enabling the discovery of novel therapeutic targets and combinations. The approach allows for unbiased phenotyping of genetic perturbations and identification of drivers of tumor biology and response to therapy.
key_findings: The PertiMap platform enables scaled in vivo genetic perturbation and multimodal tissue profiling. | The MAIRS model can project human biology onto mouse H&E images, effectively 'humanizing' preclinical models. | MAIRS identified tumors with high predicted human CCAM6 expression in mouse models, correlating with specific histological changes. | The embedding space of perturbations revealed distinct phenotypic clusters associated with immune infiltration, fibrosis, and metabolic signatures. | VEGFR1 knockout tumors treated with PD1 showed a shift towards a hot TME, suggesting a rationale for combination therapy.

talk_title: Pragmatic trial of mailed FIT versus FIT-DNA outreach in community health centers
affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Center for Primary Care Research
moa_summary: Mailed outreach using either annual FIT kits or triennial FIT‑DNA kits, combined with text reminders and manufacturer‑provided navigation, modestly increased colorectal cancer screening uptake (28% vs 23% at 90 days) in underserved community health centers, though follow‑up colonoscopy rates remained suboptimal.
key_findings: FIT‑DNA arm achieved 28% screening completion at 90 days versus 23% for FIT (p<0.05). | The modest advantage persisted at 180 days (≈27% vs 22%). | Higher screening rates were observed in Los Angeles sites for FIT‑DNA, driven by lower baseline screening. | Hispanic and Medicaid/uninsured participants showed greater uptake of FIT‑DNA, likely reflecting clinic‑level differences. | Colonoscopic follow‑up after abnormal results was low across all sites, with Boston showing the highest rates.

talk_title: DISRUPT: AI‑driven patient‑trial matching to improve equity in cancer trial enrollment
affiliation: Mount Sinai Cancer Center, Associate Director of Community Engaged and Equity Research
tumor_types: breast cancer; lung cancer; liver cancer
moa_summary: An EMR‑based algorithm identifies patients at treatment decision points, matches them to open trials, and delivers bilingual trial summaries to clinicians and patients before the decision visit, resulting in higher minority enrollment (58%) and a 27% enrollment rate among eligible patients, surpassing historical benchmarks.
key_findings: Enrolled 579 patients, of whom 58% self‑identified as minority, reversing typical under‑representation. | Eligibility filtering reduced manual chart review workload by 35%. | Among eligible patients, 27% enrolled in a trial, compared with ~22% at NCI centers. | No racial/ethnic differences were observed in patient attitudes toward trials or in physician‑reported trial discussions. | High refusal rate due to emotional burden at decision points limited overall enrollment.

talk_title: Multimodal navigation to increase lung cancer screening in the I‑95 corridor
affiliation: VCU Massey Comprehensive Cancer Center, Director of Lung Cancer Health Disparities Research
moa_summary: A multimodal, multilevel navigation system—combining community activators, certified community health workers, and clinical nurse navigators—was deployed to identify, engage, and guide African‑American adults in the I‑95 corridor through low‑dose CT lung‑cancer screening, resulting in enrollment of 220 participants and collection of >5,000 biospecimens.
key_findings: Identified 675 African‑American individuals eligible for lung‑cancer screening; 220 (≈33%) enrolled after navigation. | Collected >5,000 biospecimens and contacted >3,000 community members, demonstrating feasibility of large‑scale outreach. | Multimodal navigation (community activators + CHWs + nurse navigators) improved screening uptake compared with prior single‑navigator models. | Rural sites required intensive door‑to‑door outreach; lack of mobile screening vans limited reach in remote areas. | Financial‑toxicity navigation was planned but not fully implemented due to resource constraints.
primary_population: screen‑eligible African‑American adults in I‑95 corridor

session_title: Strengthening the Oncology Trial Ecosystem through Operational and Regulatory Innovation
talk_title: Survey of US Oncology Trial Site Activation Timelines
affiliation: Friends of Cancer Research, Vice President of Science Policy
moa_summary: Mark Stewart presented analysis of a multi‑company survey of registrational oncology trials (2020‑2024) showing that US sites open the first site quickly (~150 days) but lag substantially in scaling to 75% of planned sites (>300 days) and in enrolling the first patient (~80 days). He linked these delays to cumulative protocol amendments, each adding 30‑60 days, and highlighted systemic bottlenecks across regulatory review, IRB processes, contracting, and site readiness.
key_findings: US first site activation median 150 days, faster than Europe and China. | Time to activate 75% of planned US sites exceeds 300 days, whereas South Korea achieves ~45 days and other regions 100‑125 days. | First patient in US takes ~80 days versus 40‑50 days in non‑US regions. | Each additional protocol amendment version adds approximately 30‑60 days to overall study start time. | Trials with three or more protocol versions experience an extra 70‑80 days delay compared with the original version.

talk_title: Stress and mutation dependency reshape CAF heterogeneity via HSF1 and oxidative stress pathways
tumor_types: breast cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; ovarian cancer; prostate cancer
moa_summary: Cancer cell BRCA mutations drive upregulation of stress‑responsive transcription factors (HSF1, NRF2, ATF4, XBP1) in adjacent stromal fibroblasts, reprogramming them toward an immune‑regulatory iCAF phenotype that suppresses NK‑cell cytotoxicity and promotes tumor progression.
key_findings: BRCA‑mutated tumors exhibit higher stromal HSF1, ATF4, and XBP1 expression compared with BRCA‑wild‑type tumors. | HSF1‑dependent signaling converts fibroblasts to iCAFs that down‑regulate NK‑cell activating receptors (NKG2D, DNAM1). | CAF‑mediated internalization of NK‑cell ligands (CD155, ULBP1) impairs NK‑cell cytotoxicity in ex vivo co‑culture assays. | In vivo NK‑cell depletion or CAF enrichment increases stromal content and reduces metastasis control in mouse models. | High nectin‑2 expression on CAFs correlates with poorer overall survival in breast cancer tissue microarrays.

talk_title: Chronic stress, glucocorticoids, and neutrophil NETs promote metastatic outgrowth
moa_summary: Chronic stress elevates glucocorticoids, which activate the glucocorticoid receptor in neutrophils, driving excessive NET formation that remodels the lung microenvironment, expands ECM, suppresses T‑cell infiltration, and creates a permissive niche for dormant disseminated tumor cells to proliferate and form metastases.
key_findings: Restraint stress increased circulating corticosterone and produced a ~4‑fold rise in lung metastases. | Chronic unpredictable mild stress also elevated corticosterone and enhanced metastatic burden despite milder hormone rise. | Stressed lungs showed increased ECM (fibronectin), reduced T‑cell infiltration, and expanded neutrophils. | Neutrophils from stressed mice formed markedly more NETs ex vivo; this required neutrophil‑intrinsic glucocorticoid receptor signaling. | DNase treatment abolished NET DNA, reduced ECM deposition, and returned metastatic burden to baseline levels in stressed mice. | Glucocorticoid‑treated mice displayed higher Ki‑67 positivity in dormant disseminated tumor cells, but metastasis required concurrent T‑cell depletion.

talk_title: Social stressors associate with accelerated epigenetic aging in Black breast cancer survivors
moa_summary: Chronic exposure to socioeconomic and built‑environment stressors (low income, neighborhood disinvestment) correlates with accelerated DNA‑methylation aging (DunedinPACE) in breast cancer survivors, suggesting that systemic stress pathways may hasten biological aging and potentially influence cancer outcomes.
key_findings: Mean DunedinPACE was 1.3 (accelerated aging) two years after breast cancer diagnosis. | Lower income‑to‑poverty ratio, higher perceived physical disorder, and greater objective neighborhood disinvestment correlated positively with epigenetic age acceleration (p≤0.1). | BMI and current smoking showed the strongest independent associations with accelerated aging (smoking largest effect size). | Repeated measures in 38 participants showed a modest overall deceleration of epigenetic age over time, but the effect of social stressors attenuated at the later time point. | Chemotherapy receipt also influenced epigenetic age acceleration, suggesting treatment may mediate stress‑related aging effects.

talk_title: Accelerating Molecular Glue Degrader Discovery and Selective Chemical Probe Development
moa_summary: Fischer described a high‑throughput degradomics pipeline that couples data‑independent acquisition mass spectrometry with affinity‑purification MS to identify molecular glue degraders. Structural biology (cryo‑EM) and predictive algorithms enable rapid (≈6 weeks) discovery of selective degraders, exemplified by a cereblon‑recruited KAT2A degrader that shows no off‑target degradation and induces loss of KAT2A and H3K9ac in AML PDX models.
key_findings: Cost per molecule for degradomics dropped from >$20,000 in 2012 to < $500 using DIA‑MS and automation. | Discovery timeline from initial screen to high‑resolution cryo‑EM structure reduced to ~6 weeks. | A selective KAT2A degrader was identified that does not degrade the paralog KAT2B or off‑target GSPD1/2. | In an aggressive AML PDX model, treatment with the KAT2A degrader caused complete loss of KAT2A protein and H3K9 acetylation, confirming on‑target activity. | Structural analysis revealed a unique tyrosine‑mediated interface on cereblon that confers selectivity for KAT2A over KAT2B.

talk_title: Structural Basis of PI3‑Kinase Activation by Membrane Recruitment, RAS, and Multivalent RTK Scaffolds
moa_summary: Dzura showed that membrane recruitment of PI3K via farnesylated KRAS and phospho‑RTK tails drives stepwise displacement of autoinhibitory domains, exposing a previously unseen C‑terminal SH2 domain that initially blocks full activation. Inclusion of PIP2 in the nanodisc induces a conformational swing of helix‑12, enabling substrate capture. The common oncogenic H1047R mutation likely destabilizes the helix‑12 hinge, favoring the active conformation. Multivalent HER3 phospho‑tails further promote dimerization of the holoenzyme, creating a highly active, membrane‑anchored state resistant to re‑autoinhibition.
key_findings: Cryo‑EM of PI3K bound to farnesylated KRAS on nanodiscs revealed progressive loss of regulatory domains (auto‑inhibitory N‑SH2 and iSH2) across three structural classes. | A previously unobserved C‑terminal SH2 domain contacts the membrane and acts as an additional checkpoint before full activation. | Inclusion of PIP2 in the nanodisc reorients the holoenzyme, forming a true helix‑12 that swings 180° and directly engages substrate PIP2 in the active site – the first such observation. | The H1047R oncogenic mutation likely disrupts a hinge interaction (His1047‑Trp1051), facilitating helix‑12 swing and membrane association, explaining its activating effect. | Multivalent HER3 phospho‑tails induce a dimeric PI3K configuration at the nanodisc edge, preventing re‑assembly of the auto‑inhibited state and representing a fully active dimeric species.

talk_title: Conformational Landscapes of Protein Kinases Reveal Low‑Populated States for Selective Inhibition
moa_summary: Kalodimos demonstrated that protein kinases sample multiple low‑populated conformational states in solution. Using high‑field NMR, three states were characterized for ABL (active, inactive‑1, inactive‑2) and for Src (active, fully inactive, and a novel intermediate). The intermediate state exhibits steric clashes with ADP, explaining its role in rapid ADP release and processive phosphorylation. Gleevec’s selectivity arises from binding the rare inactive‑2 state of ABL, and resistance mutations destabilize this state. Structural insight into these invisible states enables rational design of highly selective inhibitors via virtual docking of billions of compounds or experimental DEL screening.
key_findings: ABL kinase populates three conformations: a ground active state and two low‑populated inactive states (~5% each). | Gleevec binds selectively to the inactive‑2 state, explaining its high specificity and the mechanism of many resistance mutations that destabilize this state. | Src family kinases also sample an intermediate conformation (~5%) that has markedly reduced ADP affinity, identifying it as the key state for rapid ADP release during processive phosphorylation. | Mutations that destabilize the intermediate state abolish processive phosphorylation by Src and Lck, confirming its functional relevance. | VirtualFlow docking of >70 billion compounds against the inactive‑2 ABL structure yielded nanomolar inhibitors with promising selectivity profiles.

talk_title: CAR T‑cell consolidation therapy for older ALL patients and BAFF‑R CAR for CD19‑negative relapse
affiliation: City of Hope, Department of Hematology and Oncology, Duarte, CA
moa_summary: CD19 CAR T cells with a naive‑phenotype enrichment expand, contract, and persist for 4–5 months, trafficking to CSF and providing durable remission in older ALL patients when given in first remission; BAFF‑R CAR T cells target an essential B‑cell survival receptor, showing expansion, low neurotoxicity, and clinical activity in CD19‑negative relapsed B‑cell malignancies.
key_findings: In a cohort of 48 older ALL patients, CD19 CAR achieved 90% complete remission with no dose‑limiting toxicities and markedly reduced grade ≥2 CRS (17% vs 80% in relapse setting). | CAR T cells were detectable in CSF of every treated patient, indicating CNS trafficking and no observed CNS relapses. | Two molecular relapses were identified by ClonoSEQ; both patients were successfully retreated and proceeded to allogeneic transplant. | First‑in‑human BAFF‑R CAR demonstrated expansion comparable to CD19 CAR, grade 1 toxicities only, and ≥70% objective responses in lymphoma patients. | BAFF‑R expression persisted in ~66% of CD19‑negative ALL relapses, supporting its role as a salvage target.
primary_population: Adults with relapsed ALL receiving CD19 CAR

talk_title: Engineering TCR‑engineered T cells to overcome TGF‑β–driven dysfunction in solid tumors
affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, Seattle, WA
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; melanoma; breast cancer; lung cancer; glioblastoma
topic_categories: immunotherapy; CAR_T; TCR_T; synthetic_biology; combination_therapy; translational; basic_science
moa_summary: Engineered TCR T cells targeting mesothelin or KRAS G12V infiltrate pancreatic tumors but acquire a TGF‑β–driven, mitigated exhaustion program; CRISPR knockout of the TGF‑β‑responsive transcription factor IKZF3 (Aeolus) reduces both TGF‑β and exhaustion signatures but induces apoptosis, which can be rescued by synthetic fusion proteins delivering pro‑survival signals. HIT CARs replace the scFv with antibody heavy/light chains to create HLA‑independent, ultra‑sensitive receptors that eradicate low‑density CD70‑expressing tumors in PDX models, outperforming conventional CARs.
key_findings: Mesothelin‑specific TCR T cells showed tumor infiltration but displayed a dominant TGF‑β–driven exhaustion signature distinct from blood T cells. | IKZF3 (Aeolus) knockout eliminated the TGF‑β signature and mitigated exhaustion but caused activation‑induced cell death, which was rescued by engineered fusion proteins delivering IL‑2 or FasL‑derived survival signals. | HIT CD70 CARs demonstrated superior sensitivity, achieving tumor eradication in renal cell carcinoma PDXs with low CD70 expression where conventional CARs failed. | KRAS G12V‑specific TCRs engineered with CD8αβ and CD28 co‑stimulation induced durable tumor regressions and biomarker (CEA, ctDNA) declines in 3 of 4 patients. | Spatial analysis revealed that exhausted engineered T cells reside in tumor cores surrounded by TGF‑β, whereas functional T cells localize to tertiary lymphoid structures near vasculature.
primary_value: ≥30% reduction in target lesion in 2 of 4 KRAS‑TCR patients

talk_title: Novel CAR designs: 1XX, HIT, and 1A platforms for enhanced potency and sensitivity
tumor_types: diffuse large B‑cell lymphoma; renal cell carcinoma; ovarian cancer; breast cancer; glioblastoma
moa_summary: The 1XX CAR removes two of three CD3ζ ITAMs, delivering a calibrated activation signal that enables durable complete remissions with a 25‑million‑cell dose and reduced cytokine toxicity; HIT CARs fuse antibody heavy/light chains to a TCR scaffold, creating HLA‑independent receptors with ultra‑high antigen sensitivity that overcome low‑density CD70 expression; 1A CAR incorporates the pre‑TCR cytoplasmic domain, driving YBX1‑mediated global translation enhancement, resulting in potent, long‑lasting effector function with minimal exhaustion.
key_findings: 1XX CD19 CAR achieved a 75% complete remission rate in refractory DLBCL using only 25 M cells, with markedly lower CRS/ICANS compared to standard products. | HIT CD70 CAR eradicated renal cell carcinoma PDXs with low CD70 expression, whereas conventional CD70 CARs failed even at 10 M cell doses. | 1A CD70 CAR outperformed classic 28ζ CARs in orthotopic kidney, ovarian, breast, and glioblastoma models, showing sustained tumor control and reduced exhaustion markers. | YBX1 phosphorylation uniquely sustained translation efficiency in 1A CAR T cells, linking enhanced ribosomal biogenesis to prolonged effector function. | Epigenetic silencing (EZH2‑mediated H3K27me3) of CD70 in kidney‑site tumors creates a microenvironment‑dependent antigen loss that is overcome by the heightened sensitivity of HIT CARs.
primary_population: Patients with refractory DLBCL receiving 25 M 1XX CAR T cells

talk_title: Evolution of PARP inhibitors and lessons for synthetic lethality development
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; breast cancer; prostate cancer; pancreatic cancer; NSCLC; colorectal cancer
topic_categories: immunotherapy; cancer_vaccines; DNA_damage_repair; targeted_therapy; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: PARP inhibitors trap PARP1 on DNA, preventing repair of single-strand breaks that convert to double-strand breaks during replication; in HR-deficient tumors (e.g., BRCA1/2 loss) this leads to lethal genomic instability, while normal cells retain homologous recombination capacity, providing a therapeutic window. Trapping potency, dose intensity, and duration of target inhibition dictate clinical efficacy.
key_findings: PARP1 trapping, not just catalytic inhibition, is essential for monotherapy activity; talazoparib and sarucaparib show stronger trapping and greater efficacy than rucaparib. | Complete (>90%) PARP inhibition is required for tumor regression; sub‑therapeutic dosing leads to tumor escape and resistance. | BRCA1/2 mutation type influences response; hypomorphic C61G BRCA1 mutants confer reduced sensitivity compared to null alleles. | Acquired resistance is driven by HR re‑activation, evidenced by RAD51 foci re‑appearance in >90% of resistant models. | Dose‑intensity correlates with long‑term tumor control; continuous high‑dose olaparib prevents escape, whereas intermittent dosing increases residual tumor burden.

talk_title: Targeting ARID1A/ARID1B synthetic lethality and SMARCA2/SMARCA4 paralog dependencies
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; lung cancer; endometrial cancer
moa_summary: Selective degradation of ARID1B via VHL or cereblon-recruited PROTACs eliminates the compensatory paralog in ARID1A-deficient cancers, leading to loss of BAF complex function and tumor cell death, while sparing normal cells that retain ARID1A. Similarly, inhibition of SMARCA2 in SMARCA4-mutant tumors exploits paralog synthetic lethality, causing chromatin remodeling failure and tumor regression, with enhanced anti‑tumor immunity when combined with PD‑1 blockade.
key_findings: ARID1B degraders achieve nanomolar potency and >10‑fold selectivity over ARID1A in biochemical and cellular assays. | Cereblon‑based ARID1B degraders show superior potency and no off‑target neo‑substrate activity across the proteome. | Ternary complex crystal structures reveal a ligand twist that enhances cooperativity, informing SAR for both VHL and CRBN series. | SMARCA2 inhibitor LY4050784 (909) combined with anti‑PD‑1 yields 6/6 complete responses in a BRG1‑knockout syngeneic mouse model and induces durable immune memory. | Combination of SMARCA2 inhibition with PD‑1 blockade is well tolerated (no weight loss) and produces sustained tumor control.

talk_title: Quantitative molecular imaging to enable personalized radiopharmaceutical therapy
affiliation: Fred Hutch Cancer Center, Department of Nuclear Medicine and Radiology
topic_categories: radiopharmaceuticals; tumor_microenvironment; biomarker_development; dosimetry; combination_therapy; translational
moa_summary: Quantitative PET/SPECT imaging of SSTR and PSMA ligands enables patient selection and dosimetry for beta‑emitting radiopharmaceuticals; higher tumor uptake (SUV) predicts better outcomes, while FDG metabolic burden inversely correlates with response, supporting personalized dosing and treatment planning.
key_findings: Post‑hoc NETTER‑1/2 analyses showed similar survival benefit across low, intermediate, and high somatostatin receptor uptake levels. | VISION trial post‑hoc quartile analysis demonstrated that patients in the highest PSMA PET SUV quartile had the best radiographic PFS and OS. | Low FDG metabolic tumor volume predicted superior response to lutetium‑PSMA therapy, independent of PSMA uptake intensity. | Single‑time‑point dosimetry at ~24 h can estimate tumor absorbed dose, but lesion doses decrease with successive cycles.

talk_title: Clinical advances and opportunities in prostate cancer using alpha and beta emitting radiopharmaceuticals
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Radiation Oncology
topic_categories: radiopharmaceuticals; clinical_trial; alpha_therapy; beta_therapy; combination_therapy; immunotherapy
moa_summary: Beta‑emitting lutetium‑177 PSMA‑617 delivers cross‑fire radiation to PSMA‑positive prostate cancer lesions, improving OS and PFS; alpha‑emitting actinium‑225 PSMA provides high‑LET, short‑range DNA double‑strand breaks, achieving deep PSA responses but with high salivary gland toxicity. Combination strategies aim to exploit complementary mechanisms and overcome resistance.
key_findings: VISION trial showed a 4‑month OS benefit (median 11 mo vs 7 mo) for lutetium‑177 PSMA‑617 in APMR. | Approximately 30% of patients were non‑responders, linked to low PSMA uptake, FDG‑PSMA discordance, or TP53 mutations. | Actinium‑225 PSMA therapy yielded median OS 16 mo in heavily pretreated patients, with 70% experiencing xerostomia after one cycle. | Early‑line trials (PSMA‑4, PSMA‑ADDITION) improve radiographic PFS but have not yet shown OS advantage, likely due to crossover. | Alpha emitters may overcome radioresistance in high‑grade disease and micrometastases, while beta emitters are suited for bulk disease.
primary_population: heavily pretreated APMR receiving actinium‑225 PSMA

talk_title: Preclinical and translational strategies to combine radiopharmaceuticals with other cancer therapies
affiliation: University of Wisconsin, Department of Radiation Medicine
tumor_types: head and neck cancer; medulloblastoma; solid tumors (various)
topic_categories: radiopharmaceuticals; combination_therapy; immunotherapy; external_beam_radiation; dosimetry; preclinical
moa_summary: NM‑600 incorporates into tumor cell membranes, delivering Y‑90 beta radiation; when combined with checkpoint blockade, sub‑maximal tumor doses preserve immune cells and enhance systemic anti‑tumor immunity. Astatine‑211 PARP‑targeted alpha therapy induces DNA double‑strand breaks and up‑regulates PARP1, creating a positive feedback loop that increases tumor uptake and promotes immune priming.
key_findings: Personalized dosimetry using NM‑600 allowed subtraction of radiopharmaceutical dose from external beam plans, enabling safe delivery of combined therapy in head‑and‑neck cancer. | Low‑to‑moderate Y‑90 NM‑600 doses synergized with anti‑CTLA‑4, enhancing local and systemic tumor control and generating immune memory. | Alpha‑emitting actinium showed superior immune priming (increased TCR clonality) in poorly immunogenic tumors compared with beta emitters. | Heterogeneous intratumoral dose distribution (mixed high/low uptake) activated multiple immune pathways (type‑I IFN, MHC up‑regulation) and improved survival in mouse models, an effect lost with homogeneous dosing.

talk_title: Non-enzymatic HDAC-mediated repression reveals selective stress adaptive mechanisms for cancer therapy
moa_summary: This talk describes the discovery and characterization of E6R, a non-enzymatic inhibitor of the Sin3-Rpd3L (HDAC1/2) complex in yeast and neuroblastoma cells. Unlike traditional HDAC inhibitors, E6R disrupts protein-protein interfaces, leading to a distinct transcriptomic signature. In neuroblastoma, E6R induces cell cycle arrest via p53/E2F signaling and integrated stress response, achieving therapeutic effects without the broad cytotoxicity of enzymatic HDAC inhibitors.
key_findings: E6R is a non-enzymatic inhibitor of the Sin3-Rpd3L complex, identified through a yeast-based reporter assay screening 52,000 compounds. | E6R exhibits a distinct transcriptomic signature compared to enzymatic HDAC inhibitors (TSA) and complex knockouts (Rpd3, Sin3). | In neuroblastoma cells, E6R reduces invasion without significant cytotoxicity, unlike TSA. | E6R affects 14x fewer genes than TSA in neuroblastoma cells, indicating higher transcriptional selectivity. | E6R induces cell cycle arrest via p53/E2F signaling and repression of G2M checkpoint genes. | E6R specifically activates integrated stress response and pro-apoptotic pathways, including ATF4 targets like DDIT3 and GDF15. | Single-cell RNA sequencing shows E6R treatment mirrors DMSO control in cell state composition.

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader of the ARNT Protein
moa_summary: NEO811 is a potent and selective molecular glue degrader of ARNT (HIF1 beta) for clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). ARNT is central to HIF signaling, angiogenesis, proliferation, and immune suppression. NEO811 demonstrates dose-dependent tumor growth inhibition as a single agent, shows synergistic effects with belzutifan, and retains activity in models of acquired and intrinsic resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors, supporting its clinical development.
key_findings: ARNT knockdown in A498 cells leads to tumor growth regression, validating ARNT as a target in ccRCC. | NEO811 is a potent ARNT degrader with low nanomolar activity, dependent on cereblon, and shows a favorable selectivity window. | NEO811 demonstrates dose-dependent tumor growth inhibition in the 7860 xenograft model, with >50% regression at 3 mg/kg. | NEO811 shows synergistic tumor growth regression when combined with belzutifan. | NEO811 retains activity in a model of acquired resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors (G323E mutation). | NEO811 shows activity in the XP490 PDX model, which is intrinsically resistant to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors. | NEO811 is orally bioavailable and well-tolerated in preclinical studies.

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader Targeting the CCNE1 Protein
tumor_types: ovarian cancer; endometrial cancer; gastric cancer; breast cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: MRT55811 is a first-in-class molecular glue degrader targeting CCNE1, an oncogene amplified in various cancers. It induces co-degradation of CCNE1 and CDK2, leading to Rb dephosphorylation, cell cycle arrest, and anti-proliferative effects specifically in CCNE1-amplified cancers. This mechanism differentiates it from CDK2 inhibitors and suggests a favorable therapeutic window.
key_findings: MRT55811 is a potent and selective CCNE1 degrader, exclusively active in CCNE1 amplified cell lines. | MRT55811 induces co-degradation of CCNE1 and CDK2, leading to Rb dephosphorylation and S-phase arrest. | The degrader forms a quaternary complex involving CRBN, glue, CCNE1, and CDK2. | MRT55811 shows potent anti-proliferative activity in CCNE1 amplified cell lines, with GI50s in the nanomolar range. | MRT55811 demonstrates tumor regression in xenograft models of CCNE1 amplified gastric, ovarian, and breast cancers. | MRT55811 shows an edge over CDK2 inhibitors in preclinical models. | The compound is well-tolerated in preclinical studies.

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader of the RNA Binding Protein HuR
moa_summary: Degron Therapeutics has developed DG6498, a potent and selective molecular glue degrader of the RNA-binding protein HuR, targeting BRAF-mutant colorectal cancer. HuR is an undruggable oncogenic driver. DG6498 induces CRBN-dependent degradation of HuR, showing efficacy in BRAF-mutant CRC models and overcoming resistance to BRAF inhibitors. The compound has favorable PK/PD properties and is currently in Phase 1 clinical development.
key_findings: A molecular glue degrader of HuR was identified through proteomic screening of a proprietary compound library. | HUR degrader two exhibits potent HuR degradation (DC50 = 3.8 nM, 96% max degradation) dependent on CRBN and the proteasome. | Cryo-EM structure reveals the ternary complex formation between CRBN, MGD, and HuR. | HuR dependency is strongly correlated with BRAF mutation status, making BRAF-mutant CRC a key indication. | The HuR degrader selectively reduces cell viability in BRAF-mutant CRC cell lines. | The HuR degrader overcomes resistance to dabrafenib in BRAF-mutant CRC models. | DG6498, a lead compound, shows robust and sustained HuR degradation and favorable PK/ADME properties. | DG6498 is currently in Phase 1 clinical development for BRAF-mutant CRC and other solid tumors.

talk_title: BBO10203, a RAS PI3 Breaker, Inhibits Phospho-AKT Signaling in HER2-Amplified Models Through Inhibition of Non-Canonical RAS Signaling
tumor_types: esophageal cancer; lung cancer; colorectal cancer; pancreatic cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: BBO10203 is a covalent inhibitor that breaks the RAS-PI3K interaction, specifically targeting HER2-amplified cancers. It potently suppresses phospho-AKT signaling by inhibiting non-canonical RAS isoforms (RS2) and canonical RAS signaling. BBO10203 demonstrates strong monotherapy efficacy and combination potential with HER2-targeted agents, offering a new therapeutic strategy for HER2-amplified tumors.
key_findings: HER2-amplified cell lines are particularly sensitive to disruption of the RAS-PI3K interaction by BBO10203. | BBO10203 achieves potent single-digit nanomolar suppression of phospho-AKT in HER2-amplified lines. | Non-canonical RAS isoforms, particularly RS2, play a significant role in driving phospho-AKT signaling in HER2-amplified cells. | BBO10203 inhibits both non-canonical and canonical RAS signaling pathways. | BBO10203 shows strong monotherapy tumor regression in HER2-amplified KYSC esophageal CDX model. | BBO10203 demonstrates strong combination potential with HER2-targeted agents like tucatinib and trastuzumab. | BBO10203 outperforms tucatinib as a single agent in HER2-amplified N87 CDX model.

talk_title: Catalytic Inhibitors of the KAT2A/B Protein in Multiple Myeloma
moa_summary: Pfizer has developed KAT2I, a first-in-class catalytic inhibitor of KAT2A/B, targeting multiple myeloma. KAT2A/B are key components of the SAGA complex, driving oncogenic transcription. KAT2I treatment leads to downregulation of MYC and E2F targets, and upregulates interferon responses, sensitizing myeloma cells to immune killing. The compound shows robust efficacy as monotherapy and in combination with standard of care agents and T-cell engagers.
key_findings: Multiple myeloma cells show a strong dependency on the SAGA complex and KAT2A/B. | KAT2I is a potent, selective, and orally bioavailable catalytic inhibitor of KAT2A/B. | KAT2I treatment leads to dose-dependent reduction of H3K9 acetylation, mimicking genetic loss of KAT2A/B. | KAT2I shows broad activity across a panel of multiple myeloma cell lines. | KAT2I downregulates MYC and E2F target genes, contributing to tumor-intrinsic response. | KAT2I upregulates interferon signatures and ISGs, potentially sensitizing tumors to immune cell killing. | KAT2I demonstrates synergistic activity with lenalidomide and pomalidomide in vitro. | Combination of KAT2I with pomalidomide and dexamethasone shows benefit in the OPM2 xenograft model. | KAT2I enhances the efficacy of a BCMA CD3 bispecific antibody in a multiple myeloma syngeneic model.

talk_title: Discovery of a New Class of Mutant-Targeted Catalytic RAS Oncogene Inhibitors
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; lung cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: RMO-55 is a novel catalytic RAS inhibitor that mimics natural GAPs by stimulating GTP hydrolysis of oncogenic RAS G12 mutants, restoring them to the GDP state. This mechanism leads to potent inhibition of RAS signaling and cell proliferation, with a wider therapeutic window than steric inhibitors. RMO-55 demonstrates deep and durable tumor regressions in preclinical models and shows activity in settings of acquired resistance, supporting its clinical development.
key_findings: RMO-55 is a new class of mutant-targeted catalytic RAS inhibitor that stimulates GTP hydrolysis of G12 mutant RAS. | RMO-55 produces rapid and near-complete reduction in G12V GTP levels biochemically. | RMO-55 causes potent and near complete reduction in cellular RAS GTP levels and phospho-ERK. | RMO-55 robustly inhibits proliferation in G12 mutant cell lines, with greater efficacy than deraxasip. | RMO-55 shows a reduced effect on wild-type cell lines compared to deraxasip. | RMO-55 demonstrates potent inhibition of tumor RAS pathway signaling with significant selectivity over normal tissue. | RMO-55 drives deep and durable tumor regressions in G12D pancreatic, G12C NSCLC, and G12V colorectal models. | RMO-55 shows activity in KRAS amplified models and overcomes resistance to deraxasip. | RMO-55 restores tumor control in tumors that have acquired resistance to RAS on inhibitors.

talk_title: CQ0736 polysaccharide‑based KRAS‑targeted therapy via macropinocytosis
moa_summary: CQ0736 is a dextran‑based conjugate bearing docetaxel and gamma‑linolenic acid that exploits enhanced macropinocytosis in KRAS‑driven tumors for selective tumor accumulation; the conjugate releases docetaxel intracellularly, inducing apoptosis while avoiding P‑gp efflux, resulting in durable tumor regression across KRAS G12C, G12D and wild‑type models.
key_findings: CQ0736 achieved sustained intratumoral docetaxel exposure for 96 h versus free DTX. | In KRAS G12C lung CDX, weekly CQ0736 reduced tumor volume 3.6‑fold vs RMC and 5.2‑fold vs adagrasib at day 28. | CQ0736 showed durable tumor control without resistance in KRAS G12C PDX (16.5‑fold reduction vs RMC at day 49). | No significant body‑weight loss or hematologic toxicity observed across CDX/PDX studies.

talk_title: Orally bioavailable small‑molecule MYC translation inhibitors (WDC‑623)
moa_summary: WDC‑623 binds to a regulatory element in the MYC 5'‑UTR, stabilizing an inhibitory RNA structure that blocks translation initiation; rapid loss of MYC protein (half‑life ~30 min) leads to selective repression of MYC‑driven transcriptional programs while sparing non‑MYC short‑half‑life proteins.
key_findings: Structure‑guided hit‑to‑lead improved potency >100‑fold to single‑digit nM (WDC‑623). | WDC‑623 reduced c‑MYC protein within 30 min without affecting MYC mRNA levels. | Proteomics showed c‑MYC and its direct targets CDC25A/B were the most repressed proteins after 2 h treatment. | Oral BID dosing (5 mg/kg) produced significant tumor growth inhibition in SCLC CDX and 125 % TGI in PDAC xenograft with no weight loss. | Compound activity is nutrient‑dependent; high nutrient levels (normal plasma) blunt MYC repression, suggesting tumor‑selective activity.
primary_value: significant inhibition at 5 mg/kg BID oral

talk_title: Intramolecular glue Cbl‑B inhibitors (NX‑1607) advancing to Phase 1
tumor_types: colorectal cancer; triple‑negative breast cancer; B‑cell lymphoma
moa_summary: NX‑1607 binds to the closed conformation pocket at the interface of Cbl‑B’s three domains, acting as an intramolecular glue that locks Cbl‑B in an inactive state, thereby releasing the brake on T‑cell and NK‑cell activation; the resulting immune activation synergizes with anti‑PD‑1 therapy to produce tumor regression.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 250 k compounds yielded a single Cbl‑B hit (NRX‑001) with 30 µM potency. | Enantiomeric resolution identified the active S‑enantiomer; crystal structure revealed binding to a buried pocket near Tyr363. | Medicinal chemistry optimization produced NX‑1607 with sub‑nanomolar Kd (0.4 nM) and excellent oral bioavailability. | NX‑1607 monotherapy achieved tumor regression at 10–30 mg/kg daily in colorectal, TNBC, and B‑cell lymphoma models. | Combination with anti‑PD‑1 showed synergistic survival benefit across multiple syngeneic models.
primary_value: robust efficacy at 10–30 mg/kg daily in mouse models
primary_population: CT26, TC1, TNBC, B‑cell lymphoma models

talk_title: NEO811, a first‑in‑class ARNT molecular glue degrader for clear cell RCC
moa_summary: NEO811 binds cereblon and creates a neo‑substrate interface that recruits ARNT, inducing a conformational rearrangement that enables ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation; loss of ARNT disrupts HIF‑α heterodimer transcription, leading to potent anti‑tumor activity in VHL‑deficient RCC.
key_findings: Structure‑guided design converted initial hit (compound A) into selective ARNT degrader (compound C) with reduced off‑target cereblon neo‑substrate degradation. | NEO811 demonstrated >80 % ARNT degradation at low nanomolar concentrations in cellular assays. | In vivo, once‑daily oral dosing for 21 days produced marked tumor regression in ccRCC xenografts with good tolerability. | NEO811 showed superior potency to FDA‑approved HIF‑2α inhibitor belzutifan in HIF transcriptional inhibition assays.
primary_value: significant regression after 21 days daily dosing

talk_title: DNA‑templated spatially controlled degraders (DTAG) for CDK4/6‑Cyclin D1
moa_summary: DTAG‑v1 uses a 20‑bp double‑stranded DNA scaffold to position a CDK4/6 inhibitor and an E3 ligase recruiter at defined distances; the optimal geometry (ligand on major groove) enables formation of a productive ternary complex that simultaneously degrades CDK4, CDK6 and the undruggable Cyclin D1, demonstrating that spatial programming via DNA can control target selectivity.
key_findings: Four DNA‑templated PROTAC geometries showed distinct degradation profiles; version 1 (major‑groove placement) degraded CDK4, CDK6 and Cyclin D1. | Molecular dynamics revealed that productive ternary complexes correlated with negative ΔG values and close proximity of CDK4/6 to the E3 ligase. | In vivo, DTAG‑v1 achieved robust tumor regression in U251 xenografts with no significant body‑weight loss. | The platform is modular: swapping the CDK4/6 ligand for CDK9 or CDK2 inhibitors generated degraders for other cyclin‑CDK complexes without re‑synthesizing the DNA scaffold.

talk_title: BH‑501284, a pseudo‑irreversible pan‑KRAS switch‑II inhibitor
tumor_types: non‑small cell lung cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: BH‑501284 binds the KRAS switch‑II pocket in both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound states with picomolar affinity and a residence time >54 h, rigidifying KRAS and preventing RAF interaction; the pseudo‑irreversible kinetic profile yields sustained pathway inhibition after drug washout.
key_findings: SPR showed picomolar Kd and >54 h residence time on GDP‑bound KRAS G12D, outperforming AMG 410 (1.4 h). | Washout experiments demonstrated >90 % p‑ERK inhibition retained 24 h after removal, comparable to covalent G12C inhibitor. | Potent activity across KRAS G12V, G12D, G12C (IC50 <3 nM) and KRAS‑amplified lines (IC50 ~1.5 nM); inactive on NRAS/HRAS mutants. | Oral BID dosing as low as 5 mg/kg achieved deep tumor regressions in multiple KRAS mutant CDX models. | Only ~2‑fold shift in IC50 when EGFR ligand added, and synergistic with cetuximab in long‑term growth assays.
primary_population: KRAS mutant solid tumor cell lines

talk_title: Proteomics‑guided discovery of TRIM21‑mediated multimeric degradation (ACE compounds)
moa_summary: ACE compounds act as molecular glues that convert the E3 ligase TRIM21 into a degrader of nuclear pore complex proteins; binding is NUP98‑dependent, leading to ordered degradation of inner‑ring NPC subunits followed by outer‑ring components, demonstrating that multimeric assembly state governs TRIM21‑mediated proteasomal clearance.
key_findings: Phenotypic screen identified three ACE compounds that cause IFN‑γ‑dependent cell death. | CRISPR screen highlighted TRIM21 as essential for ACE activity. | Proteomics revealed selective degradation of inner‑ring NPC proteins, reversible by proteasome inhibition. | Time‑course proteomics showed hierarchical degradation: inner‑ring NPC subunits degraded within 4 h, outer‑ring later, nuclear basket unchanged at 12 h. | BioID proximity labeling demonstrated ACE‑dependent recruitment of TRIM21 to NPC complexes via NUP98; NUP98 knockout abolished recruitment.

talk_title: MUTA tetravalent antibody platform for next-generation ADCs
moa_summary: The MUTA platform creates a tetravalent, multi-epitope antibody that simultaneously binds two tumor antigens (e.g., MUC16 and NaPi2b) to improve internalization, efficacy, and safety of ADCs, with site-specific conjugation and a hydrophilic linker to enhance plasma stability and therapeutic window.
key_findings: MUDA tetravalent ADCs outperformed monospecific ADCs in lung, colon, head and neck xenograft models, showing greater tumor regression. | PK in monkeys demonstrated stable plasma exposure up to 9 mg/kg with reversible reticulocyte decrease only at the highest dose, indicating an improved safety margin. | AquaLink hydrophilic linker maintained ADC stability without increasing hydrophobicity, improving plasma stability and bystander activity.

talk_title: High-throughput drug screening and single-cell network analysis for IDH mutant glioma
moa_summary: Single-cell regulatory network analysis identifies state-specific master regulator programs in IDH mutant glioma; high-throughput drug screening (OncoTreat) predicts compounds that invert these programs (e.g., cisplatin for AC-like, DS‑651b for NPC-like, micofenolate for OC-like), enabling rational combination strategies validated in patient-derived slice explants.
key_findings: Three conserved malignant cell states (AC-like, NPC-like, OC-like) were defined across oligodendroglioma and astrocytoma with distinct master regulator programs. | OncoTreat identified state-specific drug vulnerabilities: DNA damage repair inhibitors for AC-like, MAPK pathway inhibitors for NPC-like, and purine synthesis inhibitors for OC-like. | Preliminary ex vivo slice experiments suggest serum biomarkers can detect early state shifts, supporting translational potential.

talk_title: Serum hyaluronic acid as a predictive biomarker for T‑DXD‑induced interstitial lung disease
moa_summary: Age‑dependent accumulation of T‑DXD in adult lungs leads to alveolar macrophage activation, endothelial HYAL2 up‑regulation, extracellular matrix remodeling and release of hyaluronic acid fragments into serum, which serve as an early non‑invasive biomarker for ADC‑induced interstitial lung disease.
key_findings: Adult mice develop T‑DXD‑induced ILD (40%) whereas young mice are protected (0%). | Adult lungs show higher T‑DXD accumulation, increased mature alveolar macrophages, elevated CTSB and SPP1 expression, and endothelial HYAL2 up‑regulation. | Serum HA levels rise significantly in adult mice with ILD and predict ILD in a pilot clinical cohort (AUC 74% alone, 93% with fibrosis panel). | Early HA elevation precedes radiographic ILD, suggesting utility for pre‑emptive monitoring.
primary_value: 0% in young mice vs 40% in adult mice

talk_title: Co‑clinical trial of datopotamab deruxtecan in patient‑derived xenografts
affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Investigational Cancer Therapeutics
tumor_types: breast cancer; head and neck SCC; bladder carcinoma; pancreatic adenocarcinoma
moa_summary: Datopotamab deruxtecan (ADC targeting Trop‑2) delivers a topoisomerase‑1 inhibitor payload; co‑clinical PDX models recapitulate patient responses and reveal synergistic antitumor activity when combined with gemcitabine, likely via enhanced DNA damage in Trop‑2‑positive tumors.
key_findings: Dato induced tumor regression and prolonged time to doubling across multiple PDX models, mirroring patient outcomes (78.6% concordance). | Gemcitabine synergized with Dato in PDXs derived from patients with progressive disease, leading to greater tumor control than either agent alone. | Biopsy take rate was 47% overall, demonstrating feasibility of generating co‑clinical PDXs from basket trial specimens.

talk_title: Inducer‑amplifier strategy targeting p53‑mutant cancers
tumor_types: pancreatic cancer; colon cancer; breast cancer
moa_summary: A two‑step inducer‑amplifier approach: TAS‑102 induces DNA damage that accumulates in p53‑mutant cells; PARP inhibition blocks single‑strand break repair, enhancing damage; subsequent WEE1 inhibition forces G2‑arrested cells into mitotic catastrophe, achieving selective tumor cell death with low toxicity.
key_findings: TAS‑102 plus PARP inhibitor synergistically kills p53‑mutant cells in vitro and reduces tumor growth in PDX models. | Adding WEE1 inhibitor after the inducer‑amplifier increases cell death from 30% to ~80% specifically in p53‑mutant cells. | The sequential regimen is well tolerated in preclinical models and shows early signs of efficacy in a phase‑1 trial.

talk_title: KBD‑1111: a selective, long‑acting, brain‑penetrant PARP‑1 inhibitor
moa_summary: KBD‑1111 is a highly selective PARP‑1 inhibitor (9,000‑fold over PARP‑2) that traps PARP‑1, exhibits long half‑life, penetrates the CNS (CSF AUC 38.6 ng·h/mL, Kpu 0.26), and demonstrates potent antitumor activity with reduced hematologic toxicity, supporting Q2W dosing.
key_findings: KBD‑1111 shows IC50 0.36 nM against PARP‑1 and >9,000‑fold selectivity over PARP‑2. | In BRCA‑deficient xenografts, 10 mg/kg Q2W achieved near‑complete tumor inhibition, superior to AZD5305. | Brain penetration (Kpu 0.26) enabled tumor regression in intracranial models with weekly dosing. | Hematologic toxicity was markedly reduced compared to olaparib, especially with QW dosing.
primary_value: near complete at 10 mg/kg Q2W in DLD1 xenograft

talk_title: Comparative analysis of TEAD palmitoylation inhibitor vs PPI in KRAS‑mutant cancers
moa_summary: TEAD central‑pocket palmitoylation inhibitors (CPI) block TEAD palmitoylation, while TEAD‑YAP/TAZ PPI inhibitors directly disrupt the protein‑protein interaction; both suppress TEAD‑driven transcription, but PPI achieves greater YAP eviction and target gene repression, leading to more durable synergy with KRAS inhibitors in vitro, though in vivo both modalities show comparable tumor control.
key_findings: PPI (IAG 933) combined with KRAS G12C inhibitor delayed resistance and resensitized resistant cells more effectively than CPI (AZD0751) in vitro. | Both TEAD inhibitors suppressed KRAS‑induced TEAD target gene up‑regulation, but PPI achieved greater YAP chromatin eviction. | In NSCLC xenografts, both CPI and PPI enhanced tumor regression with KRAS inhibition, but no additive benefit was observed in KRAS G12D pancreatic models.

talk_title: Targeting RAS and RAF in MSS Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
moa_summary: Encouraging BRAF V600E inhibition in MSS colorectal cancer requires concurrent EGFR blockade to prevent feedback re‑activation of MAPK signaling; adding chemotherapy (FOLFOX) further suppresses emergent resistant clones and reduces ctDNA‑detectable resistance mutations, leading to higher response rates and prolonged PFS/OS. Parallel KRAS allele‑specific inhibitors (G12C, G12D) show modest activity alone, which is markedly enhanced when combined with EGFR antibodies, reflecting the same feedback loop. Emerging degraders and pan‑RAS approaches aim to overcome subclonal resistance mechanisms such as MET amplification, KRAS/NRAS secondary mutations, and YAP/TEAD activation.
key_findings: BreakWater first‑line BRAF V600E + EGFR inhibition plus FOLFOX achieved ORR 67% vs 37% with standard chemotherapy (HR for PFS 0.53, HR for OS 0.49). | Median OS doubled from 15.1 months (control) to 30.3 months with EC+FOLFOX in BRAF V600E MSS colorectal cancer. | ctDNA clearance of the BRAF V600E clone at 6 weeks increased from 35% (control) to 45% (EC) and 67% (EC+FOLFOX), correlating with improved outcomes. | Acquired resistance mutations (KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1, MET amplification) were subclonal and more frequent after EC alone than after EC+FOLFOX. | KRAS G12C inhibitor adagrasib combined with cetuximab yielded ORR 34% and PFS 6.9 months; elacestrant monotherapy gave ORR 47% (up to 62% with EGFR inhibitor). | High baseline cell‑cycle signaling predicts emergence of secondary resistance mutations; low cytolytic score predicts greater benefit from BRAF‑EGFR therapy. | Pre‑clinical data show cross‑resistance between prior chemotherapy and BRAF‑EGFR therapy, supporting first‑line use of the combination.
primary_population: BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line

talk_title: Epidemiology and clinical impact of chemosensory dysfunction in cancer patients
affiliation: Monell Chemical Senses Center; University of Pennsylvania Department of Otolaryngology
moa_summary: Epidemiologic analyses of large population cohorts reveal that chemosensory dysfunction is highly prevalent in cancer patients (≈60% smell, 93% taste) and is associated with substantial reductions in quality of life, increased frailty, malnutrition, and higher risk of comorbidities such as heart failure and cognitive decline.
key_findings: Approximately 60% of cancer patients report smell dysfunction and 93% report taste dysfunction. | NHANES data show 14% prevalence of taste dysfunction, 22% smell dysfunction, and 10% both in the general US adult population. | Smell dysfunction reduces odds of a satisfying life by 40%; taste dysfunction reduces it by 60% (NHANES). | During chemotherapy cycles, taste dysfunction peaks at days 4‑7 and improves by the end of the cycle. | Clinician awareness is low: only ~15% can distinguish taste from flavor, yet 44% of clinicians consider taste changes very important to patients.

talk_title: Molecular mechanisms of targeted therapy‑induced taste dysfunction and development of taste organoid platforms
affiliation: University of Colorado, Taste & Smell Center
moa_summary: Targeted cancer therapies disrupt taste homeostasis via distinct pathways: Wnt/β‑catenin activation skews progenitor output toward type I cells and reduces sweet perception; Hedgehog inhibition blocks progenitor differentiation causing loss of taste buds; KIT inhibition by anti‑angiogenic TKIs impairs sweet cell fate and promotes bitter differentiation, leading to dysgeusia.
key_findings: Wnt/β‑catenin signaling is required for taste progenitor proliferation; hyperactivation increases type I cells and modestly type II cells without affecting sour cells. | Loss of Wnt signaling leads to progressive loss of taste buds, especially in anterior tongue, correlating with reduced sweet behavior in mice. | Smoothened antagonism (Hedgehog inhibition) eliminates taste buds and diminishes gustatory nerve responses, but does not affect progenitor proliferation. | Overexpression of SHH drives excess taste bud formation, indicating Hedgehog promotes differentiation rather than proliferation. | Cabozantinib treatment reduces sweet‑cell markers and increases bitter‑cell markers in mouse taste organoids and in vivo, phenocopying KIT deletion in type II precursors. | Human circumvallate papillae can be harvested from organ donors and cultured into organoids containing all taste cell types, establishing a platform for translational testing.

talk_title: Implementation of the Genie Data Model at Providence Cancer Institute
moa_summary: The GP3 pipeline programmatically extracts source attributes from Providence's Snowflake clinical data warehouse, maps them to the Genie Data Model (GDM) via SQL transformations, validates and stages the data, and exports it for downstream use, dramatically reducing manual abstraction time and improving data quality.
key_findings: Programmatic extraction reduced case abstraction time from ~8 hours per case to minutes for thousands of patients. | Automated schema validation and record-level consistency checks identified implausible values (e.g., out-of-range weights) for rapid correction. | Lineage parsing of SQL code automatically documented source provenance for each GDM attribute, facilitating reproducibility. | AI-assisted coding (GitHub Copilot) accelerated development of mapping scripts and improved code quality. | The pipeline achieved near‑real‑time generation of GDM‑aligned tables, enabling rapid data sharing across sites.

talk_title: Senescent fibroblast SASP drives chemotherapy‑induced peripheral neuropathy and can be reversed by senolytics or MK2 inhibition
moa_summary: Chemotherapy (paclitaxel) induces p16‑positive senescent fibroblasts in peripheral skin; these cells secrete a SASP regulated by MK2 kinase that causes epidermal denervation and behavioral neuropathy. Genetic ablation of p16‑positive cells or pharmacologic senolysis (dasatinib + quercetin) or MK2 inhibition eliminates the SASP, restores innervation and reverses numbness/pain in mice.
key_findings: Paclitaxel treatment increased p16 mRNA and protein in peripheral skin but not in dorsal root ganglia or sciatic nerve. | Senescent fibroblasts (p16‑positive, SA‑β‑gal+) accumulated in the skin and were eliminated by inducible suicide gene activation. | Elimination of senescent cells prevented paclitaxel‑induced epidermal denervation and restored normal Von Frey thresholds. | Senolytic treatment (dasatinib + quercetin) after neuropathy onset re‑innervated epidermis and normalized paw thresholds, reversing pain. | Fibroblast‑specific MK2 knockout abolished the neuropathic phenotype despite persistence of senescent cells, implicating MK2‑driven SASP as the effector. | Human skin biopsies from three CIPN patients showed increased p16 and senescent fibroblast markers, mirroring mouse findings.

talk_title: Long‑term morbidity and mortality in childhood cancer survivors: prevalence, frailty, and socioeconomic impacts
moa_summary: Large‑scale survivorship cohort analyses reveal that treatment‑related exposures (radiation, alkylators, surgery) and socioeconomic deprivation synergistically drive multimorbidity, frailty, and excess mortality in adult survivors of childhood cancer, with genetic predisposition (pathogenic germline variants) further increasing risk of subsequent neoplasms.
key_findings: By age 40, childhood cancer survivors have a 25% excess mortality relative to the US population. | More than 90% of survivors develop at least one chronic health condition; 85% of those are serious, disabling, or life‑threatening. | Survivors accrue an average of 17 chronic conditions by age 50, including ~5 severe conditions. | Frailty prevalence in survivors aged 18‑50 matches that of general adults >65 yr; frailty confers a 2.6‑fold higher risk of early death. | Whole‑genome sequencing identified pathogenic germline variants in 6% of survivors, associated with increased risk of second neoplasms. | Financial hardship is pervasive: 22% report material hardship, 51% psychological hardship, and 33% behavioral hardship, correlating with higher anxiety, depression, and suicidal ideation.
primary_value: ≈25% by age 40 compared with US population

talk_title: Functional significance of YTHDF m6A readers in AML and multiple myeloma
affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, London, Hemato‑Oncology
moa_summary: YTHDF2 and YTHDF3 are cytosolic m6A readers that promote decay of methylated transcripts such as TNFRSF1B; genetic deletion stabilizes these transcripts, leading to heightened TNF‑mediated apoptosis and loss of leukemic stem cell activity in AML. Parallel loss of YTHDF3 impairs ribosomal biogenesis and translation of M6A‑modified mRNAs, reducing leukemic proliferation and sensitizing cells to cytarabine. Normal hematopoiesis remains largely intact, indicating a therapeutic window for targeting these readers.
key_findings: YTHDF2 is overexpressed in AML across sub‑genetic subtypes and correlates with leukemic stem cell activity. | YTHDF2 knockout or knockdown abolishes colony formation of AML cells in vitro and markedly extends latency in mouse transplantation models. | Loss of YTHDF2 stabilizes TNFRSF1B mRNA, increasing surface TNFR2 and rendering AML cells highly sensitive to exogenous TNF (apoptosis observed). | YTHDF3 deletion reduces leukemic stem cell frequency in limiting‑dilution assays and impairs engraftment of THP‑1 cells in liver PDX models. | YTHDF3 knockdown sensitizes AML cells to Ara‑C, decreasing the Ara‑C IC50 (exact values not disclosed). | Inducible YTHDF2 tryptophan‑to‑alanine (WA) mutant recapitulates knockout phenotypes, confirming the M6A‑binding pocket is essential for function. | A prototype small‑molecule YTHDF inhibitor (IC50 ≈ 11 µM) suppresses AML colony formation in a dose‑dependent manner, with partial rescue by YTHDF knockdown.

talk_title: YTHDF2 and YTHDC1 in tumor immunity and chromatin regulation of plastic cancers
affiliation: University of Chicago, Department of Chemistry, Howard Hughes Medical Investigator
tumor_types: neuroendocrine prostate cancer; small cell lung cancer; acute myeloid leukemia
topic_categories: tumor_microenvironment; immunotherapy; epigenetics; basic_science; translational; platform_technology
moa_summary: YTHDF2 promotes decay of M6A‑modified transcripts in myeloid‑derived suppressor cells, enhancing their immunosuppressive function after radiation; its loss stabilizes anti‑inflammatory RNAs, reduces MDSC migration, and synergizes with radiation and anti‑PD‑L1 to boost CD8⁺ T‑cell activity. YTHDC1 binds M6A‑modified promoter and enhancer RNAs (including LTR elements) in solid tumors, recruiting SETD1A/YY1 to open chromatin and drive transcription of lineage‑plastic, proliferative programs; inhibition of YTHDC1 collapses this transcriptional program and depletes cycling tumor cells.
key_findings: YTHDF2 knockout in MDSCs diminishes their suppressive capacity, reduces IL‑10 production, and enhances CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration after irradiation. | Combining YTHDF2 loss with radiation and anti‑PD‑L1 yields synergistic tumor control in mouse models, dependent on CD8⁺ T cells. | YTHDC1 inhibition (tool compound) markedly reduces viability of neuroendocrine prostate and small‑cell lung cancer cells in vitro and suppresses tumor growth in PDX models. | Loss of YTHDC1 leads to global chromatin closing (reduced DNase I signal, lower H3K27ac/H3K4me3) and down‑regulation of ~6,000 genes linked to LTR‑derived promoter RNAs. | Proteomics identifies SETD1A and YY1 as YTHDC1 interactors mediating transcriptional activation at open chromatin sites. | Single‑cell sequencing after YTHDC1 inhibition shows depletion of the cycling tumor cell population while non‑cycling cells persist.

talk_title: Precision diets targeting serine/glycine metabolism and combination with PHGDH inhibition
affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Molecular Medicine
tumor_types: lung cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; breast cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Limiting dietary serine and glycine reduces circulating one‑carbon metabolites, impairing nucleotide synthesis and antioxidant capacity in tumor cells, which slows growth in serine‑dependent GEM and organoid models. Tumors that up‑regulate de novo serine synthesis via PHGDH become resistant, but combined PHGDH inhibition restores sensitivity, especially when paired with radiotherapy that induces ROS.
key_findings: Serine/glycine‑free diet reduced tumor growth and metastasis in KRAS/LKB1 lung and KRAS/p53 pancreatic GEM models (p<0.01). | Combination of serine/glycine restriction with radiotherapy markedly increased tumor cell death compared with either modality alone (p<0.001). | PHGDH‑amplified tumors were resistant to dietary serine depletion, but addition of a PHGDH inhibitor restored tumor growth inhibition (tumor volume reduction 45%, p<0.01). | CRISPR dropout identified folate transporter as a synthetic lethal partner with serine/glycine depletion; knockdown abolished growth of resistant organoids. | Mice on serine/glycine‑free diet showed transient weight loss but no organ toxicity; cachexia markers GDF15 and IL6 were significantly lower (GDF15 ↓30%, IL6 ↓25%).

talk_title: Exercise‑derived extracellular vesicles suppress breast cancer metastasis via MTSS1 and NME2
topic_categories: exercise; tumor_microenvironment; metastasis; extracellular_vesicles; basic_science; platform_technology
moa_summary: Voluntary exercise increases the number and protein cargo of circulating small extracellular vesicles. Proteomic profiling identified MTSS1 and NME2 as enriched in exercise EVs; these proteins suppress EMT‑associated pathways, reducing breast cancer cell migration and metastasis when delivered via EVs, recapitulating the anti‑tumor effect of physical activity.
key_findings: Mice with voluntary wheel access ran ~7 km/night and showed ~35% reduction in primary tumor volume compared with locked‑wheel controls (p<0.01). | Metastatic lesions were reduced by >70% in running mice; mice running >6.5 km/day had the greatest metastasis‑free survival (p<0.001). | Exercise‑derived EVs injected into non‑running tumor‑bearing mice reproduced the tumor‑suppressive effect (tumor volume ↓30%, p<0.01). | Proteomic analysis identified 322 proteins significantly up‑regulated in exercise EVs; MTSS1 and NME2 were selected as anti‑metastatic candidates. | Overexpression of MTSS1 or NME2 in 41.2 cells inhibited migration in scratch assays by 50%–60% (p<0.001).

talk_title: Diet‑microbiome interactions in colorectal cancer prevention and the coffee‑microbiome trial
affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Department of Nutrition
topic_categories: microbiome; diet; epidemiology; prevention; early_detection; clinical_trial
moa_summary: Diet shapes gut microbial composition; unhealthy diets enrich CRC‑associated taxa (Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) while healthy diets promote butyrate‑producing microbes. Coffee consumption strongly enriches Lawsonia intracellularis, altering microbial metabolites and plasma metabolome, which is associated with improved survival in CRC patients.
key_findings: Microbiome changes are modest in incident polyps (AUC 0.52) but become pronounced in prevalent polyps (AUC 0.67) and strongest in established CRC (AUC 0.85). | A CRC‑microbial diet score derived from CRC‑associated microbes correlates positively with Western diet (r=0.62) and inversely with prudent diet (r=‑0.58). | Higher CRC‑microbial diet score is associated with increased colon cancer risk after multivariable adjustment (HR 1.34 per SD, p=0.004). | Coffee intake after CRC diagnosis is linked to a 30% reduction in mortality (HR 0.70, 95% CI 0.55‑0.89) independent of caffeine content. | Coffee consumption is the strongest single dietary factor predicting a distinct microbiome signature, driven by enrichment of Lawsonia intracellularis.

talk_title: Mutational signatures reveal hidden mutagenic and promoter exposures across cancers
affiliation: Wellcome Sanger Institute, Senior Group Leader
tumor_types: esophageal squamous carcinoma; renal clear cell cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Stratton described how whole-genome sequencing enables extraction of mutational signatures that act as permanent footprints of past carcinogen exposures, revealing both known mutagens (tobacco, aristolochic acids, colibactin) and many silent promoter agents that drive clonal expansion without increasing point mutation burden.
key_findings: Esophageal squamous carcinoma shows 10-20-fold geographic incidence variation but no distinct mutational signature, suggesting non‑mutagenic promoters drive risk. | Renal clear cell cancer in SE Europe harbors SBS22A/B signatures linked to aristolochic acid exposure in ~70% of cases. | A novel SBS12 signature is found exclusively in Japan, cause unknown. | Colorectal cancer early‑onset cases have >30% prevalence of SBS88 (colibactin) signature, present in normal colon of ~40% of UK children. | Only 3 of 20 tested carcinogens produced mutational signatures in mouse tumors; the rest act as promoters/selectogens.

talk_title: Promotion and selectogen mechanisms in tumor initiation and clonal competition
affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Professor
moa_summary: Balmain demonstrated that a single mutagenic initiation event (DMBA‑induced HRAS Q61L) remains dormant for months, and promotion by TPA selects specific mutant clones (HRAS Q61L) to expand into tumors, while many spontaneous RAS mutations expand transiently but are eliminated unless HRAS is absent, highlighting clonal competition and promoter selectivity.
key_findings: DMBA induces a permanent HRAS Q61L initiation mutation that can remain silent for a year before promotion triggers rapid tumor formation. | Only HRAS Q61L clones persist and give tumors after TPA promotion; other RAS mutants expand transiently then disappear. | In HRAS knockout mice, KRAS G13R clones, normally non‑tumorigenic, give rise to tumors under TPA promotion. | Promoter agents can act without increasing point‑mutation burden, supporting the selectogen concept.

talk_title: Epigenetic‑oncogenic cooperation reveals tissue‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities
affiliation: Harvard Medical School / Brigham and Women's Hospital, Professor
tumor_types: triple-negative breast cancer; colorectal cancer; prostate cancer; HER2‑positive breast cancer
moa_summary: Kochowski showed that simultaneous inhibition of EZH2 (PRC2) and oncogenic signaling (AKT, KRAS, PTEN, HER2) forces tumor cells into a differentiated, luminal‑like state via GATA3 induction and triggers an involution‑like apoptotic program mediated by JAK1‑STAT3‑BMF, leading to robust tumor regression in multiple tissue contexts.
key_findings: EZH2 + AKT inhibition kills ~60% of TNBC models synergistically, with no single‑agent activity. | Combination induces GATA3, shifts cells to mature luminal phenotype, and suppresses basal/stem markers. | BMF is the top up‑regulated pro‑apoptotic gene; its knockout blocks tumor regression. | Predictive transcriptional signature (basal vs mesenchymal) separates sensitive from resistant TNBC lines. | Similar EZH2‑RAS or EZH2‑AKT combos are effective in KRAS‑mutant colorectal, PTEN‑null prostate, and HER2‑positive breast cancers, each exploiting tissue‑specific differentiation pathways.

talk_title: Mechanistic dissection of cancer cachexia: brain‑tumor communication and metabolic drivers
affiliation: Rutgers Cancer Institute / Princeton University, Deputy Director
tumor_types: pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; lung cancer; colorectal cancer; breast cancer
topic_categories: cancer_cachexia; tumor_microenvironment; metabolism; neurobiology; clinical_trial; translational
moa_summary: White described how tumor‑derived cytokines (IL‑6, GDF15) act on distinct brain circuits to suppress appetite and induce apathy, while metabolic reprogramming in cachectic mice diverts tryptophan toward the kynurenine pathway, lowering serotonin and driving depression; pharmacologic restoration of serotonin improves mood but does not reverse muscle/fat wasting, indicating separable mechanisms.
key_findings: High‑fat diet worsens cachexia in mouse models via tumor‑derived prostaglandin E2 signaling through the vagus nerve. | Cachectic mice exhibit low serotonin and high kynurenine; SSRI treatment restores serotonin and improves depressive‑like behavior without affecting weight loss. | Two cachexia subtypes identified in patients: inflamed (high GDF15, IL‑6, severe symptoms, poorer survival) vs non‑inflamed (weight loss without systemic inflammation). | Longitudinal patient data reveal dynamic weight trajectories; some patients cycle between loss and stabilization. | Brain‑tumor communication pathways (GDF15‑GFRA1, IL‑6‑dopamine suppression) are key drivers of appetite loss and apathy.
primary_value: GDF15 median 2.5‑fold higher in red subtype vs orange

talk_title: Neutrophil extracellular traps drive tumor necrosis and metastasis
affiliation: Cancer Macroenvironment Lab, Francis Crick Institute
tumor_types: lung cancer; breast cancer; colorectal cancer
moa_summary: Tumor-derived signals induce a myeloid skew producing LY6C-low vascular-restricted neutrophils that aggregate in tumor vessels, form NETs, occlude perfusion, create downstream hypoxia and necrosis, and promote perinecrotic tumor cell EMT and metastasis.
key_findings: Tumors induce LY6C‑low neutrophils that are restricted to the vasculature and form abundant NETs. | Neutrophil aggregates block vessel perfusion, leading to downstream hypoxia and necrosis in mouse tumor models. | Genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of NET formation (disulfiram, DNase I, platelet‑neutrophil interaction blockade) reduces tumor necrosis and lung metastasis. | Perinecrotic tumor cells upregulate hypoxia, EMT, and TGF‑β pathways, enhancing metastatic traits.

talk_title: Ischemic tumor regions reprogram macrophage metabolism to suppress T‑cell killing
moa_summary: In ischemic tumor regions, macrophages upregulate arginase‑1, diverting ornithine into polyamine synthesis; excess polyamines are secreted as N1‑acetylspermidine, which tumor cells convert via PAOX to a metabolite that impairs antigen‑specific T‑cell cytotoxicity without altering MHC‑I expression.
